etymology.story: Old English misdæd "misdeed, evil deed, sin," common Germanic compound (compare Old Saxon misdad, Old Frisian misdede, Middle Dutch misdaet, German Missetat, Gothic missadeþs; see mis- (1) + deed (n.)..

early 15c., from Old French recliner "rest, lay; bend, lean over" (13c.) and directly from Latin reclinare "to bend back, to lean back; cause to lean," from re- "back, against" (see re-) + clinare "to bend," from PIE *klei-n-, suffixed form of *klei "to lean" (see lean (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 交战的；好战的；交战国的
交战国；参加斗殴的人或集团

etymology.story: 1570s, from Latin belligerantem (nominative belligerans), past participle of belligerare "to wage war," from bellum "war" (see bellicose) + gerere "to bear, to carry" (see gest).

early 14c., "to trip up, overthrow, defeat, dispossess," from Old French suplanter, sosplanter "to trip up, overthrow, drive out, usurp," or directly from Latin supplantare "trip up, overthrow," from sub "under" (see sub-) + planta "sole of the foot" (see plant (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 生硬的；突然的；唐突的；陡峭的

etymology.story: 1580s, from Latin abruptus "broken off, precipitous, disconnected," past participle of abrumpere "break off," from ab- "off" (see ab-) + rumpere "break" (see rupture (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 监督， 监视； 俯瞰， 眺望

etymology.story: Old English oferseon "to look down upon, keep watch over, survey, observe;" see over + see (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 自发的；自然的；无意识的

etymology.story: 1650s, "occurring without external stimulus," from Late Latin spontaneus "willing, of one's free will," from Latin (sua) sponte "of one's own accord, willingly;" of uncertain origin.
"fact of pretending," 1888, from children's talk, from pretend (v.).
late 15c., from Middle French licite or directly from Latin licitus "lawful," past participle of licere "be allowed, be lawful" (see licence (n.)).
1580s, probably from gloom (n.) even though that word is not attested as early as this one.

meanings.definitionCn: 根据；证明；正当理由；委任状

etymology.story: late 13c., "to keep safe from danger," from Old North French warantir "safeguard, protect; guarantee, pledge" (Old French garantir), from warant (see warrant (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 缺乏的；吝啬的；仅有的；稀疏的

morphology.mnemonic: act of scanning + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1650s, "meager, barely sufficient for use;" 1701, "too small, limited in scope," from scant + -y (2).

1590s, "force of expression," from Middle French énergie (16c.), from Late Latin energia, from Greek energeia "activity, action, operation," from energos "active, working," from en "at" (see en- (2)) + ergon "work, that which is wrought; business; action" (see organ).
1640s, from French déférence (16c.), from déférer (see defer (v.2))..

meanings.definitionCn: 固体的;结实的,稳固的,可靠的
豪华的， 华丽的， 富裕的； 丰富的， 丰饶的
叙事的，叙述的；叙事体的

etymology.story: "a tale, story," 1560s, from Middle French narrative and from narrative (adj.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 同时发生；巧合；一致；相符；

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 落入/巧合

etymology.story: 1705, "be identical in substance or nature," but from 1640s as a verb in English in Latin form, "occupy the same space, agree in position," from Medieval Latin coincidere (used in astrology), literally "to fall upon together," from Latin com- "together" (see co-) + incidere "to fall upon" (in- "upon" + cadere "to fall;" see case (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: ring环，圆 + 与…有关的/像…的

meanings.definitionCn: 爱开玩笑的人；顽皮的人；恶作剧的人

etymology.story: 1927, American English, from prank + -ster..

meanings.definitionCn: 有同情心的， 表示怜悯的

etymology.story: 1580s, from compassion + -ate (1).

1540s, from Middle French perspicacité (15c.) and directly from Late Latin perspicacitas "sharp-sightedness, discernment," from Latin perspicax "sharp-sighted, having the power of seeing through," from perspicere "look through, look closely at" (see perspective)..

meanings.definitionCn: 急躁的， 易怒的， 易受刺激的； 过敏的

etymology.story: 1660s, "susceptible to mental irritation," from French irritable and directly from Latin irritabilis "easily excited," from irritare "excite, provoke" (see irritate).
early 15c., from Old French triple or directly from Latin triplus (see triple (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 说话不多的， 内向的； 有所保留的， 预订的

etymology.story: "guarded" (in manner), c.

meanings.definitionCn: 脾气；韧度vt.调和，使缓和；使回火

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French munificence, from Latin munificentia "bountifulness, liberality, generosity," from stem of munificus "generous, bountiful, liberal," literally "present-making," from munus "gift or service, duty, office" (see municipal) + unstressed stem of facere "to do" (see factitious)..

meanings.definitionCn: 清晰的；易读的；易辨认的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Late Latin legibilis "that can be read, written plainly," from Latin legere "to read" (see lecture (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 恢复；重新开始；取回；重获；恢复硬币支付

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + sume + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., "repossessing by grant," from Middle French resumption and directly from Late Latin resumptionem (nominative resumptio), noun of action from past participle stem of Latin resumere (see resume (v.))..
1808, from obsolete verb resurge "to rise again" (1570s), from Latin resurgere "rise again, lift oneself, be restored," from re- "again" (see re-) + surgere "to rise" (see surge)..

meanings.definitionCn: 激动得发狂似的；狂暴的

etymology.story: mid-14c., "insane," unexplained variant of Middle English frentik (see frenetic).

meanings.definitionCn: 夸张的；夸大的；大言不惭的

etymology.story: 1590s, probably a back-formation from grandiloquence.

late 13c., from Old French statut, estatut "(royal) promulgation, (legal) statute," from Late Latin statutum "a law, decree," noun use of neuter past participle of Latin statuere "enact, establish," from status "condition, position" (see status)..

meanings.definitionCn: 心脏的；心脏病的；贲门的
物体;客体,对象;目标;宾语
能接近的； 易达到的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French indigence "indigence, need, privation" (13c.), from Latin indigentia "need, want; insatiable desire," from indigentem (nominative indigens) "in want of, needing," present participle of indigere "to need, stand in need of," from indu "in, within" (see indigenous) + egere "be in need, want," from PIE *eg- "to lack" (source also of Old Norse ekla "want, lack," Old High German eccherode "thin, weak")..

meanings.definitionCn: 值得注意的，显著的；著名的

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French notable "well-known, notable, remarkable" (13c.) and directly from Latin notabilis "noteworthy, extraordinary," from notare "to note" (see note (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 承认；容许；(比赛结束前)认输；退让

etymology.story: 1630s, from Middle French concéder or directly from Latin concedere "give way, yield, go away, depart, retire," figuratively "agree, consent, give precedence," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + cedere "to go, grant, give way" (see cede).

meanings.definitionCn: 无双的， 无比的， 空前的

etymology.story: 1590s, from un- (1) "not" + past participle of parallel (v.).
Old English mid-weg "the middle of a way or distance;" see mid + way (n.).
1620s, from Latin venosus "full of veins," from vena (see vein)..

meanings.definitionCn: 发泄；报仇；造成（巨大的破坏或伤害）

etymology.story: Old English wrecan "avenge," originally "to drive, drive out, punish" (class V strong verb; past tense wræc, past participle wrecen), from Proto-Germanic *wrekan (source also of Old Saxon wrekan, Old Norse reka, Old Frisian wreka, Middle Dutch wreken "to drive, push, compel, pursue, throw," Old High German rehhan, German rächen "to avenge," Gothic wrikan "to persecute"), from PIE root *wreg- "to push, shove, drive, track down" (see urge (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 蒸发,挥发;沉淀;发射;消失

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + vapor + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "draw off (humors or spirits) as vapor," from Late Latin evaporatum, past participle of evaporare "disperse in vapor" (see evaporation).

meanings.definitionCn: 委员会；佣金；犯；委任；委任状

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 发送 + 状态/行为/过程
muli + 折叠/交织 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French multiplicacion (12c.) "multiplication, duplication; multiplicity, diversity," from Latin multiplicationem (nominative multiplicatio), noun of action from past participle stem of multiplicare (see multiply).

late 14c., "pertaining to reason;" mid-15c., "endowed with reason," from Old French racionel and directly from Latin rationalis "of or belonging to reason, reasonable," from ratio (genitive rationis) "reckoning, calculation, reason" (see ratio)..
1630s, from French rescinder "cut off, cancel" (15c.), and directly from Latin rescindere "to cut off, tear off, abolish," from re- "back" (see re-) + scindere "to cut, split" (see shed (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 测试/证明 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "eliciting," from Middle French provocatif (15c.) and directly from Late Latin provocativus "calling forth," from provocat-, past participle stem of Latin provocare (see provoke).

name of a group of sugars (in commercial use, "sugar-syrup from starch"), 1840, from French glucose (1838), said to have been coined by French professor Eugène Melchior Péligot (1811-1890) from Greek gleukos "must, sweet wine," related to glykys "sweet" (see gluco-).

meanings.definitionCn: 咸水的；含盐的，咸味的；盐腌的；猥亵的
用盐腌；给…加盐；将盐撒在道路上使冰或雪融化

etymology.story: Old English sealtan, from Proto-Germanic *salto- (see salt (n.)), and in part from the noun.

meanings.definitionCn: 使充满激情（impassion的过去式和过去分词）

etymology.story: "expressive of strong feeling, filled with passion," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 缩短了的；被删节的；切去顶端的
缩短（truncate的过去分词）；截去…的顶端

etymology.story: late 15c., past participle adjective from truncate.

"shamelessness, impudence, boldness in transgressing the bounds of modesty and propriety," 1715, from French effronterie, from effronté "shameless," from Old French esfronte "shameless, brazen," probably from Late Latin effrontem (nominative effrons) "barefaced, shameless," from Latin ex- "out" (see ex-) + frontem (nominative frons) "brow" (see front (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 职业;召唤;天命;天职;才能

etymology.story: early 15c., "spiritual calling," from Old French vocacion "call, consecration; calling, profession" (13c.) or directly from Latin vocationem (nominative vocatio), literally "a calling, a being called" from vocatus "called," past participle of vocare "to call" (see voice (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 占优势的；突出的；压倒性的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Latin praeponderantem (nominative praeponderans), present participle of praeponderare (see preponderate)..

meanings.definitionCn: 身高，身材；（精神、道德等的）高度

etymology.story: early 14c., "natural height of a body, height," from Old French stature, estature "build, structure," from Latin statura "height, size of body, size, growth," from PIE *ste-tu-, from root *stā- "to stand," with derivatives meaning "place or thing that is standing" (see stet).
"having the qualities of a dog" (mostly in a negative sense), c.

meanings.definitionCn: [等离子] 等离子体；血浆；[矿物] 深绿玉髓

etymology.story: 1712, "form, shape" (earlier plasm), from Late Latin plasma, from Greek plasma "something molded or created," hence "image, figure; counterfeit, forgery; formed style, affectation," from plassein "to mold," originally "to spread thin," from PIE *plath-yein, from root *pele- (2) "flat, to spread" (see plane (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + orbit + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: mid-15c., a legal term, "deviating from rule or principle, eccentric;" from Late Latin exorbitantem (nominative exorbitans), present participle of exorbitare "deviate, go out of the track," from ex- "out of" (see ex-) + orbita "wheel track" (see orb).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + in + 钱包/偿还

etymology.story: 1610s, from re- "back" + imburse "to pay, enrich," literally "put in a purse" (c.

morphology.mnemonic: 暴力/违反 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French violent or directly from Latin violentus, related to violare (see violation).

meanings.definitionCn: 难达到的；难接近的；难见到的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French inaccessible (14c.), from Late Latin inaccessibilis "unapproachable," from in- "not" (see in- (1)) + accessibilis "approachable" (see accessible).

1560s, "to stand in front of," from Middle French confronter (15c.), from Medieval Latin confrontare "assign limits, adjoin," from Latin com- "together" (see com-) + frontem (nominative frons) "forehead" (see front (n.)).
1640s, from Latin onus "load, burden," figuratively "tax, expense; trouble, difficulty," from PIE *en-es- "burden" (source of Sanskrit anah "cart, wagon").
1610s, of medicines, from Latin catharticus, from Greek kathartikos "fit for cleansing, purgative," from katharsis "purging, cleansing" (see catharsis).

meanings.definitionCn: 复调；对位法；旋律配合；对应物

etymology.story: early 15c., of stitching, from Old French cuilte contrepointe "quilt stitched through and through," altered from coute pointe, from Medieval Latin culcita puncta "quilted mattress," from Latin culcita "cushion" + puncta, fem.
1650s, back-formation from obesity and in part from Latin obesus "fat, stout, plump," past participle of obedere "that has eaten itself fat" (see obesity).

meanings.definitionCn: 起伏，波动；震动；呈波浪形
波动的；起伏的；波浪形的

etymology.story: "to move in waves," 1660s, back-formation from undulation.
1630s, from French béatifique or directly from Late Latin beatificus, from Latin beatus "blessed, happy," past participle of beare "make happy, bless" (see beatitude).

meanings.definitionCn: 生殖;翻版;繁殖;复制,仿造

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 向前/支持 + 引导

etymology.story: 1610s, "to produce again," from re- "again" + produce (v.), probably on model of French reproduire (16c.).

meanings.definitionCn: 骨架，骨骼；纲要；骨瘦如柴的人
骨骼的；骨瘦如柴的；概略的

etymology.story: 1570s, from Modern Latin sceleton "bones, bony framework of the body," from Greek skeleton soma "dried-up body, mummy, skeleton," from neuter of skeletos "dried-up" (also, as a noun, "dried body, mummy"), from skellein "dry up, make dry, parch," from PIE root *skele- "to parch, wither" (see sclero-).

meanings.definitionCn: 文盲的；不识字的；没受教育的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 石 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "uneducated, unable to read and write" (originally meaning Latin), from Latin illiteratus "unlearned, unlettered, ignorant; without culture, inelegant," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + literatus "educated," literally "furnished with letters" (see literate).

meanings.definitionCn: 同盟的；联邦制度下的；联合的
使结成同盟；使结成联邦

etymology.story: 1814 (implied in federated), a back-formation from federation, or else from Latin foederatus "leagued, federated, combined; having a treaty, bound by treaty," past participle of foederare "to establish by treaty," from foedus "covenant, treaty, alliance" (see federal).

morphology.mnemonic: in + to burn, set on fire + able

etymology.story: "able to be set alight," c.
1650s, from Latin pervadere "spread or go through," from per- "through" + vadere "to go" (see vamoose).

meanings.definitionCn: 古怪的，反复无常的，不稳定的

etymology.story: late 14c., "wandering, moving," from Old French erratique "wandering, vagrant" (13c.) and directly from Latin erraticus "wandering, straying, roving," from erratum "an error, mistake, fault," past participle of errare "to wander; to err" (see err).

meanings.definitionCn: 可辩解的，有道理的；可证明为正当的

etymology.story: "capable of being proved just or true, morally defensible," 1520s, from Old French justifiable, from justifiier (see justify).

meanings.definitionCn: 焚化， 焚毁， 把…烧成灰烬

etymology.story: "burn to ashes" (transitive), 1550s, from Medieval Latin incineratus, past participle of incinerare "reduce to ashes," from in- "into" (see in- (2)) + a verb from Latin cinis (genitive cineris) "ashes," from PIE root *keni- "dust, ashes" (source also of Greek konis "dust").
1640s, from French cajolerie "persuasion by flattery" (16c.), from cajoler (see cajole)..

morphology.mnemonic: to honor very highly + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1540s, from Middle French adoration, from Latin adorationem (nominative adoratio) "worship, adoration," noun of action from past participle stem of adorare; see adore, the original sense of which is preserved in this word..

meanings.definitionCn: 睿智的，聪慧的；有远见的，聪慧的

etymology.story: 1650s, "of or pertaining to the hair," from Latin capillaris "of hair," from capillus "hair" (of the head); perhaps related to caput "head" (but de Vaan finds this "difficult on the formal side" and "far from compelling, since capillus is a diminutive, and would mean 'little head', which hardly amounts to 'hair'").

meanings.definitionCn: 侵蚀， 腐蚀； 削弱， 损害

etymology.story: 1610s, "gnaw or eat away" (transitive), a back-formation from erosion, or else from French éroder, from Latin erodere "to gnaw away, consume," from assimilated form of ex- "away" (see ex-) + rodere "gnaw" (see rodent).

Old English besettan "to put, place; own, keep; occupy, settle; cover, surround with, besiege," from Proto-Germanic *bisatjan (source also of Old Saxon bisettjan, Dutch bezetten, Old High German bisezzan, German besetzen, Gothic bisatjan); see be- + set (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 舒适地坐定；偎依；半隐半现地处于

etymology.story: Old English nestlian "build a nest," from nest (see nest (n.)) + suffix -el (3).
mid-15c., from Latin frustratus, past participle of frustrari "to deceive, disappoint, make vain," from frustra (adv.) "in vain, in error," related to fraus "injury, harm" (see fraud).

1727, from Latin sparsus "scattered," past participle of spargere "to scatter, spread," from PIE root *(s)preg- (2) "to jerk, scatter" (source also of Sanskrit parjanya- "rain, rain god," Avestan fra-sparega "branch, twig," literally "that which is jerked off a tree," Old Norse freknur "freckles," Swedish dialectal sprygg "brisk, active," Lithuanian sprogti "shoot, bud," Old Irish arg "a drop").

meanings.definitionCn: 务实的； 实事求是的； 实用主义观点的

etymology.story: 1610s, "meddlesome, impertinently busy," short for earlier pragmatical, or else from Middle French pragmatique (15c.), from Latin pragmaticus "skilled in business or law," from Greek pragmatikos "fit for business, active, business-like; systematic," from pragma (genitive pragmatos) "a deed, act; that which has been done; a thing, matter, affair," especially an important one; also a euphemism for something bad or disgraceful; in plural, "circumstances, affairs" (public or private), often in a bad sense, "trouble," literally "a thing done," from stem of prassein/prattein "to do, act, perform" (see practical).
1863, coined by Francis Galton (1822-1911), English polymath, explorer, and meteorologist, from anti- + cyclone.

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 咬，啮 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1540s, from Middle French erosion (16c.), from Latin erosionem (nominative erosio) "a gnawing away," noun of action from past participle stem of erodere "gnaw away" (see erosion).
"inflammable colorless and odorless gas, marsh gas," 1867, coined from chemical suffix -ane + syllable abstracted from methyl..

morphology.mnemonic: condition of general abundance + 具有…性质的

meanings.definitionCn: 迷信的； 根据迷信的， 由迷信引起的； 受迷信思想支配的

etymology.story: late 14c., "involving faith in supernatural powers or magic; characteristic of pagan religion or false religion," from Anglo-French supersticius, Old French supersticios, or directly from Latin superstitiosus "prophetic; full of dread of the supernatural," from superstitio "prophecy, soothsaying, excessive fear of the gods" (see superstition)..

meanings.definitionCn: 神经的；神经系统的；背的；神经中枢的

morphology.mnemonic: 神经 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "pertaining to a nerve or nerves," 1830, from Greek neuron "nerve" (see neuro-) + adjectival suffix -al (1).
early 15c., "make away with money or property of another, steal," from Anglo-French enbesiler "to steal, cause to disappear" (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 心不在焉的， 出神的

etymology.story: 1660s, from French élasticité, or else from elastic + -ity..

1640s, from French émollient (16c.), from Latin emollientem (nominative emolliens), present participle of emollire "to make soft, soften," from assimilated form of ex- "out" (see ex-) + mollire "soften," from mollis "soft" (see melt (v.)).
1690s, "glass to regulate light rays," from Latin lens (genitive lentis) "a lentil," on analogy of the double-convex shape.

meanings.definitionCn: 体现，使具体化；具体表达

etymology.story: 1540s, in reference to a soul or spirit invested with a physical form; from 1660s of principles, ideas, etc.; from em- (1) "in" + body (n.).
"entertain splendidly," 1650s, from French régaler "to entertain or feast," from Old French regale, rigale, from gale "merriment," from galer "make merry" (see gallant (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 可证实的；能作证的；可检验的

etymology.story: 1590s, from verify + -able.

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 抓住/理解 + able

etymology.story: 1520s, "able to be contained," from Latin comprehensibilis, from comprehensus, past participle of comphrehendere (see comprehend).

1620s, "set free from control," from Latin emancipatus, past participle of emancipare "put (a son) out of paternal authority, declare (someone) free, give up one's authority over," in Roman law, the freeing of a son or wife from the legal authority (patria potestas) of the pater familias, to make his or her own way in the world; from assimilated form of ex- "out, away" (see ex-) + mancipare "deliver, transfer or sell," from mancipum "ownership," from manus "hand" (see manual (adj.)) + capere "take" (see capable).
1650s, "elliptic in shape;" see elliptic + -al (1).

"underground part of a plant," late Old English rot, from a Scandinavian source akin to Old Norse rot "root," figuratively "cause, origin," from Proto-Germanic *wrot (source also of Old English wyrt "root, herb, plant," Old High German wurz, German Wurz "a plant," Gothic waurts "a root," with characteristic Scandinavian loss of -w- before -r-), from PIE *wrad- (see radish (n.), and compare wort).

meanings.definitionCn: 占有的；所有的；所有格的；占有欲强的

morphology.mnemonic: to hold as property + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1828 in reference to the philosophy; 1844 as "self-indulgence," from Greek hedone "pleasure" (see hedonist) + -ism..

meanings.definitionCn: 投射；规划；突出；发射；推测

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + 投/掷 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 15c., in alchemy, "transmutation by casting a powder on molten metal; 1550s in the cartographical sense "drawing of a map or chart according to scale," from Middle French projection, from Latin proiectionem (nominative proiectio), from past participle stem of proicere (see project (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 乘；使增加；使繁殖；使相乘

etymology.story: mid-12c., multeplier, "to cause to become many," from Old French multiplier, mouteplier (12c.) "increase, get bigger; flourish; breed; extend, enrich," from Latin multiplicare "to increase," from multiplex (genitive multiplicis) "having many folds, many times as great in number," from comb.

meanings.definitionCn: （在重量、数量、力量等方面）占优势

etymology.story: 1610s, "to weigh more than," from Latin praeponderatus, past participle of praeponderare "outweigh, make heavier," from prae "before" (see pre-) + ponderare "to weigh" (see pound (n.1)).
1784, literally "rainbow-colored," coined from Latin iris (genitive iridis) "rainbow" (see iris).

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1680s, from approve + -al (2).
early 15c., from Middle French cartilage (16c.) and directly from Latin cartilaginem (nominative cartilago) "cartilage, gristle," possibly related to Latin crates "wickerwork.".
1640s, "involving many parts," from French multiple (14c.), from Late Latin multiplus "manifold," from Latin multi- "many, much" (see multi-) + -plus "-fold" (see -plus).
late 14c., "exorcism," from Late Latin adjurationem (nominative adjuratio) "a swearing to," noun of action from past participle stem of adjurare (see adjure).

meanings.definitionCn: 通用的;多才多艺的,多方面的
磁铁；[电磁] 磁体；磁石

etymology.story: Old English bemænan "to bemoan, wail, lament;" see be- + moan (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 承认；公开宣称；坦率承认

etymology.story: early 13c., from Anglo-French avouer, Old French avoer "acknowledge, accept, recognize," especially as a protector (Modern French avouer), from Latin advocare (see advocate).

meanings.definitionCn: [生态] 苔原；[地理] 冻原；冻土地带

etymology.story: an Arctic steppe, 1841, from Russian tundra, from Lappish (Finno-Ugric) tundar, said to mean "elevated wasteland, high-topped hill," or "a marshy plain.".

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 爆发/鼓掌 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1620s, "action of driving out with violence and noise," from French explosion, from Latin explosionem (nominative explosio) "a driving off by clapping," noun of action from past participle stem of explodere "drive out by clapping" (see explode for origin and sense evolution).

meanings.definitionCn: 使悲痛；冒犯；侵害…的合法权利

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old French agrever "make worse; become worse," from Latin aggravare "make heavier" (see aggravation).

1560s, "wild, unruly" (originally in reference to hawks), from Middle French haggard, probably from Old French faulcon hagard "wild falcon," literally "falcon of the woods," from hagard, hagart, from Middle High German hag "hedge, copse, wood," from Proto-Germanic *hagon, from PIE root *kagh- "to catch, seize;" also "wickerwork, fence" (see hedge (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 杂食的；什么都读的；无所不吃的

morphology.mnemonic: 全部 + vor + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1650s, from Latin omnivorus "all-devouring," from omnis "all" (see omni-) + vorare "devour, swallow" (see voracity).

meanings.definitionCn: 尖锐的；辛酸的；深刻的；切中要害的

etymology.story: late 14c., "painful to physical or mental feeling" (of sauce, spice, wine as well as things that affect the feelings), from Old French poignant "sharp, pointed" (13c.), present participle of poindre "to prick, sting," from Latin pungere "to prick, pierce, sting," figuratively, "to vex, grieve, trouble, afflict," related to pugnus "a fist" (see pugnacious).

mid-14c., "removal of a saint's body or relics to a new place," also "rendering of a text from one language to another," from Old French translacion "translation" of text, also of the bones of a saint, etc.

Old English cancer "spreading sore, cancer" (also canceradl), from Latin cancer "a crab," later, "malignant tumor," from Greek karkinos, which, like the Modern English word, has three meanings: crab, tumor, and the zodiac constellation (late Old English), from PIE root *qarq- "to be hard" (like the shell of a crab); source also of Sanskrit karkatah "crab," karkarah "hard;" and perhaps cognate with PIE root *qar-tu- "hard, strong," source of English hard.
1837, from French personnel (a contrastive term to matériel), noun use of personnel (adj.) "personal," from Old French personel (see personal)..

morphology.mnemonic: 使/变成 + advance rapidly, make progress

etymology.story: Old English bestridan "to bestride, mount," from be- + stridan "to stride" (see stride (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 难治的；难熔的；不听话的

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 打破 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: "stubborn, obstinate, perverse," 1610s (earlier refractorious, 1550s, refractary, c.

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 提起/相关 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1680s, from assimilated form of in- (1) "not, opposite of" + relevant.
"to heap up," mid-14c.; see pile (n.1).

meanings.definitionCn: 艺术性；工艺；艺术技巧；艺术效果；艺术工作

morphology.mnemonic: 技艺/技巧 + …主义者/…专家 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: "artistic ability," 1837, from artist + -ry; as chemistry from chemist, etc..
1823, "cheerful," from Latin hilaris "cheerful, lively, merry, joyful, of good cheer" (see hilarity) + -ous.

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 死亡 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., "deathless," from Latin immortalis "deathless, undying" (of gods), "imperishable, endless" (of fame, love, work, etc.), from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + mortalis "mortal" (see mortal (adj.)).

morphology.mnemonic: hang out + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1560s, "causing a sensation of cold," from chill (n.) + -y (2).

meanings.definitionCn: 神话；虚构的人，虚构的事

etymology.story: 1830, from French Mythe (1818) and directly from Modern Latin mythus, from Greek mythos "speech, thought, story, myth, anything delivered by word of mouth," of unknown origin.General sense of "untrue story, rumor" is from 1840..

meanings.definitionCn: 含义；暗示；牵连，卷入；可能的结果，影响

morphology.mnemonic: in + 折叠/交织 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., "action of entangling," from Latin implicationem (nominative implicatio) "an interweaving, an entanglement," noun of state from past participle stem of implicare "involve, entangle; embrace; connect closely, associate," from assimilated form of in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + plicare "to fold" (see ply (v.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 补品；兴奋剂；甜香酒，甘露酒

morphology.mnemonic: 心 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., "of the heart," from Middle French cordial, from Medieval Latin cordialis "of or for the heart," from Latin cor (genitive cordis) "heart," from PIE root *kerd- (1) "heart" (see heart (n.)).
"fragrant," early 15c., from Medieval Latin odorosus, from Latin odorus "having a smell," from odor (see odor)..

meanings.definitionCn: 命令;次序;整齐;定货单;等级

etymology.story: early 13c., carione, from Anglo-French carogne (Old North French caroigne; Old French charogne, 12c., "carrion, corpse," Modern French charogne), from Vulgar Latin *caronia "carcass" (source of Italian carogna, Spanish carroña "carrion"), from Latin caro "meat" (see carnage)..

morphology.mnemonic: 呼叫/声音 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: early 15c., "showing the person or thing spoken to," from Middle French vocatif, from Latin vocativus (casus) "(case of) calling," from vocatus, past participle of vocare "to call" (see voice (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 下流的；说话粗鄙恶劣的；无礼的

etymology.story: "using such language as only the licence of a buffoon can warrant" [Johnson], 1570s, from scurrile "coarsely joking" (c.
1610s, in music, back-formation from modulation, or else from Latin modulatus, past participle of modulari.
1520s, from Latin indelebilis "indelible, imperishable," from in- "not, opposite of, without" (see in- (1)) + delebilis "able to be destroyed," from delere "destroy, blot out" (see delete).

late 14c., "happiness; that which is a source of happiness," from Old French felicite "happiness" (14c.), from Latin felicitatem (nominative felicitas) "happiness, fertility," from felix (genitive felicis) "happy, fortunate, fruitful, fertile," from Latin root *fe-, equivalent of PIE *dhe(i)- "to suck, suckle, produce, yield" (see fecund).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 声音 + ance

etymology.story: mid-15c., in acoustics, "prolongation of sound by reverberation;" 1660s, "act of resonating;" from Middle French resonance (15c.), from Latin resonantia "echo," from resonare "to sound again" (see resound).

meanings.definitionCn: 快乐，兴高采烈；庆祝活动，喜庆；（服饰）华丽，艳丽

etymology.story: "cheerfulness, mirth," 1630s, from French gaieté (Old French gaiete, 12c.), from gai "gay" (see gay).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 形状/形态
变换 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "action of changing," from Old French mutacion (13c.), and directly from Latin mutationem (nominative mutatio) "a changing, alteration, a turn for the worse," noun of action from past participle stem of mutare "to change" (see mutable).

"pale yellow, brownish yellow," Old English fealu "reddish yellow, yellowish-brown, tawny, dusk-colored" (of flame, birds' feet, a horse, withered grass or leaves, waters, roads), from Proto-Germanic *falwa- (source also of Old Saxon falu, Old Norse fölr, Middle Dutch valu, Dutch vaal, Old High German falo, German falb), from PIE *pal-wo- "dark-colored, gray" (source also of Old Church Slavonic plavu, Lithuanian palvas "sallow;" Greek polios "gray" (of hair, wolves, waves), Sanskrit palitah, Welsh llwyd "gray;" Latin pallere "to be pale"), suffixed form of root *pel- (2) "pale" (see pallor).

morphology.mnemonic: 搬运/承担 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., in alchemy, with a broad sense; modern scientific sense is from c.
1590s (agent noun homager is from c.
mid-15c., "turned away in mind or feeling," from Old French avers and directly from Latin aversus "turned away, turned back," past participle of avertere (see avert).

meanings.definitionCn: 接替的人或事物，继任者

etymology.story: "one who comes after," late 13c., from Anglo-French successor and Old French successour "successor, heir" (12c., Modern French successeur), from Latin successor "follower, successor," agent noun from past participle stem of succedere "to come after" (see succeed)..

meanings.definitionCn: 讽刺；反语；具有讽刺意味的事

morphology.mnemonic: shackle with irons + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: "of or resembling iron," late 14c., from iron (n.) + -y (2)..

morphology.mnemonic: 命令/委托 + 使…/具有…的 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1570s, "of the nature of a mandate," from Late Latin mandatorius "pertaining to a mandator," from Latin mandatus, past participle of mandare (see mandate (n.)).

"a number of symptoms occurring together," 1540s, from medical Latin, from Greek syndrome "concurrence of symptoms, concourse of people," from syndromos "place where several roads meet," literally "a running together," from syn- "with" (see syn-) + dromos "a running, course" (see dromedary).

morphology.mnemonic: neutr + 与…有关的/形容词 + ity

etymology.story: late 15c., "the neutral party in any dispute," from Middle French neutralite (14c.) or directly from Medieval Latin neutralitatem (nominative neutralitas), from Latin neutralis (see neutral).

1705, from Latin recumbentem (nominative recumbens), present participle of recumbere "recline, lie down, lie down again;" of things, "to fall, sink down, settle down," from re- "back" (see re-) + -cumbere "to lie down" (see succumb).

meanings.definitionCn: 精力充沛的；积极的；有力的

etymology.story: 1650s, "powerful in operation," from Greek energetikos "active," from energein "to work, be in action, act upon" (see energy).

early 15c., "mathematical quantity obtained by multiplication," from Medieval Latin productum, in classical Latin "something produced," noun use of neuter past participle of producere "bring forth" (see produce (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 在前/预先 + to decree, enact

etymology.story: 1711 in the political sense, a shortening of premier minister (1680s); see premier (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 智力的，理智的，有理解力的
开拓；回收再利用；改造某人，使某人悔改

etymology.story: early 14c., "call back a hawk to the glove," from Old French reclamer "to call upon, invoke; claim; seduce; to call back a hawk" (12c.) and directly from Latin reclamare "cry out against, contradict, protest, appeal," from re- "opposite, against" (see re-) + clamare "cry out" (see claim (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 可互换的；可交换的；可交替的

morphology.mnemonic: 之间/相互 + a different situation + able

etymology.story: late 14c., entrechaungeable, "mutual, reciprocal," from inter- + changeable.

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French allegorie (12c.), from Latin allegoria, from Greek allegoria "figurative language, description of one thing under the image of another," literally "a speaking about something else," from allos "another, different" (see alias (adv.)) + agoreuein "speak openly, speak in the assembly," from agora "assembly" (see agora)..

morphology.mnemonic: naut + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1550s, from -al (1) + nautic from Middle French nautique, from Latin nauticus "pertaining to ships or sailors," from Greek nautikos "seafaring, naval," from nautes "sailor," from naus "ship," from PIE *nau- (2) "boat" (see naval)..

root.meaning: attract and occupy the attention of

morphology.mnemonic: 不/分离 + attract and occupy the attention of

etymology.story: "speak rapidly and inarticulately," c.

1670s, "secret or private stories," from French anecdote (17c.) or directly from Greek anekdota "things unpublished," neuter plural of anekdotos, from an- "not" (see an-) + ekdotos "published," from ek- "out" + didonai "to give" (see date (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 奢侈， 挥霍； 放肆的言行

etymology.story: 1640s, "an extravagant act," from French extravagance, from Late Latin extravagantem (see extravagant).

meanings.definitionCn: 脾气坏的；爱吵架的；难相处的

etymology.story: 1772, said to be "a Wiltshire word," probably from an alteration (influenced by raucous) of Middle English contakour "troublemaker" (c.

"fame, renown, glory," 1799, probably originally in university slang, from Greek kydos "glory, fame," especially in battle, "a poetical word, found chiefly in the Iliad and Odyssey" [Century Dictionary], literally "that which is heard of," from PIE root *skeue- "to pay attention, perceive" (see caveat).

meanings.definitionCn: 不可逆的；不能取消的；不能翻转的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 再/重新/回 + metrical composition + able

etymology.story: 1620s, of decrees, etc., "that cannot be overturned or undone," from assimilated form of in- (1) "not, opposite of" + reversible.

meanings.definitionCn: 腼腆的；忸怩作态的；怕羞的

etymology.story: early 14c., "quiet, modest, demure," from Old French coi, earlier quei "quiet, still, placid, gentle," ultimately from Latin quietus "resting, at rest" (see quiet (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 和平 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: late 15c., "appease, allay the anger of (someone)," from Middle French pacifier "make peace," from Latin pacificare "to make peace; pacify," from pacificus (see pacific).

"planking or woodwork round the uppermost parts of a vessel," early 15c., from Middle Dutch bulwerke or Middle High German bolwerc, probably from bole "plank, tree trunk" (from Proto-Germanic *bul-, from PIE root *bhel- (2) "to blow, swell;" see bole) + werc "work" (see work (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 相同/识别 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1610s as a term in logic; general sense of "being the same or very similar" is from 1630s, from Medieval Latin identicus "the same," from Late Latin identitas "identity, sameness," ultimately from comb.

meanings.definitionCn: 卑鄙的；可怜的；不幸的；（境况）凄惨的，绝望的

etymology.story: early 15c., "cast off, rejected," from Latin abiectus, past participle of abicere "to throw away, cast off; degrade, humble, lower," from ab- "away, off" (see ab-) + iacere "to throw" (past participle iactus; see jet (v.)).

1660s, "long speech by one person," from French monologue, from Late Greek monologos "speaking alone," from Greek monos "single, alone" (see mono-) + logos "speech, word," from legein "to speak" (see lecture (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 无可争议的；无疑的；明白的

etymology.story: 1640s, from in- (1) "not" + controvertible (see controvert).

meanings.definitionCn: 野心勃勃的；有雄心的；热望的；炫耀的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Latin ambitiosus "going around to canvass for office," from ambitio (see ambition).
1590s, from Latin contemptus (see contempt).
1580s, "relating to an academy," also "collegiate, scholarly," from Latin academicus "of the Academy," from academia (see academy).

Old English grislic (in compounds) "horrible, dreadful," from root of grisan "to shudder, fear," a general Germanic word (cognates: Old Frisian grislik "horrible," Middle Dutch grisen "to shudder," Dutch griezelen, German grausen "to shudder, fear," Old High German grisenlik "horrible;" of unknown origin; Watkins connects it with the PIE root *ghrei- "to rub," on notion of "to grate on the mind" (see chrism).
1660s, from French révérer, from Latin revereri "revere, fear" (see reverence (n.), which also was the earlier form of the verb).
1660s, from Latin salax (genitive salacis) "lustful," probably originally "fond of leaping," as in a male animal leaping on a female in sexual advances, from salire "to leap" (see salient (adj.)).

mid-14c., from Old French comparaison (12c.), from Latin comparationem (nominative comparatio), noun of action from past participle stem of comparare "make equal with, liken, bring together for a contest," literally "to couple together, to form in pairs," from com- "with" (see com-) + parare "prepare" (see pare)..

meanings.definitionCn: 法律,法规,法学,规律,定律
有害的；恶性的；致命的；险恶的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French pernicios (13c., Modern French pernicieux) and directly from Latin perniciosus "destructive," from pernicies "destruction, death, ruin," from per- "completely" (see per) + necis "violent death, murder," related to necare "to kill," nocere "to hurt, injure, harm," noxa "harm, injury" (see noxious).
also centilitre, 1801, from French centilitre; see centi- + liter..

meanings.definitionCn: 回复；重提；返祖遗传；归还

etymology.story: "plant-eating animal," 1851, from Modern Latin Herbivora (1830) or French herbivore (1748), from neuter plural of Latin herbivorus, from herba "a plant" (see herb) + vorare "devour, swallow" (see voracity)..
early 15c., from Medieval Latin successivus "successive," from success-, stem of Latin succedere "to come after" (see succeed).
"to retail," 1837 in modern use, a colloquial back-formation from peddler.

meanings.definitionCn: 违反；妨碍，侵害；违背；强奸

morphology.mnemonic: 暴力/违反 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程
meta + bol + 主义/学说/运动

etymology.story: in physiology sense, 1878, from French métabolisme, from Greek metabole "a change," from metaballein "to change," from meta- "over" (see meta-) + ballein "to throw" (see ballistics)..

1540s, "to disown, disinherit (children)," from Latin abdicatus, past participle of abdicare "to disown, disavow, reject" (specifically abdicare magistratu "renounce office"), from ab- "away" (see ab-) + dicare "proclaim" (see diction).

meanings.definitionCn: 放射（过去式emanated，过去分词emanated，现在分词emanating，第三人称单数emanates，形容词emanative）；发散

etymology.story: 1680s, "to flow out," from Latin emanatus, past participle of emanare "flow out," figuratively "arise from, proceed from" (see emanation).

meanings.definitionCn: 顽固的人；不知悔改的人

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + pen + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin impaenitentem, from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + paenitens (see penitence).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 呼叫/声音 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1520s, "a calling away from one's occupation," from Latin avocationem (nominative avocatio) "a calling away, distraction, diversion," noun of action from past participle stem of avocare, from ab- "off, away from" (see ab-) + vocare "to call" (see voice (n.))..
1520s, from Latin exterior "outward, outer, exterior," comparative of exterus "on the outside, outward, outer, of another country, foreign," itself a comparative of ex "out of" (see ex-).

meanings.definitionCn: 指责， 谴责， 责难

morphology.mnemonic: 发光/闪耀 + 行为/过程/结果

meanings.definitionCn: 技巧，手艺，技能；技术，工艺

etymology.story: 1817, at first especially in criticism of art and music, from French technique "formal practical details in artistic expression" (18c.), noun use of technique (adj.) "of art, technical," from Greek tekhnikos "pertaining to art," from tekhne "art, skill, craft in work" (see techno-)..

early 13c., chatel "property, goods," from Old French chatel "chattels, goods, wealth, possessions, property; profit; cattle," from Late Latin capitale "property" (see cattle, which is the Old North French form of the same word).
"an act of levying," early 15c., from Anglo-French leve, Old French levée "a raising, lifting; levying," noun use of fem.
1640s, from Medieval Latin intimidatus, past participle of intimidare "to frighten, make afraid," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + Latin timidus "fearful" (see timid).

meanings.definitionCn: 仇外；害怕生人者；畏惧和憎恨外国人的人
[电子] 定时器；计时器；计时员，记时员；跑表；延时调节器

etymology.story: 1908 as a mechanical device, agent noun from time (v.)..

late 14c., "abnormally copious flow," from Old French flus "a flowing, a rolling; a bleeding" (Modern French flux), or directly from Latin fluxus (adj.) "flowing, loose, slack," past participle of fluere "to flow" (see fluent).

meanings.definitionCn: 巧合；同时发生，共同存在；符合，一致

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 落入/巧合 + ance

etymology.story: 1650s; see detriment + -al (1).
1580s, from well (adv.) + agent noun from wish (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 认输，屈服；屈从，停止反抗；有条件投降；让步

etymology.story: 1570s, "to draw up in chapters" (i.e., under "heads"), in part a back-formation from capitulation, in part from Medieval Latin capitulatus, past participle of capitulare "to draw up in heads or chapters, arrange conditions." Often of terms of surrender, hence meaning "to yield on stipulated terms" (1680s).
1610s, "state of celibacy" (especially as mandated to clergy in the Catholic church) from French célibat (16c.), from Latin caelibatus (see celibacy).

meanings.definitionCn: 替代的；代理的；发同感的

etymology.story: 1630s, "taking the place of another," from Latin vicarius "that supplies a place; substituted, delegated," from vicis "a change, exchange, interchange; succession, alternation, substitution," from PIE root *weik- (4) "to bend, wind" (source also of Sanskrit visti "changing, changeable;" Old English wician "to give way, yield," wice "wych elm;" Old Norse vikja "to bend, turn;" Swedish viker "willow twig, wand;" German wechsel "change").

mid-15c., from Latin satiatus, past participle of satiare "fill full, satisfy," from satis "enough," from PIE root *sa- "to satisfy" (source also of Gothic saþs "satiated," Old English sæd "satisfied;" see sad).
"having length" (especially "immoderately long"), 1759, American English, from length + -y (2).

"reproductive body in flowerless plants corresponding to the seeds of flowering ones," 1836, from Modern Latin spora, from Greek spora "a seed, a sowing, seed-time," related to sporas "scattered, dispersed," sporos "a sowing," and speirein "to sow, scatter," from PIE *spor-, variant of root *sper- (4) "to strew" (see sprout (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 感觉上的，依感观的；诉诸美感的

root.meaning: be conscious inwardly of (one's state or condition) is from 1680s. Meaning

morphology.mnemonic: be conscious inwardly of (one's state or condition) is from 1680s. Meaning  + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1640s, "pertaining to the senses" apparently coined by Milton to recover the original meaning of sensual and avoid the lascivious connotation that the older word had acquired, but by 1870 sensuous, too, had begun down the same path and come to mean "alive to the pleasures of the senses." Rare before Coleridge popularized it "To express in one word all that appertains to the perception, considered as passive and merely recipient ...." (1814).
1580s, "having quantity," from Medieval Latin quantitativus, from stem of Latin quantitas (see quantity).

morphology.mnemonic: allow to remain unpaid + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., "apply hot liquids," from Old French fomenter "apply hot compress (to a wound)"  (13c.), from Late Latin fomentare, from Latin fomentum "warm application, poultice," contraction of *fovimentum, from fovere "to warm; cherish, encourage" (see fever).

meanings.definitionCn: 小冲突，小规模战斗；小争论
进行小规模战斗；发生小争论
丧失的；被剥夺的；失去亲人的

etymology.story: late 14c., past participle adjective from bereave (v.)..

1905, from German Narzissismus, coined 1899 (in "Die sexuellen Perversitäten"), by German psychiatrist Paul Näcke (1851-1913), on a comparison suggested 1898 by Havelock Ellis, from Greek Narkissos, name of a beautiful youth in mythology (Ovid, "Metamorphoses," iii.370) who fell in love with his own reflection in a spring and was turned to the flower narcissus (q.v.).

morphology.mnemonic: 落于/发生 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story:
late 15c., "likely to happen," from Latin incidentem (nominative incidens), present participle of 
incidere "to happen, befall" (see incident (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 维护，维修；保持；生活费用

morphology.mnemonic: to carry on, keep up + ance

etymology.story: mid-14c., "bearing, deportment," from Old French maintenance "upkeep; shelter, protection,: from maintenir (see maintain).

root.meaning: one who judges merits of books, plays, etc.

morphology.mnemonic: hyper + one who judges merits of books, plays, etc. + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "a nerve cell with appendages," 1891, from German Neuron, from Greek neuron (see neuro-).

meanings.definitionCn: 吸收；吸引；承受；理解；使…全神贯注

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French absorber (Old French assorbir, 13c.), from Latin absorbere "to swallow up," from ab- "from" (see ab-) + sorbere "suck in," from PIE root *srebh- "to suck, absorb" (source also of Armenian arbi "I drank," Greek rhopheo "to sup greedily up, gulp down," Lithuanian srebiu "to drink greedily").

meanings.definitionCn: 市民（现主要指某些欧洲国家中产阶级的市民或镇民）；公民

etymology.story: 1560s, "freeman of a burgh," from Middle Dutch burgher or German Bürger, from Middle High German burger, from Old High German burgari "inhabitant of a fortress," from burg "fortress, citadel" (see borough).

late 13c., from a Scandinavian source, probably Old Norse leggr "a leg, bone of the arm or leg," from Proto-Germanic *lagjaz (cognates Danish læg, Swedish läg "the calf of the leg"), a word with no certain ulterior connections.
1867, coined to serve as opposite of synonym, from Greek anti- "equal to, instead of, opposite" (see anti-) + -onym "name" (see name (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 有责任的，有义务的；应受罚的；有…倾向的；易…的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "bound or obliged by law," probably from Anglo-French *liable, from Old French lier "to bind, tie up, fasten, tether; bind by obligation," from Latin ligare "to bind, to tie" (see ligament).
late 14c., from mid + point (n.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 沼泽，沼泽地；无法脱身的困境

etymology.story: 1570s, "bog, marsh," from obsolete quag "bog, marsh" + mire (n.).
late 14c., "not owing or payable; unjustly demanded," also "not appropriate, unseasonable," also "excessive," from un- (1) "not" + past participle of due (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 元素；要素；原理；成分；自然环境

etymology.story: "universal remedy," 1540s, from Latin panacea, a herb (variously identified) that would heal all illnesses, from Greek panakeia "cure-all," from panakes "all-healing," from pan- "all" (see pan-) + akos "cure," from iasthai "to heal" (see -iatric).

morphology.mnemonic: 命令/委托 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1610s, from Middle French mendacieux, from Latin mendacium "a lie, untruth, falsehood, fiction," from mendax (genitive mendacis) "lying, deceitful," from menda "fault, defect, carelessness in writing," from PIE root *mend- "physical defect, fault" (see amend (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 能被通过的；能接受的；可渗透的

etymology.story: "penetrable, accessible, permeable," 1610s, from Latin pervius "that may be passed through" (see impervious)..

meanings.definitionCn: 刻苦钻研；苦心而成的著作

etymology.story: 1590s, "literary work showing signs of too-careful elaboration," from Latin lucubrationem (nominative lucubratio) "nocturnal study, night work," noun of action from past participle stem of lucubrare, literally "to work by artificial light," from stem of lucere "to shine" (see light (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 弹药筒，打印机的（墨盒）；[摄] 暗盒；笔芯；一卷软片

etymology.story: 1570s, cartage, corruption of French cartouche "a full charge for a pistol," originally wrapped in paper (16c.), from Italian cartoccio "roll of paper," an augmentative form of Medieval Latin carta "paper" (see card (n.)).

1550s, from Middle French théorème (16c.) and directly from Late Latin theorema, from Greek theorema "spectacle, sight," in Euclid "proposition to be proved," literally "that which is looked at," from theorein "to look at, behold" (see theory)..
Old English læwede "nonclerical," of uncertain origin but probably ultimately from Vulgar Latin *laigo-, from Latin laicus (see lay (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 柔软的；灵活的；顺从的；易弯曲的；逢迎的
侵犯， 侵害； 违背， 触犯； 干涉， 干扰

etymology.story: mid-15c., enfrangen, "to violate," from Latin infringere "to damage, break off, break, bruise," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + frangere "to break" (see fraction).
1580s, from French bravade "bragging, boasting," from Italian bravata "bragging, boasting" (16c.), from bravare "brag, boast, be defiant," from bravo (see brave (adj.)).

early 14c., of alcoholic distillates, brandy (ardent spirits), etc., from Old French ardant (13c.) "burning, hot; zealous," from Latin ardentem (nominative ardens) "glowing, fiery, hot, ablaze," also used figuratively of passions, present participle of ardere "to burn," from PIE root *as- "to burn, glow" (source also of Old English æsce "ashes;" see ash (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 并发的；一致的；同时发生的
[数] 共点；同时发生的事件

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French concurrent or directly from Latin concurrentem (nominative concurrens), present participle of concurrere (see concur).

1727, "resemblance of sounds between words," from French assonance, from assonant, from Latin assonantem (nominative assonans), present participle of assonare "to resound, respond to," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + sonare "to sound" (see sonata).

meanings.definitionCn: 不幸的；麻烦的；倔强的；困难的

etymology.story: 1520s, "not having inclination" (to or for something), also "difficult to manage, unruly," from un- (1) "not" + toward (adj.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 电池(组)；(器具等的)一组，一套；炮兵连

etymology.story: 1530s, "action of battering," from Middle French batterie, from Old French baterie (12c.) "beating, thrashing, assault," from batre "beat," from Latin battuere "beat" (see batter (v.)).

1610s, "to lay waste, devastate" (obsolete); 1620s, "to vex by repeated attacks," from French harasser "tire out, vex" (16c.), which is of uncertain origin; possibly from Old French harer "stir up, provoke; set a dog on," and perhaps blended with Old French harier "to harry, draw, drag" [Barnhart].

meanings.definitionCn: 片刻，瞬间，时刻；重要，契机

etymology.story: mid-14c., "very brief portion of time, instant," in moment of time, from Old French moment (12c.) "moment, minute; importance, weight, value" or directly from Latin momentum "movement, motion; moving power; alteration, change;" also "short time, instant" (also source of Spanish, Italian momento), contraction of *movimentum, from movere "to move" (see move (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + rever + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: "deficient in veneration or respect," mid-15c., from Old French irreverent or directly from Latin irreverentem "disrespectful, irreverent" (see irreverence).

meanings.definitionCn: 促进,发扬;提升,提拔;增进,助长

etymology.story: late 14c., "to advance (someone) to a higher grade or office," from Old French promoter and directly from Latin promotus, past participle of promovere "move forward, advance; cause to advance, push onward; bring to light, reveal," from pro- "forward" (see pro-) + movere "to move" (see move (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 乘用马；出租马车；出租汽车；操贱役的人

etymology.story: "small saddle horse let out for hire," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 形象的；图表的；绘画似的

etymology.story: "vivid, describing accurately ," 1660s (graphically "vividly" is from 1570s), from Latin graphicus "picturesque," from Greek graphikos "of or for writing, belonging to drawing, picturesque," from graphe "writing, drawing," from graphein "to write" (see -graphy).

late 14c., "action of creating, a created thing," from Old French creacion (14c., Modern French création) "creation, coming into being," from Latin creationem (nominative creatio) "a creating, a producing," in classical use "an electing, appointment, choice," noun of action from past participle stem of creare (see create).

meanings.definitionCn: 爱好； 趋势； 倾斜

morphology.mnemonic: in + 倾斜 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: "condition of being mentally disposed" (to do something), late 14c., from Middle French inclination (14c.) and directly from Latin inclinationem (nominative inclinatio) "a leaning, bending," figuratively "tendency, bias, favor," noun of action from past participle stem of inclinare "to bend, turn; cause to lean" (see incline (v.)).

in early use also engredient, early 15c., "something forming part of a mixture," from Latin ingredientem (nominative ingrediens) "that which enters into" (a compound, recipe, etc.), present participle of ingredi "go in, enter," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + gradi "to step, go" (see grade (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: (in)参与,参加;分享,分担;含有,带有

etymology.story: 1530s, back-formation from participation, or else from Latin participatus, past participle of participare "to share, share in, participate in; to impart," from particeps "partaking, sharing," from parti, past participle of partir "to divide" (see part (n.)) + -cip-, weak form of stem of capere "to take" (see capable).
1760; see authentic + -ity.
1650s, from Latin rapaci-, stem of rapax "grasping," itself from stem of rapere "to seize" (see rapacity) + -ous.
"ornamental passage near the close of a song or solo," 1836, from Italian cadenza (see cadence)..
"remission, forgiveness," c.
"proud and disdainful," 1520s, a redundant extension of haught (q.v.) "high in one's own estimation" by addition of -y (2) on model of might/mighty, naught/naughty, etc.

meanings.definitionCn: 轮廓；等高线；周线；电路；概要

etymology.story: 1660s, a term in painting and sculpture, from French contour "circumference, outline," from Italian and Medieval Latin contornare "to go around," from Latin com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + tornare "to turn (on a lathe);" see turn (v.).
"a lyric poem," 1580s, from Middle French lyrique "short poem expressing personal emotion," from Latin lyricus "of or for the lyre," from Greek lyrikos "singing to the lyre," from lyra (see lyre).

morphology.mnemonic: 错误地/不当地 + to place before

etymology.story: 1640s, from mis- (1) + represent.

meanings.definitionCn: 可抢救的；可打捞的；可挽回的；能利用的

etymology.story: by 1915, from salvage (v.) + -able.

late 14c., omelye, from Old French omelie "homily" (12c., Modern French homélie), from Church Latin homilia "a homily, sermon," from Greek homilia "conversation, discourse," used in New Testament Greek for "sermon," from homilos "an assembled crowd," from homou "together" (from PIE *somo-, from root *sem- (1) "one, as one, together with;" see same) + ile "troop, band, crowd" (cognate with Sanskrit melah "assembly," Latin miles "soldier").

meanings.definitionCn: 藏书癖；（尤指珍本书）收藏狂

etymology.story: 1734, after French bibliomanie, from biblio- + mania..
early 15c., from Gaelic clann "family, stock, offspring," akin to Old Irish cland "offspring, tribe," both from Latin planta "offshoot" (see plant (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 经济/管理 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1570s, "pertaining to household management;" from economic + -al (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 赋予…生命；激发；使有生气

etymology.story: 1670s, "to give life to," from vital + -ize.

meanings.definitionCn: 磨光，擦亮；擦亮剂；优雅，精良
内阁；橱柜；展览艺术品的小陈列室

etymology.story: 1540s, "secret storehouse, treasure chamber," from Middle French cabinet "small room" (16c.), diminutive of Old French cabane "cabin" (see cabin); perhaps influenced by (or rather, from) Italian gabbinetto, diminutive of gabbia, from Latin cavea "stall, stoop, cage, den for animals" (see cave (n.)).

1762, "capable of being shown, presentable," from French ostensible, from Latin ostens-, past participle stem of ostendere "to show, expose to view; to stretch out, spread before; exhibit, display," from ob "in front of" (see ob-) + tendere "to stretch" (see tenet).

late 14c., "origin, source, beginning; rule of conduct; axiom, basic assumption; elemental aspect of a craft or discipline," from Anglo-French principle, Old French principe "origin, cause, principle," from Latin principium (plural principia) "a beginning, commencement, origin, first part," in plural "foundation, elements," from princeps (see prince).
1630s (adj.); 1650s (n.), from French litigant, from Latin litigantem (nominative litigans), present participle of litigare (see litigation)..
early 15c., from Old French tedieus, from Late Latin taediosus "wearisome, irksome, tedious," from Latin taedium (see tedium).
"obtain from a specified source," 1972, from source (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 开始，创始；发起；使初步了解
开始；新加入者，接受初步知识者
新加入的；接受初步知识的

morphology.mnemonic: 开始/初始 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French domestique (14c.) and directly from Latin domesticus "belonging to the household," from domus "house," from PIE *dom-o- "house," from root *dem- "house, household" (source also of Sanskrit damah "house;" Avestan demana- "house;" Greek domos "house," despotes "master, lord;" Latin dominus "master of a household;" Old Church Slavonic domu, Russian dom "house;" Lithuanian dimstis "enclosed court, property;" Old Norse topt "homestead").

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 坚固/稳定 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: mid-15c., "sick bay in a monastery," formerly also enfermerie, also firmary, fermery, from Old French enfermerie "hospital" and directly from Medieval Latin infirmaria "a place for the infirm," from Latin infirmus "weak, frail," (see infirm).

meanings.definitionCn: 给人好感的；有魅力的；引人注意的
预先影响；使先具有（prepossess的现在分词）

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + to hold as property

etymology.story: 1640s, "causing prejudice," present participle adjective from prepossess.

meanings.definitionCn: 使显得一本正经；把…打扮得整整齐齐
(Prim)人名；(法、德、匈、捷、瑞典、西、葡)普里姆

etymology.story: 1680s (v.) "to assume a formal, precise demeanor," perhaps from French prim "thin, small, delicate," from Old French prim "fine, delicate," from Latin primus "finest," literally "first" (see prime (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 陆地的， 陆生的， 陆栖的； 地球的

etymology.story: late 14c., "of or pertaining to the earth," with + -al (1) + from Latin terrestris "earthly, of the earth, on land," from terra "earth" (see terrain).
Old English smelt "sardine, small salmon-like sea fish," cognate with Dutch smelt "sand eel," Danish smelt (c.
1590s, from Latin vitalitatem (nominative vitalitas) "vital force, life," from vitalis "pertaining to life" (see vital)..

meanings.definitionCn: 懒散的；不整洁的；马虎的
邋遢地；马虎地；不整洁地

root.meaning: person careless of dress or negligent of cleanliness

morphology.mnemonic: person careless of dress or negligent of cleanliness + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: 1510s, "low, base, lewd," later "untidy, dirty" (1560s), from sloven + -ly (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 懒洋洋的；无精打采的；伤感的

etymology.story: "sentimentally woebegone" [Century Dictionary], 1768, lack-adaysical (Sterne), from interjection lackadaisy "alas, alack" (1748), a ludicrous alteration of lack-a-day (1690s), an exclamation of sorrow or regret, from alack the day (1590s).

meanings.definitionCn: 贪婪的；渴望的；狼吞虎咽的

morphology.mnemonic: temporary popular enthusiasm + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: late 14c., "obsessed with plundering, extremely greedy," from Old French ravinos, of people, "rapacious, violent," of water, "swift-flowing," from raviner "to seize," from ravine "violent rush, robbery" (see ravine).

meanings.definitionCn: 女修道院院长；女庵主持
[医] 后天的；已获得的；已成习惯的

etymology.story: mid-14c., "firmness of resolve, mental equilibrium" (of persons), from Old French stablete, establete "firmness, solidity, stability; durability, constancy" (Modern French stabilité), from Latin stabilitatem (nominative stabilitas) "a standing fast, firmness," figuratively "security, steadfastness," from stabilis "steadfast, firm" (see stable (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 辐射；传播；流露；发射；广播

morphology.mnemonic: 射线/放射 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: "having rays, furnished with rays, shining," 1660s, from Latin radiatus (see radiate (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 张力，拉力；紧张，不安；电压

morphology.mnemonic: 持有/保持 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1530s, "a stretched condition," from Middle French tension (16c.) or directly from Latin tensionem (nominative tensio) "a stretching" (in Medieval Latin "a struggle, contest"), noun of state from tensus, past participle of tendere "to stretch," from PIE root *ten- "stretch" (see tenet).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 弯曲 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., reflexion, in reference to surfaces throwing back light or heat, from Late Latin reflexionem (nominative reflexio) "a reflection," literally "a bending back," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin reflectere "to bend back, bend backwards, turn away," from re- "back" (see re-) + flectere "to bend" (see flexible).

1590s, "thin, unsubstantial," irregularly formed from Latin tenuis "thin, drawn out, meager, slim, slender," figuratively "trifling, insignificant, poor, low in rank," from PIE root *ten- "to stretch" (source also of Sanskrit tanuh "thin," literally "stretched out;" see tenet) + -ous.

meanings.definitionCn: 迷人的；可爱的；引人注目的

etymology.story: Old English wynsum "agreeable, pleasant," from wynn "pleasure, delight," from Proto-Germanic *wunjo- (source also of Old Saxon wunnia, Old High German wunja, German Wonne "joy, delight;" see win (v.)) + -sum (see -some (1)).
1650s, from Late Latin ultimatus, past participle of ultimare "to be final, come to an end," from Latin ultimus (fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 歌谣，民谣；叙事歌谣；流行抒情歌曲

etymology.story: late 15c., from French ballade "dancing song" (13c.), from Old Provençal ballada "(poem for a) dance," from balar "to dance," from Late Latin ballare "to dance" (see ball (n.2))..

meanings.definitionCn: 腐蚀性的； 讽刺的， 挖苦的

etymology.story: early 15c., "follow up, pursue" (some course or action), from Latin prosecutus, past participle of prosequi "follow after, accompany; chase, pursue; attack, assail, abuse" (see pursue).

1640s, "developed before the usual time" (of plants), with -ous + Latin praecox (genitive praecocis) "maturing early," from prae "before" (see pre-) + coquere "to ripen," literally "to cook" (see cook (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 有助于；报偿，报应；被转移

etymology.story: late 14c., "to overflow," from Old French redonder "overflow, abound, be in profusion" (12c.), from Latin redundare "to overflow" (see redundant).

meanings.definitionCn: 树木的； 栖于树木的

etymology.story: 1660s, from Latin arboreus "pertaining to trees," from arbor "tree," which is of unknown origin, + -al (1)..

morphology.mnemonic: 肉/肉体 + ivore + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1640s, from Latin carnivorus "flesh-eating, feeding on flesh," from caro (genitive carnis) "flesh" (see carnage) + vorare "to devour" (see voracity)..

morphology.mnemonic: 爱 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin fetidus (commonly, but incorrectly, foetidus) "stinking," from fetere "have a bad smell, stink." This is perhaps connected with fimus "dung," or with fumus "smoke.".
late 14c., "to foreordain," from Old French prédestiner (12c.) "predestine, ordain" (of God) and directly from Latin praedestinare "determine beforehand" (see predestination).
1520s, "sideways, asquint," of obscure origin.
early 15c., from Latin contortus, past participle of contorquere "to whirl, twist together," from com- "together" or intensive (see com-) + torquere "to twist" (see torque (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 站立/设置 + …的人/处于…状态

meanings.definitionCn: 火蜥蜴；蝾螈目动物；耐火的人；烤箱

etymology.story: mid-14c., "legendary lizard-like creature that can live in fire," from Old French salamandre "legendary fiery beast," also "cricket" (12c.), from Latin salamandra, from Greek salamandra, probably of eastern origin.
1570s, "to come between in space or time" (obsolete); c.
1830, "catholicism," from catholic + -ity.

early 14c., from Old French progenie (13c.) and directly from Latin progenies "descendants, offspring, lineage, race, family," from stem of progignere "beget," from pro- "forth" (see pro-) + gignere "to produce, beget" (see genus)..

late 13c., anhaunsen "to raise, make higher," from Anglo-French enhauncer, probably from Old French enhaucier "make greater, make higher or louder; fatten, foster; raise in esteem," from Vulgar Latin *inaltiare, from Late Latin inaltare "raise, exalt,"  from altare "make high," from altus "high" (see old).
Old English wiccecræft "witchcraft, magic," from wicce (see witch) + cræft "power, skill" (see craft).
1750, coined from anti- "against" + septic.

meanings.definitionCn: 各种工艺的；综合技术的
工艺学校；理工专科学校

etymology.story: 1805, "pertaining to instruction in many (technical) subjects," from French École Polytechnique, engineering school founded 1794 (as École des Travaux publics) in Paris; from Greek polytekhnos "skilled in many arts," from polys "many" (see poly-) + tekhne "art" (see techno-).

root.meaning: device that breaks an electrical circuit

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + device that breaks an electrical circuit + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 15c., from refuse + -al (2)..

meanings.definitionCn: 没有礼貌的；脾气暴躁的

etymology.story: late Old English cierlisc "of or pertaining to churls," from churl + -ish.

"uniform in time, of equal time, performed in equal times," 1706, with suffix -ous, from Modern Latin isochronus, from Greek isokhronos "equal in age or time," from iso- "equal" (see iso-) + khronos "time" (see chrono-).

meanings.definitionCn: 教条主义者；独断家；独断论者

root.meaning: that which one thinks is true,

morphology.mnemonic: that which one thinks is true, + …主义者/…专家

meanings.definitionCn: 倾泻； 充满， 遍布

etymology.story: "indicate beforehand," 1570s, figurative, from fore- + shadow (v.); the notion seems to be a shadow thrown before an advancing material object as an image of something suggestive of what is to come.

meanings.definitionCn: 重力， 引力；庄重； 严重

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French replet "filled up" (14c.), from Latin repletus "filled, full," past participle of replere "to fill; fill again, re-fill," from re- (see re-) + plere "to fill" (see pleio-)..

meanings.definitionCn: 给予…政治权利； 解放

etymology.story: early 15c., "grant (someone) the status or privilege of citizenship, admit to membership in a town," from Old French enfranchiss-, present participle stem of enfranchir "to set or make free; grant a franchise to;" from en- "make, put in" (see en- (1)) + franc "free" (see franchise (n.)).
"on fire," early 15c., apparently from Middle English aliht, past participle of alihton (Old English on-lihtan) "to light up," also "to shine upon" (see light (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 淹没， 泛滥； 使不胜负荷， 使应接不暇

etymology.story: 1620s, back-formation from inundation, or else from Latin inundatus, past participle of inundare "to overflow, run over" (source also of Spanish inundar, French inonder).

root.meaning: that which one thinks is true,

morphology.mnemonic: that which one thinks is true, + ic

etymology.story: 1670s, from Late Latin dogmaticus, from Greek dogmatikos "pertaining to doctrines," from dogma (see dogma).

meanings.definitionCn: 业余爱好者；一知半解者
浅薄的；业余艺术爱好的
（在艺术、科学等方面）浅尝辄止

etymology.story: 1733, borrowing of Italian dilettante "lover of music or painting," from dilettare "to delight," from Latin delectare (see delight (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 调节，规定；控制；校准；有系统的管理

etymology.story: early 15c., "adjust by rule, control," from Late Latin regulatus, past participle of regulare "to control by rule, direct," from Latin regula "rule" (see regular).

meanings.definitionCn: 倦怠的；无精打采的；百无聊赖的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Middle English liste "pleasure, joy, delight" (see list (v.4)) + -less.

1620s, "inflicting or involving punishment," from French punitif (16c.) or directly from Medieval Latin punitivus, from Latin punitus, past participle of punire "to punish, correct, chastise" (see punish)..

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 填充/补充 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1620s, "ceremonious," from complement + -ary.

1530, "goat sent into the wilderness on the Day of Atonement, symbolic bearer of the sins of the people," coined by Tyndale from scape (n.1) + goat to translate Latin caper emissarius, itself a translation in Vulgate of Hebrew 'azazel (Lev.

1540s, "standing out above a surface," from Latin extantem (nominative extans), present participle of extare "stand out, be visible, exist," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + stare "to stand," from PIE root *stā- "to stand" (see stet).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 站立/设置 + ancy

etymology.story: 1590s, "firmness of matter," from Medieval Latin consistentia or directly from Latin consistentem, from consistere (see consist).
1650s, "pleasing," from Latin complacentem (nominative complacens) "pleasing," present participle of complacere "be very pleasing" (see complacence).

meanings.definitionCn: 引出，预先提出；作为…的前提
前提；上述各项；房屋连地基

etymology.story: mid-15c., "to lower in worth or value," from Late Latin vilificare "to make cheap or base; to esteem of little value," from Latin vilis "cheap, base" (see vile) + root of facere "to make" (see factitious).
1580s, from Late Latin controversialis "pertaining to controversy," from Latin controversia (see controversy)..

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + tra + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1874, "uncompromising, refusing to agree or come to understanding," (used of extreme political factions or parties), from French intransigeant (18c.), from Spanish los intransigentes, literally "those not coming to agreement," name for extreme left in the Spanish Cortes and the extreme republicans of the 1870s, from in- "not" (see in- (1)) + transigente "compromising," from Latin transigentem (nominative transigens), present participle of transigere "come to an agreement, accomplish, to carry through" (see transaction).

meanings.definitionCn: 不适当的，不相称的；不合身的，不得体的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + to look well

etymology.story: 1590s, from un- (1) "not" + becoming "fitting."

meanings.definitionCn: 恢复；复位；王政复辟；归还

root.meaning: place or thing that is standing

morphology.mnemonic: place or thing that is standing + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "a means of healing or restoring health; renewing of something lost," from Old French restoration (Modern French restauration) and directly from Late Latin restorationem (nominative restoratio), noun of action from past participle stem of Latin restaurare (see restore).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之上/之中 + 皮肤 + 过程/状态

etymology.story: 1620s, from Late Latin epidermis, from Greek epidermis "the outer skin," from epi "on" (see epi-) + derma "skin" (see derma).

meanings.definitionCn: 良性的；仁慈的；有益的；和蔼的
象限；[海洋][天] 象限仪；四分之一圆

etymology.story: late 14c., "a quarter of a day, six hours," from Middle French quadrant, from Latin quadrantem (nominative quadrans) "fourth part," also the name of a coin worth a quarter of an as, noun use of present participle of quadrare "to make square; put in order, arrange, complete; run parallel, be exact," figuratively "to fit, suit, be proper," related to quadrus "a square," quattuor "four" (see four).

Old English bidan "to stay, continue, live, remain," also "to trust, rely," from Proto-Germanic *bidan "to await" (source also of Old Norse biða, Old Saxon bidan, Old Frisian bidia, Middle Dutch biden, Old High German bitan, Gothic beidan "to wait"), which is of uncertain origin.
formerly also enveigh, late 15c., "to introduce," from Latin invehere "to bring in, carry in, introduce," also "assault, assail," from in- "against" (see in- (1)) + vehere "to carry" (see vehicle).

meanings.definitionCn: 诅咒；革出教门；被诅咒者；令人厌恶的人

etymology.story: 1520s, "an accursed thing," from Latin anathema "an excommunicated person; the curse of excommunication," from Greek anathema "a thing accursed," originally "a thing devoted," literally "a thing set up (to the gods)," from ana- "up" (see ana-) + tithenai "to put, place" (see theme).

Old English wrecca "wretch, stranger, exile," from Proto-Germanic *wrakjon "pursuer; one pursued" (source also of Old Saxon wrekkio, Old High German reckeo "a banished person, exile," German recke "renowned warrior, hero"), related to Old English wreccan "to drive out, punish" (see wreak).
1717, from prude (adj.) + -ish.

early 15c., "exposition of myths," from Middle French mythologie and directly from Late Latin mythologia, from Greek mythologia "legendary lore, a telling of mythic legends; a legend, story, tale," from mythos "myth" (of unknown origin) + -logy "study." Meaning "a body of myths" first recorded 1781..
1690s, from French symptomatique or directly from Late Latin symptomaticus, from symptomat-, stem of symptoma (see symptom).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 地方 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1630s, from verbal used of adjective allocate (mid-15c.

meanings.definitionCn: 滋补品，补药；强身的，健体的

etymology.story: 1640s, "relating to or characterized by muscular tension," from Greek tonikos "of stretching," from tonos "a stretching" (see tenet).

meanings.definitionCn: 蒙住；裹住；抑制；发低沉的声音
低沉的声音；消声器；包裹物（如头巾，围巾等）；唇鼻部
发誓放弃；公开放弃；避免

morphology.mnemonic: 远离/脱离 + 判断/法律

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French abjurer or directly from Latin abiurare "deny on oath," from ab- "away" (see ab-) + iurare "to swear," related to ius (genitive iuris) "law" (see jurist).

meanings.definitionCn: 事业,企(事)业单位;事业心,进取心

etymology.story: early 15c., "an undertaking," formerly also enterprize, from Old French enterprise "an undertaking," noun use of fem.

morphology.mnemonic: 独自/太阳 + 与…有关的/像…的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "pertaining to the sun," from Latin solaris "of the sun," from sol "sun" (see sol).
"round, vaulted roof," 1650s, from French dome (16c.), from Provençal doma, from Greek doma "house, housetop" (especially a style of roof from the east), related to domos "house" (see domestic).

meanings.definitionCn: 学院的学生；学院的一员

etymology.story: late 14c., from college + -ian..
mid-15c., from Late Latin benefactor, from Latin phrase bene facere, from bene "well" (see bene-) + facere "to do" (see factitious).
1580s, from Dutch domineren "to rule" (16c.), from Middle French dominer, from Latin dominari "to rule, 'lord' it over" (see domination).

meanings.definitionCn: 未知的；陌生的，默默无闻的
未知数；未知的事物，默默无闻的人

etymology.story: "combustible cord or tube for lighting an explosive device," also fuze, 1640s, from Italian fuso, literally "spindle" (the ignition device so called for its shape, because the originals were long, thin tubes filled with gunpowder), from Latin fusus "a spindle," which is of uncertain origin.
1590s, "rivalry" (based on compete); c.
mid-15c., from Latin frivolus "silly, empty, trifling, worthless," diminutive of *frivos "broken, crumbled," from friare "break, rub away, crumble" (see friable).

1510s, from French prétexte, from Latin praetextum "a pretext, outward display," noun use of neuter past participle of praetexere "to disguise, cover," literally "weave in front" (for sense, compare pull the wool over (someone's) eyes); from prae- "in front" (see pre-) + texere "to weave," from PIE root *teks- "to weave, to make" (see texture (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 紧急的；浮现的；意外的；自然发生的

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 沉入/合并 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: late 14c., "rising from what surrounds it, coming into view," from Latin emergentem (nominative emergens), present participle of emergere "to rise out or up" (see emerge)..

meanings.definitionCn: 同音异义词；同形异义词；同形同音异义词；同名异物

etymology.story: "word pronounced and perhaps spelled the same as another but different in meaning," 1807, from French homonyme and directly from Latin homonymum (Quintilian), from Greek homonymon, neuter of homonymos, from homos "same" (see homo- (1)) + onyma, dialectal form of onoma "name" (see name (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 不足/在…之下 + 强壮/价值

etymology.story: 1590s, "to rate as inferior in value" (to), from under + value (v.).

early 15c., inscisif, "slashing, cutting with a sharp edge," from Old French incisif (medical) "invasive, effective," and directly from Medieval Latin incisivus, from Latin incis-, past participle stem of incidere "to cut into" (see incision).

meanings.definitionCn: 基本的， 初步的； 未充分发展的， 发育不成熟的； 退化的

morphology.mnemonic: 原始，粗野 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1827; see rudiment + -ary.

meanings.definitionCn: 不安全的， 充满危险的； 不牢靠的， 不稳固的

etymology.story: 1640s, a legal word, "held through the favor of another," from Latin precarius "obtained by asking or praying," from prex (genitive precis) "entreaty, prayer" (see pray).

meanings.definitionCn: 大批出动，成群出现(鼠类及害虫)

etymology.story: late 15c., "to attack, assail, hurt, distress, annoy," from Old French infester (14c.), from Latin infestare "to attack, disturb, trouble," from infestus "unsafe, hostile, threatening, dangerous," originally "inexorable, not able to be handled," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + -festus "(able to be) seized." Sense of "swarm over in large numbers, attack parasitically" first recorded c.
