late 14c., from Old French eternel "eternal," or directly from Late Latin aeternalis, from Latin aeternus "of an age, lasting, enduring, permanent, everlasting, endless," contraction of aeviternus "of great age," from aevum "age" (see eon).

meanings.definitionCn: 救济；赔偿；纠正；重新调整

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to correct, reform;" late 14c., "restore, put right" (a wrong, error, offense); "repair; relieve; improve; amend," from Old French redrecier "reform, restore, rebuild" (Modern French redresser), from re- "again" (see re-) + drecier "to straighten, arrange" (see dress (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 出身；亲子关系；门第；起源

morphology.mnemonic: 平等 + 行为/结果/集合

etymology.story: late 15c., "parental conduct," from Middle French parentage (12c.), from parent (see parent).
"member of the lowest class," 1530s, from Latin plebius "person not of noble rank," from adjective meaning "of the common people" (see plebeian (adj.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 随便地，任意地；无目的地，胡乱地；未加计划地

root.meaning: person who does not belong on our dormitory floor.

morphology.mnemonic: person who does not belong on our dormitory floor. + 以…方式/副词化
在…之前/预先 + 强壮/价值 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: early 15c., "having great power or force," from Latin praevalentem (nominative praevalens) "of superior strength; mighty," present participle of praevalere "to be more able" (see prevail).

meanings.definitionCn: 讽刺；讽刺文学，讽刺作品
抛射的；抛掷的；供抛射用的；（触角等）能伸出的
射弹；抛射体；自动推进武器

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + 投/掷 + ile

etymology.story: 1660s, from Modern Latin projectilis, from Latin proiectus, past participle of proicere (see project (n.)).
1590s, from spright, an early 16c.
"to overcome with superior power," 1590s, from over- + power (v.).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + to yield on stipulated terms

etymology.story: 1560s, back-formation from recapitulation and also from Late Latin recapitulatus, past participle of recapitulare.

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 强壮/价值 + able

etymology.story: 1570s, "above value, too valuable for exact estimate," from in- (1) "not" + value (v.) "estimate the worth of" + -able.

meanings.definitionCn: 亡命之徒，逃犯，被剥夺法律权益者
宣布…为非法， 使…成为非法

etymology.story: Old English utlagian "to outlaw, banish," from utlaga "an outlaw" (see outlaw (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 全部 + 知道 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1560s (implied in shriveled), of unknown origin, not found in Middle English; perhaps from a Scandinavian source (compare Swedish skryvla "to wrinkle, to shrivel"), perhaps ultimately connected with shrimp (n.) and shrink (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 语法错误，文理不通；谬误，失礼

etymology.story: "gross grammatical error;" loosely "any absurdity or incongruity," 1570s, from Middle French solécisme (16c.), from Latin soloecismus "mistake in speaking or writing," from Greek soloikismos "to speak (Greek) incorrectly," from soloikos "ungrammatical utterance," properly "a speaking like the people of Soloi," an Athenian colony in Cilicia (modern Mezitli in Turkey), whose dialect the Athenians considered barbarous.

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + notice, attention, reputation

etymology.story: 1590s, from Middle French dénoter (14c.), from Latin denotare "denote, mark out," from de- "completely" (see de-) + notare "to mark" (see note (v.)).
1560s, from be- + deck (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 庆祝（celebrate的过去式和过去分词）

etymology.story: "much-talked-about," 1660s, past participle adjective from celebrate (v.)..
1520s, from Middle French audible and directly from Late Latin audibilis, from Latin audire "to hear" (see audience).

meanings.definitionCn: 繁殖， 增殖； 传播， 宣传， 使普及

etymology.story: 1560s, "to cause to multiply," from Latin propagatus, past participle of propagare "to set forward, extend, procreate" (see propagation).

meanings.definitionCn: 事情；事务；私事；（尤指关系不长久的）风流韵事

etymology.story: 1670s, from Irish go leór, and equivalent Scottish Gaelic gu leóir "sufficiently, enough," from Old Irish roar "enough," from Proto-Celtic *ro-wero- "sufficiency." The particle go/gu usually means "to," but it also is affixed to adjectives to form adverbs, as here.

meanings.definitionCn: 正统的；传统的；惯常的；东正教的

etymology.story: mid-15c., of opinions, faith, from Late Latin orthodoxus, from Greek orthodoxos "having the right opinion," from orthos "right, true, straight" (see ortho-) + doxa "opinion, praise," from dokein "to seem," from PIE root *dek- "to take, accept" (see decent).

meanings.definitionCn: 惯常的；习惯的，适应的(一般作表语)

etymology.story: late 15c., "made customary, habitual," past participle adjective from accustom (v.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 没打烙印的动物；持不同意见的人
未打烙印的；行为不合常规的；特立独行的

etymology.story: 1867, "calf or yearling found without an owner's brand," so called for Samuel A.

meanings.definitionCn: 结合；组合；联合；[化学] 化合

meanings.definitionCn: 蓬乱的，不整洁的；（言语等）粗野的

etymology.story: 1570s, from un- (1) "not" + kempt "well-combed, neat," from variant past participle of Middle English kemben "to comb," from Old English cemban "to comb," from Proto-Germanic *kambijan, from *kamb- "comb" (see comb (n.)).
late 15c., embushe, from the English verb or from Middle French embusche, from Old French embuscher (see ambush (v.)).
"an expert," especially "one who is skilled in the secrets of anything," 1660s, from Latin adeptus (see adept (adj.)).
"agile, light-footed," c.

meanings.definitionCn: (Amiable)人名；(法)阿米亚布勒；(英)阿米娅布尔

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French amiable, from Late Latin amicabilis "friendly," from amicus "friend," related to amare "to love" (see Amy).

meanings.definitionCn: [法] 非法的；违法的；违反规则的

etymology.story: 1630s, from French illégal (14c.) or directly from Medieval Latin illegalis, from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + Latin legalis (see legal).
"numberless, uncountable," 1580s, from count (v.) + -less..

mid-14c., "warmth or glow of feeling," from Old French fervor "heat; enthusiasm, ardor, passion" (12c., Modern French ferveur), from Latin fervor "a boiling, violent heat; passion, ardor, fury," from fervere "to boil; be hot" (see brew (v.))..
mid-15c., from Middle French ample, from Latin amplus "large, spacious," related to ampla "handle, grip.".
1808, mainly in poetry, from wave (n.) + diminutive suffix -let..

Old English oferyrnan; see over- + run (v.).

mid-14c., "a shame, a disgrace," also "a censure, a rebuke," from Old French reprove "reproach, rejection," verbal noun from reprover "to blame, accuse" (see reprove)..
1620s, from homogene "of the same kind" (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 最重要的,主要的n.负责人,校长;资本,本金

etymology.story: "to trim by cutting close," c.
"word having the same sense as another," early 15c.

meanings.definitionCn: 忧郁的；昏暗的；严峻的；阴天的

etymology.story: 1760 "gloomy, shadowy" (earlier sombrous, c.

meanings.definitionCn: （语言、文章、表达等的）明晰；简明

etymology.story: late 15c., of things; 1540s, of expressions, from Latin perspicuitas "transparency, clearness," from perspicuus, from perspicere "look through, look closely at" (see perspective)..

suffix.meaning: thing or animal spoken about before.

morphology.mnemonic: equi + thing or animal spoken about before. + able

etymology.story: 1640s, from French équitable (16c.), from équité (see equity).
1610s, "pattern, model," from Latin specimen "indication, mark, example, sign, evidence; that by which a thing is known, means of knowing," from specere "to look at" (see scope (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 异国的；外来的；异国情调的

etymology.story: 1590s, "belonging to another country," from Middle French exotique (16c.) and directly from Latin exoticus, from Greek exotikos "foreign," literally "from the outside," from exo "outside" (see exo-).

meanings.definitionCn: 精神的；心灵的；灵魂的；超自然的

etymology.story: "a medium;" 1870; see psychic (adj.)..
1550s, from ample + -ly (2)..
1880, noun and verb, from Irish Land League ostracism of Capt.

1540s, from Latin praeposterus "absurd, contrary to nature, inverted, perverted, in reverse order," literally "before-behind" (compare topsy-turvy, cart before the horse), from prae "before" + posterus "subsequent."

meanings.definitionCn: 一年两次的；半年一次的

morphology.mnemonic: 年 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1560s, from Latin annales libri "chronicles," literally "yearlies, yearly books," noun use of plural of annalis "pertaining to a year," from annus "year" (see annual (adj.))..

early 15c., "to sign at the bottom of a document," from Latin subscribere "write, write underneath, sign one's name; register," also figuratively "assent, agree to, approve," from sub "underneath" (see sub-) + scribere "write" (see script (n.)).
also distil, late 14c., from Old French distiller (14c.), from Latin distillare "trickle down in minute drops," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + stillare "to drip, drop," from stilla "drop."

meanings.definitionCn: 浓度；集中；浓缩；专心；集合

root.meaning: to rest as at a center

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + to rest as at a center + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1630s, "action of bringing to a center," noun of action from verb concentrate (v.).
1789, from French aristocrate, a word of the Revolution, a back-formation from aristocratie (see aristocracy)..
1530s, from Middle French collègue (16c.), from Latin collega "partner in office," from com- "with" (see com-) + leg-, stem of legare "to choose" (see legate).

meanings.definitionCn: 渎职，违法行为；不正当，坏事

etymology.story: 1690s, from French malfaisance "wrongdoing," from malfaisant, from mal- "badly" (see mal-) + faisant, present participle of faire "to do," from Latin facere "to do" (see factitious).

late 14c., "action of confessing," originally in religion, from Old French confession (10c.), from Latin confessionem (nominative confessio) "confession, acknowledgement," noun of action from past participle stem of confiteri (see confess).

meanings.definitionCn: 轻盈的（等于lithesome）；柔软的；易弯曲的

etymology.story: Old English liðe "soft, mild, gentle, meek," from Proto-Germanic *linthja- (source also of Old Saxon lithi "soft, mild, gentle," Old High German lindi, German lind, Old Norse linr, with characteristic loss of "n" before "th" in English), from PIE root *lent- "flexible" (source also of Latin lentus "flexible, pliant, slow," Sanskrit lithi).

morphology.mnemonic: in front of, before; end + 出生

etymology.story: "before birth," 1798, from Latin ante "before" (see ante) + natal..

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 喊/叫 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 15c., "a revoking" (of a grant, etc.), from Old French réclamacion and directly from Latin reclamationem (nominative reclamatio) "a cry of 'no,' a shout of disapproval," noun of action from past participle stem of reclamare "cry out against, protest" (see reclaim).

morphology.mnemonic: 忍受/容忍 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1530s, of authorities, "to allow without interference," from Latin toleratus, past participle of tolerare (see toleration).

meanings.definitionCn: 无形的，触摸不到的；难以理解的

etymology.story: 1630s, "incapable of being touched," from French intangible (c.
1550s, soldier with a bombard, from French bombardier, from bombard (see bombard (n.)).
"take (something) ill; be in some degree angry or provoked at," c.
late Old English, "one who owes allegiance to a sovereign or ruler," from under + diminutive suffix -ling..
1540s, back-formation from instigation or else from Latin instigatus, past participle of instigare "to urge on, incite" (source also of French instiguer).

meanings.definitionCn: 模糊不清的；引起歧义的

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin ambiguus "having double meaning, shifting, changeable, doubtful," adjective derived from ambigere "to dispute about," literally "to wander," from ambi- "about" (see ambi-) + agere "drive, lead, act" (see act (n.)).

early 15c., earlier asile (late 14c.), from Latin asylum "sanctuary," from Greek asylon "refuge," noun use of neuter of asylos "inviolable, safe from violence," especially of persons seeking protection, from a- "without" + syle "right of seizure." So literally "an inviolable place." General sense of "safe or secure place" is from 1640s; meaning "benevolent institution to shelter some class of persons" is from 1776..
Old English alecgan "to put down, remit, give up," a Germanic compound (cognates: Gothic uslagjan, Old High German irleccan, German erlegen), from a- "down, aside" + lecgan "to lay" (see lay (v.)).
mid-15c., "eagerness, zeal," from Old French avidite "avidity, greed," from Latin aviditatem (nominative aviditas) "eagerness, avidity," noun of quality from avidus (see avid)..
Old English paþ, pæþ "path, track," from West Germanic *patha- (source also of Old Frisian path, Middle Dutch pat, Dutch pad, Old High German pfad, German Pfad "path"), of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 冲动的；受感情驱使的；任性的

morphology.mnemonic: in + 推/驱动 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: early 15c., originally in reference to medicine that reduces swelling or humors, from Medieval Latin impulsivus, from Latin impuls-, past participle stem of impellere "strike against, push against" (see impel).

"alleviate without curing," early 15c., from Medieval Latin palliatus, literally "cloaked," from past participle of Late Latin palliare "cover with a cloak, conceal," from Latin pallium "cloak" (see pall (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 重力；万有引力；地心吸力

morphology.mnemonic: be affected by gravity + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1640s in physics, "force that gives weight to objects," also figurative, "act of tending toward a center of attraction," from Modern Latin gravitare (see gravitate).

morphology.mnemonic: botan + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1650s, from botanic + -al.
"tending to produce sleep," 1680s, from French soporifique (17c.), formed in French from Latin sopor (genitive soporis) "deep sleep" (see sopor).

meanings.definitionCn: 天使的；似天使的；天国的

etymology.story: late 15c., "pertaining to angels," from Old French angelique "angelic" (Modern French angélique (13c.), from Latin angelicus, from Greek angelikos "angelic," from angelos (see angel).
1680s, "science of sound," from acoustic (also see -ics).

meanings.definitionCn: [计] 扫描仪；扫描器；光电子扫描装置

etymology.story: 1550s, "person who examines critically," agent noun from scan (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 可能的；想得到的，可想像的

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 拿/领会 + able

meanings.definitionCn: 使成立方形；使自乘二次；量…的体积

etymology.story: 1530s, back-formation from adulteration, or else from Latin adulteratus, past participle of adulterare "to falsify, corrupt," also "to commit adultery." Earlier verb was adulter (late 14c.).

meanings.definitionCn: 幽灵；幻影；鬼怪；离奇出现的东西

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: 冲击， 碰撞；效果； 影响
侧面；轮廓；外形；剖面；简况
欺骗，诈骗；使受挫折；赖帐

etymology.story: 1650s, from or along with the noun (1630s), first used as a cribbage term; as a verb, "to spoil (someone's) score." Origin obscure, it was believed in 17c.

meanings.definitionCn: 瘫痪， 麻痹， 中风

morphology.mnemonic: 平等 + lys + 过程/状态

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin paralysis, from Greek paralysis "paralysis, palsy," literally "loosening," from paralyein "disable, enfeeble," from para- "beside" (see para- (1)) + lyein "loosen, untie" (see lose).
"act of praising, commendation," late 15c., from Latin laudationem (nominative laudatio) "a praising, commendation," noun of action from past participle stem of laudare "to praise" (see laud)..

meanings.definitionCn: 半岛的；形成半岛的；半岛状的

etymology.story: 1610s, from peninsula + -ar..
1712, formed irregularly from Latin acer (fem.

1660s, from French bile (17c.) "bile," also, informally, "anger," from Latin bilis "fluid secreted by the liver," also one of the four humors (also known as choler), thus "anger, peevishness" (especially as black bile, 1797)..

morphology.mnemonic: 两/双 + 婚姻 + …主义者/…专家

etymology.story: 1630s; see bigamy + -ist.

meanings.definitionCn: 仰卧的；懒散的；掌心向上的；向后靠的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French dilater, from Late Latin dilatare "make wider, enlarge," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + latus "wide" (see latitude).
1610s, from Latin census "the enrollment of the names and property assessments of all Roman citizens," originally past participle of censere "to assess" (see censor (n.)).

early 15c., in Scottish, from Middle French domaine "domain, estate," from Old French demaine "lord's estate," from Latin dominium "property, dominion," from dominus "lord, master, owner," from domus "house" (see domestic).

meanings.definitionCn: 代理人；受托人；分配到任务的人

morphology.mnemonic: to fix, settle, determine, appoint + ee

etymology.story: early 15c., "one who is appointed to act for another," from Old French assigne, past participle of assignier (see assign)..

meanings.definitionCn: 初期的；开始存在的，发生中的

etymology.story: 1620s, from Latin nascentem (nominative nascens) "arising young, immature," present participle of nasci "to be born" (Old Latin gnasci; see genus).

morphology.mnemonic: in + 流动 + ance + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "powerful, having influence," 1650s, from Medieval Latin influentialis, from influentia (see influence (n.)).
1530s, "fasten or fix forcibly," from Latin impingere "drive into, strike against," from assimilated form of in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + pangere "to fix, fasten" (see pact).

morphology.mnemonic: a plural number + 主义/学说/运动

etymology.story: 1818, as a term in church administration, from plural + -ism.

meanings.definitionCn: 舒适；礼仪；愉快；便利设施

etymology.story: late 14c., "quality of being pleasant or agreeable," from Old French amenite, from Latin amoenitatem (nominative amoenitas) "delightfulness, pleasantness," from amoenus "pleasant," perhaps related to amare "to love" (see Amy)..

meanings.definitionCn: 严厉的；顽强的；阴沉的；不爱讲话的

etymology.story: mid-14c., "severe," from Scottish and northern England dialect, probably from Latin durus "hard" (see endure); sense of "gloomy, sullen" is late 15c..

early 15c., "a yearly allowance, grant payable in annual installments," from Anglo-French and Old French annuité (14c.) or directly from Medieval Latin annuitatem (nominative annuitas), from Latin annus "year" (see annual (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 透明的，透光的；易理解的；明显的

morphology.mnemonic: 穿越/转变 + 平等 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: early 15c., from Medieval Latin transparentem (nominative transparens), present participle of transparere "show light through," from Latin trans- "through" (see trans-) + parere "come in sight, appear" (see appear).

meanings.definitionCn: 院士；大学生；学会会员；大学教师
冒称；霸占；没来由地将…归属于

etymology.story: 1530s, from Latin arrogatus, past participle of arrogare "to claim for oneself" (see arrogance).
late 14c., "pomp; arrogance, pride;" see brag (v.); the exact relationship of the forms is uncertain.

meanings.definitionCn: 学期;期限,期间;(pl.)条件,条款;术语

etymology.story: "steep slope," in northern England especially "the sides of a hill," early 14c., from Scottish, "slope, river bank," from Old Norse bra "eyelash," cognate with Old English bræw "eyelid," German Braue "eyebrow" (see brow).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + quire + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "act of obtaining," from Old French acquisicion (13c.) or directly from Latin acquisitionem (nominative acquisitio), noun of action from past participle stem of acquirere "get in addition, accumulate," from ad- "extra" (see ad-) + quaerere "to seek to obtain" (see query (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 沉入/合并 + ance

etymology.story: 1640s, "unforeseen occurrence," from French émergence, from emerger, from Latin emergere "rise up" (see emerge).

"important arrival," 1742, an extended sense of  Advent "season before Christmas" (Old English), from Latin adventus "a coming, approach, arrival," in Church Latin "the coming of the Savior," from past participle stem of advenire "arrive, come to," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + venire "to come" (see venue).

meanings.definitionCn: 中世纪的,中古(时代)的,老式的,原始的

etymology.story: 1827, "pertaining to or suggestive of the Middle Ages," coined in English from Latin medium "the middle" (see medium (n.)) + aevum "age" (see eon)..

meanings.definitionCn: 卷入,陷入,连累;包含,含有,涉及

etymology.story: late 14c., "envelop, surround; make cloudy or obscure," from Old French involver and directly from Latin involvere "envelop, surround, overwhelm," literally "roll into," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + volvere "to roll" (see volvox).
mid-15c., from Latin requisitus, past participle of requirere (see require).

1530s, "delude, make a fool of," from Latin eludere "finish play, win at play; escape from or parry (a blow), make a fool of, mock, frustrate; win from at play," from assimilated form of ex- "out, away" (see ex-) + ludere "to play" (see ludicrous).
1640s, from Latin herbaceus "grassy," from herba "grass, herbage" (see herb)..

late 15c., "prompt to serve," from Middle French obséquieux (15c.), from Latin obsequiosus "compliant, obedient," from obsequium "compliance, dutiful service," from obsequi "to accommodate oneself to the will of another," from ob "after" (see ob-) + sequi "to follow" (see sequel).

meanings.definitionCn: 健忘症，[内科] 记忆缺失

etymology.story: "loss of memory," 1786 (as a Greek word in English from 1670s), Modern Latin, coined from Greek amnesia "forgetfulness," from a-, privative prefix, "not" (see a- (3)) + stem from mnasthai "to recall, remember," related to mnemnon "mindful," mneme "memory;" from PIE root *men- "to think, remember" (see mind (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 暴动；煽动性的言论或行为；妨害治安

etymology.story: mid-14c., "rebellion, uprising, revolt, concerted attempt to overthrow civil authority; violent strife between factions, civil or religious disorder, riot; rebelliousness against authority," from Old French sedicion (14c., Modern French sédition) and directly from Latin seditionem (nominative seditio) "civil disorder, dissention, strife; rebellion, mutiny," literally "a going apart, separation," from se- "apart" (see secret) + itio "a going," from past participle of ire "to go" (see ion).

meanings.definitionCn: 倒退的；退化的；次序颠倒的

root.meaning: to reduce (a road, etc.) to a level or degree of inclination

morphology.mnemonic: retro + to reduce (a road, etc.) to a level or degree of inclination

etymology.story: late 14c., originally of the apparent motions of planets, from Latin retrogradus "going back, going backward," from retrogradi "move backward," from retro- "backward" (see retro-) + gradi "to go, step" (see grade (n.)).

"paranormal gift of seeing things out of sight," 1837, from special use of French clairvoyance (Old French clerveans, 13c.) "quickness of understanding, sagacity, penetration," from clairvoyant (see clairvoyant).

mid-15c., Scottish, "postpone, defer," from Middle French superceder "desist, delay, defer," from Latin supersedere literally "sit on top of;" also, with ablative, "stay clear of, abstain from, forbear, refrain from," from super "above" (see super-) + sedere "to sit" (see sedentary).

1530s, "to turn over in the mind," also "to chew cud" (1540s), from Latin ruminatus, past participle of ruminare "to chew the cud; turn over in the mind," from rumen (genitive ruminis) "gullet," of uncertain origin.

"to leave one's duty," late 14c., from Old French deserter (12c.) "leave," literally "undo or sever connection," from Late Latin desertare, frequentative of Latin deserere "to abandon, to leave, forsake, give up, leave in the lurch," from de- "undo" (see de-) + serere "join together, put in a row" (see series).

common European bird, Old English finc "finch," from Proto-Germanic *finkiz "finch" (source also of Middle Low German and Middle Dutch vinke, Dutch vink, Old High German finco, German Fink), perhaps imitative of the bird's note (compare Breton pint "chaffinch," Russian penka "wren")..
1650s, "table of contents of a series of lectures, etc.," from Late Latin syllabus "list," ultimately a misreading of Greek sittybos "parchment label, table of contents," of unknown origin.
early 15c., from Late Latin pulverizare "reduce to powder or dust," from Latin pulvis (genitive pulveris) "dust, powder" (see pollen).

1570s, "form of speech of a region or group," from Middle French dialecte, from Latin dialectus "local language, way of speaking, conversation," from Greek dialektos "talk, conversation, speech;" also "the language of a country, dialect," from dialegesthai "converse with each other," from dia- "across, between" (see dia-) + legein "speak" (see lecture (n.))..

late 14c., from Old French rigor "strength, hardness" (13c., Modern French rigueur), from Latin rigorem (nominative rigor) "numbness, stiffness, hardness, firmness; roughness, rudeness," from rigere "be stiff" (see rigid)..
1610s, from Latin turgidus "swollen, inflated, distended," from turgere "to swell," of unknown origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 紧急情况,突然事件,非常时刻

etymology.story: "unforeseen occurrence requiring immediate attention," 1630s, from Latin emergens, present participle of emergere "to rise out or up" (see emerge).
1804, from French paralyser (16c.), from Old French paralisie "paralysis," from Latin paralysis (see paralysis).
also mis-state, 1640s, from mis- (1) + state (v.).

early 15c., from Latin gesticulationem (nominative gesticulatio), noun of action from past participle stem of gesticulari "to gesture, mimic," from gesticulus "a mimicking gesture," diminutive of gestus "a gesture; carriage, posture," noun use of past participle of gerere "to bear, to carry" (see gest)..
1775; see anachronism + -istic..

meanings.definitionCn: 典范的；惩戒性的；可仿效的

morphology.mnemonic: that which is taken out, + 与…有关的/像…的 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1580s, "fit to be an example," from Middle French exemplaire, from Late Latin exemplaris "that serves as an example, pattern, or motto," from exemplum "example, pattern, model" (see example).

meanings.definitionCn: 现任的；依靠的；负有职责的
在职者；现任者；领圣俸者

etymology.story: 1560s, in relation to duties or obligations, from Medieval Latin incumbentem (nominative incumbens), present participle of incumbere (see incumbent (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 加速，促进；[物] 加速度

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + celer + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1530s, from Latin accelerationem (nominative acceleratio) "a hastening," noun of action from past participle stem of accelerare "to hasten, quicken" (see accelerate)..

meanings.definitionCn: 固执的，坚强的；坚定不移的；坚硬无比的

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French adamant and directly from Latin adamantem (nominative adamas) "adamant, hardest iron, steel," also figuratively, of character, from Greek adamas (genitive adamantos) "unbreakable, inflexible" metaphoric of anything unalterable, also the name of a hypothetical hardest material, perhaps literally "invincible," from a- "not" + daman "to conquer, to tame" (see tame (adj.)), or else a word of foreign origin altered to conform to Greek.

meanings.definitionCn: 液压的；水力的；水力学的

etymology.story: "pertaining to fluids in motion," c.

"cool, calm, self-possessed," and in a more pejorative sense, "cold, dull, apathetic," 1570s, from literal sense "abounding in phlegm (as a bodily humor)" (mid-14c., fleumatik), from Old French fleumatique (13c., Modern French flegmatique), from Late Latin phlegmaticus, from Greek phlegmatikos "abounding in phlegm" (see phlegm)..

meanings.definitionCn: 半径，半径范围；[解剖] 桡骨；辐射光线；有效航程

etymology.story: 1590s, "cross-shaft," from Latin radius "staff, stake, rod; spoke of a wheel; ray of light, beam of light; radius of a circle," of unknown origin.
early 15c., from Middle French fortitude, from Latin fortitudo "strength, force, firmness, manliness," from fortis "strong, brave" (see fort)..
late 14c., a re-Latinizing of earlier ampayre, apeyre "make worse, cause to deteriorate" (c.
1630s, "a setting free," from French émancipation, from Latin emancipationem (nominative emancipatio), noun of action from past participle stem of emancipare (see emancipate).

late 15c., from Old French sumptueux or directly from Latin sumptuosus "costly, very expensive; lavish, wasteful," from sumptus, past participle of sumere "to borrow, buy, spend, eat, drink, consume, employ, take, take up," contraction of *sub-emere, from sub- "under" (see sub-) + emere "to take, buy" (see exempt (adj.)).

1640s, from French cajoler "to cajole, wheedle, coax," perhaps a blend of Middle French cageoler "to chatter like a jay" (16c., from gajole, southern diminutive of geai "jay;" see jay (n.)), and Old French gaioler "to cage, entice into a cage" (see jail (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 排出；耗尽；使精疲力尽；彻底探讨

etymology.story: "waste gas," 1848, originally from steam engines, from exhaust (v.).

late 14c., "capable of being dissolved," from Old French soluble "expungable, eradicable" (13c.), from Late Latin solubilis "that may be loosened or dissolved," from stem of Latin solvere "loosen, dissolve" (see solve).

meanings.definitionCn: 引起…的兴趣；策划阴谋；私通（intrigue的ing形式）

etymology.story: 1680s, "plotting, scheming," present-participle adjective from intrigue (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 招摇的；卖弄的；夸耀的；铺张的；惹人注目的

etymology.story: 1701, from ostentation + -ous.
mid-15c., "the proving of guilt," from Late Latin convictionem (nominative convictio) "proof, refutation," noun of action from past participle stem of convincere (see convince).

morphology.mnemonic: different in character or quality. + 使…化/造成

etymology.story: late 15c., from Old French diversifier (13c.) "to make diverse," from Medieval Latin diversificare, from Latin diversus (see diverse).
"beautiful, handsome," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 发明的， 创造的； 善于发明创造的

etymology.story: early 15c., "skilled in invention," from Old French inventif (15c.), from Latin invent-, past participle stem of invenire "devise, discover, find" (see invention).
1878, shortening of graphic formula (see graphic).

late 14c., from Old French imbiber, embiber "to soak into," and directly from Latin imbibere "absorb, drink in, inhale," from assimilated form of in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + bibere "to drink," related to potare "to drink," from PIE *po(i)- "to drink" (see potion).

meanings.definitionCn: 晕头转向，[临床] 眩晕

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin vertigo "dizziness, sensation of whirling," originally "a whirling or spinning movement," from vertere "to turn" (see versus)..

meanings.definitionCn: 自命不凡的， 自负的； 炫耀的； 做作的

etymology.story: 1836, from French prétentieux (17c.), from prétention "pretension," from Medieval Latin pretentionem (nominative pretentio) "pretension," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin praetendere (see pretend (v.))..

morphology.mnemonic: 太阳/独自 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: "substance able to dissolve other substances," 1670s, from Latin solventem (see solvent (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 裸的，裸体的；无装饰的；与生俱有的

etymology.story: "nude figure in visual art," 1708, from French nud, obsolete variant of nu "naked, nude, bare," from Latin nudus (see nude (adj.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 成比例的；相称的；适当的

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + portion + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: late 14c., "of proper proportion," from Medieval Latin proportionatus "proportioned," past participle of proportionare (see proportion (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: [计] 虚拟的；实质上的，事实上的（但未在名义上或正式获承认）

etymology.story: late 14c., "influencing by physical virtues or capabilities, effective with respect to inherent natural qualities," from Medieval Latin virtualis, from Latin virtus "excellence, potency, efficacy," literally "manliness, manhood" (see virtue).

late 14c., araynen, "to call to account," from Old French araisnier "speak to, address; accuse (in a law court)," from Vulgar Latin *arrationare, from Latin adrationare, from ad- "to" (see ad-) + *rationare, from ratio "argumentation, reckoning, calculation" (see ratio).

1620s, "well-put or applied, appropriate," from Latin appositus "contiguous, neighboring;" figuratively "fit, proper, suitable," past participle of apponere "apply to, put near," from ad- "near" (see ad-) + ponere "to place" (past participle positus; see position (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 狂饮， 暴饮； 大量消耗
婚姻的；结婚的；夫妻之间的

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + jug + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1540s, from Middle French conjugal (13c.), from Latin coniugalis "relating to marriage," from coniunx (genitive coniugis) "spouse," related to coniugare "to join together," from com- "together" (see com-) + iugare "to join," from iugum "yoke" (see jugular)..
late 14c., "crooked, askew," from a- (1) "on" + wry (adj.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 期间；持久， 持续时间

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French duration, from Medieval Latin durationem (nominative duratio), noun of action from past participle stem of Latin durare "harden" (see endure).
1530s, from Latin aquaeductus "conveyance of water," from aquae, genitive of aqua "water" (see aqua-), + ductus "a leading, conducting," past participle of ducere "to lead" (see duke (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 岁差（等于precession of the equinoxes）；先行；优先

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 走/让步 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1590s, from Late Latin praecissionem (nominative praecissio) "a coming before," from past participle stem of Latin praecedere "to go before" (see precede).
1796, past participle adjective from frenzy (v.).

late 14c., from Old French arrogant (14c.), from Latin arrogantem (nominative arrogans) "assuming, overbearing, insolent," present participle of arrogare "to claim for oneself, assume" (see arrogance).

early 15c., "learned, well-instructed," from Latin eruditus "learned, accomplished, well-informed," past participle of erudire "to educate, teach, instruct, polish," literally "to bring out of the rough," from assimilated form of ex- "out" (see ex-) + rudis "unskilled, rough, unlearned" (see rude).
1530s (implied in abusively), originally "improper," from Middle French abusif, from Latin abusivus, from abus-, past participle stem of abuti (see abuse (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 不足/在…之下 + to give up for money

meanings.definitionCn: 长度，长；时间的长短；（语）音长

etymology.story: Old English lengðu "property of being long or extended in one direction; distance along a line," from Proto-Germanic *langitho, abstract noun from *langaz "long" (root of Old English lang; see long (adj.)) + *-itho, abstract noun suffix (see -th (2)).
colloquial shortening of advertisement, attested by 1860..

meanings.definitionCn: 安排，计划；编制目录；将……列入计划表

morphology.mnemonic: in + rise high and roll forcefully + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: "one who rises in revolt" against a government or its laws, 1745, from Latin insurgentem (nominative insurgens), present participle of insurgere "rise up, lift oneself; rise against; stand high, gather force," from in- "against," or here perhaps merely intensive, + surgere "to rise" (see surge (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 消色差的；非染色质的；非彩色的

etymology.story: 1766, from Latinized form of Greek akhromatos "colorless," from a-, privative prefix (see a- (3)), + khromat-, comb.

meanings.definitionCn: 派别；内讧；小集团；纪实小说

morphology.mnemonic: 事实 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1530s, "hard to accomplish, difficult to do," from Latin arduus "high, steep," also figuratively, "difficult," from PIE root *eredh- "to grow, high" (see ortho-).

1610s, "pertaining to play or sport," from Latin ludicrus, from ludicrum "a sport, game, toy, source of amusement, joke," from ludere "to play," which, with Latin ludus "a game, play," perhaps is from Etruscan, or perhaps from PIE root *leid- "to play." Sense of "ridiculous" is attested from 1782.
1660s, from stem of Latin atrox "fierce, savage, cruel" (see atrocity) + -ous.

meanings.definitionCn: 设置数据文件的搜索路径

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 悬挂/称量/支付

etymology.story: late 14c., "to belong to as a possession or right," from Old French apendre (13c.) belong, be dependent (on); attach (oneself) to; hang, hang up," and directly from Latin appendere "to cause to hang (from something), weigh," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + pendere "hang" (see pendant).

"depravity, infamy," late 15c., from Middle French turpitude (early 15c.), from Latin turpitudinem (nominative turpitudo) "baseness," from turpis "vile, physically ugly, base, unsightly," figuratively "morally ugly, scandalous, shameful," of unknown origin.

1620s, "intensely cold," from Latin frigidus "cold, chill, cool," figuratively "indifferent," also "flat, dull, trivial," from stem of frigere "be cold;" related to noun frigus "cold, coldness, frost," from Proto-Italic *srigos-, from PIE root *srig- "cold" (source also of Greek rhigos "cold, frost").

early 15c., from Old French invincible (14c.) or directly from Latin invincibilis "unconquerable," from in- "not" (see in- (1)) + vincibilis "to be gained, easily maintained, conquerable," from vincere "to overcome" (see victor).

1640s, from verbal phrase tant amount "be equivalent" (1620s), from Anglo-French tant amunter "amount to as much" (late 13c.), from Old French tant "as much" (11c., from Latin tantus, from tam "so;" see tandem) + amonter "amount to, go up" (see amount (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 耗尽；用尽；使…精疲力尽（exhaust的过去式）

etymology.story: mid-17c., "consumed, used up;" of persons, "tired out," past participle adjective from exhaust (v.).

1620s, "reckon as an abatement or deduction," from Old French desconter (13c., Modern French décompter), from Medieval Latin discomputare, from dis- (see dis-) + computare "to count" (see count (v.)).
late 14c., from Anglo-French and Old French verite "truth, sincerity, loyalty" (12c.), from Latin veritatem (nominative veritas) "truth, truthfulness," from verus "true" (see very).

meanings.definitionCn: 变换,转换；改变（信仰等）；兑换（钱）
滋味；风味；食欲；开胃小菜；含义；调味品
盼望;期待;享受; 品味；喜爱；给…加佐料
分,分割;部门,科,处;除法;分界线

morphology.mnemonic: watershed, separation between river valleys + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French division, from Latin divisionem (nominative divisio), from divid-, stem of dividere (see divide).

meanings.definitionCn: 精馏，蒸馏，净化；蒸馏法；精华，蒸馏物

etymology.story: late 14c., "process of distilling," from Late Latin distillationem (nominative distillatio), noun of action from past participle stem of distillare (see distill).
1530s, "irritate, provoke to anger," from Latin exasperatus, past participle of exasperare "make rough, roughen, irritate, provoke," from ex- "thoroughly" (see ex-) + asper "rough" (see asperity).

meanings.definitionCn: 偶像破坏者；提倡打破旧习的人

etymology.story: "breaker or destroyer of images," 1590s, from French iconoclaste and directly from Medieval Latin iconoclastes, from Late Greek eikonoklastes, from eikon (genitive eikonos) "image" + klastes "breaker," from klas- past tense stem of klan "to break" (see clastic).

meanings.definitionCn: 沥青；音高；程度；树脂；倾斜；投掷；球场

etymology.story: mid-14c., "action, deeds," from Anglo-French fet, from Old French fait "action, deed, achievement" (12c.), from Latin factum "thing done," a noun based on the past participle of facere "make, do" (see factitious, and compare fact).

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 走/让步 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Old French *incessant or directly from Late Latin incessantem (nominative incessans) "unceasing," from Latin in- "not" (see in- (1)) + cessans, present participle of cessare "to cease" (see cease (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: in + 看/观察 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French inspeccion "inspection, examination" (13c., Modern French inspection), from Latin inspectionem (nominative inspectio) "a looking into," noun of action from past participle stem of inspicere "look at, observe, view; look into, inspect, examine," from in- "into" (see in- (2)) + specere "to look" (see scope (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 完美的；洁净的；无瑕疵的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + macul + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "free from mental or moral pollution, pure," from a figurative use of Latin immaculatus "unstained," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + maculatus "spotted, defiled," past participle of maculare "to spot," from macula "spot, blemish," a word of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 疏忽的；不注意的（副词inadvertently）；无意中做的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 统治 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1650s, "not properly attentive" (of persons), a back-formation from inadvertence.

late 14c., from Old French statue, estatue "(pagan) statue, graven image" (12c.), from Latin statua "image, statue, monumental figure, representation in metal," properly "that which is set up," back-formation from statuere "to cause to stand, set up," from status "a standing, position" (see status).

meanings.definitionCn: 声称， 自命； 自负， 自命不凡

etymology.story: mid-15c., "assertion, allegation; objection; intention; signification," from Medieval Latin pretensionem (nominative praetensio), noun of action from past participle stem of Latin praetendere "stretch in front, put forward, allege" (see pretend (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: /n.有危险性的人(或物)；威胁，威吓
精华；典范；第五元素（被视为地、水、火、风以外之构成宇宙的元素）

root.meaning: ingredient which gives something its particular character

morphology.mnemonic: quint + ingredient which gives something its particular character

etymology.story: early 15c., in ancient and medieval philosophy, "pure essence, substance of which the heavenly bodies are composed," literally "fifth essence," from Middle French quinte essence (14c.), from Medieval Latin quinta essentia, from Latin quinta, fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 过多；过剩；[医] 多血症

etymology.story: 1540s, a medical word for "excess of body fluid," from Late Latin plethora, from Greek plethore "fullness," from plethein "be full" (see pleio-).

meanings.definitionCn: 揭穿；拆穿…的假面具；暴露

etymology.story: 1923,  from de- + bunk (n.2); first used by U.S.

meanings.definitionCn: 领域；牧场；旷野；战场；运动场
有道德的， 品性好的， 品德高的； 自命清高的； 贞洁的

root.meaning: sufficiently supported by facts or authority, well-grounded

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + sufficiently supported by facts or authority, well-grounded

meanings.definitionCn: 间隔,间歇;(幕间或工间)休息

etymology.story: early 14c., "time elapsed between two actions or events," from Old French intervalle "interval, interim" (14c.), earlier entreval (13c.) and directly from Late Latin intervallum "a space between, an interval of time, a distance," originally "space between palisades or ramparts" [OED], from inter "between" (see inter-) + vallum "rampart, palisade, wall," which is apparently a collective form of vallus "stake," from PIE *walso- "a post" (see wall (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 合法的；合理的，合乎逻辑的

etymology.story: "establish the legitimacy of, make lawful," 1590s, from Medieval Latin legitimatus, past participle of legitimare "make lawful" (see legitimate (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 世界主义者； 四海为家者

etymology.story: 1844, from cosmopolite "citizen of the world" (q.v.) on model of metropolitan.

meanings.definitionCn: 地位；状态；情形；重要身份

etymology.story: 1670s, "height" of a situation or condition, later "legal standing of a person" (1791), from Latin status "condition, position, state, manner, attitude" from past participle stem of stare "to stand," from PIE *ste-tu-, from root *stā- "to stand" (see stet).

meanings.definitionCn: 断然拒绝；回绝；漠不关心

morphology.mnemonic: the British Empire, + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1540s, "of a dominating character," from Latin imperiosus "commanding, mighty, powerful," from imperium "empire, command" (see empire).
1724, from blithe + -some (1).

morphology.mnemonic: suitable for a family, + 与…有关的/像…的 + ity

root.meaning: device that breaks an electrical circuit

morphology.mnemonic: 分开/二 + device that breaks an electrical circuit + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Latin diffusionem (nominative diffusio) "a pouring forth," noun of action from past participle stem of diffundere "scatter, pour out," from dis- "apart, in every direction" (see dis-) + fundere "pour" (see found (v.2))..
1590s, from Middle French pétrifier "to make or become stone" (16c.), from Latin petra "rock, crag" (see petrous) + -ficare, from facere "to make, do" (see factitious).

meanings.definitionCn: 外来的；没有关联的；来自体外的

etymology.story: 1630s, from Latin extraneus "external, strange," literally "that is without, from without" (as a noun, "a stranger"), from extra "outside of" (see extra-).

morphology.mnemonic: 不/分离 + semin + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French liquefier "liquefy, dissolve," from Latin liquefacere "make liquid, melt," from liquere "be fluid" (see liquid (adj.)) + facere "to make" (see factitious)..

meanings.definitionCn: 厌恶；憎恨；令人厌恶的事物

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 14c., "abominable thing or action;" late 14c., "feeling of disgust, hatred, loathing," from Old French abominacion "abomination, horror, repugnance, disgust" (13c.), from Latin abominationem (nominative abominatio) "abomination," noun of action from past participle stem of abominari "shun as an ill omen," from ab- "off, away from" (see ab-) + omin-, stem of omen (see omen).
"a lawgiver, a maker of laws," c.

morphology.mnemonic: 分开/二 + 步/走 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Latin digressionem (nominative digressio) "a going away, departing," noun of action from past participle stem of digredi "to deviate," from dis- "apart, aside" (see dis-) + gradi "to step, go" (see grade (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 预告， 先兆；传令官 A <
(尤指不好的事)一再发生；重现

etymology.story: late 14c., "recover from illness or suffering;" mid-15c., "to return" (to a place), from Latin recurrere "to return, run back, hasten back," figuratively "revert, recur," from re- "back, again" (see re-) + currere "to run" (see current (adj.)).

1610s, "the growing together of parts," from French coalition (1540s), from Late Latin coalitus "fellowship," originally past participle of Latin coalescere "unite, grow together, become one in growth" (see coalesce).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 堆积/积累 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin accumulatus, past participle of accumulare "to heap up" (see accumulation).
1530s, "pertaining to the earliest period, primitive, ancient," from Middle French pristin or directly from Latin pristinus "former, early, original," from Old Latin pri "before" (see prime (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 剔出来杀掉的动物；拣出的等外品

etymology.story: early 14c., leisir, "opportunity to do something," also "free time, time at one's disposal," from Old French leisir, loisir "capacity, ability, freedom (to do something); permission; spare time; free will; idleness, inactivity," noun use of infinitive leisir "be permitted," from Latin licere "to be allowed" (see licence (n.)).

early 15c., "to flood, overflow," from Anglo-French surounder, Middle French soronder "to overflow, abound; surpass, dominate," from Late Latin superundare "overflow," from Latin super "over" (see super-) + undare "to flow in waves," from unda "wave" (see water (n.1); and compare abound).

morphology.mnemonic: 连接 + 行为/过程/结果

etymology.story: late 14c., "place where two things are joined," from Latin iunctura "a joining, uniting, a joint," from iunctus, past participle of iungere "to join" (see jugular).
1670s, "vaulted recess," from French alcôve (17c.), from Spanish alcoba, from Arabic al-qobbah "the vaulted chamber," from Semitic base q-b-b "to be bent, crooked, vaulted.".
1680s, probably from Low German oeglen, frequentative of oegen "look at," from oege "eye," from Proto-Germanic *augon-, from PIE *okw- "to see" (see eye (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 相似/相同 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin assimilatus "feigned, pretended, fictitious," past participle of assimilare "to make like," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + simulare "make similar," from similis "like, resembling" (see similar).

root.meaning: moral principles of a person or group

morphology.mnemonic: moral principles of a person or group + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "full of zeal" (in the service of a person or cause), 1520s, from Medieval Latin zelosus "full of zeal" (source of Italian zeloso, Spanish celoso), from zelus (see zeal).

meanings.definitionCn: 尖刻的；锐利的；苛刻的；锋利的（名词trenchancy，副词trenchantly）

etymology.story: early 14c., "cutting, sharp," from Old French trenchant "cutting, sharp" (literal and figurative), present participle of trenchier "to cut" (see trench).

meanings.definitionCn: 俯卧的；拜倒的；降伏的；沮丧的
艺术品鉴赏家；古董收藏家；艺术大师
行家里手的；艺术爱好者的

etymology.story: 1610s, "scholar, connoisseur," from Italian virtuoso (plural virtuosi), noun use of adjective meaning "skilled, learned, of exceptional worth," from Late Latin virtuosus (see virtuous).
1901, from glamour + -ize, with typical dropping of the -u- in derivatives (see -or).
late 13c., "help or relieve when in difficulty," from Old French succurre "to help, assist" (Modern French secourir), from Latin succerrere "to help, assist" (see succor (n.)).
early 15c., from Middle French comparable, from Latin comparabilis "capable of comparison," from comparare (see comparison).

meanings.definitionCn: (言行)谨慎的；慎重的；有判断力的

etymology.story: mid-14c., "morally discerning, prudent, circumspect," from Old French discret "discreet, sensible, intelligent, wise," from Latin discretus "separated, distinct," in Medieval Latin "discerning, careful," past participle of discernere "distinguish" (see discern).

early 15c., "supreme happiness," from Middle French béatitude (15c.) and directly from Latin beatitudinem (nominative beatitudo) "state of blessedness," from past participle stem of beare "make happy" (see bene-).

meanings.definitionCn: 混乱，骚乱；精神病院，疯人院

etymology.story: "scene of mad confusion," 1660s, from colloquial pronunciation of "Hospital of Saint Mary of Bethlehem" in London, founded 1247 as a priory, mentioned as a hospital 1330 and as a lunatic hospital 1402; converted to a state lunatic asylum on dissolution of the monasteries in 1547.

mid-15c., "recover (property) by judicial means," from Latin evictus, past participle of evincere "overcome and expel, conquer, subdue, vanquish; prevail over; supplant," from assimilated form of ex- "out," or perhaps here merely intensive (see ex-) + vincere "conquer" (see victor).
late 15c., from Late Latin sufficientia, from Latin sufficiens "adequate" (see sufficient) + -cy.

meanings.definitionCn: 垄断,专卖,专利权,专利事业

morphology.mnemonic: 单一 + 多 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: "exclusive control of a commodity or trade," 1530s, from Latin monopolium, from Greek monopolion "right of exclusive sale," from mono- + polein "to sell," from PIE root *pel- (5) "to sell" (source also of Sanskrit panate "barters, purchases," Lithuanian pelnas "gain," Old Church Slavonic splenu, Russian polon "prey, booty," Old Norse falr, Dutch veil, German feil "for sale, venal").

morphology.mnemonic: put into a play + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: also stagey, 1845, from stage (n.) in the theatrical sense + -y (2).

"capable of being used in place of another; capable of being replaced," 1818, a word in law originally, from Medieval Latin fungibilis, from Latin fungi "perform" (see function (n.)) via phrases such as fungi vice "to take the place." Earlier as a noun (1765)..

morphology.mnemonic: 两/双 + language语言 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1818, from bi- + lingual.

meanings.definitionCn: 两党连立的；代表两党的

morphology.mnemonic: 两/双 + to separate the hair + …人/与…有关

etymology.story: also bi-partisan, 1894, from bi- + partisan..

meanings.definitionCn: 加强的；集中的；透彻的；加强语气的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "intense, fervent, great," from Old French intensif (14c.) and Medieval Latin intensivus, from Latin intens-, past participle stem of intendere "turn one's attention; strain, stretch" (see intend).
"put to shame," 1570s, from Middle French descontenancer (16c.) "to abash," from des- (see dis-) + contenancer (see countenance)..

meanings.definitionCn: (Alter)人名；(英)奥尔特；(德、捷、葡、爱沙、立陶、拉脱、俄、西、罗、瑞典)阿尔特

etymology.story: late 14c., "to change (something)," from Old French alterer "change, alter," from Medieval Latin alterare "to change," from Latin alter "the other (of the two)," from PIE *al- "beyond" (see alias (adv.)) + comparative suffix -ter (as in other).

root.meaning: a fraudulent production, something intended to deceive

morphology.mnemonic: a fraudulent production, something intended to deceive + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French fraudulent, from Latin fraudulentus "cheating, deceitful, dishonest," from stem of fraus "deceit" (see fraud).

meanings.definitionCn: 暗示；使逐渐而巧妙地取得；使迂回地潜入

etymology.story: 1520s, "to covertly and subtly introduce into the mind or heart" (trans.), from Latin insinuatus, past participle of insinuare "to thrust in, push in, make a way; creep in, intrude, bring in by windings and curvings, wind one's way into," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + sinuare "to wind, bend, curve," from sinus "a curve, winding" (see sinus).

meanings.definitionCn: [数] 近似法；接近；[数] 近似值

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 近/接近 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

late 14c., from Anglo-French sillable, alteration of Old French silabe "syllable" (12c., Modern French syllabe), from Latin syllaba, from Greek syllabe "that which is held together; a syllable, several sounds or letters taken together," i.e.

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + device that breaks an electrical circuit

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin past participle adjective effusus "poured out," also "extensive, vast, broad, wide" (see effuse (v.))..

etymology.story: 1670s, "literary burlesque of a serious work," from adjective meaning "dressed so as to be made ridiculous, parodied, burlesqued" (1660s), from French travesti "dressed in disguise," past participle of travestir "to disguise" (1590s), from Italian travestire "to disguise," from Latin trans- "over" (see trans-) + vestire "to clothe" (see wear (v.))..

morphology.mnemonic: 远离/脱离 + 引导 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1620s, "a leading away," from Latin abductionem (nominative abductio), noun of action from past participle stem of abducere "to lead away, take away" (often by force), from ab- "away" (see ab-) + ducere "to lead" (see duke (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 恰当的；善于措辞的；幸福的

etymology.story: 1726, "blissful, very happy," from felicity + -ous.

etymology.story: 1540s, "fixed, permanent," adjectival use of stayed, past participle of stay (v.).

"remedy counteracting poison," 1510s (earlier in English as a Latin word), from Middle French antidot and directly from Latin antidotum "a remedy against poison," from Greek antidoton "given as a remedy," literally "given against," verbal adjective of antididonai "give in return," from anti- "against" + didonai "to give" (see date (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 有髓的，多髓的；精练的；简洁有力的

etymology.story: early 14c., "strong, vigorous," from pith (n.) + -y (2).

meanings.definitionCn: 刚毅，大胆；厚颜；胆大无敌

root.meaning: with effort or energy, with difficulty

morphology.mnemonic: with effort or energy, with difficulty + 具有…特征/名词化 + hood

etymology.story: 1848, from German Zeitgeist (Herder, 1769), "spirit of the age," literally "time-spirit," from Zeit "time" (see tide (n.)) + Geist "spirit" (see ghost (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 端庄的；娴静的；严肃的；假装正直的

etymology.story: 1670s, "glass filled to the brim;" perhaps from notion of bumping as "large," or from a related sense of "booming" (see bump (v.)).
late 14c., from Low German wrangeln "to dispute, to wrestle," related to Middle Low German wringen, from Proto-Germanic *wrang-, from PIE *wrengh-, nasalized variant of *wergh- "to turn" (see wring).

meanings.definitionCn: 普遍存在的；无所不在的

etymology.story: "being, existing, or turning up everywhere," 1800, from ubiquity + -ous.

meanings.definitionCn: 变异，[生物] 分化；区别

morphology.mnemonic: day日 + …的人/处于…状态 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: 典范; 范例; 示例

etymology.story: late 15c., from Late Latin paradigma "pattern, example," especially in grammar, from Greek paradeigma "pattern, model; precedent, example," from paradeiknynai "exhibit, represent," literally "show side by side," from para- "beside" (see para- (1)) + deiknynai "to show" (cognate with Latin dicere "to show;" see diction).

meanings.definitionCn: [电] 变压器；促使变化的人

morphology.mnemonic: 穿越/转变 + 形状/形态 + er

etymology.story: 1560s, from Middle French présager (16c.), from présage "omen," from Latin praesagium (see presage (n.)).

early 15c., "faithfulness, devotion," from Middle French fidélité (15c.), from Latin fidelitatem (nominative fidelitas) "faithfulness, adherence, trustiness," from fidelis "faithful, true, trusty, sincere," from fides "faith" (see faith).

morphology.mnemonic: diabol + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

meanings.definitionCn: 辉耀的；炽热的；发白热光的

etymology.story: 1794, from French incandescent (18c.) or directly from Latin incandescentem (nominative incandescens), present participle of incandescere "become warm, glow, kindle," from in- "within" (see in- (2)) + candescere "begin to glow, become white," inceptive of candere "to glow, to shine" (see candle).

"one of the two ends of an open electrical circuit," 1834, coined by English physicist and chemist Michael Faraday (1791-1867) from electro- + Greek hodos "way" (see cede) on the same pattern as anode, cathode..

meanings.definitionCn: 鲁莽的，不顾后果的；粗心大意的

root.meaning: direct in a straight line, rule

morphology.mnemonic: direct in a straight line, rule + 没有…的/无…的

etymology.story: Old English receleas "careless, thoughtless, heedless," earlier reccileas, from *rece, recce "care, heed," from reccan "to care" (see reck (v.)) + -less.

"large bundle or package," early 14c., from Old French bale "rolled-up bundle," from Frankish or some other Germanic source (such as Old High German balla "ball"), from Proto-Germanic *ball-, from PIE *bhel- (2) "to blow, swell" (see bole)..

meanings.definitionCn: 似非而是的话,自相矛盾的话,反论

etymology.story: 1530s, "statement contrary to common belief or expectation," from Middle French paradoxe (14c.) and directly from Latin paradoxum "paradox, statement seemingly absurd yet really true," from Greek paradoxon, noun use of neuter of adjective paradoxos "contrary to expectation, incredible," from para- "contrary to" (see para- (1)) + doxa "opinion," from dokein "to appear, seem, think" (see decent).

meanings.definitionCn: 分泌脂质的；脂肪的，脂肪质的；似油脂或皮脂的

etymology.story: 1728, "secreting sebum," from Latin sebaceus "of tallow," from sebum "tallow, grease" (see sebum).

morphology.mnemonic: just + 使…化/造成 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "administration of justice," from Late Latin iustificationem (nominative iustificatio), noun of action from past participle stem of iustificare "act justly toward; make just" (see justify).

meanings.definitionCn: 轴；纺锤，锭子；细长的人或物
锭子的，锭子似的；细长的

etymology.story: early 13c., with intrusive -d-, from Old English spinel "small wooden bar used in hand-spinning," properly "an instrument for spinning," from stem of spinnan (see spin (v.)) + instrumental suffix -el (1).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 抓住/理解 + able

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French reprehensible (14c.) or directly from Late Latin reprehensibilis, from reprehens-, past participle stem of Latin reprehendere "to blame, censure, rebuke; seize, restrain" (see reprehend).

meanings.definitionCn: 废除；撤销；废止；放弃；否定

root.meaning: struggle, work hard, labor for considerable time

morphology.mnemonic: struggle, work hard, labor for considerable time + remarkable

etymology.story: 1580s, from toil (v.) + -some (1).

etymology.story: late 14c., "harmful, noxious," from noye "harm, misfortune," shortened form of anoi "annoyance" (from Old French anoier, see annoy) + -some (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 陈腐的；平庸的；老一套的

etymology.story: "trite, commonplace," 1840, from French banal, "belonging to a manor, common, hackneyed, commonplace," from Old French banel "communal" (13c.), from ban "decree; legal control; announcement; authorization; payment for use of a communal oven, mill, etc." (see ban (v.)).
"given to biting," 1640s (originally figurative), from Latin mordac-, stem of mordax, from mordere "to bite," perhaps from PIE root mer- (2) "to rub, harm" (see smart (v.)).

"open large-mouthed vessel," mid-14c., from Old Norse bikarr or Middle Dutch beker "goblet," probably (with Old Saxon bikeri, Old High German behhari, German Becher) from Medieval Latin bicarium, which itself is probably a diminutive of Greek bikos "earthenware jug, wine jar" (said to be an oriental word, perhaps a borrowing from Syrian buqa "a two-handed vase or jug").
1580s, "the action of admitting," formed in English from admit + -ance (if from Latin, it would have been *admittence; French uses accès in this sense).
early 15c., "warlike," from Latin bellicosus "warlike, valorous, given to fighting," from bellicus "of war," from bellum "war," Old Latin duellum, dvellum, which is of uncertain origin..

early 15c., "increase efficacy" (of medicine); mid-15c., "provide (a town) with walls and defenses," from Old French fortifiier (14c.) "to fortify, strengthen," from Late Latin fortificare "to strengthen, make strong," from Latin fortis "strong" (see fort) + facere "to make" (see factitious).

meanings.definitionCn: 偶然性， 可能性； 意外事件； 附带事件

morphology.mnemonic: con + ance + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1560s, "quality of being contingent," from contingent + -cy.

mid-14c., "authoritative," from Old French autentique (13c., Modern French authentique) "authentic; canonical," and directly from Medieval Latin authenticus, from Greek authentikos "original, genuine, principal," from authentes "one acting on one's own authority," from autos "self" (see auto-) + hentes "doer, being," from PIE *sene- "to accomplish, achieve." Sense of "entitled to acceptance as factual" is first recorded mid-14c.

morphology.mnemonic: universally accepted, + 主义/学说/运动

etymology.story: "faith and practice of the Catholic church," 1610s, from Catholic + -ism..

meanings.definitionCn: 旅馆；门房；集会处；山林小屋
复合，再结合；[遗] 重组

etymology.story: 1791, from re- + combination..

mid-13c., derthe "scarcity" (originally used of famines, when food was costly because scarce; extended to other situations of scarcity from early 14c.), abstract noun formed from root of Old English deore "precious, costly" (see dear) + abstract noun suffix -th (2).

meanings.definitionCn: 犯罪，做错事；把交托给；指派…作战；使…承担义务

etymology.story: late 14c., "to give in charge, entrust," from Latin committere "to unite, connect, combine; to bring together," from com- "together" (see com-) + mittere "to put, send" (see mission).

meanings.definitionCn: 不可救药的；积习难改的

etymology.story: mid-14c., "incurable (of diseases, venom, etc.); extravagant (of expense); implacable (of hearts)," from Old French incorrigible "perfect, beyond rebuke or discipline" (14c.) or directly from Latin incorrigibilis "not to be corrected," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + corrigibilis, from corrigere "to correct" (see correct (v.)).
1590s, from Latin secantem (nominative secans) "a cutting," present participle of secare "to cut" (see section (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 降雨， 降水； 仓促， 急躁； 【化】 沉淀作用

etymology.story: late 15c., "a casting down" (of the evil angels from heaven), also, in alchemy "separation of a solid substance from a solution," from Middle French precipitation (15c.) and directly from Latin praecipitationem (nominative praecipitatio) "act or fact of falling headlong, haste," noun of action from past participle stem of praecipitare "fall, be hasty," from praeceps "steep" (see precipice).
1520s, "to bring to completion," from Latin consummatus, past participle of consummare "to sum up, make up, complete, finish" (see consummation).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 向/朝/在 + yes, certainly

etymology.story: "restore (someone) to confidence," 1590s, from re- "back, again" + assure.
mid-15c., from Latin divulgare "publish, make common," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + vulgare "make common property," from vulgus "common people" (see vulgar).

Old English bana "killer, slayer, murderer; the devil," from Proto-Germanic *banon, cognate with *banja- "wound" (source also of Old Frisian bona "murderer," Old Norse bani, Old High German bana "murder," Old English benn "wound," Gothic banja "stroke, wound"), from PIE root *gwhen- "to strike, kill, wound" (source also of Avestan banta "ill").

1540s, from Middle French crasse (16c.), from Latin crassus "solid, thick, fat; dense." The literal sense always has been rare in English; meaning "grossly stupid" is recorded from 1650s, from French.
1794, literally "something added," from Latin addendum, neuter of addendus "that which is to be added," gerundive of addere (see add (v.)).

1620s, "flat thin tablet used by an artist to lay and mix colors," from French palette, from Old French palete "small shovel, blade" (13c.) diminutive of pale "shovel, blade," from Latin pala "spade, shoulder blade," probably from PIE *pak-slo-, from root *pag- (see pact).

meanings.definitionCn: 轨道；眼眶；势力范围；生活常规
有益的，有用的；有益健康的

etymology.story: early 15c., "to include," from Old French compris, past participle of comprendre "to contain, comprise" (12c.), from Latin comprehendere (see comprehend).
Old English hiehþu, Anglian hehþo "highest part or point, summit; the heavens, heaven," from root of heah "high" (see high) + -itha, Germanic abstract noun suffix (as in width, depth; see -th (2)).

morphology.mnemonic: 向上/提高 + a dismissal + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1620s, "to purge" (in anatomy), back-formation from expurgation or from Latin expurgatus, past participle of expurgare "to cleanse out, purge, purify."

1660s, from Latin augustus "venerable, majestic, magnificent, noble," probably originally "consecrated by the augurs, with favorable auguries" (see augur (n.)); or else "that which is increased" (see augment)..

morphology.mnemonic: 分开/二 + male member, penis + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1690s, from Modern Latin divergentem (nominative divergens), present participle of divergere (see diverge).

meanings.definitionCn: 天真的；朴实的；无虚饰的

morphology.mnemonic: 技艺/技巧 + 没有…的/无…的

etymology.story: 1580s, "unskillful," from art (n.) + -less.

meanings.definitionCn: 通晓数种语言的人；用通晓数种语言记载的书
通数国语言，数国语言的

meanings.definitionCn: 流畅的，流利的；液态的；畅流的

etymology.story: 1580s, "flowing freely" (of water), also, of speakers, "able and nimble in the use of words," from Latin fluentem (nominative fluens) "lax, relaxed," figuratively "flowing, fluent," present participle of fluere "to flow, stream, run, melt," from extended form of PIE root *bhleu- "to swell, well up, overflow" (source also of Latin flumen "river;" Greek phluein "to boil over, bubble up," phlein "to abound"), an extension of root *bhel- (2) "to blow, inflate, swell;" see bole.

meanings.definitionCn: 大量的；实质的；内容充实的

morphology.mnemonic: any kind of corporeal matter + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: mid-14c., "ample, sizeable," from Old French substantiel (13c.) and directly from Latin substantialis "having substance or reality, material," in Late Latin "pertaining to the substance or essence," from substantia "being, essence, material" (see substance).

meanings.definitionCn: 实行;实施;执行;完成;履行;处死

etymology.story: late 15c., from Old French celeritee (14c., Modern French célérité), from Latin celeritatem (nominative celeritas) "swiftness," from celer "swift," from possible PIE root *kel- "to drive, set in swift motion" (source also of Sanskrit carati "goes," Greek keles "fast horse or ship," keleuthos "journey, road," Lithuanian sulys "a gallop," Old High German scelo "stallion")..

meanings.definitionCn: 虚无主义；无政府主义；恐怖行为

etymology.story: 1817, "the doctrine of negation" (in reference to religion or morals), from German Nihilismus, from Latin nihil "nothing at all" (see nil), coined by German philosopher Friedrich Heinrich Jacobi (1743-1819).

16c., from Middle French furtif (16c.), from Latin furtivus "stolen," hence also "hidden, secret," from furtum "theft, robbery; a stolen thing," from fur (genitive furis) "a thief, extortioner," also a general term of abuse, "rascal, rogue," probably from PIE *bhor-, from root *bher- (1) "to carry" (see infer).

meanings.definitionCn: 异端；恶棍；罪大恶极之人
好色的；淫荡的；挑动情欲的

etymology.story:
mid-15c., "lustful, inclined to lust," from Middle French lascivieux or directly from Late Latin lasciviosus (used in a scolding sense by Isidore and other early Church writers), from Latin lascivia "lewdness, playfulness, fun, frolicsomeness, jolity," from lascivus "lewd, playful, undesigned, frolicsome, wanton."

This is from PIE *las-ko-, from the root *las- "to be eager, wanton, or unruly" (source also of Sanskrit -lasati "yearns," lasati "plays, frolics," Hittite ilaliya- "to desire, covet," Greek laste "harlot," Old Church Slavonic laska "flattery," Slovak laska "love," Russian lasyj "greedy, eager, affectionate," Old Irish lainn "greedy, eager," Gothic lustus, Old English lust "lust").

meanings.definitionCn: 非常华丽的；廉价而俗丽的
俗丽的东西；廉价而俗丽之物

etymology.story: 1640s, from French prolifique (16c.), from Medieval Latin prolificus, from Latin proles "offspring" + root of facere "to make" (see factitious).

"an attacking in words," 1520s, from Medieval Latin invectiva "abusive speech," from Late Latin invectivus "abusive, scolding" from invect-, past participle stem of invehere "to carry in, bring in, introduce; assault, assail; attack with words" (see inveigh).

meanings.definitionCn: 市议员；总督；市府参事；高级市政官

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + limin + 使…/具有…的

meanings.definitionCn: 切线的，相切的；接触的；离题的

to separate the hair + 状态/行为/过程

良性的；和蔼的，亲切的；吉利的

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old French benigne (12c., "kind, benign, merciful, gracious;" Modern French bénin, fem.

morphology.mnemonic: 过度/超越 + to play the lord, domineer

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + to reward, give reward

etymology.story: early 13c., "to satisfy a debt" (either for oneself or on behalf of another), from Old French aquiter "pay, pay up, settle a claim" (12c.), from a "to" (see ad-) + quite "free, clear" (see quit (adj.)).
1805 (implied in redirected), from re- "back, again" + direct (v.).

late 14c., "reverend;" late 15c., "characterized by reverence, deeply respectful," from Old French reverent and directly from Latin reverentem (nominative reverens), present participle of revereri (see reverence).

1530s, from Middle French atrocité or directly from Latin atrocitatem (nominative atrocitas) "cruelty, fierceness, harshness," noun of quality from atrox "fierce, cruel, frightful," from PIE *atro-ek-, from root *ater- "fire" (see atrium) + *okw- "see" (see eye (n.)); thus "of fiery or threatening appearance." The meaning "an atrocious deed" is from 1793..

meanings.definitionCn: 表现，表示，表达；表情，脸色，态度，腔调，声调；式，符号；词句，语句，措辞，说法

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + device for pressing cloth + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., "action of pressing out;" later "action of manifesting a feeling" (mid-15c.); "a putting into words" (late 15c.); from Middle French expression (14c.), from Late Latin expressionem (nominative expressio) "expression, vividness," in classical Latin "a pressing out, a projection," noun of action from past participle stem of exprimere "represent, describe," literally "press out" (see express (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 呼吁，恳求；上诉；诉诸，求助；有吸引力，迎合爱好；（体育比赛中）诉诸裁判
呼吁，请求；吸引力，感染力；上诉；诉诸裁判
使陷于沼泽；使沉没；使陷入困境

meanings.definitionCn: 强大的，强盛的；有势力的，有权力的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Middle French puissant "strong, mighty, powerful," earlier poissant (12c.), from stem of Old French poeir "to be able" (see power (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 伸展/趋向 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: "move in a circle or spiral," 1763 (implied in gyrated), back-formation from gyration.

"fate, destiny," 1834, from Turkish qismet, from Arabic qismah, qismat "portion, lot, fate," from root of qasama "he divided."

Popularized as the title of a novel in 1877..

meanings.definitionCn: 居住的， 住宅的； 寄宿的

morphology.mnemonic: to dwell permanently + ance + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1650s, "serving as a residence," from resident (n.) + -ial.

morphology.mnemonic: 单一 + 音 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1750, of sound, from Greek monotonos "of one tone" (see monotony).

meanings.definitionCn: 合作， 协作； 勾结， 通敌

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 劳动/工作 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1860, from French collaboration, noun of action from Latin collaborare (see collaborate).
1795 (with an isolated use from 1620s), back-formation from negation, or else from Latin negatus, past participle of negare.

mid-14c., enspiren, "to fill (the mind, heart, etc., with grace, etc.);" also "to prompt or induce (someone to do something)," from Old French enspirer (13c.), from Latin inspirare "blow into, breathe upon," figuratively "inspire, excite, inflame," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + spirare "to breathe" (see spirit (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 官僚主义；官僚机构；官僚政治

etymology.story: 1818, from French bureaucratie, coined by French economist Jean Claude Marie Vincent de Gournay (1712-1759) on model of democratie, aristocratie, from bureau "office," literally "desk" (see bureau) + Greek suffix -kratia denoting "power of" (see -cracy)..

meanings.definitionCn: 使劳累过分；对…使用过度；滥用
过重的工作； 工作过度

root.meaning: have the expected or desired effect

morphology.mnemonic: 过度/超越 + have the expected or desired effect

etymology.story: "work beyond a person's strength," 1819; see overwork (v.).

"pertaining to the seashore," 1650s, from Latin littoralis "of or belonging to the seashore," from litus (genitive litoris) "seashore" (compare Lido), of unknown origin, possibly from PIE root *lei- "to flow." The noun is first recorded 1828, from Italian littorale, originally an adjective, from Latin littoralis..
"food," late 15c., from Latin alimentum "nourishment," in plural, "food, provisions," from alere "to nourish" (see alimentary)..
late 14c., from Anglo-French odious, from Old French odieus (late 14c., Modern French odieux) or directly from Latin odiosus "hateful, offensive, unpleasant," from odium "hatred" (see odium)..
"pertaining to appeals," 1726, from Latin appellatus, past participle of appellare (see appeal).

morphology.mnemonic: 时间 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1590s, from Middle French chronologie or directly from Modern Latin chronologia; see chrono- + -logy.
1560s, from Latin hexagonum, from Greek hexagonon, neuter of hexagonos "six-cornered, hexagonal," from hex "six" (see hexa-) + gonia "angle" (see -gon)..

meanings.definitionCn: 折衷的；选择的；折衷学派的
折衷派的人；折衷主义者

etymology.story: 1680s, "not confined to or following any one model or system," originally in reference to ancient philosophers who selected doctrines from every system; from French eclectique (1650s), from Greek eklektikos "selective," literally "picking out," from eklektos "selected," from eklegein "pick out, select," from ek "out" (see ex-) + legein "gather, choose" (see lecture (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 做/制造 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: "studied display," 1540s, from French affectation (16c.) or directly from Latin affectationem (nominative affectatio) "a striving after, a claiming," noun of action from past participle stem of affectare "to strive for" (see affect (v.2))..

1540s, "to call by name," back-formation from nomination or else from Latin nominatus, past participle of nominare "to name, call by name, give a name to," also "name for office,"" from nomen "name" (see name (n.)).
1590s, from assimilated form of in- "into, in" (see in- (2)) + peril.
late 14c., from Old French diametre, from Latin diametrus, from Greek diametros (gramme) "diagonal of a circle," from dia- "across, through" (see dia-) + metron "a measure" (see meter (n.2))..

meanings.definitionCn: 固定的；静止的；定居的；常备军的

morphology.mnemonic: 站立/设置 + 状态/行为/过程 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: late 14c., "having no apparent motion" (in reference to planets), from Middle French stationnaire "motionless" and directly from Latin stationarius, from the stem of statio "a standing, post, job, position" (see station (n.)).

Old English grædig (West Saxon), gredig (Anglian) "voracious, hungry," also "covetous, eager to obtain," from Proto-Germanic *grædagaz (source also of Old Saxon gradag "greedy," Old Norse graðr "greed, hunger," Danish graadig, Dutch gretig, Old High German gratag "greedy," Gothic gredags "hungry"), from *græduz (source also of Gothic gredus "hunger," Old English grædum "eagerly"), possibly from PIE root *gher- (5) "to like, want" (source of Sanskrit grdh "to be greedy").
"goods taken from an enemy, etc.," 1788, Anglo-Indian, from Hindi lut, from Sanskrit loptram, lotram "booty, stolen property," from PIE *roup-tro-, from root *reup- "to snatch" (see rip (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 远离/脱离 + rog + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin abrogatus, past participle of abrogare "to annul, repeal (a law)," from ab- "away" (see ab-) + rogare "propose (a law), ask, request" (see rogation).

meanings.definitionCn: 强大的;令人敬畏的;可怕的;艰难的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "causing fear," from Middle French formidable (15c.), from Latin formidabilis "causing fear, terrible," from formidare "to fear," from formido "fearfulness, fear, terror, dread." Sense has softened somewhat over time, in the direction of "so great (in strength, size, etc.) as to discourage effort."
marine shellfish, Old English loppestre "lobster, locust," corruption of Latin locusta, lucusta "lobster, locust," by influence of Old English loppe "spider," a variant of lobbe.

"slight sin," 1590s (earlier in corrupt form peccadilian, 1520s), from Spanish pecadillo, diminutive of pecado "a sin," from Latin peccatum "a sin, fault, error," noun use of neuter past participle of peccare "to miss, mistake, make a mistake, do amiss; transgress, offend, be licentious, sin," a word of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 多种子的；结籽的；破烂的；没精打采的；下流的

morphology.mnemonic: remove the seeds from + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: mid-15c., "fruitful, abundant," from seed (n.) + -y (2).
early 15c., vomiting, from Latin nausea "seasickness," from Ionic Greek nausia (Attic nautia) "seasickness, nausea, disgust," literally "ship-sickness," from naus "ship" (see naval).
mid-15c., from Old French unanimite (14c.), from Late Latin unanimitatem (nominative unanimitas) "unanimity, concord," from unanimus (see unanimous)..

meanings.definitionCn: 保证，担保；（人寿）保险；确信；断言；厚脸皮，无耻

etymology.story: late 14c., "formal or solemn pledge, promise," also "certainty," from Old French asseurance (11c., Modern French assurance) "assurance, promise; truce; certainty, safety, security," from asseurer (see assure).

morphology.mnemonic: 之间/相互 + 走/让步 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., "act of interceding;" c.

meanings.definitionCn: 不变的；不可变的；不能变的

etymology.story: early 15c., "unchanging, unalterable," from Old French immutable (Modern French immuable), and directly from Latin immutabilis "unchangeable, unalterable," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + mutabilis "changeable," from mutare "to change" (see mutable).

meanings.definitionCn: 有节制的，不过分的；(气候)温和的

root.meaning: degree of hardness and resiliency in steel

morphology.mnemonic: degree of hardness and resiliency in steel + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: late 14c., of persons, "modest, forbearing, self-restrained, not swayed by passion;" of climates or seasons, "not liable to excessive heat or cold," from Latin temperatus "restrained, regulated, limited, moderate, sober, calm, steady," from past participle of temperare "to moderate, regulate" (see temper (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 破坏；使瓦解；使分裂；使中断；使陷于混乱

etymology.story: 1650s, but rare before c.

meanings.definitionCn: 肉体的；肉欲的；淫荡的；性欲的

morphology.mnemonic: 肉/肉体 + 与…有关的/形容词

root.meaning: to rest as at a center

morphology.mnemonic: ec + to rest as at a center + ic

etymology.story: 1550s, from Middle French eccentrique and directly from Medieval Latin eccentricus (noun and adjective; see eccentric (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 年轻而无经验的；羽毛未丰的

etymology.story: Old English calu "bare, bald," from Proto-Germanic *kalwa- (source also of Middle Dutch calu, Dutch kaal, Old High German kalo, German Kahl), from PIE root *gal- (1) "bald, naked" (source also of Russian golyi "smooth, bald").

meanings.definitionCn: 昏昏欲睡的；沉寂的；催眠的

etymology.story: 1520s, probably ultimately from Old English drusan, drusian "sink," also "become languid, slow, or inactive" (related to dreosan "to fall"), from Proto-Germanic *drus- (see dreary).

meanings.definitionCn: 块，团；群众，民众；大量，众多；质量
群众的，民众的；大规模的，集中的

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French revoquer (13c.), from Latin revocare "rescind, call back," from re- "back" (see re-) + vocare "to call" (see voice (n.)).
