meanings.definitionCn: 法律体系；法学及其分支；法律知识；法院审判规程

etymology.story: 1620s, "systematic knowledge of law," from French jurisprudence (17c.) and directly from Late Latin iurisprudentia "the science of law," from iuris "of right, of law" (genitive of ius; see jurist) + prudentia "knowledge, a foreseeing" (see prudence).
1550s, from Late Latin despicabilis, from Latin despicari "despise, disdain, look down on," from de- "down" (see de-) + spicare, variant of specere "to look" (see scope (n.1))..

meanings.definitionCn: 可鄙的；卑劣的；可轻视的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Latin contemptibilis "worthy of scorn," from contempt-, past participle stem of contemnere (see contempt).

meanings.definitionCn: 超速，加速；加速，迅速前行；兴隆
速度，速率；迅速，快速；昌盛，繁荣

etymology.story: Old English spedan (intransitive) "to succeed, prosper, grow rich, advance," from the stem of speed (n.).

late 15c., from Old French aloter (Modern French allotir) "to divide by lots, to divide into lots," from à "to" (see ad-) + loter "lot," a word of Germanic origin (cognates: Gothic hlauts, Old High German hloz, Old English hlot; see lot).
mid-15c., "to drive away, expel," from Latin propellere "push forward, drive forward, drive forth; move, impel," from pro- "forward" (see pro-) + pellere "to push, drive" (see pulse (n.1)).
1530s, probably from urgent + -cy..

meanings.definitionCn: 猖獗的， 肆虐的， 泛滥的； 蔓生的， 疯长的

etymology.story: late 14c., "standing on the hind legs" (as a heraldic lion often does), thus, also, "fierce, ravenous" (late 14c.), from Old French rampant, present participle of ramper "to climb, scale, mount" (see rampage (v.)).
also back-stage, 1898, from back (adj.) + stage (n.)..

meanings.definitionCn: [有化] 有机的；组织的；器官的；根本的

etymology.story: 1510s, "serving as an organ or instrument," from Latin organicus, from Greek organikos "of or pertaining to an organ, serving as instruments or engines," from organon "instrument" (see organ).

early 14c., from Old French prologue (12c.) and directly from Latin prologus, from Greek prologos "preface to a play, speaker of a prologue," literally "a speech beforehand," from pro- "before" (see pro-) + logos "discourse, speech," from legein "to speak" (see lecture (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 有道德的；善良的；贞洁的；简朴的
不受影响的；自然的；真挚的；不矫揉造作的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + 向/朝/在 + 做/制造

etymology.story: 1580s, "not influenced, untouched in mind or feeling," from un- (1) "not" + past participle of affect (v.).

morphology.mnemonic: drying-up season + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1570s, from Latin autumnalis "pertaining to autumn," from autumnus (see autumn)..

meanings.definitionCn: 油质的；虚情假意的；油腔滑调的

etymology.story: late 14c., "oily, having a greasy or soapy feeling when touched," from Old French unctueus, from Medieval Latin unctuosus "greasy," from Latin unctus "act of anointing," from past participle stem of unguere "to anoint" (see unguent).

type of small songbird, 1650s (short for Canary-bird, 1570s), from French canarie, from Spanish canario "canary bird," literally "of the Canary Islands," from Latin Insula Canaria "Canary Island," largest of the Fortunate Isles, literally "island of dogs" (canis, genitive canarius; see canine (n.)), so called because large dogs lived there.
1550s, from Middle French cubique (14c.), from Latin cubicus, from Greek kybikos, from kybos "cube" (see cube (n.)).

mid-14c., from Medieval Latin barbarinus (source of Old French barbarin "Berber, pagan, Saracen, barbarian"), from Latin barbaria "foreign country," from Greek barbaros "foreign, strange, ignorant," from PIE root *barbar- echoic of unintelligible speech of foreigners (compare Sanskrit barbara- "stammering," also "non-Aryan," Latin balbus "stammering," Czech blblati "to stammer").
mid-14c., "unintelligible talk, gibberish; chattering, jabbering," from Old French jargon "a chattering" (of birds), also "language, speech," especially "idle talk; thieves' Latin" (12c.).
also post-war, 1906, in reference to the U.S.

meanings.definitionCn: 忽略；俯瞰；远眺；检查；高耸于…之上

morphology.mnemonic: 过度/超越 + appearance of a person

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to examine, scrutinize, inspect," from over- + look (v.).
formerly also etherial, 1510s, "of the highest regions of the atmosphere," from ether + -ial; extended sense of "light, airy" is from 1590s.
"to write," mid-15c., from Latin scribere "to write" (see script (n.))..
1530s, "to digest," from Latin concoctus, past participle of concoquere "to digest; to boil together, prepare; to consider well," from com- "together" (see com-) + coquere "to cook" (see cook (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 鲈鱼；男低音；低音部；椴树
晒太阳；取暖；愉快或舒适

etymology.story: late 14c., basken "to wallow (in blood)," with loss of middle syllable, from Old Norse baðask "to bathe oneself," reflexive of baða "bathe" (see bathe).

1630s, "words used in a ceremony or ritual" (earlier as a Latin word in English), from Latin formula "form, draft, contract, regulation;" in law, "a rule, method;" literally "small form," diminutive of forma "form" (see form (n.)).

1763, from French avalanche (17c.), from Romansch (Swiss) avalantze "descent," altered (by metathesis of -l- and -v-, probably influenced by Old French avaler "to descend, go down") from Savoy dialect lavantse, from Provençal lavanca "avalanche," perhaps from a pre-Latin Alpine language (the suffix -anca suggests Ligurian).

meanings.definitionCn: 戒酒；节欲；（气候等的）温和

root.meaning: degree of hardness and resiliency in steel

morphology.mnemonic: degree of hardness and resiliency in steel + ance

etymology.story: mid-14c., "self-restraint, moderation," from Anglo-French temperaunce (mid-13c.), from Latin temperantia "moderation, sobriety, discretion, self-control," from temperans, present participle of temperare "to moderate" (see temper (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 礼物；[税收] 贡物；颂词；（尤指对死者的）致敬，悼念，吊唁礼物

etymology.story: mid-14c., "stated sum of money or other valuable consideration paid by one ruler or country to another in acknowledgment of submission or as the price of peace or protection," from Anglo-French tribute, Old French tribut and directly from Latin tributum "tribute, a stated payment, a thing contributed or paid," noun use of neuter of tributus, past participle of tribuere "to pay, assign, grant," also "allot among the tribes or to a tribe," from tribus (see tribe).
late 15c., from en- (1) "make, put in" + danger.

early 15c., from Middle French oblique (14c.) and directly from Latin obliquus "slanting, sidelong, indirect," from ob "against" (see ob-) + root of licinus "bent upward," from PIE root *lei- "to bend, be movable" (see limb (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: in + 音 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1610s, "opening phrase of a melody," from French intonation (14c.), from Medieval Latin intonationem (nominative intonatio), noun of state from past participle stem of intonare (see intone).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之下 + 顺序/等级 + 使…/具有…的
出/从 + 说话 + 状态/行为/过程 + …主义者/…专家

etymology.story: 1560s, from Middle French parallélogramme (1550s) and directly from Late Latin parallelogrammum, from Greek parallelogrammon noun use of neuter adjective meaning "bounded by parallel lines," from parallelos (see parallel) + stem of graphein "to write" (see -graphy)..

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + obtain by effort or striving + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: 1610s, "unfit, improper," from Middle English ungeinliche, from ungein (late 14c.) "inconvenient, disagreeable, troublesome," from un- (1) "not" + gein "kind, helpful; reliable; beneficial; suitable, appropriate; convenient," from Old Norse gegn "straight, direct, helpful," from Proto-Germanic *gagina "against" (see again).

meanings.definitionCn: 行程表； 旅行路线， 旅行计划

etymology.story: mid-15c., "route of travel," from Late Latin itinerarium "account of a journey, description of a route of travel, road-book," noun use of neuter of itinerarius "of a journey," from Latin itineris "a journey," from ire "go" (see ion).

1640s, a back-formation from pugnacity or else from Latin pugnacis, genitive of pugnax "combative, fond of fighting," from pugnare "to fight," especially with the fists, "contend against," from pugnus "a fist," from PIE *pung-, nasalized form of root *peuk-, *peug- "to stick, stab, to prick" (source also of Greek pyx "with clenched fist," pygme "fist, boxing," pyktes "boxer;" Latin pungere "to pierce, prick")..
"to make the characteristic sound of a cow," 1540s, of imitative origin.

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + volve + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., originally of celestial bodies, from Old French revolucion "course, revolution (of celestial bodies)" (13c.), or directly from Late Latin revolutionem (nominative revolutio) "a revolving," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin revolvere "turn, roll back" (see revolve).

meanings.definitionCn: 拒绝；否定；批判；与…断绝关系；拒付

etymology.story: 1540s, "to cast off by divorce," from Latin repudiatus, past participle of repudiare "to cast off, put away, divorce, reject, scorn, disdain," from repudium "divorce, rejection, a putting away, dissolution of marriage," from re- "back, away" (see re-) + pudium, which is probably related to pes/ped- "foot" [Barnhart].

mid-13c., "theft, action or practice of stealing," from a probable Old English *stælþ, which is related to stelen (see steal (v.)), from Proto-Germanic *stælitho (source also of Old Norse stulþr), with Proto-Germanic abstract noun suffix *-itho (see -th (2)).

early 13c., aspy, from Old French espiier "observe, watch closely, spy on; guard, keep in custody" (12c., Modern French épier), from Vulgar Latin *spiare, from a Germanic source (compare Old High German spehon "to spy;" see spy (v.)).

1560s, "cotton padding," corrupted from earlier bombace (1550s), from Old French bombace "cotton, cotton wadding," from Late Latin bombacem, accusative of bombax "cotton, 'linteorum aut aliae quaevis quisquiliae,' " a corruption and transferred use of Latin bombyx "silk," from Greek bombyx "silk, silkworm" (which also came to mean "cotton" in Medieval Greek), from some oriental word, perhaps related to Iranian pambak (modern panba) or Armenian bambok, perhaps ultimately from a PIE root meaning "to twist, wind." From stuffing and padding for clothes or upholstery, meaning extended to "pompous, empty speech" (1580s).

meanings.definitionCn: 一丝不苟的；精密细心的；拘泥形式的

etymology.story: 1630s, probably from Italian puntiglioso, from puntiglio "fine point," from Latin punctum "prick" (see point (n.)).
early 14c., "bad luck, unlucky accident," from mis- (1) "bad" + hap "luck." Probably on analogy of Old French meschance (see mischance (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 偏僻的；弯曲的；不光明正大的

morphology.mnemonic: sand spout, dust storm + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1590s, "out of the way," from Latin devius "out of the way, remote, off the main road," from de via (see deviate).

meanings.definitionCn: 静止的，不动的；寂静的，平静的；不起泡的
蒸馏；使…静止；使…平静下来
放大；放大率；放大的复制品

morphology.mnemonic: 大/伟大 + 使…化/造成 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., from Late Latin magnificationem (nominative magnificatio), noun of action from past participle stem of magnificare (see magnify)..

morphology.mnemonic: 生命/生物 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1819, from Greek bios "life" (see bio-) + -logy.
1550s, from Latin obtrudere "to thrust into, press upon," from ob "toward" (see ob-) + trudere "to thrust," "to thrust, push," from PIE *treud- "to press, push, squeeze" (see threat).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 相似/聚集 + ance

etymology.story: late 14c., from Anglo-French resemblance (c.

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 取/抓 + 状态/行为/过程 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1828, from exception + -al (1).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 建立/放置

etymology.story: mid-15c., verb use of adjective constitute, "made up, formed" (late 14c.), from Latin constitutus "arranged, settled," past participle adjective from constituere "to cause to stand, set up, fix, place, establish, set in order; form something new; resolve," of persons, "to appoint to an office," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + statuere "to set," from PIE root *stā- "to stand," with derivatives meaning "place or thing that is standing" (see stet).

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1630s, "collection of poetry," from Latin anthologia, from Greek anthologia "collection of small poems and epigrams by several authors," literally "flower-gathering," from anthos "a flower" (see anther) + logia "collection, collecting," from legein "gather" (see lecture (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: [天] 精密记时表；高度精确的钟表；航海经线仪

etymology.story: 1735, from chrono- "time" + -meter.

meanings.definitionCn: 宽阔；广阔的区域；苍天；膨胀扩张

etymology.story: 1660s, from Latin expansum, noun use of neuter of expansus, past participle of expandere "to spread out" (see expand)..

1520s, "to enclose with stakes, fence in" (a sense continued in specialized uses into 19c.), from Middle French empaler or directly from Medieval Latin impalare "to push onto a stake," from assimilated form of in- "into, in" (see in- (2)) + Latin palus "a stake, prop, stay; wooden post, pole," from PIE *pak-slo-, from root *pag-/*pak- "to fasten" (see pact).

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + equi + 与…有关的/形容词 + ity

etymology.story: early 15c., "difference of rank or dignity," from Old French inequalité (14c., Modern French inégalité) and directly from Medieval Latin inaequalitas, from Latin inaequalis "unequal, unlike, different (in size); changeable, inconstant," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + aequalis "equal" (see equal).
"to travel from place to place," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 助手，大副；配偶；同事；配对物

etymology.story: also pipet, 1818, from French pipette, from Middle French pipette "tube," diminutive of Old French pipe, from Vulgar Latin *pipa (see pipe (n.1))..

meanings.definitionCn: 理论； 原理； 学说；见解， 看法

morphology.mnemonic: 神 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1590s, "conception, mental scheme," from Late Latin theoria (Jerome), from Greek theoria "contemplation, speculation; a looking at, viewing; a sight, show, spectacle, things looked at," from theorein "to consider, speculate, look at," from theoros "spectator," from thea "a view" (see theater) + horan "to see," possibly from PIE root *wer- (4) "to perceive" (see ward (n.)).
late 14c., "soiled, dirty, filthy," from bawd + -y (2).
1801, "stimulate by galvanic electricity," from French galvaniser, from galvanisme (see galvanism).

meanings.definitionCn: 反对；抨击；轻视；声明不赞成

etymology.story: 1620s, "to pray against or for deliverance from," from Latin deprecatus, past participle of deprecari "to pray (something) away" (see deprecation).

meanings.definitionCn: 一本正经的；自负的；死板的
文学的；书面的；精通文学的

etymology.story: 1640s, "pertaining to alphabet letters," from French littéraire, from Latin literarius/litterarius "belonging to letters or learning," from littera/litera "letter" (see letter (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 迟钝的；缺乏热情的；冷漠的

etymology.story: "earthen elevation around a place for fortification," sometimes also including parapets, 1580s, from Middle French rempart, rampart, from remparer "to fortify," from re- "again" (see re-) + emparer "fortify, take possession of," from Old Provençal amparer, from Vulgar Latin *anteparare "prepare," properly "to make preparations beforehand," from Latin ante- "before" (see ante) + parare "prepare" (see pare).

meanings.definitionCn: 伪造；篡改；歪曲；证明虚假

etymology.story: mid-15c., "to prove false," from Middle French falsifier (15c.), from Late Latin falsificare "make false, corrupt," from Latin falsus "erroneous, mistaken" (see false).

meanings.definitionCn: 呕吐的；不稳定的；催吐的

etymology.story: mid-15c., kyse, coysy, of uncertain origin, possibly from a Scandinavian source, such as Old Norse kveisa "boil," perhaps influenced by Anglo-French queisier, from Old French coisier "to wound, hurt, make uneasy," which seems to be from the same Germanic root as kveisa.

early 14c., from Old French verifier "substantiate, find out the truth about" (14c.), from Medieval Latin verificare "make true," from Latin verus "true" (see very) + root of facere "to make" (see factitious)..

meanings.definitionCn: 短途旅行,游览;离题;[物理]偏移,漂移

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + to swear profanely + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1570s, "a deviation in argument," also "a military sally," from Latin excursionem (nominative excursio) "a running forth, sally, excursion, expedition," figuratively "an outset, opening," noun of action from past participle stem of excurrere "run out, run forth, hasten forward; project, extend," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + currere "to run" (see current (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 痉挛，绞痛；[五金] 铁夹钳
束缚，限制；使…抽筋；以铁箍扣紧
狭窄的；难解的；受限制的
开玩笑的；幽默的；爱嬉戏的

etymology.story: mid-13c., from play (v.) + -ful.
early 15c., from Old French plenitude and directly from Latin plenitudinem (nominative plenitudo) "abundance, completeness, fullness," from plenus "complete, full" (see plenary)..

meanings.definitionCn: 学院；研究院；学会；专科院校

etymology.story: late 15c., "the classical Academy," from French Académie, from Latin Academia, from Greek Akademeia "grove of Akademos," a legendary Athenian of the Trojan War tales (his name apparently means "of a silent district"), whose estate, six stadia from Athens, was the enclosure where Plato taught his school.

morphology.mnemonic: 通过/彻底 + 跟随 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-14c., "oppression for the holding of a belief or opinion," from Old French persecucion "persecution, damage, affliction, suffering" (12c.) and directly from Latin persecutionem (nominative persecutio), noun of action from past participle stem of persequi "follow, pursue, hunt down; proceed against, prosecute, start a legal action," from per- "through" (see per) + sequi "follow" (see sequel).
"bury, lay in the grave," c.
early 15c., from by (adv.) + gone.

1620s, "very common from the beginning of the 19th c." [OED], from Italian gusto "taste," from Latin gustus "a tasting," related to gustare "to taste, take a little of," from PIE *gus-tu-, suffixed form of root *geus- "to taste, choose" (source also of Sanskrit jus- "enjoy, be pleased," Avestan zaosa- "pleasure," Old Persian dauš- "enjoy").
Old English ebbian "flow back, subside," from the root of ebb (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 不愉快的；厌恶的；不为人喜的；难相处的；脾气坏的

root.meaning: to be in harmony in opinions

morphology.mnemonic: 不/分离 + to be in harmony in opinions + able

etymology.story: late 13c., from Old French haster "hurry, make haste; urge, hurry along" (Modern French hâter), from haste "haste, urgency" (see haste).

meanings.definitionCn: 破坏的；毁灭性的；有害的，消极的

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 建造 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: late 15c., from Old French destructif (14c.), from Late Latin destructivus, from destruct-, past participle stem of Latin destruere (see destroy)..

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + gener + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1540s, from Latin degeneratus, past participle of degenerare "fall from ancestral quality" (see degenerate (adj.)).
mid-15c., "to howl like a dog," from Old Norse baula "to low like a cow," and/or Medieval Latin baulare "to bark like a dog," both echoic.

morphology.mnemonic: 通过/彻底 + amble + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1560s, from Latin perambulatus, past participle of perambulare "to walk through, go through, ramble through," from per- "through" (see per) + ambulare "to walk" (see amble).

meanings.definitionCn: 节俭的；有先见之明的；顾及未来的

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + 看见 + …的人/处于…状态

meanings.definitionCn: 狼吞虎咽地吃；挥霍，耗尽

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old French devorer (12c.) "devour, swallow up, engulf," from Latin devorare "swallow down, accept eagerly," from de- "down" (see de-) + vorare "to swallow" (see voracity).
"commotion, disturbance, tumult," late 14c.

meanings.definitionCn: 相应的；[生物] 同源的；类似的；一致的

etymology.story: "having the same position, value, structure, etc.," 1650s, from Latinized form of Greek homologos "agreeing, of one mind," from homos "same" (see homo- (1)) + logos "relation, reasoning, computation," related to legein "reckon, select, speak" (see lecture (n.))..

mid-14c., from Old French compunction (12c., Modern French componction), from Late Latin compunctionem (nominative compunctio) "remorse; a pricking" (of conscience), noun of action from past participle stem of Latin compungere "to severely prick, sting," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + pungere "to prick, pierce," related to pugnus "a fist" (see pugnacious).

1580s, from Dutch or Low German belegeren "to besiege," from be- "around" (see be-) + legeren "to camp," from leger "bed, camp, army, lair," from Proto-Germanic *leg-raz-, from PIE *legh-to- "lie" (see lie (v.2)).

morphology.mnemonic: to remove, severely reduce, + 过程/状态

etymology.story: 1540s, "imaginary straight line around which a body (such as the Earth) rotates," from Latin axis "axle, pivot, axis of the earth or sky," from PIE *aks- "axis" (source also of Old English eax, Old High German ahsa "axle;" Greek axon "axis, axle, wagon;" Sanskrit aksah "an axle, axis, beam of a balance;" Lithuanian aszis "axle").

morphology.mnemonic: juxta + 放置 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1660s, from French juxtaposition (17c.), from Latin iuxta "beside, very near, close to, near at hand" + French position (see position (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 不合格的；无资格的；不胜任的；不受限制的；无条件的；绝对的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + to limit, modify

etymology.story: 1550s, "not having necessary qualifications, from un- (1) "not" + past participle of qualify (v.).
late 14c., from Old French negligent "careless, negligent" (13c.) or directly from Latin negligentem "heedless, careless, unconcerned" (see negligence).

meanings.definitionCn: 公文包；文件夹； 作品集；投资组合

etymology.story: 1722, porto folio; 1719 as port folio, from Italian portafoglio "a case for carrying loose papers," from porta, imperative of portare "to carry" (see port (n.1)) + foglio "sheet, leaf," from Latin folium (see folio).

morphology.mnemonic: 向上/提高 + roar + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1791, from uproar + -ous.
1510s, "escape," from Middle French evader, from Latin evadere "to escape, get away," from assimilated form of ex- "away" (see ex-) + vadere "to go, walk" (see vamoose).
mid-14c., "passage in a house; open passage between buildings; walkway in a garden," from Old French alee (13c., Modern French allée) "a path, passage, way, corridor," also "a going," from fem.

1530s, "to pile up, accumulate," from Latin exaggeratus, past participle of exaggerare "heighten, amplify, magnify," literally "to heap, pile, load, fill," from ex- "thoroughly" (see ex-) + aggerare "heap up, accumulate," figuratively "amplify, magnify," from agger (genitive aggeris) "heap," from aggerere "bring together, carry toward," from assimilated form of ad- "to, toward" (see ad-) + gerere "carry" (see gest).

root.meaning: an act of turning, a single revolution or part of a revolution

morphology.mnemonic: 向上/提高 + an act of turning, a single revolution or part of a revolution

etymology.story: 1868, "upturned part," from up (adv.) + turn (n.).
1650s, "outer shell, husk," from Latin cortex "bark of a tree" (see corium).

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1590s, "a wandering, straying," from Latin aberrationem (nominative aberratio) "a wandering," noun of action from past participle stem of aberrare "to wander out of the way, lose the way, go astray," from ab- "away" (see ab-) + errare "to wander, stray, roam, rove" (see err).
late 15c., "use up, wear out, go through," from Middle English per- "completely" (see per) + use (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 背诵；朗诵；详述；背诵的诗

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 召唤/引用 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 15c., "act of detailing," from Old French récitation (14c.) and directly from Latin recitationem (nominative recitatio) "public reading, a reading aloud," noun of action from past participle stem of recitare (see recite).

meanings.definitionCn: 资格；条件；限制；赋予资格

morphology.mnemonic: to limit, modify + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1540s, "restriction, modification," from Middle French qualification and directly from Medieval Latin qualificationem (nominative qualificatio), noun of action from past participle stem of qualificare (see qualify).

early 15c., from Old French combustion (13c.), from Latin combustionem (nominative combustio) "a burning," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin comburere "to burn up, consume," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + *burere, faulty separation of amburere "to burn around," actually ambi-urere, from urere "to burn, singe," from PIE root *eus- "to burn" (see ember)..

meanings.definitionCn: 补充；聘用；征募；使…恢复健康

etymology.story: 1880, a back-formation from pathogenic..

meanings.definitionCn: 高龄的；老衰的；高龄所致的

etymology.story: 1660s, "suited to old age," from French sénile (16c.), from Latin senilis "of old age," from senex (genitive senis) "old, old man," from PIE root *sen- "old" (source also of Sanskrit sanah "old;" Avestan hana- "old;" Old Persian hanata- "old age, lapse of time;" Armenian hin "old;" Greek enos "old, of last year;" Lithuanian senas "old," senis "an old man;" Gothic sineigs "old" (used only of persons), sinistra "elder, senior;" Old Norse sina "dry standing grass from the previous year;" Old Irish sen, Old Welsh hen "old").
mid-15c., insuren, spelling variant of ensuren "to assure, give formal assurance" (late 14c.), also "make secure, make safe" (c.
1590s, from French bombarder, from bombarde "mortar, catapult" (see bombard (n.)).
1530s, a back-formation from protraction and in part from Latin protractus, past participle of protrahere "to draw forth, prolong." Etymologically identical with portray, which was altered in French.

meanings.definitionCn: 普通的n.寻常的事物，平庸的东西

etymology.story: 1540s, "a statement generally accepted," literal translation of Latin locus communis, from Greek koinos topos "general topic." See common (adj.) + place (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 诽谤， 中伤， 诬蔑

etymology.story: mid-15c., "make an initial statement setting out a plaintiff's case" (modern sense from 1560s), from libel (n.), q.v.

morphology.mnemonic: capt + 有…倾向的 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1520s, "to enthrall with charm," from Late Latin captivatus, past participle of captivare "to take, capture," from captivus (see captive).

late 14c., "hostile feeling, rivalry, malice; internal conflict," from Old French enemite, variant of enemistié "enmity, hostile act, aversion" (Modern French inimité), from Vulgar Latin *inimicitatem (nominative *inimicitas), from Latin inimicitia "enmity, hostility," usually plural, from inimicus "enemy" (see enemy).

meanings.definitionCn: 天性快活的；主神朱庇特的

etymology.story: 1580s, "under the influence of the planet Jupiter," from Middle French jovial (16c.), from Italian joviale, literally "pertaining to Jupiter," and directly from Late Latin Iovialis "of Jupiter," from Latin Iovius (used as genitive of Iuppiter) "of or pertaining to Jupiter," Roman god of the sky (see Jove).

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + rog + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "impair (authority); disparage (reputation)," from Latin derogatus, past participle of derogare "diminish" (see derogatory)..
1650s in geometry, from Modern Latin parameter (1630s), from Greek para- "beside, subsidiary" (see para- (1)) + metron "measure" (see meter (n.2)).
early 15c., "extinguished, quenched," from Latin extinctus/exstinctus, past participle of extinguere/exstinguere "to put out, quench; go out, die out; kill, destroy" (see extinguish).
1590s, probably from Middle French souiller "to soil," also figurative, from Old French soillier "make dirty" (see soil (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + com + 具有…特征/名词化 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1744, from French accompagnement (13c.), from accompagner (see accompany).

meanings.definitionCn: 暴躁的；任性的；难以取悦的

etymology.story: 1590s, "immodest, wanton, saucy," from Middle French petulant (mid-14c.), from Latin petulantem (nominative petulans) "wanton, froward, saucy, insolent," present participle of petere "to attack, assail; strive after; ask for, beg, beseech" (see petition (n.)).
1540s, of the air, "full of thick mist," perhaps from a Scandinavian source, or formed from fog (n.1) + -y (2).

late 14c., from Anglo-French apartenir, Old French apartenir (12c.) "be related to; be incumbent upon," from Late Latin appertinere "to pertain to," from ad- "to, completely" (see ad-) + pertinere "to belong to" (see pertain).
1620s, a back-formation from percolation, or else from Latin percolatus, past participle of percolare "to strain through." Figurative sense by 1670s.

meanings.definitionCn: 不透明的；不传热的；迟钝的

etymology.story: early 15c., opake, from Latin opacus "shaded, in the shade, shady, dark, darkened, obscure," of unknown origin.
late 14c., from Old French retenue "group of followers, state of service," literally "that which is retained," noun use of fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 优点，长处，有利条件；利益，好处

etymology.story: early 14c., avantage, "position of being in advance of another," from Old French avantage "advantage, profit, superiority," from avant "before," probably via an unrecorded Late Latin *abantaticum, from Latin abante (see advance).

meanings.definitionCn: 微温的，温热的；不太热烈的；不热情的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French humide, umide "damp, wet" (15c.) or directly from Latin humidus "moist, wet," variant (probably by influence of humus "earth") of umidus, from umere "be moist, be wet," from Proto-Italic *umo- "wet" (also source of Latin umidus "wet, moist," umiditas "moisture," umor "moisture, fluid," umectus "moist, wet"), perhaps from PIE *uhrmo- "wet," from the same source as Latin urina [de Vaan]..

late 14c., from Old French controversie or directly from Latin controversia, from controversus "turned in an opposite direction, disputed, turned against," from contra- "against" (see contra) + versus (see verse)..
1707, from French grimacer, from grimace "grotesque face" (see grimace (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 义务的；必修的；被强制的
（花样滑冰、竞技体操等的）规定动作

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 推/驱动 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1580s, from Medieval Latin compulsorius, from Latin compulsus, past participle of compellere "to drive together, force, compel" (see compel)..

meanings.definitionCn: 预料；期望；预先考虑；抢先；提前使用

etymology.story: 1530s, "to cause to happen sooner," a back-formation from anticipation, or else from Latin anticipatus, past participle of anticipare "take (care of) ahead of time," literally "taking into possession beforehand," from ante "before" (see ante) + capere "to take" (see capable).

meanings.definitionCn: 变量的；可变的；易变的，多变的；变异的，[生物] 畸变的
[数] 变量；可变物，可变因素
分开, 分离, 分遣, 派遣(军队)

etymology.story: 1680s, from French détacher "to detach, untie," from Old French destachier, from des- "apart" + attachier "attach" (see attach).
mid-15c., "to fashion, make, build," from Latin fabricatus, past participle of fabricare "to make, construct, fashion, build," from fabrica (see fabric).

meanings.definitionCn: 根除，连根拔起；迫使某人离开出生地或定居处

etymology.story: 1590s (implied in uprooted), in the figurative sense, from up (adv.) + root (v.).

(plural loci), 1715, "locality," from Latin locus "a place, spot, position," from Old Latin stlocus, literally "where something is placed," from PIE root *st(h)el- "to cause to stand, to place." Used by Latin writers for Greek topos.

meanings.definitionCn: 对…作景观美化， 美化
无感觉的；缺乏感知力的

etymology.story: "not perceiving," 1660s, from assimilated form of in- (1) "not, opposite of" + perceptive..
1680s, from Latin effervescentem (nominative effervescens), present participle of effervescere "to boil up, boil over" (see effervescence).

"lasting only for a time," 1540s, from Latin temporarius "of seasonal character, lasting a short time," from tempus (genitive temporis) "time, season" (see temporal, late 14c., which was the earlier word for "lasting but for a time").

morphology.mnemonic: 眼/视 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "most favorable," 1890, from Latin optimus (see optimum).

meanings.definitionCn: 不顾一切的，铤而走险的；绝望的，危急的

etymology.story: early 15c., "despairing, hopeless," from Latin desperatus "given up, despaired of," past participle of desperare (see despair (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + notice, attention, reputation + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1733, from Latin annotatus, past participle of annotare "to note down" (see annotation).
early 15c., "plunder," from Middle French rapine (12c.), from Latin rapina "act of robbery, plundering, pillage," from rapere "seize, carry off, rob" (see rapid)..

1560s, "to bet, make a bet," (literally "make a vie, the noun attested from 1530s in cards), especially in card-playing, "to wager the value of one's hand against an opponent's," shortened form of Middle English envie "make a challenge," from Old French envier "compete (against), provoke; invite, summon, subpoena;" in gambling, "put down a stake, up the bet;" from Latin invitare "to invite," also "to summon, challenge" (see invitation).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 打破 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1570s, from Late Latin refractionem (nominative refractio) "a breaking up," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin refringere "to break up," from re- "back" (see re-) + comb.
1580s, in torrid zone "region of the earth between the tropics," from Medieval Latin torrida zona, from fem.

meanings.definitionCn: [动] 杂食动物；不偏食的人

etymology.story: 1890, formed from omni- on model of carnivore (see omnivorous)..

morphology.mnemonic: 看见 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., "pertaining to the faculty of sight;" also "coming from the eye or sight" (as a beam of light was thought to do), from Late Latin visualis "of sight," from Latin visus "a sight, a looking; power of sight; things seen, appearance," from visus, past participle of videre "to see" (see vision).

meanings.definitionCn: 迂腐的；学究式的；卖弄学问的；假装学者的

root.meaning: person who trumpets minor points of learning

morphology.mnemonic: person who trumpets minor points of learning + ic
向下/移除/取消 + 水 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: 类人猿的；类人的；（猿等）似人类的

etymology.story: "manlike," 1835, from Greek anthropoeides "like a man, resembling a man; in human form;" see anthropo- + -oid.

meanings.definitionCn: 勒索；敲诈；强夺；被勒索的财物

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 扭曲 + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: （尤指宗教原因的）隐士；隐居者

etymology.story: early 12c., "religious recluse, one who dwells apart in a solitary place for religious meditation," from Old French hermit, ermit "hermit, recluse," from Late Latin eremita, from Greek eremites, literally "person of the desert," from eremia "a solitude, an uninhabited region, a waste," from eremos "uninhabited, empty, desolate, bereft," from PIE *ere- (2) "to separate" (source also of Latin rete "net," Lithuanian retis "sieve").

root.meaning: to be in harmony in opinions

morphology.mnemonic: to be in harmony in opinions + 结果/过程/状态

meanings.definitionCn: 无常的；短暂的；易逃逸的

etymology.story: "fleeing, likely to flee," 1630s, with -ous + Latin fugaci-, stem of fugax "apt to flee, timid, shy," figuratively "transitory, fleeting," from fugere "to flee" (see fugitive).

early 15c., "to tell, inform, impart knowledge or information," also "furnish with authoritative directions," from Latin instructus, past participle of instruere "arrange, prepare, set in order; inform, teach," literally "to build, erect," from in- "on" (see in- (2)) + struere "to pile, build" (see structure (n.)).
1726, back-formation from oscillation, or else from Latin oscillatus, past participle of oscillare (see oscillation).
early 15c., from Old French auborne, from Medieval Latin alburnus "off-white, whitish," from Latin albus "white" (see alb).

meanings.definitionCn: 有偏见的； 结果偏倚的

etymology.story: 1610s in reference to bowling, 1660s in reference to persons; past participle adjective from bias (v.)..
1590s, Oxford University slang for "pedant," also "whim," etc., later (1790) "riddle, puzzle." Also spelled quonundrum.

meanings.definitionCn: 穿孔于，打孔穿透；在…上打齿孔
同一地区全体居民,社会,社区;共同体

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French comunité "community, commonness, everybody" (Modern French communauté), from Latin communitatem (nominative communitas) "community, society, fellowship, friendly intercourse; courtesy, condescension, affability," from communis "common, public, general, shared by all or many," (see common (adj.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: late 14c., "hot-tempered, fierce;" late 15c., "done or given with a rush of force," from Old French impetuos (13c., Modern French impétueux) and directly from Late Latin impetuosus "impetuous, violent" (source also of Spanish and Italian impetuoso), from Latin impetus "attack" (see impetus).

1580s, "a translation," from Middle French version, from Medieval Latin versionem (nominative versio) "a turning, a translation," from past participle stem of Latin vertere "to turn, change, alter, translate" (see versus).

meanings.definitionCn: 喷射剂； 推进物， 推进剂

root.meaning: to drive onward, cause to move forward

morphology.mnemonic: to drive onward, cause to move forward + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: less-etymological, but more usual, spelling of propellent; 1881 as a firearm explosive; 1919 as "fuel for a rocket engine.".

meanings.definitionCn: 概念， 观念；设想， 构想

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 取/抓 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 14c., "act of conceiving," from Old French concepcion (Modern French conception) "conception, grasp, comprehension," from Latin conceptionem (nominative conceptio) "a comprehending, conception," noun of action from stem of concipere (see conceive).

meanings.definitionCn: 被抛弃的；衰退的，破败的
遗弃物； 无家可归者， 社会弃儿

etymology.story: 1640s, from Latin derelictus "solitary, deserted," past participle of dereliquere "to abandon, forsake, desert," from de- "entirely" + relinquere "leave behind" (see relinquish).

meanings.definitionCn: 追溯的；有追溯效力的；反动的

morphology.mnemonic: retro + 做/驱动 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1610s, from French rétroactif (16c.) "casting or relating back," from Latin retroact-, past participle stem of retroagere "drive or turn back," from retro- "back" (see retro-) + agere "to drive, set in motion" (see act (n.)).

1660s, "church benefice with an emolument but without parish duties," from Medieval Latin beneficium sine cura "benefice without care" (of souls), from Latin sine "without" (see sans) + cura, ablative singular of cura "care" (see cure (n.1))..
1640s, from French despotique (14c.), from Greek despotikos, from despotes (see despot).

meanings.definitionCn: 挫败；扰乱，破坏；使…为难；使…破灭
远景，狭长的街景；展望；回顾

etymology.story: 1650s, "a view or prospect," from Italian vista "sight, view," noun use of fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 适当的恰当的特有的vt.拨给,挪用,盗用

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + propri + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: "specially suitable, proper," early 15c., from Latin appropriatus, past participle of appropriare (see appropriate (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 内涵；含蓄；暗示，隐含意义；储蓄的东西（词、语等）

etymology.story: 1530s, from Medieval Latin connotationem (nominative connotatio), from connotat-, past participle stem of connotare "signify in addition to the main meaning," a term in logic, literally "to mark along with," from Latin com- "together" (see com-) + notare "to mark" (see note).
1650s, from French brusque "lively, fierce," from Italian adjective brusco "sharp, tart, rough," perhaps from Vulgar Latin *bruscum "butcher's broom plant.".

late 14c., "fact of being useful," from Old French utilite "usefulness" (13c., Modern French + utilité), earlier utilitet (12c.), from Latin utilitatem (nominative utilitas) "usefulness, serviceableness, profit," from utilis "usable," from uti "make use of, profit by, take advantage of" (see use (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: to mark + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "act of resigning" (an office, etc.), from Old French resignation (14c.) and directly from Medieval Latin resignationem (nominative resignatio), noun of action from past participle stem of Latin resignare (see resign).

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1650s, from Medieval Latin abrasionem (nominative abrasio) "a scraping," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin abradere "to scrape away, shave off," from ab- "off" (see ab-) + radere "to scrape" (see raze)..

meanings.definitionCn: 测量；[计量] 度量；尺寸；量度制

root.meaning: size or quantity as ascertained by measuring

morphology.mnemonic: size or quantity as ascertained by measuring + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1751, "act of measuring," from measure (v.) + -ment.

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/一起 + 声音 + 具有…特征/名词化

meanings.definitionCn: 支持者；建议者；提出认证遗嘱者

etymology.story: 1580s, "one who brings forth a proposition or argument," from Latin proponentem (nominative proponens), present participle of proponere "put forward" (see propound).

meanings.definitionCn: 偷懒的；无计谋的；无能的

root.meaning: change the gear setting of an engine

morphology.mnemonic: change the gear setting of an engine + 没有…的/无…的

etymology.story: "wanting in resources or energy and ability to shift for oneself," 1580s, from shift (n.1) in the sense "resources" + -less.

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之下 + collective shipping of a country
反对/相反 + 感情/疾病 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1650s, in the medical sense, from empiric + -ism.

meanings.definitionCn: 提高的；高尚的；严肃的；欢欣的
提高；抬起；振奋；提拔（elevate的过去分词）

etymology.story: 1824, from catastrophe + -ic.

meanings.definitionCn: 禁止；禁令；禁酒；诉讼中止令

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + 拥有/保持 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "act of prohibiting, a forbidding by authority," from Anglo-French and Old French prohibition (early 13c.), from Latin prohibitionem (nominative prohibitio) "a hindering, forbidding; legal prohibition," noun of action from past participle stem of prohibere "hold back, restrain, hinder, prevent," from pro- "away, forth" (see pro-) + habere "to hold" (see habit (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 粒子,微粒;极小量;小品词,虚词

etymology.story: late 14c., "small part or division of a whole, minute portion of matter," from Latin particula "little bit or part, grain, jot," diminutive of pars (genitive partis) "part;" see part (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 代表；作为…的典型；具有…的特点

etymology.story: 1630s, "to represent by a symbol," from Late Latin typus (see type (n.)) + -fy.
1610s, from Medieval Latin alimentarius "pertaining to food," from Latin alimentum "nourishment," from alere "to nourish, rear, support, maintain," from PIE root *al- "to grow, nourish" (see old)..

meanings.definitionCn: 因停滞或不流动而发臭的；不景气的

etymology.story: 1660s, from French stagnant (early 17c.), from Latin stagnantem (nominative stagnans), present participle of stagnare "to stagnate" (see stagnate).

meanings.definitionCn: 感知的，知觉的；有知觉力的

morphology.mnemonic: 通过/彻底 + 取/抓 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1650s, from Latin percept-, past participle stem of percipere (see perceive) + -ive.

morphology.mnemonic: to put on the back, + crine

etymology.story: "secreting internally," 1914, from endo- + Latinized form of Greek krinein "to separate, distinguish" (see crisis).

meanings.definitionCn: 最高的；最高级的；过度的
最高级；最好的人；最高程度；夸大话

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French superlatif "absolute, highest; powerful; best" (13c.) and directly from Late Latin superlativus "extravagant, exaggerated, hyperbolic," from Latin superlatus "exaggerated" (used as past participle of superferre "carry over or beyond"), from super "beyond" (see super-) + lat- "carry," from *tlat-, past participle stem of tollere "to take away" (see extol).

meanings.definitionCn: 犬的；犬齿的；犬科的；似犬的
力学（用作单数）；结构；技术；机械学（用作单数）

etymology.story: 1640s, based on Late Latin mechanica, from Greek mekhanike, mekhanika (see mechanic (adj.)); also see -ics..
1530s, "attack, assault," from on + set (n.); compare verbal phrase to set (something) on (someone).

morphology.mnemonic: logical argumentation + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., "based on reason," from logic + -al (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 阻碍…的正常生长或发展

etymology.story: "feat to attract attention," 1878, American English college sports slang, of uncertain origin.
1540s, ridyculouse, from Latin ridiculus "laughable, funny, absurd," from ridere "to laugh" (see risible).

meanings.definitionCn: 孔，穴；（照相机，望远镜等的）光圈，孔径；缝隙

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin apertura "an opening," from apertus, past participle of aperire "to open" (see overt)..

1530s, from Latin assiduus "attending; continually present, incessant; busy; constant," from assidere "to sit down to" (thus "be constantly occupied" at one's work); from ad "to" (see ad-) + sedere "to sit" (see sedentary).

1510s, "refuse, reject," from Middle French réfuter (16c.) and directly from Latin refutare "drive back; rebut, disprove; repress, repel, resist, oppose," from re- "back" (see re-) + -futare "to beat," probably from PIE root *bhau- "to strike down" (see bat (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 无节制的，过度的；不适中的

etymology.story: "excessive, extreme, lacking moderation," late 14c., from Latin immoderatus "boundless, immeasurable," figuratively "unrestrained, excessive," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + moderatus "restrained" (see moderate (adj.)).

1560s, from Middle French émerger and directly from Latin emergere "bring forth, bring to light," intransitively "arise out or up, come forth, come up, come out, rise," from assimilated form of ex- "out" (see ex-) + mergere "to dip, sink" (see merge).

meanings.definitionCn: 男性的；有男子气概的；刚健的

etymology.story: late 15c., "characteristic of a man; marked by manly force," from Middle French viril (14c.) and directly from Latin virilis "of a man, manly, worthy of a man," from vir "a man, a hero," from PIE *wi-ro- "man, freeman" (source also of Sanskrit virah, Avestan vira-, Lithuanian vyras, Old Irish fer, Welsh gwr, Gothic wair, Old English wer "man").
1660s, "subservient, subordinate," from Latin ancillaris "relating to maidservants," from ancilla "handmaid," fem.
1816, literally "of equal heat," from French isotherme (see isotherm) + -al (1).

meanings.definitionCn: [数] 梯形；[数] 不规则四边形
梯形的；[数] 不规则四边形的

etymology.story: 1706, "a trapezium," from Modern Latin trapezoides, from Late Greek trapezoeides, noun use by Euclid of Greek trapezoeides "trapezium-shaped," from trapeza, literally "table" (see trapezium), + -oeides "shaped" (see -oid).

meanings.definitionCn: 明显;显著;根据;证据;迹象
无理由的，无端的；免费的

etymology.story: 1650s, "freely bestowed," from Latin gratuitus "done without pay, spontaneous, voluntary," from gratus "pleasing, agreeable," from gratia "favor" (see grace (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 艳丽的；火焰似的；炫耀的

etymology.story: 1832, originally in reference to a 15c.-16c.

meanings.definitionCn: 友好；亲善关系；友好关系

etymology.story: mid-15c., "friendly relations," from Old French amitie (13c.); earlier amistie (11c.), from Vulgar Latin *amicitatem (nominative *amicitas) "friendship," corresponding to Latin amicitia, from amicus (adj.) "friendly;" related to amare "to love" (see Amy)..

meanings.definitionCn: 致命的， 破坏性的， 毁灭性的； 有害的

etymology.story: 1580s, from Late Latin lethalis, alteration of Latin letalis "deadly, fatal," from letum "death," of uncertain origin.
early 15c., from en- (1) + tangle (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 为增加色香味而添加的配菜；装饰品
历史的；史学的；基于史实的

root.meaning: the recorded events of the past

morphology.mnemonic: the recorded events of the past + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., "of or pertaining to history, conveying information from the past," with -al (1) + Latin historicus "of history, historical," from Greek historikos "historical; of or for inquiry," from historia (see history).

Old English burg, burh "a dwelling or dwellings within a fortified enclosure," from Proto-Germanic *burgs "hill fort, fortress" (source also of Old Frisian burg "castle," Old Norse borg "wall, castle," Old High German burg, buruc "fortified place, citadel," German Burg "castle," Gothic baurgs "city"), from PIE root *bhergh- (2) "high," with derivatives referring to hills, hill forts, fortified elevations (source also of Old English beorg "hill;" see barrow (n.2)).

meanings.definitionCn: 排斥的；防水的；弹回的；令人讨厌的
伦理学；伦理观；道德标准

etymology.story: "the science of morals," c.

early 15c., "act of saying over again," from Old French repetition and directly from Latin repetitionem (nominative repetitio) "a repeating," noun of action from past participle stem of repetere "do or say again" (see repeat (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 使震惊；打昏；给以深刻的印象
昏迷；打昏；惊倒；令人惊叹的事物

etymology.story: early 14c., "to daze or render unconscious" (from a blow, powerful emotion, etc.), probably a shortening of Old French estoner "to stun" (see astonish).

meanings.definitionCn: 平息，缓和；使…平静；使…变软

etymology.story: late 14c., "to soften (a substance)," from Old French mollifier or directly from Late Latin mollificare "make soft, mollify" from mollificus "softening," from Latin mollis "soft" (see melt (v.)) + root of facere "to make" (see factitious).

1748, back-formation from narration or else from Latin narratus, past participle of narrare "to tell, relate, recount." "Richardson and Johnson call it Scottish" [OED], a stigma which kept it from general use until 19c.

morphology.mnemonic: 自我/自动 + name名 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1620s, of states, from Greek autonomia "independence," noun of quality from autonomos "independent, living by one's own laws," from auto- "self" (see auto-) + nomos "custom, law" (see numismatics).
1620s, from Latin ambulatus, past participle of ambulare "to walk" (see amble).

meanings.definitionCn: 无赖；流氓；（纸牌中的）杰克

etymology.story: late Old English cnafa "boy, male child; male servant," from Proto-Germanic *knabon- (source also of Old High German knabo "boy, youth, servant," German knabe "boy, lad"); it is also probably related to Old English cnapa "boy, youth, servant," Old Norse knapi "servant boy," Dutch knaap "a youth, servant," Middle High German knappe "a young squire," German Knappe "squire, shield-bearer." Original sense unknown; Klein suggests the prehistoric meaning might have been "stick, piece of wood." For pronunciation, see kn-.
1680s, "ear trumpet for the hard-of-hearing," coined from Greek mikros "small" (see mica) + phone "sound," from PIE root *bha- (2) "to speak, tell, say" (see fame (n.)).

early 15c., in law, "a giving up" (of an estate, land grant, interest in property, etc.), from Anglo-French surrendre, Old French surrendre noun use of infinitive, "give up, deliver over" (see surrender (v.))..

1620s, of drugs, "inducing sleep," from French hypnotique (16c.) "inclined to sleep, soporific," from Late Latin hypnoticus, from Greek hypnotikos "inclined to sleep, putting to sleep, sleepy," from hypnoun "put to sleep," from hypnos "sleep" (see hypno-).
1620s, from Latin collidere "strike together," from com- "together" (see com-) + laedere "to strike, injure by striking," which is of unknown origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 红衣主教；枢机主教；鲜红色；【鸟类】(北美)主红雀
主要的，基本的；深红色的

etymology.story: "chief, pivotal," early 14c., from Latin cardinalis "principal, chief, essential," from cardo (genitive cardinis) "that on which something turns or depends; pole of the sky," originally "door hinge," which is of unknown origin.

late 14c., "roll of parchment containing writing; a bound book," from Old French volume "scroll, book; work, volume; girth, size" (13c.) and directly from Latin volumen (genitive voluminis) "roll (of a manuscript); coil, wreath," literally "that which is rolled," from volvere "to turn around, roll" (see volvox).
1580s, back-formation from transaction, or else from Latin transactus, past participle of transigere "to drive through, accomplish, bring to an end, settle."

meanings.definitionCn: 邪恶的；穷凶极恶的；不法的
[金融] 受益人，受惠者；封臣

etymology.story: 1610s (n.), 1620s (adj.), probably via French bénéficiaire, from Latin beneficiarius "enjoying a favor, privileged," from beneficium (see benefice)..

meanings.definitionCn: 催化剂；造成变化的人或事

etymology.story: "substance which speeds a chemical reaction but itself remains unchanged," 1902, formed in English (on analogy of analyst) from catalysis.

meanings.definitionCn: 不定的；周游的；错误的；偏离正路的

morphology.mnemonic: 漫游/错误 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: mid-14c., "travelling, roving," from Anglo-French erraunt, from two Old French words that were confused even before they reached English: 1.
mid-14c.; see glutton + -ous.

meanings.definitionCn: 刚愎的；坚持错误的，行为反常的

etymology.story: mid-14c., "wicked," from Old French pervers "unnatural, degenerate; perverse, contrary" (12c.) and directly from Latin perversus "turned away, contrary, askew," figuratively, "turned away from what is right, wrong, malicious, spiteful," past participle of pervertere "to corrupt" (see pervert (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 不知不觉地；出其不意地；不料

etymology.story: 1530s, "without being aware," from un- (1) "not" + aware + adverbial genitive -s.

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + dign + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1580s, from Latin indignantem (nominative indignans) "impatient, reluctant, indignant," present participle of indignari "to be displeased at, be offended, resent, deem unworthy" (see indignation).
"continuing in time," late Old English, present participle adjective from last (v.).

morphology.mnemonic: sneering sarcastic person + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1580s, "resembling Cynic philosophers," from cynic + -al (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 故障；崩溃；分解；分类；衰弱；跺脚曳步舞

etymology.story: "a collapse," 1832, from break (v.) + down (adv.).

late 14c., "full of twists and turns," from Anglo-French tortuous (12c.), Old French tortuos, from Latin tortuosus "full of twists, winding," from tortus "a twisting, winding," from stem of torquere "to twist, wring, distort" (see torque (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 外表；虚饰；饰面；胶合板的一层；薄木片

etymology.story: 1728 (earlier fineer, 1708), from German furnieren (see veneer (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 言谈粗俗的人；说下流话的人

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French calamite (14c.), from Latin calamitatem (nominative calamitas) "damage, loss, failure; disaster, misfortune, adversity," origin obscure.

late 14c., from Old French acompliss-, present participle stem of acomplir "to fulfill, fill up, complete" (12c.), from Vulgar Latin *accomplere, from Latin ad- "to" (see ad-) + complere "fill up" (see complete (adj.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 建立/放置 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1620s, "one who appoints or elects a representative," from Latin constituentem (nominative constituens), present participle of constituere (see constitute).

morphology.mnemonic: 激怒/刺激 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1530s, "stimulate to action, rouse, incite," from Latin irritatus, past participle of irritare "excite, provoke, annoy;" according to de Vaan, probably a verb from Proto-Italic *rito- "stirred," from the same PIE root that produced English run (v.).
early 15c., from the expression cry havoc "give the signal to pillage" (Anglo-French crier havok, late 14c.).

meanings.definitionCn: 照亮，照明；用灯光装饰；说明，阐释
学校的；学者的；学术的（等于scholastical）
学者；学生；墨守成规者；经院哲学家

etymology.story: 1590s, "of or pertaining to Scholastic theologians" (Churchmen in the Middle Ages whose theology and philosophy was based on Church Fathers and Aristotle), from Middle French scholastique (14c.), from Latin scholasticus "of a school," from Greek skholastikos "enjoying leisure; devoting one's leisure to learning," hence, as a noun, "a scholar," also in a bad sense, "a pedant; a simpleton," from skhola (see school (n.1)).
late 14c., "play in a cycle of mystery plays," from Medieval Latin pagina, of uncertain origin, perhaps from Latin pagina "page of a book" (see page (n.1)) on notion of "manuscript" of a play.

1610s, "to thrust forward or onward, to drive along;" 1640s, "to cause to stick out," from Latin protrudere "thrust forward; push out," from pro- "forward" (see pro-) + trudere "to thrust, push," from PIE *treud- "to press, push, squeeze" (see threat).
late 14c., "act of fastening with a bar," verbal noun from bar (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 挣扎，折腾；错乱地做事或说话

etymology.story: also enthral "to hold in mental or moral bondage," 1570s, from en- (1) "make, put in" + thrall (n.).

mid-14c., from Old French residu (14c.), from Latin residuum "a remainder, that which is left behind," noun use of neuter of adjective residuus "remaining, left over," from residere "remain behind" (see reside)..
late 14c., from Latin contemptus "scorn," from past participle of contemnere "to scorn, despise," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + *temnere "to slight, scorn," which is of uncertain origin.

early 15c., "publish a 'thundering' denunciation; hurl condemnation (at an offender)," a figurative use, from Latin fulminatus, past participle of fulminare "hurl lightning, lighten," figuratively "to thunder," from fulmen (genitive fulminis) "lightning flash," related to fulgor "lightning," fulgere "to shine, flash," from PIE *bhleg- "to shine, flash," from root *bhel- (1) "to shine, flash, burn" (see bleach (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 比喻的；修饰丰富的；形容多的

morphology.mnemonic: to shape into + 使…/具有…的 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: late 14c., "emblematical," from Old French figuratif "metaphorical," from Late Latin figurativus "figurative" (of speech), from figurat-, past participle stem of Latin figurare "to form, shape," from figura "a shape, form, figure" (see figure (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 故意的；深思熟虑的；从容的

etymology.story: 1540s, from Latin deliberatus, past participle of deliberare (see deliberation).
late 13c., collere "charcoal maker and seller," agent noun from Middle English col (see coal).

morphology.mnemonic: separate, distinct + 状态/行为/过程 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1680s (implied in discretionarily); see discretion + -ary..

Old English rife "abundant, common, prevalent," from Proto-Germanic *rif- (source also of Old Norse rifr, Swedish river, Norwegian riv, Middle Dutch riif, Middle Low German rive "abundant, generous"), said to be from PIE root *rei- "to scratch, tear, cut" "The prevalence of the word in early southern texts is in favour of its being native in English, rather than an adoption from Scandinavian." [OED].

meanings.definitionCn: 加尔文主义；加尔文教派

etymology.story: 1560s, from John Calvin (1509-1564), Protestant reformer, + -ism.

meanings.definitionCn: 特许；包租；发给特许执照
释放；发射；让与；允许发表

etymology.story: early 14c., "abatement of distress; means of deliverance," from Old French relais, reles (12c.), a back-formation from relesser, relaissier (see release (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 用 + 具有…特征/名词化 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1620s, from Latin liberatus, past participle of liberare "set free," from liber "free" (see liberal).

meanings.definitionCn: 通行费；代价；钟声；伤亡人数

etymology.story: "to sound with slow single strokes" (intransitive), mid-15c., probably a special use of tollen "to draw, lure," early 13c.
as an exclamation, "well done!," 1761, from Italian bravo, literally "brave" (see brave (adj.)).
alternative plural of brother (q.v.); predominant c.

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 爆发/鼓掌

etymology.story: 1530s (transitive), "to reject with scorn," from Latin explodere "drive out or off by clapping, hiss off, hoot off," originally theatrical, "to drive an actor off the stage by making noise," hence "drive out, reject, destroy the repute of" (a sense surviving in an exploded theory), from ex- "out" (see ex-) + plaudere "to clap the hands, applaud," which is of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 给…上颈手枷；使…惹人嘲笑
示众；颈手枷；使惹人嘲笑
狂热者；犹太教狂热信徒

etymology.story: early 14c., "member of a militant 1st century Jewish sect which fiercely resisted the Romans in Palestine," from Late Latin zelotes, from Greek zelotes "one who is a zealous follower," from zeloun "to be zealous," from zelos "zeal" (see zeal).

meanings.definitionCn: 繁茂的； 肥沃的； 丰富的

etymology.story: 1530s, from Middle French luxuriant and directly from Latin luxuriantem (nominative luxurians), present participle of luxuriare "have to excess, grow profusely" (see luxuriate).
"eruption" (of disease, hostilities, etc.), c.

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 15c., "action of appealing" (to a higher authority), from Old French apelacion (13c.), from Latin appellationem (nominative appellatio) "an addressing, accosting; an appeal; a name, title," noun of action from past participle stem of appellare (see appeal).
1640s, back-formation from abomination or from Latin abominatus, past participle of abominari "shun as an ill omen" (see abomination).

morphology.mnemonic: 相似/相同 + 与…有关的/像…的

etymology.story: "having characteristics in common," 1610s (earlier similary, 1560s), from French similaire, from a Medieval Latin extended form of Latin similis "like, resembling," from Old Latin semol "together," from PIE root *sem- (1) "one, as one, together with" (see same).
1570s, "speak against," also "assert the contrary" (1580s), from Latin contradictus, past participle of contradicere (see contradiction).

meanings.definitionCn: 开业者，从业者，执业医生

morphology.mnemonic: 实践/做 + 状态/行为/过程 + er

etymology.story: 1540s, a hybrid formed from practitian "practitioner" (c.
late 14c., from Old French comporter "endure, admit, behave" (13c.), from Latin comportare "to bring together, collect," from com- "together" (see com-) + portare "to carry" (see port (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: 运动/重要 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1650s, from moment + -ous to carry the sense of "important" while momentary kept the meaning "of an instant of time."

meanings.definitionCn: 肥沃的；多产的；丰饶的；生殖力旺盛的

etymology.story: music instruction, "loud, loudly," from Italian forte, literally "strong," from Latin fortis "strong" (see fort).

meanings.definitionCn: 刺耳的；不谐和的；不调和的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French dissonant and directly from Latin dissonantem (nominative dissonans), present participle of dissonare "differ in sound," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + sonare "to sound" (see sonata)..
1918, from Modern Latin altimeter, from alti- "high" (from Latin altus; see old) + -meter..

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + typ + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1847, from a- (2) "not" + typical.

morphology.mnemonic: lively, brisk, in good spirits + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: late 14c., from Anglo-French purtinaunt (late 13c.), Old French partenant (mid-13c.) and directly from Latin pertinentem (nominative pertinens) "pertaining," present participle of pertinere "to relate, concern" (see pertain).
long-necked ruminant animal of Africa, 1590s, giraffa, from Italian giraffa, from Arabic zarafa, probably from an African language.

late 14c., from Latin circumscribere "to make a circle around, encircle, draw a line around; limit, restrain, confine, set the boundaries of," from circum- "around" (see circum-) + scribere "write" (see script (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 目空一切的，高傲的；傲慢的，自大的

morphology.mnemonic: 超/在…之上 + cili + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1520s, "lofty with pride, haughtily contemptuous," from Latin superciliosus "haughty, arrogant," from supercilium "haughty demeanor, pride," literally "eyebrow" (via notion of raising the eyebrow to express haughtiness), from super "above" (see super-) + second element akin to cilium "eyelid," related to celare "to cover, hide," from PIE root *kel- (2) "to conceal" (see cell).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 推/驱动 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1670s, "spongy, absorbent," from Latin bibulus "drinking readily, given to drink;" of things, "absorbent; moistened," from bibere "to drink" (see imbibe).

morphology.mnemonic: 眼/视 + 主义/学说/运动

etymology.story: 1759 (in translations of Voltaire), from French optimisme (1737), from Modern Latin optimum, used by Gottfried Leibniz (in "Théodicée," 1710) to mean "the greatest good," from Latin optimus "the best" (see optimum).

meanings.definitionCn: 尽情享受；使有风味；加调味品于；品尝，欣赏
唯物主义者；实利主义者

etymology.story: 1660s and after in various philosophical and theological senses, on model of French matérialiste, from material (n.) + -ist.

morphology.mnemonic: 坏/恶 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Old French maledicion "a curse" (15c.), from Latin maledictionem (nominative maledictio) "the action of speaking evil of, slander," in Late Latin "a curse," noun of action from past participle stem of maledicere "to speak badly or evil of, slander," from male "badly" (see mal-) + dicere "to say" (see diction)..

meanings.definitionCn: 细胞；电池；蜂房的巢室；单人小室

etymology.story: early 12c., "small monastery, subordinate monastery" (from Medieval Latin in this sense), later "small room for a monk or a nun in a monastic establishment; a hermit's dwelling" (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 粗糙的；天然的，未加工的；粗鲁的

etymology.story: late 14c., "in a raw state," from Latin crudus "rough; not cooked, raw, bloody," from PIE *krue-do-, from PIE *kreue- (1) "raw flesh" (see raw).
Old English wiðstandan "resist, oppose," from wið "against" (see with) + standan "to stand" (see stand (v.)); perhaps a loan-translation of Latin resistere "to resist" (see resist).

meanings.definitionCn: 有效的;有根据的;正当的

etymology.story: 1570s, "having force in law, legally binding," from Middle French valide (16c.), from Latin validus "strong, effective, powerful, active," from valere "be strong" (see valiant).

mid-15c., "too plain to admit of doubt," from Latin indubitabilis "that cannot be doubted," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + dubitabilis "doubtful," from dubitare "hesitate, doubt" (see doubt (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 圆滑的；肥皂似的；口齿伶俐的
[力] 惯性；惰性，迟钝；不活动

etymology.story: 1713, "that property of matter by virtue of which it retains its state of rest or of uniform rectilinear motion so long as no foreign cause changes that state" [Century Dictionary], introduced as a term in physics 17c.
1590s, "shorten, condense, abridge," from epitome + -ize.
Old English wintrig (see winter (n.) + -y (2)); also winterlic; "but the modern word appears to be a new formation" [Barnhart].

meanings.definitionCn: 欠款，逾期债款；待完成的工作
乞丐；穷人；靠救济度日者

etymology.story: 1510s, "person destitute of property or means of livelihood," from Latin pauper "poor, not wealthy, of small means" (see poor (adj.)).

1716, earlier aera (1610s), from Late Latin aera, era "an era or epoch from which time is reckoned" (7c.), probably identical with Latin aera "counters used for calculation," plural of aes (genitive aeris) "brass, copper, money" (see ore, also compare copper).
1590s, "to sway unsteadily," from Latin vacillatus, past participle of vacillare "sway to and fro; hesitate" (see vacillation).

late 14c., "moral qualities," from Old French moralité "moral (of a story); moral instruction; morals, moral character" (13c.) and directly from Late Latin moralitatem (nominative moralitas) "manner, character," from Latin moralis (see moral (adj.)).

morphology.mnemonic: in + 判定/控告 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1730, back-formation from incrimination (q.v.) or else from Medieval Latin incriminatus, past participle of incriminare "to incriminate, accuse."

meanings.definitionCn: 灾难；大洪水，地震；（社会政治的）大变动

etymology.story: 1630s, from French cataclysme (16c.), from Latin cataclysmos or directly from Greek kataklysmos "deluge, flood, inundation," from kataklyzein "to deluge," from kata "down" (see cata-) + klyzein "to wash," from PIE *kleue- "to wash, clean" (see cloaca)..

meanings.definitionCn: 秘密的；鬼鬼祟祟的；暗中的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Latin surrepticius "stolen, furtive, clandestine," from surreptus, past participle of surripere "seize secretly, take away, steal, plagiarize," from assimilated form of sub "from under" (hence, "secretly;" see sub-) + rapere "to snatch" (see rapid).

meanings.definitionCn: 移植；移植器官；被移植物；移居者
没有骨气的；无脊椎的；懦弱的

morphology.mnemonic: the back of a book + 没有…的/无…的

etymology.story: 1827 of animals (1805 of plants), from spine + -less.

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 光 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1560s, perhaps via Middle French élucider (15c.) or directly from Late Latin elucidatus, past participle of elucidare "make light or clear," from assimilated form of ex- "out, away" (see ex-) + lucidus "clear" (see lucid).

meanings.definitionCn: 赤字,逆差;亏损,亏空;不足,缺乏

etymology.story: 1782, from French déficit (late 17c.), from Latin deficit "it is wanting," an introductory word in clauses of inventory, third person singular present indicative of deficere "to be deficient" (see deficient)..

meanings.definitionCn: 顽固的，倔强的，不易屈服的，较难治愈的

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Latin obstinatus "resolute, resolved, determined, inflexible, stubborn," past participle of obstinare "persist, stand stubbornly, set one's mind on," from ob "by" (see ob-) + stinare (related to stare "stand") from PIE *ste-no-, from root *stā- "to stand" (see stet).
1727, "to administer medicine in too large a dose" (transitive); from 1968 as "to take an overdose of drugs;" see over- + dose (v.).
1570s, of uncertain etymology; perhaps literally "on skew" (see skew), or from the Old Norse form, a ska.

meanings.definitionCn: 蚱蜢；[植保] 蝗虫；小型侦察机

etymology.story: popular name of insects with hind legs suited to jumping, mid-14c.

meanings.definitionCn: （权利的）转让，出让；（领土的）割让

etymology.story: late 14c., "a relinquishing," from Old French cession "cession; death" (13c.), from Latin cessionem (nominative cessio) "a giving up, surrendering," noun of action from past participle stem of cedere "to go away, yield" (see cede).

morphology.mnemonic: 堆积/积累 + 使…/具有…的 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: early 15c., "beg for, beseech," back-formation from  supplication or else from Latin supplicatus, past participle of supplicare "plead humbly, beseech, kneel down."
"sum total of genes in a set," 1930, genom, modeled on German genom, coined 1920 by German botanist Hans Winkler, from gen "gene" (see gene) + (chromos)om "chromosome" (see chromosome)..

meanings.definitionCn: 神秘的；高深莫测的；谜一般的

etymology.story: 1640s, from Late Latin aenigmaticus, from aenigmat-, stem of aenigma (see enigma).

morphology.mnemonic: 过度/超越 + decline to do something

etymology.story: "stretch of road that passes over another," 1929, American English, from over- + pass (v.).

early 15c., "seizing property or citizens of another nation in retaliation for loss inflicted on one's own," from Anglo-French reprisaille (14c.), from Old French reprisaille (Modern French représaille), from early Italian ripresaglia, from ripreso, past participle of riprendere "take back," from Latin reprendere, earlier reprehendere (see reprehend).
"to happen, befall," late 12c., from be- + tiden "to happen" (see tide)..
(also avant garde, avantgarde); French, literally "advance guard" (see avant + guard (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 与…有关的/像…的

etymology.story: "pertaining to alveoli," 1799, from Modern Latin alveolus "socket of a tooth" (see alveolus)..

meanings.definitionCn: 清爽的；气候有益健康的

etymology.story: 1540s, from Latin salubris "promoting health, healthful," from salus (genitive salutis) "welfare, health" (see salute (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + name名 + 具有…特征/名词化 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French *ignominieus or directly from Latin ignominiosus "disgraceful, shameful," from ignominia "disgrace, infamy, loss of a (good) name," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + nomen (genitive nominis) "name" (see name (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 脚/儿童 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1580s, from Middle French pédagogie (16c.), from Latin paedagogia, from Greek paidagogia "education, attendance on boys," from paidagogos "teacher" (see pedagogue)..
1530s, from French dégradation (14c., Old French degradacion), from Medieval Latin degradationem (nominative degradatio), noun of action from past participle stem of degradare (see degrade)..

morphology.mnemonic: 自我/自动 + name名 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1800, from Greek autonomos "having one's own laws," of animals, "feeding or ranging at will," from autos "self" (see auto-) + nomos "law" (see numismatic).

morphology.mnemonic: vibr + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1650s, from Latin vibrationem (nominative vibratio) "a shaking, a brandishing," noun of action from past participle stem of vibrare "set in tremulous motion" (see vibrate).

meanings.definitionCn: 空中的，航空的；空气的；空想的

morphology.mnemonic: 空气 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1902 (short for aerial antenna, etc.); see aerial (adj.)..
mid-15c., "act or process of testing, a putting to proof by examination, experiment, etc.," from Anglo-French trial, noun formed from triet "to try" (see try (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 看法； 感觉； 洞察力

morphology.mnemonic: 通过/彻底 + 取/抓 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "receiving, collection," from Latin perceptionem (nominative perceptio) "perception, apprehension, a taking," from percipere "perceive" (see perceive).

meanings.definitionCn: 饱和；色饱和度；浸透；磁化饱和

morphology.mnemonic: soak thoroughly + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1550s, formed in English from saturate, or else from Late Latin saturationem (nominative saturatio), noun of action from past participle stem of saturare.

meanings.definitionCn: 土产的， 本地的， 本土的

etymology.story: "born or originating in a particular place," 1640s, from Late Latin indigenus "born in a country, native," from Latin indigena "sprung from the land, native," as a noun, "a native," literally "in-born," or "born in (a place)," from Old Latin indu (prep.) "in, within" + gignere (perfective genui) "beget," from PIE root *gene- "to produce, give birth, beget" (see genus).
"to control, dominate," c.

morphology.mnemonic: 看见 + 与…有关的/形容词 + ize

etymology.story: 1817, first attested in, and perhaps coined by, Coleridge ("Biographia Literaria"); see visual + -ize.

meanings.definitionCn: 使年轻；使更新；使恢复精神；使复原

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + young年轻，年少 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1807, irregular formation from re- "again" + Latin juvenis "young" (see young (adj.)) + -ate (2).

1690s, "desk with drawers, writing desk," from French bureau "office; desk, writing table," originally "cloth covering for a desk," from burel "coarse woolen cloth" (as a cover for writing desks), Old French diminutive of bure "dark brown cloth," which is perhaps either from Latin burrus "red," or from Late Latin burra "wool, shaggy garment." Offices being full of such desks, the meaning expanded 1720 to "division of a government." Meaning "chest of drawers" is from 1770, said to be American English but early in British use..
"to throw into a trance," 1590s, from en- (1) "put in" + trance (n.).
1702, "slow, sluggish," from lag (v.) + -ard.

meanings.definitionCn: 杂色的；混杂的；五颜六色的

etymology.story: late 14c., "parti-colored" (originally of fabric), from Anglo-French motteley, probably from Old English mot "speck" (see mote).
1690s, from frolic + -some (1)..
1610s, from French charlatan "mountebank, babbler" (16c.), from Italian ciarlatano "a quack," from ciarlare "to prate, babble," from ciarla "chat, prattle," perhaps imitative of ducks' quacking.

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 行为/结果/集合

meanings.definitionCn: 压力计；[流] 测压计；血压计

etymology.story: 1730, from French manomètre (1706), said to have been coined by French mathematician Pierre Varignon (1654-1722) from Greek manos "thin, rare; loose in texture, porous; scanty, few" (see mono-) + -mètre (see -meter).

meanings.definitionCn: 原声的； 声音的； 听觉的

morphology.mnemonic: 平等 + 具有…特征/名词化

meanings.definitionCn: 烹饪法，美食法；享乐主义

morphology.mnemonic: 胃 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1814, from French gastronomie, coined 1800 by Joseph de Berchoux (1762-1838) as title of poem on good living, after Gastrologia, title of a now-lost poem of antiquity, quoted by Athenaeus (see gastrology).

meanings.definitionCn: 使高贵；增威严；授以荣誉

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Middle French dignifier, from Medieval Latin dignificare "make worthy," from Latin dignus (see dignity) + -ficare, from facere "to make, do" (see factitious).

meanings.definitionCn: 古怪的；奇异的；异国风格的；偏僻的

morphology.mnemonic: 超过/外部 + land + 有点…/像…

etymology.story: Old English utlendisc "of a foreign country, not native," from utland "foreign land," literally "outland" (see out + land (n.)) + -ish.
"given to jokes and jesting," 1670s, from Latin iocosus "full of jesting, fond of jokes, funny," from iocus "pastime, sport; a jest, joke" (see joke (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 严格的，严厉的；严密的；严酷的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French rigorous (13c., Modern French rigoureux), from Medieval Latin rigorosus, from Latin rigor (see rigor).

meanings.definitionCn: 衰老的；疲惫的；（土地）贫瘠的；（动植物）不育的

etymology.story: 1620s, "functionless as a result of age or exhaustion," from Latin effetus (usually in fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 紧张的；费力的；奋发的；艰苦的；热烈的

etymology.story: "characterized by great effort," mid-15c.

late 14c., litarge, from Old French litargie or directly from Medieval Latin litargia, from Late Latin lethargia, from Greek lethargia "forgetfulness," from lethargos "forgetful," originally "inactive through forgetfulness," from lethe "forgetfulness" (see latent) + argos "idle" (see argon).

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1590s, from Latin supplicantem (nominative supplicans), present participle of supplicare "plead humbly" (see supplication).

meanings.definitionCn: 猛然抛下；突然发生；加速

morphology.mnemonic: 价格/价值 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1560s, probably a back formation from precipitation..

meanings.definitionCn: 命令，训令；授权(选民对代表)

morphology.mnemonic: 命令/委托 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1825, American English spelling alteration to reflect a dialectal pronunciation of roil (q.v.); compare heist from hoist and in the same era spile for spoil (v.).

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + 步/走 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "action of moving from one condition to another," from Old French progression and directly from Latin progressionem (nominative progressio) "a going forward, advancement, growth, increase," noun of action from past participle stem of progredi "go forward," from pro- "forward" (see pro-) + gradi "to step, walk," from gradus "step" (see grade (n.))..

1703, "union of parts, parts of a thing taken together," from French ensemblée "all the parts of a thing considered together," from Late Latin insimul "at the same time," from in- intensive prefix + simul "at the same time," related to similis (see similar).

meanings.definitionCn: 诡计；恶作剧；窍门；花招；骗局；欺诈
特技的；欺诈的；有决窍的

morphology.mnemonic: prud + ance + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Medieval Latin prudentialis, from Latin prudentia "a foreseeing, foresight" (see prudence).

1620s, short for plaudite "an actor's request for applause" (1560s), from Latin plaudite! "applaud!" second person plural imperative of plaudere "to clap, strike, beat; applaud, approve," of unknown origin (also in applaud, explode).
late 14c., "to defile," a back formation from pollution, or else from Latin pollutus, past participle of polluere "to defile, pollute, contaminate."

morphology.mnemonic: 穿越/转变 + 放置 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1530s, from Middle French transposition or directly from Medieval Latin transpositionem (nominative transpositio), noun of action from past participle stem of transponere (see transpose)..

"theft; wrongful or fraudulent taking of the personal goods of another with felonious intent," late 15c., from Anglo-French larcin (late 13c.), Old French larrecin, larcin "theft, robbery" (11c.), from Latin latrocinium "robbery, freebooting, highway-robbery, piracy," from latro "robber, bandit," also "hireling, mercenary," ultimately from a Greek source akin to latron "pay, hire, wages," from a suffixed form of PIE root *le- (1) "to get" (source also of Greek latreia "worship, service paid to the gods, hired labor," latron "pay, hire," latris "servant, worshipper").

meanings.definitionCn: 可逆的；可撤消的；可反转的

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + metrical composition + able

etymology.story: 1640s, from reverse (v.) + -ible.

meanings.definitionCn: 无味的，枯燥乏味的；单调的

etymology.story: 1610s, "without taste or perceptible flavor," from Middle French insipide "insipid" (16c.), from Late Latin inspidus "tasteless," from in- "not" (see in- (1)) + Latin sapidus "tasty," from sapere "have a taste" (also "be wise;" see sapient).
1590s, "commit to writing;" see memory + -ize.

meanings.definitionCn: 相称的；同量的；同样大小的

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 测量 + 使…/具有…的
