etymology.story: 1640s, from Late Latin commensuratus, from Latin com- "with" (see com-) + Late Latin mensuratus, past participle of mensurare "to measure," from mensura (see measure (v.))..

1610s, from Medieval Latin diaphanus, from Greek diaphanes "transparent," from dia- "through" (see dia-) + phainesthai, middle voice form (subject acting on itself) of phainein "to show" (see phantasm)..

morphology.mnemonic: stimul + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1520s, "act of pricking or stirring to action," from Latin stimulationem (nominative stimulatio) "an incitement," noun of action from past participle stem of stimulare "prick, goad, urge," from stimulus "spur, goad" (see stimulus)..

1776, "throw into confusion," from French déranger, from Old French desrengier "disarrange, throw into disorder," from des- "do the opposite of" (see dis-) + Old French rengier (Modern French ranger) "to put into line," from reng "line, row," from a Germanic source (see rank (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 怠慢的；迟缓的；不小心的

etymology.story: early 15c., "weak, dissolved," from Latin remissus "relaxed, languid; negligent," past participle of remittere "slacken, abate, let go" (see remit).
"ship's cargo," 1706; see manifest (adj.).

1560s, from Middle French prélude "notes sung or played to test the voice or instrument" (1530s), from Medieval Latin preludium "prelude, preliminary," from Latin praeludere "to play beforehand for practice, preface," from prae- "before" (see pre-) + ludere "to play" (see ludicrous).
late 14c., "make furious or mad" (implied in enraged), from Old French enragier "go wild, go mad, lose one's senses," from en- "make, put in" (see en- (1)) + rage "rabies, rage" (see rage (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 明确的,具体的;特定的,特有的
不光彩的；卑鄙的；卑贱的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "of low birth;" 1590s as "not honorable, of low character;" from Middle French ignoble (14c.), from Latin ignobilis "unknown, undistinguished, obscure; of base birth, not noble," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + gnobilis "well-known, famous, renowned, of superior birth" (see noble (adj.)).
1650s, from Greek kakophonia, from kakophonos "harsh sounding," from kakos "bad, evil" (see caco-) + phone "voice, sound," from PIE root *bha- (2) "to speak, tell, say" (see fame (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 统治的；占优势的；流行的，广泛的

etymology.story: "reigning, exercising authority," c.

1650s, "dexterous," originally "rightly," from French adroit, from phrase à droit "according to right," from Old French à "to" (see ad-) + droit "right," from Late Latin directum "right, justice," accusative of Latin directus "straight" (see direct (v.)).
1796, from French frivolité, from Old French frivole "frivolous," from Latin frivolus (see frivolous)..

meanings.definitionCn: [数] 余数，残余；剩余物；其余的人

etymology.story: late 14c., from Anglo-French remainder, Old French remaindre, noun use of infinitive, a variant of Old French remanoir (see remain (v.)).
"practice of innovation in language," 1772 (in a translation from French), from French néologisme, from neo- (see neo-) + Greek logos "word" (see lecture (n.)) + -ism.

1560s, "break or opening" in a material object, especially in anatomy, from Latin hiatus "opening, aperture, rupture, gap," from past participle stem of hiare "to gape, stand open," from PIE root *ghai- "to yawn, gape" (see yawn (v.)).

1590s, "acting as if benumbed," alteration of Middle English clumsid "numb with cold" (14c.), past participle of clumsen "to benumb, stiffen or paralyze with cold or fear," from a Scandinavian source akin to Old Norse klumsa "make speechless, palsy; prevent from speaking," intensive of kluma "to make motionless." For insertion of -s-, see flimsy.

meanings.definitionCn: 召唤;传讯,传唤;鼓起(勇气),振作(精神)

etymology.story: 1590s, "born out of wedlock," from Latin spurius "illegitimate, false" (source also of Italian spurio, Spanish espurio), from spurius (n.) "illegitimate child," probably from Etruscan spural "public." Sense of "having an irregular origin, not properly constituted" is from c.

meanings.definitionCn: 抽象拚贴画（用报纸、布、压平的花等碎片拼合而成的）

etymology.story: 1919, from French collage "a pasting," from Old French coller "to glue," from Greek kolla "glue." Earliest reference is in Wyndham Lewis..

meanings.definitionCn: 维持；继续；维修；主张；供养

etymology.story: mid-13c., "to practice habitually," from Anglo-French meintenir (Old French maintenir, 12c.) "keep (a wife), sustain; persevere in, practice continually," from Latin manu tenere "hold in the hand," from manu, ablative of manus "hand" (see manual) + tenere "to hold" (see tenet).

meanings.definitionCn: 改造， 使恢复正常生活； 使恢复原状， 修复； 复职； 恢复…的名誉

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + obtain necessary qualifications

etymology.story: 1570s, "to bring back to a former condition after decay or damage," back-formation from rehabilitation and in part from Medieval Latin rehabilitatus, past participle of rehabilitare.

meanings.definitionCn: 与生俱来的权利；长子继承权

etymology.story: also birth-right, 1530s, from birth (n.) + right (n.).

early 15c., "branch out," from Middle French ramifier (early 14c.), from Medieval Latin ramificare "to form branches," from Latin ramus "branch" (see ramus) + root of facere "to make" (see factitious).
1530s, originally "to bind up (a corpse);" sense of "hinder, restrain" is from 1727, from trammel (n.), a figurative use from the literal sense "bind (a horse's legs) with a trammel" (c.

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 无 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1520s, from an obsolete adjective meaning "reduced to nothing" (late 14c.), originally the past participle of a verb, anihil, from Old French annichiler (14c.), from Late Latin annihilare "to reduce to nothing," from Latin ad- "to" (see ad-) + nihil "nothing" (see nil).

root.meaning: wounded or killed by a bullet or other projectile

morphology.mnemonic: 向上/提高 + wounded or killed by a bullet or other projectile

etymology.story: 1530s, from up (adj.) + shot (n.); originally, the final shot in an archery match, hence the figurative sense of "result, issue, conclusion" (c.

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French acusacion or directly from Latin accusationem (nominative accusatio), noun of action from past participle stem of accusare (see accuse)..

meanings.definitionCn: 激烈的；狂暴的；偏激的；患狂犬病的

etymology.story: 1610s, "furious, raving," from Latin rabidus "raging, furious, enraged; inspired; ungoverned; rabid," from rabere "be mad, rave" (see rage (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 难以置信的，不像真实的

etymology.story: "not having an appearance of truth or credibility," 1670s, from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + plausible.

late 14c., from Old French remors (Modern French remords), from Medieval Latin remorsum, noun use of neuter past participle of Latin remordere "to vex, disturb," literally "to bite back," from re- "back" (see re-) + mordere "to bite" (see mordant).
1630s, from chimera + -ical.
"quality of being beneficent, kind, charitable," mid-15c., from Latin beneficentia "kindness, generosity," a back-formation from beneficentior (see beneficent)..
1510s, from Middle French dissuader and directly from Latin dissuadere "to advise against, oppose by argument," from dis- "off, against" (see dis-) + suadere "to urge" (see suasion).

meanings.definitionCn: 宫殿似的；宏伟的；壮丽的

etymology.story: 1754, from French palatial "magnificent," from Latin palatium (see palace).

meanings.definitionCn: 爱称；指小词；身材极小的人
包含； 包围； 环绕

etymology.story: 1550s, from en- (1) "make, put in" + compass (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 电脑高速缓冲存储器；贮存物；隐藏处

etymology.story: 1797, "hiding place," from French Canadian trappers' slang, "hiding place for stores" (1660s), a back-formation from French cacher "to hide, conceal" (13c., Old French cachier), from Vulgar Latin *coacticare "store up, collect, compress," frequentative of Latin coactare "constrain," from coactus, past participle of cogere "to collect" (see cogent).

late 13c., weyveren, "to show indecision," probably related to Old English wæfre "restless, wavering," from Proto-Germanic *wæbraz (source also of Middle High German wabern "to waver," Old Norse vafra "to hover about"), a frequentative form from the root of wave (v.).

1520s, from Middle French imminent (14c.) and directly from Latin imminentem (nominative imminens) "overhanging; impending," present participle of imminere "to overhang, lean towards," hence "be near to," also "threaten, menace, impend, be at hand, be about to happen," from assimilated form of in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + -minere "jut out," which is related to mons "hill" (see mount (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: 第一/首要 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: also primaeval, 1650s, with -al (1) + Latin primaevus "early in life, youthful," from primus "first" (see prime (adj.)) + aevum "an age" (see eon)..

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + genit + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "existing from birth," 1796, from Latin congenitus, from com- "together, with" (see com-) + genitus, past participle of gignere "to beget" (see genus).

meanings.definitionCn: 天鹅绒般柔软的；醇和的，可口的

morphology.mnemonic: shaggy cloth, + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1712, from velvet + -y (2).

meanings.definitionCn: 刺；刺痛；植物的皮刺；针刺般的感觉
针一般地刺；戳；使感到刺痛

etymology.story: Old English pricel "thing to prick with, goad, point," from the same source as Old English prician (see prick (v.)) with instrumental suffix -el (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 不道德的；邪恶的；淫荡的

etymology.story: 1650s, "not consistent with moral law or standards, ethically wrong," from assimilated form of in- (1) "not" + moral (adj.).

early 15c., from Latin dissipatus, past participle of dissipare "to spread abroad, scatter, disperse; squander, disintegrate," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + supare "to throw, scatter," from PIE *swep- "to throw, sling, cast" (source also of Lithuanian supu "to swing, rock," Old Church Slavonic supo "to strew").

meanings.definitionCn: 易怒的，暴躁的；爱发牢骚的，抱怨的；爱挑剔的

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Medieval Latin flasco "container, bottle," from Late Latin flasconem (nominative flasco) "bottle," which is of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 波动， 起伏； 动摇

morphology.mnemonic: 流动 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Old French fluctuacion (12c.) or directly from Latin fluctuationem (nominative fluctuatio) "a wavering, vacillation," noun of action from past participle stem of fluctuare "to undulate, to move in waves," from fluctus "a wave, billow, surge, a flowing," from past participle of fluere "to flow" (see fluent)..
1660s, "not feeling pain, insen" from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + passive.

meanings.definitionCn: 神秘的；超自然的；难以理解的

etymology.story: 1530s, "secret, not divulged," from Middle French occulte and directly from Latin occultus "hidden, concealed, secret," past participle of occulere "cover over, conceal," from ob "over" (see ob-) + a verb related to celare "to hide," from PIE root *kel- (2) "to cover, conceal" (see cell).
"sewing, embroidery, etc.," late 14c., from needle (n.) + work (n.)..

morphology.mnemonic: 相似/相同 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1650s, from Medieval Latin simultaneus, perhaps from simultim "at the same time," extended from Latin simul "at the same time" (see similar (adj.)), or from simul with ending abstracted from Late Latin spontaneus, where the -t- is organic.

late 15c., "act of plundering or pillaging," from Latin praedationem (nominative praedatio) "a plundering, act of taking booty," from praedari "to rob, to plunder," from praeda "plunder, booty, prey" (see prey (n.)).

early 15c., nebule "a cloud, mist," from Latin nebula "mist, vapor, fog, smoke, exhalation," figuratively "darkness, obscurity," from PIE *nebh- "cloud" (source also of Sanskrit nabhas- "vapor, cloud, mists, fog, sky;" Greek nephele, nephos "cloud;" German Nebel "fog;" Old English nifol "dark, gloomy;" Welsh niwl "cloud, fog;" Slavic nebo).

morphology.mnemonic: 石 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., "taking words in their natural meaning" (originally in reference to Scripture and opposed to mystical or allegorical), from Old French literal and directly from Late Latin literalis/litteralis "of or belonging to letters or writing," from Latin litera/littera "letter, alphabetic sign; literature, books" (see letter (n.1)).
1550s, "ominous," from Middle French prodigieux and directly from Latin prodigiosus "strange, wonderful, marvelous, unnatural," from prodigium (see prodigy).

meanings.definitionCn: 多情的；恋爱的；热情的；色情的

etymology.story: 1620s, from com- + mingle.

meanings.definitionCn: 懒惰的， 倦怠的； 不活跃的， 无行动的

etymology.story: 1660s, "causing no pain, painless," from French indolent (16c.) or directly from Late Latin indolentem (see indolence).

meanings.definitionCn: 卖主；小贩；供应商；[贸易] 自动售货机

etymology.story: 1590s, from late Anglo-French vendor, from vendre "to vend," from Latin vendere "to sell" (see vend).

meanings.definitionCn: 令人作呕的，令人厌恶的；自作多情的；淡而无味的

etymology.story: 1660s, "sickly, nauseated," from Middle English mawke "maggot" (see maggot).
"quality ascribed to someone," late 14c., from Latin attributum "anything attributed," noun use of neuter of attributus (see attribute (v.)).
early 13c., from Old French repentant "penitent" (12c.), present participle of repentir (see repent)..
mid-15c., from Old French triumpher (13c.), from Latin triumphare, from triumphus (see triumph (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: [眼科] 白内障；大瀑布；暴雨，洪水；奔流

etymology.story: early 15c., "a waterfall, floodgate," from Latin cataracta "waterfall," from Greek katarhaktes "waterfall, broken water; a kind of portcullis," noun use of an adjective compound meaning "swooping, down-rushing," from kata "down" (see cata-).
mid-15c., from Middle French ratification (14c.) and directly from Medieval Latin ratificationem (nominative ratificatio), noun of action from past participle stem of ratificare (see ratify)..

meanings.definitionCn: 矾土，[矿物] 铁铝氧石；[矿物] 铝土矿

etymology.story: 1861, clayey mineral containing aluminum, from French bauxite (1821), from Les Baux, near Soles, where it was first found.

meanings.definitionCn: 兜售；招徕；刺探赛马情报

etymology.story: 1700, thieves' cant, "to act as a lookout, spy on," from Middle English tuten "to peep, peer," probably from a variant of Old English totian "to stick out, peep, peer," from Proto-Germanic *tut- "project" (source also of Dutch tuit "sprout, snout," Middle Dutch tute "nipple, pap," Middle Low German tute "horn, funnel," Old Norse tota "teat, toe of a shoe").

meanings.definitionCn: 明显的；可感知的；易觉察的

etymology.story: late 14c., "that can be touched," from Late Latin palpabilis "that may be touched or felt," from Latin palpare "touch gently, stroke" (see feel (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 幸运的；侥幸的；吉祥的；带来幸运的

etymology.story: late 14c., "having good fortune; bringing good fortune," from Latin fortunatus "prospered, prosperous; lucky, happy," past participle of fortunare "to make prosperous," from fortuna (see fortune).

meanings.definitionCn: 旅行，在……旅游；在……作巡回演出
说服，劝说；使某人相信；劝某人做（不做）某事

etymology.story: 1510s, from Middle French persuader (14c.), from Latin persuadere "to bring over by talking," (see persuasion).

meanings.definitionCn: (意见等的)一致，一致同意，共识

etymology.story: 1854 as a term in physiology; 1861 of persons; from Latin consensus "agreement, accord," past participle of consentire (see consent (v.)).
"very demanding, severe in requirement," 1580s, present participle adjective from exact (v.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 优柔寡断的；踌躇不定的；无决断的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + determined, decided, absolute, final

etymology.story: "not firm in purpose, wavering, given to doubt or hesitation," 1570s, from Latin irresolutus "not loosed, not loosened," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + resolutus (see resolute).

meanings.definitionCn: 司法；法官；司法权；法官的职位

etymology.story: 1520s, "legal power of administering judgment," from Medieval Latin iudicatura, from iudicat-, past participle stem of Latin iudicare "to judge" (see judge (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 使成为/放入 + 战争 + 有点…/像…

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to render beautiful," from Old French embelliss-, stem of embellir "make beautiful, ornament," from assimilated form of en- (see en- (1)) + bel "beautiful," from Latin bellus "handsome, pretty, fine" (see bene-).
early 15c., "to draw (something) back," from Old French retracter (14c.) and directly from Latin retractus, past participle of retrahere "to draw back" (see retraction).

meanings.definitionCn: 小道传闻；随笔；爱说长道短的人

etymology.story: 1570s, sarcasmus, from Late Latin sarcasmus, from late Greek sarkasmos "a sneer, jest, taunt, mockery," from sarkazein "to speak bitterly, sneer," literally "to strip off the flesh," from sarx (genitive sarkos) "flesh," properly "piece of meat," from PIE root *twerk- "to cut" (source also of Avestan thwares "to cut").
1650s, from French didactique, from Greek didaktikos "apt at teaching," from didaktos "taught," past participle of didaskein "teach," from PIE root *dens- "wisdom, to teach, learn."

meanings.definitionCn: 白话的；通俗的；口语体的

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 说话 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "spoken," 1751, from colloquy "a conversation" + -al (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 金银标准成份的，货真价实的，纯正的

morphology.mnemonic: 谷物，谷粒 + 与…有关的/像…的

etymology.story: 1790, from Late Latin granulum "granule, a little grain," diminutive of Latin granum "grain, seed," from PIE root *gre-no- "grain" (see corn (n.1)) + -ar.
"foolish, stupid," 1530s, from Latin fatuus "foolish, insipid, silly;" which is of uncertain origin.

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + body体（corpor也作corp） + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: late 14c., "to put (something) into the body or substance of (something else), blend; absorb, eat," also "solidify, harden," often in medical writing, from Late Latin incorporatus, past participle of incorporare "unite into one body, embody, include," from Latin in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + verb from corpus (genitive corporis) "body" (see corporeal).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + capt + 行为/过程/结果

etymology.story: 1783, from re- "back, again" + capture (v.).

1550s, "mildness or gentleness shown in exercise of authority," from Latin clementia "calmness, gentleness," from clemens "calm, mild," related to clinare "to lean" (see lean (v.)) + participial suffix -menos (also in alumnus).

meanings.definitionCn: 祝福；赐福；恩赐；祈求上帝赐福的仪式

morphology.mnemonic: 好/善 + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: 重复， 反复； 反复地说， 重申

etymology.story: early 15c., "repeat again and again," from Late Latin reiteratus, past participle of reiterare "to repeat," from re- "again" (see re-) + iterare "to repeat," from iterum "again" (see iteration).

meanings.definitionCn: 不吉祥的， 凶兆的； 险恶的， 邪恶的

etymology.story: early 15c., "prompted by malice or ill-will, intending to mislead," from Old French senestre, sinistre "contrary, false; unfavorable; to the left" (14c.), from Latin sinister "left, on the left side" (opposite of dexter), of uncertain origin.
1540s, present participle adjective from menace (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 钟摆；摇锤；摇摆不定的事态

etymology.story: 1660, from Modern Latin pendulum (1643), noun use of neuter of Latin adjective pendulus "hanging down," from pendere "to hang" (see pendant).

meanings.definitionCn: [物化] 气溶胶；气雾剂；喷雾器；浮质

etymology.story: 1919, from aero- "air" + first syllable in solution.

morphology.mnemonic: 终止/界限 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "authoritative resolution of a matter," from Old French terminacion (13c.) and directly from Latin terminationem (nominative terminatio) "a fixing of boundaries, a bounding, determining," noun of action from past participle stem of terminare "to mark the end or boundary," from terminus "end, limit" (see terminus).
1540s, "noble, magnificent" (of buildings, etc.), from Latin superbus "grand, proud, splendid; haughty, vain, insolent," from super "above, over" (see super-).
1550s, "to return to a former state," from Latin regressus (see regress (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: du + 折叠/交织 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1620s, "to double," from Latin duplicatus, past participle of duplicare (see duplicate (adj.)).

late 15c., from Old French afable (14c.), from Latin affabilis "approachable, courteous, kind, friendly," literally "who can be (easily) spoken to," from affari "to speak to," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + fari "to speak," from PIE root *bha- (2) "to speak, tell, say" (see fame (n.)).

root.meaning: a fold in a length of cloth

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + a fold in a length of cloth

etymology.story: late 14c., "detriment, harm;" early 15c.
early 15c., legal, "a charge, a formal accusation," from Old French surmise "accusation," noun use of past participle of surmettre (see surmise (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 射线/放射 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "destroy utterly," literally "pull up by the roots," from Latin eradicatus, past participle of eradicare "to root out, annihilate" (see eradication).

meanings.definitionCn: 发芽， 生长； 发展

morphology.mnemonic: 父亲 + ot + 主义/学说/运动

etymology.story: 1726, from patriot + -ism..

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1550s, from medical Latin abdominalis, from abdomen (genitive abdominis); see abdomen..

meanings.definitionCn: 出租（马匹、马车等）；役使（hackney的过去式）

etymology.story: "trite, so overused as to have become uninteresting," 1749, figurative use of past participle adjective from hackney (v.) "use a horse for riding" (1570s), hence "make common by indiscriminate use" (1590s), from hackney (n.), and compare hack (n.2) in its specialized sense of "one who writes anything for hire." From 1769 as "kept for hire.".
1690s, from Latin despondentem (nominative despondens), present participle of despondere (see despondence).

1580s (earlier complice, late 15c.), from Old French complice "a confederate," from Late Latin complicem (nominative complex) "partner, confederate," from Latin complicare "fold together" (see complicate).

meanings.definitionCn: 位于远地点；位于最高点

etymology.story: "point at which the moon is farthest from the earth," 1590s, from French apogée, from Latin apogaeum, from Greek apogaion, neuter adjective, "away from the earth," a term from Ptolemaic astronomy, from apo "off, away" (see apo-) + gaia/ge "earth" (see Gaia).

Old English streng "line, cord, thread, string of a bow or harp," in plural "tackle, rigging; lineage, race," from Proto-Germanic *strangiz (source also of Old Norse strengr, Danish streng, Middle Dutch strenge, Dutch streng, Old High German strang, German Strang "rope, cord"), from *strang- "taut, stiff," from PIE root *strenk- "tight, narrow." Gradually restricted by early Middle English to lines that are smaller than a rope.

late 14c., "to place (oneself) under the control of another, to yield oneself," from Latin submittere "to yield, lower, let down, put under, reduce," from sub "under" (see sub-) + mittere "let go, send" (see mission).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 流动 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: mid-15c., "flowing," from Middle French affluent (14c.) or directly from Latin affluentem (nominative affluens) "flowing toward, abounding, rich, copious," present participle of affluere "flow toward," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + fluere "to flow" (see fluent)..

meanings.definitionCn: 可怕的；以死亡为主题的；令人毛骨悚然的（等于macaber）

etymology.story: early 15c., originally in reference to a kind of morality show or allegorical representation of death and his victims, from Old French (danse) Macabré "(dance) of Death" (1376), of uncertain origin, probably a translation of Medieval Latin (Chorea) Machabæorum, literally "dance of the Maccabees" (leaders of the Jewish revolt against Syro-Hellenes; see Maccabees).

meanings.definitionCn: 多余的，过剩的；被解雇的，失业的；冗长的，累赘的

etymology.story: 1590s, from Latin redundantem (nominative redundans), present participle of redundare, literally "overflow, pour over; be over-full;" figuratively "be in excess," from re- "again" (see re-) + undare "rise in waves," from unda "a wave," from PIE *unda-, nasalized form of root *wed- (1) "water, wet" (see water (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 生动的；保持原味的；适于赛跑的；猥亵的

morphology.mnemonic: worn out, ground to pieces, + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: 声明；陈述，叙述；报表，清单

etymology.story: early 14c., "unfortunate, disastrous," probably from mischief + -ous.
1650s, from dumb (adj.) + ending from confound..
1847, "pertaining to the ear," from Latin auris "ear" (see ear (n.1)) + -al (1).

etymology.story: 1660s, from Greek baros "weight," from suffixed form of PIE root *gwere- (2) "heavy" (see grave (adj.)) + -meter.

morphology.mnemonic: tri + 年 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1630s, "lasting three years;" 1640s, "occurring every three years," with -al (1) + Latin triennium "three-year period," from tri- "three" (see tri-) + annus "year" (see annual).

meanings.definitionCn: 感觉,知觉;激动,轰动,轰动一时的事情

meanings.definitionCn: [光][天] 亮度；聪明，活泼；鲜艳；愉快

late 14c., "to trace back (to a first cause), attribute, assign," from Old French referer (14c.) and directly from Latin referre "to relate, refer," literally "to carry back," from re- "back" (see re-) + ferre "carry" (see infer).

meanings.definitionCn: 星的；星球的；主要的；一流的

etymology.story: 1650s, "pertaining to stars, star-like," from Late Latin stellaris "pertaining to a star, starry," from stella "star" (see star (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 火焰， 火苗；热情；光辉

etymology.story: Middle English flaumen, also flaumben, flomben, flamben, flamen, flammen, c.

1590s, "one who takes possession of something having no owner," from Middle French occupant (15c.) or directly from Latin occupantem (nominative occupans), present participle of occupare "to take possession of" (see occupy).

meanings.definitionCn: 排印；[印刷] 活版印刷术；印刷格式

1560s, "having moderate self-regard," from Middle French modeste (14c.), from Latin modestus "keeping due measure" (see modesty).

1660s, from Modern Latin miasma "noxious vapors," from Greek miasma (genitive miasmatos) "stain, pollution, defilement, taint of guilt," from stem of miainein "to pollute," from possible PIE root *mai- "to stain, soil, defile" (source of Old English mal "stain, mark," see mole (n.1)).

early 13c., from Old French broche "long needle" (see broach (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: ambi + 强壮/价值 + …的人/处于…状态

meanings.definitionCn: 可能性；机率；[数] 或然率

"to bully," originally "to bear down with stern or arrogant looks," 1580s, from brow + beat (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 明确的；清楚的；直率的；详述的

mid-15c., "proper character, disposition," from Old French proprieté "individuality, peculiarity; property" (12c.), from Latin proprietatem (nominative proprietas) "appropriateness," also "ownership" (see property).

meanings.definitionCn: 使困窘；使羞愧；使局促不安

1720, "an outstanding intellect," from master (n.) + mind (n.).

early 15c., "to keep wet; to soak, saturate;" also figuratively "to cause to absorb" (feelings, opinions, etc.), from Latin imbuere "moisten, wet, soak, saturate," figuratively "to fill; to taint," a word of uncertain origin, perhaps from the same root as imbrication.
1794, from French utiliser, from Italian utilizzare, from utile "usable," from Latin utilis "usable," from uti "make use of, profit by, take advantage of" (see use (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 空想家，理想主义者；唯心主义者

1610s, past participle adjective from elate..
"to divide in portions," early 14c., from Old French porcioner "share out, divide in portions," from porcion (see portion (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 沉思的，忧郁的；悲伤的，哀愁的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French pensif "thoughtful, distracted, musing" (11c.), from penser "to think," from Latin pensare "weigh, consider," frequentative of pendere "weigh" (see pendant).

1913, literally "having the same place," from Greek isos "equal" (see iso-) + topos "place" (see topos); so called because, despite having different atomic weights, the various forms of an element occupy the same place on the periodic table.
mid-14c., clother; late 15c., clothyer (late 13c.

"extremely small," mid-15c., from earlier noun use in sense of "quantity, amount" (such as a littel wei "a little thing or amount," c.

etymology.story: 1550s, "given to laughter," from Middle French risible (14c.) and directly from Late Latin risibilis "laughable, able to laugh," from Latin risus, past participle of ridere "to laugh," a word which, according to de Vaan, "has no good PIE etymology." Meaning "capable of exciting laughter, comical" is from 1727..

mid-14c., from Old French eschiver "shun, eschew, avoid, dispense with," from Frankish *skiuhan "dread, avoid, shun," from Proto-Germanic *skeukhwaz (source also of Old High German sciuhen "to avoid, escape," German scheuen "to fear, shun, shrink from," scheu "shy, timid"); see shy (adj.).
1640s, from French mêlée, from Old French meslee "brawl, confused fight; mixture, blend" (12c.), noun use of fem.
late 15c., "to heap up for oneself," from Old French amasser, from à "to" (see ad-) + masser (see mass (n.1)).

1775, "a remembrance or memory," from French souvenir (12c.), from Old French noun use of souvenir (v.) "to remember, come to mind," from Latin subvenire "come to mind," from sub- "up from below" (see sub-) + venire "to come" (see venue).

meanings.definitionCn: 神学院；学校；发源地；高级中学

late 14c., from Latin circumferentia, neuter plural of circumferens, present participle of circumferre "to lead around, take around, carry around," from circum "around" (see circum-) + ferre "to carry" (see infer).

1590s, "pertaining to management of a household," perhaps shortened from economical, or else from French économique or directly from Latin oeconomicus "of domestic economy," from Greek oikonomikos "practiced in the management of a household or family" (also the name of a treatise by Xenophon on the duties of domestic life), hence, "frugal, thrifty," from oikonomia "household management" (see economy (n.)).
1660s, "that which indicates or points out," from Late Latin indicator, agent noun from indicare "to point out, show" (see indication).

"very thin sheet of metal," early 14c., foile, from Old French foil, fueill, fueille "leaf; foliage; sheet of paper; sheet of metal" (12c., Modern French fueille), from Latin folia, plural (mistaken for fem.

etymology.story: mid-13c., from Old French respit "delay, respect" (Modern French répit), from Latin respectus "consideration, recourse, regard" (see respect (n.))..

mid-15c., from late Old English batswegen, from bat "boat" (see boat (n.)) + Old Norse sveinn "boy" (see swain).

"narrow, strict" (late 13c.), from Old French estreit, estrait "tight, close-fitting, constricted, narrow" (Modern French étroit), from Latin strictus, past participle of stringere (2) "bind or draw tight" (see strain (v.)).

etymology.story: 1540s, in mathematics, from Medieval Latin denominator, agent noun from past participle stem of denominare (see denomination)..

mid-15c., "full of pride," from Latin fastidiosus "disdainful, squeamish, exacting," from fastidium "loathing, squeamishness; dislike, aversion; excessive nicety," which is of uncertain origin; perhaps from *fastu-taidiom, a compound of fastus "contempt, arrogance, pride," and taedium "aversion, disgust." Fastus is possibly from PIE *bhars- (1) "projection, bristle, point," on the notion of "prickliness" (Watkins) or "a semantic shift from 'top' to 'haughtiness' which is conceivable, but the
u-stem is not attested independently" [de Vaan], who adds that "fastidium would be a tautology." Early use in English was both in passive and active senses.

1530s, from a- (1) + Middle English loof "weather gage," also "windward direction," probably from Dutch loef (Middle Dutch lof) "the weather side of a ship." Originally a nautical order to keep the ship's head to the wind, thus to stay clear of a lee-shore or some other quarter; hence the figurative sense of "at a distance, apart" (1580s).
1786, "make ideal, consider as ideal," probably formed from ideal (adj.) + -ize.

1660s, from Latin stagnatum, stagnatus, past participle of stagnare "to stagnate," from stagnatum "standing water, pond, swamp," perhaps from a PIE root *stag- "to seep drip" (source also of Greek stazein "to ooze, drip;" see stalactite).

1590s, "pertaining to love" (especially sexual love), from Latin amatorius "loving," from amatus, past participle of amare (see Amy)..
1590s, from Latin translucentem (nominative translucens), present participle of translucere "to shine through," from trans- "through" (see trans-) + lucere "to shine" (see light (n.)).

"morally unrestrained," 1530s, from Medieval Latin licentiosus "full of license, unrestrained," from Latin licentia (see license (n.)).

early 15c., proccy, prokecye, "agency of one who acts instead of another; letter of power of attorney," contraction of Anglo-French procuracie (c.

late 14c., "venomous substance," from Latin virus "poison, sap of plants, slimy liquid, a potent juice," probably from PIE root *weis- "to melt away, to flow," used of foul or malodorous fluids, with specialization in some languages to "poisonous fluid" (source also of Sanskrit visam "poison," visah "poisonous;" Avestan vish- "poison;" Latin viscum "sticky substance, birdlime;" Greek ios "poison," ixos "mistletoe, birdlime;" Old Church Slavonic višnja "cherry;" Old Irish fi "poison;" Welsh gwy "fluid, water," gwyar "blood").

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin deductus, past participle of deducere "lead down, bring away;" see deduce, with which it formerly was interchangeable.

meanings.definitionCn: 似是而非的；外表美观的；华而不实的；徒有其表的

etymology.story: late 14c., "pleasing to the sight, fair," from Latin speciosus "good-looking, beautiful, fair," also "showy, pretended, plausible, specious," from species "appearance, form, figure, beauty" (see species).

meanings.definitionCn: 贪婪的；贪吃的；狼吞虎咽的

etymology.story: 1630s, formed as an adjectival form of voracity.
late 14c., "to form a mental picture (of something), imagine," from Old French imagier, from image (see image (n.)).

1590s (implied in tersely), "clean-cut, burnished, neat," from French ters "clean," and directly from Latin tersus "wiped off, clean, neat," from past participle of tergere "to rub, polish, wipe." Sense of "concise or pithy in style or language" is from 1777, which led to a general sense of "neatly concise." The pejorative meaning "brusque" is a fairly recent development.

meanings.definitionCn: 苍白的；暗淡的；无生气的

etymology.story: "lacking color," 1580s, from Latin pallidus "pale, colorless," from root of pallere "be pale" (see pallor)..
early 15c., "description of a place," from Late Latin topographia, from Greek topographia "a description of a place," from topos "place" (see topos) + -graphia (see -graphy).
early 15c., in reference to certain eminent justices of the peace, from Latin quorum "of whom," genitive plural (masc.

meanings.definitionCn: 描写，描述；画(人物、景象等)

etymology.story: mid-13c., "to draw, paint" (something), from Anglo-French purtraire, Old French portraire "to draw, to paint, portray" (12c.), literally "trace, draw forth," from por- "forth" (from Latin pro-; see pro-) + traire "trace, draw," from Latin trahere "to drag, draw" (see tract (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 费解的；无法说明的；不能解释的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French inexplicable or directly from Latin inexplicabilis "that cannot be unfolded or disentangled, very intricate," figuratively, "inexplicable," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + explicabilis "that may be explained" (see explicable).
late Old English rudig "rubicund," probably from rudu "redness," related to read "red" (see red (adj.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + front + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1630s, "action of bringing two parties face to face," from Medieval Latin confrontationem (nominative confrontatio), noun of action from past participle stem of confrontare (see confront).

1837, "article of food," from French comestible (14c.), from Late Latin comestibilis, from Latin comestus, past participle of comedere "eat up, consume," from com- "thoroughly" (see com-) + edere "to eat" (see edible).

meanings.definitionCn: 深厚的；意义深远的；渊博的
同种类的，同性质的，有相同特征的

etymology.story: 1640s, from Medieval Latin homogeneus, from Greek homogenes "of the same kind," from homos "same" (see homo- (1)) + genos "kind, gender, race, stock" (see genus).
1660s, from Modern Latin penumbra "partial shadow outside the complete shadow of an eclipse," coined 1604 by Kepler from Latin pæne "almost" + umbra "shadow" (see umbrage).

early 14c., from Old French usurper "to (wrongfully) appropriate" (14c.), from Latin usurpare "make use of, seize for use," in later Latin "to assume unlawfully, trespass on," from usus "a use" (see use (v.)) + rapere "to seize" (see rapid (adj.)).

"attack," 1714, back-formation from aggression, but used earlier with a sense of "approach" (1570s) and in this sense from French aggresser, from Late Latin aggressare, frequentative of Latin aggredi "to approach, attack."

meanings.definitionCn: 宽恕， 赦免； 纵容， 容忍

etymology.story: 1857, from Latin condonare "to give up, remit, permit," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + donare "to give" (see donation).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 呼吸 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程
用 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: late 14c., "constraining power of circumstances," from Old French necessité "need, necessity; privation, poverty; distress, torment; obligation, duty" (12c.), from Latin necessitatem (nominative necessitas) "compulsion, need for attention, unavoidableness, destiny," from necesse (see necessary).
1640s, "weak point of a sword blade" (contrasted to forte), from French foible "a weak point, a weakness, failing," from noun use of Old French adjective feble "feeble" (see feeble).

meanings.definitionCn: 中止，停顿；归属待定，暂搁

etymology.story: 1520s, from Anglo-French abeiance "suspension," also "expectation (especially in a lawsuit)," from Old French abeance "aspiration, desire," noun of condition of abeer "aspire after, gape" from à "at" (see ad-) + ba(y)er "be open," from Latin *batare "to yawn, gape" (see abash).

meanings.definitionCn: 引人注目的；强制的；激发兴趣的
强迫；以强力获得（compel的ing形式）

etymology.story: "calm, quiet, peaceful," 1540s, in halcyon dayes (translating Latin alcyonei dies, Greek alkyonides hemerai), 14 days of calm weather at the winter solstice, when a mythical bird (also identified with the kingfisher) was said to breed in a nest floating on calm seas.

morphology.mnemonic: 呼叫/声音 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., "spoken, oral," from Old French vocal (13c.), from Latin vocalis "sounding, sonorous, speaking," as a noun, "a vowel," from vox (genitive vocis) "voice" (see voice (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + caut + 状态/行为/过程
person + 与…有关的/形容词 + ity

etymology.story: late 14c., "quality or fact of being a person," from Medieval Latin personalitatem (nominative personalitas), from Late Latin personalis (see personal).

1570s, from Middle French subterfuge (14c.) or directly from Medieval Latin subterfugium "an evasion," from Latin subterfugere "to evade, escape, flee by stealth," from subter "beneath, below;" in compounds "secretly" (from PIE *sup-ter-, suffixed (comparative) form of *(s)up-; see sub-) + fugere "flee" (see fugitive (adj.))..
"estimated value or worth," early 15c., from Old French rate "price, value" and directly from Medieval Latin rata (pars) "fixed (amount)," from Latin rata "fixed, settled," fem.

morphology.mnemonic: 神圣 + 状态/行为/过程
向下/移除/取消 + 引导

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin deducere "lead down, derive" (in Medieval Latin, "infer logically"), from de- "down" (see de-) + ducere "to lead" (see duke (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 荒废的；即将过时的；逐渐被废弃的

etymology.story: 1755, from Latin obsolescentum (nominative obsolescens), present participle of obsolescere "fall into disuse" (see obsolete)..

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 时间 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: "made, done, procured, or furnished 'at the time,'" hence "unpremeditated," 1650s, from Medieval Latin extemporaneus, from Latin ex tempore (see extempore).

Old English ac "oak tree," from Proto-Germanic *aiks (source also of Old Norse eik, Old Saxon and Old Frisian ek, Middle Dutch eike, Dutch eik, Old High German eih, German Eiche), of uncertain origin with no certain cognates outside Germanic.

meanings.definitionCn: 谚语，格言；众所周知的人或事

mid-15c., from Old French contendre, from Latin contendere "to stretch out, strive after," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + tendere "to stretch" (see tenet).

meanings.definitionCn: 隐藏处；树丛；蔽覆羽翮基部的羽毛
隐蔽的；隐密的；偷偷摸摸的；在丈夫保护下的
赌博；孤注一掷；投机；打赌

etymology.story: "an awkward action; awkwardness," 1798, from French gaucherie, from gauche (see gauche)..

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 携带

etymology.story: 1889, from luminescence + -ent..
"go against," late 14c., from Old French countre "facing opposite" (see counter-).

root.meaning: to come over the top of

morphology.mnemonic: to come over the top of + 使变成/变得

etymology.story: 1580s, past participle adjective, but the verb crestfall is recorded only from 1610s, in reference to diseased horses, and is rare.

meanings.definitionCn: 圆筒；汽缸；[数] 柱面；圆柱状物

etymology.story: 1560s, from Middle French cylindre (14c.), from Latin cylindrus "roller, cylinder," from Greek kylindros "a cylinder, roller, roll," from kylindein "to roll," which is of unknown origin..
"treat with sedatives," 1945, a back-formation from the noun derivative of sedative (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 提升， 晋级； 促进， 增进； 发起， 发扬； 宣传， 推销

etymology.story: 1520s, "distorted, somewhat twisted to one side," from obsolete verb wry "to contort, to twist or turn," from Old English wrigian "to turn, bend, move, go," from Proto-Germanic *wrig- (source also of Old Frisian wrigia "to bend," Middle Low German wrich "turned, twisted"), from PIE *wreik- "to turn" (source also of Greek rhoikos "crooked," Lithuanian raisas "paralysed"), from root *wer- (3) "to turn, bend" (see versus).

meanings.definitionCn: 简单的；坦率的；明确的；径直的

etymology.story: 1550s, "directly forward, right ahead," from straight (adj.1) + forward (adv.).

morphology.mnemonic: 穿越/转变 + 写 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1590s, from Middle French transcription, from Late Latin transcriptionem (nominative transcriptio), noun of action from past participle stem of transcribere (see transcribe).

meanings.definitionCn: 憔悴；凋萎；失去活力；苦思

"to put into form of a table, collect or arrange in columns," 1734, from Latin tabula (see table (n.)) + -ate (2).
1590s, from French capricieux "whimsical" (16c.), from Italian capriccioso, from capriccio (see caprice).

meanings.definitionCn: 立法的，有立法权的；根据法规执行的；立法机关的

etymology.story: 1640s; from legislator + -ive.

meanings.definitionCn: 抄写， 誊写； 打印； 转录

etymology.story: 1550s, from Latin transcribere "to copy, write again in another place, write over, transfer," from trans- "over" (see trans-) + scribere "write" (see script (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 疯狂的；精神错乱的；愚蠢的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin intricatus "entangled," past participle of intricare "to entangle, perplex, embarrass," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + tricae (plural) "perplexities, hindrances, toys, tricks," a word of uncertain origin (compare extricate).

meanings.definitionCn: 宽敞的；广阔的；容积大的

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 课文，本文，正文

1640s, from abide + -ance..

morphology.mnemonic: language语言 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1640s, from Medieval Latin lingualis "of the tongue," from Latin lingua "tongue," also "speech, language," from Old Latin dingua, from PIE *dnghu- "tongue" (source also of Old English tunge, Gothic tuggo "tongue;" see tongue (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之下 + 站立/设置 + ance

etymology.story: early 15c., "existence, independence," from Late Latin subsistentia "substance, reality," in Medieval Latin also "stability," from Latin subsistens, present participle of subsistere "stand still or firm" (see subsist).

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 投/掷

mid-15c., from Medieval Latin audacitas "boldness," from Latin audacis genitive of audax (see audacious)..

morphology.mnemonic: 自我 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1660s, "absolute sovereignty," from Greek autarkhia, from autarkhein "to be an absolute ruler," from autos "self" (see auto-) + arkhein "to rule" (see archon)..

morphology.mnemonic: germ + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Latin germinationem (nominative germinatio) "a sprouting forth, budding," noun of action from past participle stem of germinare "to sprout, put forth shoots," from germen (genitive germinis) "a sprout or bud" (see germ)..

meanings.definitionCn: 适于居住的， 可居住的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French habitable "suitable for human dwelling" (14c.), from Latin habitabilis "that is fit to live in," from habitare "to live, inhabit, dwell," frequentative of habere "to have, to hold, possess" (see habit (n.))..

"to slander," mid-15c., from earlier more literal sense of "to plot, to contrive" (early 15c.), from Old French malignier "to plot, deceive, pervert," from Late Latin malignare "to do maliciously," from malignus (see malign (adj.)).
late 14c., "lamenting," from Old French plaintif "complaining; wretched, miserable," from plainte (see plaint).

meanings.definitionCn: 活泼的；生动的；真实的；生气勃勃的

etymology.story: Old English liflic "living, existing," literally "life-like;" from life + -ly (2).

late 14c., "capable of being shaped by hammering," from Middle French malleable and directly from Medieval Latin malleabilis, from malleare "to beat with a hammer," from Latin malleus "hammer" (see mallet).

meanings.definitionCn: 增补，补充；补充物；增刊，副刊

etymology.story: late 14c., "action of altering," from Old French alteracion (14c.) "change, alteration," and directly from Medieval Latin alterationem (nominative alteratio), noun of action from past participle stem of Late Latin alterare (see alter).

late 14c., britel, perhaps from an unrecorded Old English adjective *brytel, related to brytan "to crush, pound, to break to pieces," from Proto-Germanic stem *brutila- "brittle," from *breutan "to break up" (source also of Old Norse brjota "to break," Old High German brodi "fragile"), from PIE *bhreu- "to cut, break up" (see bruise (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 密切关系；吸引力；姻亲关系；类同
吉卜赛人；吉卜赛语；像吉布赛的人；歌舞剧中的歌舞队员
象吉卜赛人的；吉卜赛人的；无照的

etymology.story: 1560s, "base supporting a column, statue, etc.," from Middle French piédestal (1540s), from Italian piedistallo "base of a pillar," from pie "foot" + di "of" + stallo "stall, place, seat," from a Germanic source (see stall (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: lively, brisk, in good spirits + ity
太阳/独自 + 状态/品质

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French solicitude (Modern French sollicitude), and directly from Latin sollicitudinem (nominative solicitudo) "anxiety, uneasiness of mind," noun of state from past participle stem of solicitare (see solicit)..

mid-14c., amonesten "remind, urge, exhort, warn, give warning," from Old French amonester (12c.) "urge, encourage, warn," from Vulgar Latin *admonestare, from Latin admonere "bring to mind, remind, suggest;" also "warn, advise, urge," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + monere "advise, warn" (see monitor (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 不确定地，无限期地；模糊地，不明确地

meanings.definitionCn: 发生,出现;事件,事故,发生的事情

morphology.mnemonic: to come into one's mind + ance

etymology.story: 1530s, from Middle French occurrence "unexpected happening" or directly from Medieval Latin occurrentia, from Latin occurentem (nominative occurens), present participle of occurrere (see occur)..

meanings.definitionCn: 退回,后退;收回,撤回;跌落,缩减,贬值

late 14c., from Latin literatura/litteratura "learning, a writing, grammar," originally "writing formed with letters," from litera/littera "letter" (see letter (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 包围；放入球内；使…成球形

"assimilate food in bowels," late 14c., from Latin digestus (see digest (n.)).

late 14c., earlier abime (c.

late 12c., bileave, replacing Old English geleafa "belief, faith," from West Germanic *ga-laubon "to hold dear, esteem, trust" (source also of Old Saxon gilobo, Middle Dutch gelove, Old High German giloubo, German Glaube), from *galaub- "dear, esteemed," from intensive prefix *ga- + *leubh- "to care, desire, like, love" (see love (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 再演唱的要求；经要求而再唱

meanings.definitionCn: 繁茂的， 丰富的； 非凡的； 华而不实的

1580s, back-formation from proficiency or else from Old French proficient (15c.), from Latin proficientem (nominative proficiens), present participle of proficere "to make progress, go forward, effect, accomplish, be useful" (see proficiency).

meanings.definitionCn: 回想，回忆；纪念品；记忆力

etymology.story: Middle English halven, halfen "to divide in halves" (c.

1580s, "subject to the authority of another," from Latin obnoxiosus "hurtful, injurious," from obnoxius "subject, exposed to harm," from ob "to, toward" (see ob-) + noxa "injury, hurt, damage entailing liability" (see noxious).
mid-14c., from Old French pénitent (14c.) and directly from Latin paenitentem (see penitence).
"wordy," 1670s, from Latin verbosus "full of words, wordy," from verbum "word" (see verb).

1550s, "goal, end, final point," from Latin terminus (plural termini) "end, boundary line," from PIE *ter-men-, from root *ter-, base of words meaning "peg, post, boundary, marker, goal" (source also of Sanskrit tarati "passes over, crosses over," tarantah "sea;" Hittite tarmaizzi "he limits;" Greek terma "boundary, end, limit;" Gothic þairh, Old English þurh "through;" Old English þyrel "hole;" Old Norse þrömr "edge, chip, splinter").

1640s, "to be equivalent;" 1650s, "to counterbalance, make up for," from Latin compensatus, past participle of compensare "to weigh one thing (against another)," thus, "to counterbalance," from com- "with" (see com-) + pensare, frequentative of pendere "to weigh" (see pendant).

meanings.definitionCn: 专员，公使；使馆随员；大使馆专员

meanings.definitionCn: 意识到的；故意的；神志清醒的
拖拉的；缓慢的，不慌不忙的

etymology.story: late 15c., from Late Latin dilatorius, from dilator "procrastinator," from dilatus, serving as past participle of differe "delay" (see defer)..

1590s, "criticism, blame," also sometimes in early use simply "notice, attention" (now obsolete), from Latin animadversionem (nominative animadversio) "investigation, inquiry; perception, observation," noun of action from past participle stem of animadverte "to take cognizance of," literally "to turn the mind to," from animum, accusative of animus "mind" (see animus), + advertere "to turn to" (see advertise).

mid-15c., a back-formation from prodigality, or else from Middle French prodigal and directly from Late Latin prodigalis, from Latin prodigus "wasteful," from prodigere "drive away, waste," from pro- "forth" (see pro-) + agere "to drive" (see act (n.)).
1620s, from French placide (15c.) and directly from Latin placidus "pleasing, peaceful, quiet, gentle, still, calm," from placere "to please" (see please).
1580s, originally in slang phrase to make a coax of, from earlier noun coax, cox, cokes "a fool, ninny, simpleton" (1560s); modern spelling is 1706.
mid-15c., from Old French contentment, from contenter (see content (v.))..

1530s, "to make very happy," from Middle French béatifer, from Late Latin beatificare "make happy, make blessed," from Latin beatus "supremely happy, blessed" (past participle of beare "make happy, bless") + -ficare, from stem of facere "to make, do" (see factitious).
early 15c., from Old French laudable "praiseworthy, glorious" and directly from Latin laudabilis "praiseworthy," from laudare "to praise, commend, extol" (see laud).

meanings.definitionCn: 使锐利；将…开刃；给…加上边
兄弟关系；手足情谊；四海之内皆兄弟的信念

etymology.story: equivalent of Old English broþerrede "fellowship, brotherhood," with ending as in kindred; in early Middle English the word was brotherhede with ending as in maidenhead.

meanings.definitionCn: 普遍的，广泛的；分布广的

etymology.story: also wide-spread, 1705, from wide + past participle of spread (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 古老的，年代久远的；过时的，古董的；古风的，古式的

[生物] 无性的；无性生殖的

root.meaning: quality of being male or female

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + quality of being male or female + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1830, as a term in biology, a hybrid from a- (2) "not" + sexual.
1530s "gloomy," from Latin morosus "morose, peevish, hypercritical, fastidious," from mos (genitive moris) "habit, custom" (see moral (adj.)).

1660s, from French toxique and directly from Late Latin toxicus "poisoned," from Latin toxicum "poison," from Greek toxikon (pharmakon) "(poison) for use on arrows," from toxikon, neuter of toxikos "pertaining to arrows or archery," and thus to a bow, from toxon "bow," probably from a Scythian word that also was borrowed into Latin as taxus "yew." Watkins suggests a possible source in Iranian taxša- "bow," from PIE *tekw- "to run, flee." As a noun from 1890..

morphology.mnemonic: mineral consisting of fossilized carbon + ance
共同/联合 + 见证/证明

etymology.story: 1520s, of uncertain origin, perhaps an alteration of Middle French tremouille "mill hopper," in reference to the hopper's constant motion to and fro, from Latin trimodia "vessel containing three modii," from modius, a Roman dry measure, related to modus "measure." Attested earlier in English as a verb (1510s), though this now is obsolete..
1909, from Modern Latin organella, a diminutive from Latin organum "instrument," in Medieval Latin "organ of the body" (see organ)..

morphology.mnemonic: dia + 知道/认识 + 过程/状态

etymology.story: 1680s, medical Latin application of Greek diagnosis "a discerning, distinguishing," from stem of diagignoskein "discern, distinguish," literally "to know thoroughly," from dia- "apart" (see dia-) + gignoskein "to learn" (see gnostic)..

meanings.definitionCn: 教条主义的；空谈理论的
教条主义者；空论家，纯理论家

etymology.story: 1820, from French doctrinaire "impractical person," originally "adherent of doctrines" (14c.), from Latin doctrina (see doctrine).

1570s, the oral rendering of the arithmetical sign +, from Latin plus "more, in greater number, more often" (comparative of multus "much"), altered (by influence of minus) from *pleos, from PIE *pele- (1) "to fill" (see poly-).

meanings.definitionCn: 夷为平地；破坏；消除；拆毁

etymology.story: 1540s, alteration of racen "pull or knock down" (a building or town), from earlier rasen (14c.) "to scratch, slash, scrape, erase," from Old French raser "to scrape, shave" (see rase).

morphology.mnemonic: 不/分离 + 发送 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1806, formed on model of refusal, etc., from dismiss + -al (2); replacing earlier dismission (1540s)..

1580s (implied in squandering), "to spend recklessly or prodigiously," of unknown origin; Shakespeare used it in "Merchant of Venice" (1593) with a sense of "to be scattered over a wide area." Squander-bug, a British symbol of reckless extravagance and waste during war-time shortages, represented as a devilish insect, was introduced 1943.
early 15c., "line around a figure or surface," from Latin perimetros, from Greek perimetron "circumference," from peri-  "around" (see peri-) + metron "measure" (see meter (n.2)).

root.meaning: to act so as to call up the idea of (something else)

morphology.mnemonic: to act so as to call up the idea of (something else) + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1630s, "conveying a hint," from suggest + -ive.

early 15c., retreve, originally in reference to dogs finding lost game, from Middle French retruev-, stem of Old French retreuver (Modern French retrouver) "find again, recover, meet again, recognize," from re- "again" (see re-) + trouver "to find," probably from Vulgar Latin *tropare "to compose" (see trove).

meanings.definitionCn: 大合唱；（意）清唱剧；康塔塔（一种声乐套曲）

etymology.story: 1724, from Italian cantata, literally "that which is sung," past participle of cantare "to sing" (see chant (v.))..
1630s, from French thermomètre (1620s), coined by Jesuit Father Jean Leuréchon from Greek thermos "hot" (see thermal) + metron "measure" (see meter (n.2)).
late 15c., from a- (1) + thwart..
Old English hætan "to make hot; to become hot," from Proto-Germanic *haita- (see heat (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 取代；置换；转移；把…免职；排水

etymology.story: 1550s, from Middle French desplacer (15c.), from des- (see dis-) + placer "to place."

meanings.definitionCn: 营养物；促进生长的东西

morphology.mnemonic: 营养 + 结果/过程/状态

1782, back-formation from swindler "cheater."

suffix.meaning: thing or animal spoken about before.

morphology.mnemonic: veri + thing or animal spoken about before. + able

late 14c., "beyond estimation or measure, not to be computed," from Old French inestimable "priceless" (14c.) or directly from Latin inaestimabilis "invaluable, incalculable," also "not estimable, valueless," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + aestimabilis "valuable, estimable," from aestimare (see esteem (v.)).

"organic poison," especially one produced by bacteria in an animal body, 1886, from toxic + -in (2)..

meanings.definitionCn: 简短介绍， 简要汇报

etymology.story: 1630s, perhaps a back-formation from devastation.

etymology.story: late 15c., from Middle French prophétique (15c.) and directly from Late Latin propheticus, from Greek prophetikos "pertaining to a prophet, oracular," from prophetes (see prophet).
early 13c., "fragrant substance," from Latin aroma "sweet odor," from Greek aroma "seasoning, any spice or sweet herb," which is of unknown origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 改变； 变化， 变形， 变质

etymology.story: Old English wræstan "to twist, wrench," from Proto-Germanic *wraistjan (source of Old Norse reista "to bend, twist"), from PIE  *wreik- "to turn" (see wry).

mid-14c., "decision, sentence," from Old French déterminacion (14c.) "determination, settlement, definition," from Latin determinationem (nominative determinatio) "conclusion, boundary," noun of action from past participle stem of determinare "to enclose, bound, set limits to" (see determine).

1660s, from Latin calculus "reckoning, account," originally "pebble used as a reckoning counter," diminutive of calx (genitive calcis) "limestone" (see chalk (n.)).

early 15c., from Latin antecedere "to go before" (see antecedent).

Old English searian (intransitive) "dry up, to wither," from Proto-Germanic *saurajan (source also of Middle Dutch soor "dry," Old High German soren "become dry"), from root of sear "dried up, withered" (see sere).

1530s, "not capable of sin," from Middle French impeccable (15c.) or directly from Late Latin impeccabilis "not liable to sin," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + peccare "to sin" (see peccadillo).

morphology.mnemonic: 错误地/不当地 + 忠诚的，真实的

1756, "military quarters," from French cantonnement, from  cantonner "to divide into cantons" (14c.), from canton (see canton).

Old English cwealm (West Saxon) "death, murder, slaughter; disaster; plague; torment," utcualm (Anglian) "utter destruction," probably related to cwellan "to kill, murder, execute," cwelan "to die" (see quell).

"bucket," late Old English scutel "dish, platter," from Latin scutella "serving platter" (source also of French écuelle, Spanish escudilla, Italian scudella "a plate, bowl"), diminutive of scutra "flat tray, dish," perhaps related to scutum "shield" (see hide (n.1)).

late 14c., from Anglo-French enseurer, from en- "make" (see en- (1)) + Old French seur "sure" (see sure); probably influenced by Old French asseurer "assure."

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 知道 + ance

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French prescience (13c.) and directly from Late Latin praescientia "fore-knowledge," from *praescientem, present participle of *praescire "to know in advance," from Latin prae "before" (see pre-) + scire "to know" (see science)..

meanings.definitionCn: 贪污的；用金钱买得的；贿赂的；贪赃枉法的

etymology.story: 1650s, "capable of being obtained for a price; that can be corrupted;" 1660s, "offered for sale," from French vénal, Old French venel "for sale" (of prostitutes, etc.; 12c.), from Latin venalis "for sale, to be sold; capable of being bribed," from venum (nominative *venus) "for sale," from PIE root *wes- (1) "to buy, sell" (source also of Sanskrit vasnah "purchase money," vasnam "reward," vasnayati "he bargains, haggles;" Greek onos "price paid, purchase," oneisthai "to buy").

morphology.mnemonic: 使成为/放入 + treat + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: mid-15c., "treatment; negotiation;" see entreat + -y (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 传染病；流行病；风尚等的流行

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Latin alacritatem (nominative alacritas) "liveliness, ardor, eagerness," from alacer (genitive alacris) "cheerful, brisk, lively;" which is of uncertain origin, perhaps cognate with Gothic aljan "zeal," Old English ellen "courage, zeal, strength," Old High German ellian..

meanings.definitionCn: 塑性，可塑性；适应性；柔软性

etymology.story: 1782, from plastic + -ity..

meanings.definitionCn: 无政府状态；混乱；无秩序

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 统治/首领 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1530s, from French anarchie or directly from Medieval Latin anarchia, from Greek anarkhia "lack of a leader, the state of people without a government" (in Athens, used of the Year of Thirty Tyrants, 404 B.C., when there was no archon), noun of state from anarkhos "rulerless," from an- "without" (see an- (1)) + arkhos "leader" (see archon)..
1560s, from French équidistant (14c.), from Late Latin aequidistantem (nominative aequidistans), from aequi- (see equal (adj.)) + distans (see distant).
"genetic constitution of an individual," 1910, from German Genotypus (Wilhelm Johannsen, 1909); see gene + type (n.).
Old English ærcebiscop, from Late Latin archiepiscopus, from Greek arkhi- "chief" (see archon) + episkopos "bishop," literally "overseer." Replaced earlier Old English heah biscop (see bishop).

meanings.definitionCn: 暗的,朦胧的;模糊的,晦涩的
被发现的东西；收藏的东西

etymology.story: 1888, from treasure trove (c.

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 拿/领会

meanings.definitionCn: 机敏的;有独创性的;精致的;精巧制成的

etymology.story:
early 15c., "intellectual, talented," from Middle French ingénieux "clever, ingenious" (Old French engeignos), from Latin ingeniosus "of good natural capacity, full of intellect, clever, gifted with genius," from ingenium "innate qualities, ability; inborn character," literally "that which is inborn," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + gignere, from PIE *gen(e)-yo-, suffixed form of root 
*gen- "to give birth, beget, produce" (see genus).

meanings.definitionCn: 人口， 全体居民； 平民， 大众

etymology.story: 1570s, from Middle French populace (16c.), from Italian popolaccio "riffraff, rabble," from popolo "people" (from Latin populus "people;" see people (n.)) + pejorative suffix -accio..

meanings.definitionCn: 两栖的； 水陆两用的

etymology.story: 1640s, from Latinized form of Greek amphibios "having a double life" (see amphibian).

meanings.definitionCn: 印记；痕迹；特征；版本说明

root.meaning: to set a mark on any surface

morphology.mnemonic: in + to set a mark on any surface

etymology.story: mid-15c., emprente "an imprint or mark, impression made by printing or stamping," from Old French empreinte "mark, impression, imprint" (see imprint (v.)).

1590s, "act of appointing," from Latin destinationem (nominative destinatio) "purpose, design," from past participle stem of destinare "determine, appoint, choose, make firm or fast," from de- "completely, formally" (see de-) + -stinare (related to stare "to stand") from PIE *ste-no-, from root *sta- "to stand" (see stet).

meanings.definitionCn: (不受干扰的)独处，自由，隐私；私生活

etymology.story: 1590s, "a private matter, a secret;" c.

1560s, with -al (1) + stem of Latin reciprocus "returning the same way, alternating," from pre-Latin *reco-proco-, from *recus (from re- "back;" see re-, + -cus, adjective formation) + *procus (from pro- "forward;" see pro-, + -cus.
1550s, "of or at the vertex, directly overhead," from Middle French vertical (1540s), from Late Latin verticalis "overhead," from Latin vertex (genitive verticis) "highest point" (see vertex).

meanings.definitionCn: 粗暴的；粗哑的；脾气坏的

etymology.story: 1530s, of physical things, "coarse, coarse-grained," from Middle Dutch or Middle Low German grof "coarse (in quality), thick, large," of uncertain origin, regarded by some as related to Old English hreof, Old Norse hrjufr "rough, scabby," with Germanic completive prefix ga-.

meanings.definitionCn: [物] 渗透；[物] 渗透性；渗透作用

etymology.story: 1867, Latinized from osmose (1854), shortened from endosmosis (1830s), from endosmose "inward passage of a fluid through a porous septum" (1829), from French endo- "inward" + Greek osmos "a thrusting, a pushing," from stem of othein "to push, to thrust," from PIE *wedhe- "to push, strike" (source also of Sanskrit vadhati "pushes, strikes, destroys," Avestan vadaya- "to repulse").

early 13c., from causative of Old English cringan "give way, fall (in battle), become bent," from Proto-Germanic *krank- "bend, curl up" (source also of Old Norse kringr, Dutch kring, German Kring "circle, ring").

meanings.definitionCn: 幼稚的；孩子气的；未成熟的；天真的

etymology.story: 1660s, "youthful, boyish," a back-formation from puerility, or else from French puéril (15c.), from Latin puerilis "boyish; childish," from puer "boy, child" (see puerility).

meanings.definitionCn: 气体；[矿业] 瓦斯；汽油；毒气

etymology.story: 1765, from emphasis + -ize.

morphology.mnemonic: 过度/超越 + bring one's influence to bear + 热

etymology.story: "in excess of proper or ordinary weight," 1630s, from over- + weight (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 轻微的，少量的；脆弱的；细长的；不重要的

etymology.story: 1540s, from Middle French tumultuous (Modern French tumultueux), from Latin tumultuosus "full of bustle or confusion, disorderly, turbulent," from tumultus (see tumult).

meanings.definitionCn: 政权，政体；社会制度；管理体制

etymology.story: "system of government or rule," 1792, from French régime, from Old French regimen (14c.), from Latin regimen "rule, guidance, government, means of guidance, rudder," from regere (see regal).

meanings.definitionCn: 使柔弱；使无男子汉气概
变得无男子汉气概；变得柔弱

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + femin + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: late 14c., "womanish; voluptuous; tender," from Latin effeminatus "womanish, effeminate," past participle of effeminare "make a woman of," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + femina "woman" (see feminine).

"point of the heavens directly overhead at any place," late 14c., from Old French cenith (Modern French zénith), from Medieval Latin cenit, senit, bungled scribal transliterations of Arabic samt "road, path," abbreviation of samt ar-ras, literally "the way over the head." Letter -m- misread as -ni-.

meanings.definitionCn: 拱廊， 有拱廊的街道

etymology.story: 1731 (as arcado, from 1640s), from Italian arcata "arch of a bridge," from arco "arc," from Latin arcus (see arc).

morphology.mnemonic: 太阳/独自 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1610s, from Late Latin soliloquium "a talking to oneself," from Latin solus "alone" (see sole (adj.)) + loqui "speak" (see locution).
1798, from anthropology + -ist..

meanings.definitionCn: 家谱； 门第； 血统

etymology.story: early 15c., "genealogical table or chart," from Anglo-French pe de gru, a variant of Old French pied de gru "foot of a crane," from Latin pedem accusative of pes "foot," from PIE root *ped- (1) "a foot" (see foot (n.)) + gruem (nominative grus) "crane," cognate with Greek geranos, Old English cran; see crane (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 隆重的；讲究仪式的；正式的

etymology.story: 1550s, from Middle French cérémonieux or directly from Late Latin caerimoniosus, from Latin caerimonia (see ceremony).

meanings.definitionCn: 掩饰；与…不符；使失望；证明…虚假错误

etymology.story: Old English beleogan "to deceive by lies," from be- + lie (v.1) "to lie, tell lies." Current sense of "to contradict as a lie" is first recorded 1640s.

meanings.definitionCn: 上级的；优秀的，出众的；高傲的
上级，长官；优胜者，高手；长者

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin superior (see superior (adj.)), used in Medieval Latin with a noun sense of "one higher, a superior.".

meanings.definitionCn: 早产的；不成熟的；比预期早的
早产儿；过早发生的事物

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Latin praematurus "early ripe" (as fruit), "too early, untimely," from prae "before" (see pre-) + maturus "ripe, timely" (see mature (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 建立/放置 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old French restitucion or directly from Latin restitutionem (nominative restitutio) "a restoring," noun of action from past participle stem of restituere "set up again, restore, rebuild, replace, revive, reinstate, re-establish," from re- "again" (see re-) + statuere "to set up," from PIE root *stā- "to stand," with derivatives meaning "place or thing that is standing" (see stet)..

meanings.definitionCn: 资助；给与奖助金；向…行贿

etymology.story: 1755, from subsidy + -ize.

meanings.definitionCn: 杂色的；斑驳的；富于变化的
好斗的；野蛮的；言词刻毒的

morphology.mnemonic: 忠诚的，真实的 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1530s, from Latin truculentus "fierce, savage, stern, harsh, cruel," from trux (genitive trucis) "fierce, rough, savage, wild."

meanings.definitionCn: 附件,附属品;(全套衣服增加美感的)服饰

etymology.story: 1550s, "subordinate," from Late Latin accessorius, from accessor, agent noun from accedere "to approach" (see accede).

"animal with two feet," 1640s, from Latin bipedem (nominative bipes) "two-footed," as a plural noun, "men;" from bi- "two" (see bi-) + pedem (nominative pes) "foot," from PIE root *ped- (1) "a foot" (see foot (n.))..

1550s, "make return in kind" (especially of an injury), from Old French retort and directly from Latin retortus, past participle of retorquere "turn back, twist back, throw back," from re- "back" (see re-) + torquere "to twist" (see torque (n.)).

root.meaning: to make or form on a machine

morphology.mnemonic: to make or form on a machine + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 15c., "a plotting, intrigue," from Old French machinacion "plot, conspiracy, scheming, intrigue," from Latin machinationem (nominative machinatio) "device, contrivance, machination," noun of action from past participle stem of machinari "contrive skillfully, to design; to scheme, to plot," from machina (see machine (n.)).
1887, "special group-study class for advanced students," from German Seminar "group of students working with a professor," from Latin seminarium "breeding ground, plant nursery" (see seminary).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 无 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1630s, from Middle French annihilation (restored from Old French anichilacion, 14c.), or directly from Late Latin annihilationem (nominative annihilatio), noun of action from past participle stem of annihilare (see annihilate)..

meanings.definitionCn: 有暴风雨的；暴乱的；剧烈的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Late Latin tempestuosus "stormy, turbulent," from Latin tempestas, tempestus "storm, commotion; weather, season; occasion, time," related to tempus "time, season" (see temporal).

meanings.definitionCn: 呼吸作用；一次呼吸；植物光合作用

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 呼吸 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Latin respirationem (nominative respiratio) "breathing, respiration," noun of action from past participle stem of respirare (see respire)..
1670s, "officer in command of a brigade," from French brigadier, from brigade (see brigade)..

meanings.definitionCn: 贮藏室，仓库；知识库；智囊团

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 放置 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: late 15c., "vessel, etc., for storage," Middle French repositoire or directly from Late Latin repositorium "store," in classical Latin, "a stand on which food is placed," from noun use of repositus, past participle of reponere "put away, store" (see repose (v.2)).

meanings.definitionCn: 治疗的；治疗学的；有益于健康的

etymology.story: pertaining to the healing of disease, 1640s, from Modern Latin therapeuticus "curing, healing," from Greek therapeutikos, from therapeutein "to cure, treat medically," primarily "do service, take care of, provide for," of unknown origin, related to therapon "attendant." Therapeutic was used from 1540s as a noun meaning "the branch of medicine concerned with treatment of disease."
late 14c., "practitioner," agent noun from meddle (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 赌运气；冒…的危险，使遭受危险

etymology.story: "put something at stake in a game of chance," 1520s, from Middle French hasarder "to play at gambling, throw dice" (15c.), from hasard (see hazard (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 情节剧；音乐剧；耸人听闻的事件，闹剧

etymology.story: 1784 (1782 as melo drame), "a stage-play in which songs were interspersed and music accompanied the action," from French mélodrame (18c.), from Greek melos "song" (see melody) + French drame "drama" (see drama).

meanings.definitionCn: 植物的；植物人状态的，无所作为的；促使植物生长的；有生长力的

etymology.story: late 14c., "endowed with the power of growth," from Old French vegetatif "(naturally) growing," from Medieval Latin vegetativus, from vegetat-, past participle stem of vegetare (see vegetable (adj.)).

late 15c., herbengar "one sent ahead to arrange lodgings" (for a monarch, an army, etc.), alteration of Middle English herberger "provider of shelter, innkeeper" (late 12c.), from Old French herbergeor "one who offers lodging, innkeeper," agent noun from herbergier "provide lodging," from herber "lodging, shelter," from Frankish *heriberga "lodging, inn" (cognate with Old Saxon, Old High German heriberga "army shelter"), from Germanic compound *harja-bergaz "shelter, lodgings," which is also the source of harbor (n.).
1550s, from Latin dilutus, past participle of diluere "dissolve, wash away, dilute," from dis- "apart" + -luere, comb.

meanings.definitionCn: [建][化工] 促凝剂；凝血剂；凝结剂

etymology.story: 1770, from Latin coagulantem (nominative coagulans), present participle of coagulare (see coagulate)..
1630s, apparently from baste "to thrash" (see baste (v.3)) + the obscure verb lam "to beat, to lame" or the related Elizabethan noun lam "a heavy blow" (implied by 1540s in puns on lambskin).
1580s, from Latin ominosus "full of foreboding," from omen (genitive ominis) "foreboding" (see omen).

meanings.definitionCn: 诚实；精确性；老实；说真实话

etymology.story: 1620s, from French véracité (17c.), from Medieval Latin veracitatem (nominative veracitas) "truthfulness," from Latin verax (genitive veracis) "truthful," from verus "true" (see very)..

early 14c., from Old French contract (Modern French contrat), from Latin contractus "a contract, agreement," from past participle of contrahere "to draw together," metaphorically, "to make a bargain," from com- "together" (see com-) + trahere "to draw" (see tract (n.1)).
mid-15c., from Middle English slugge "lazy person" (see sluggard) + -ish.
mid-15c., from mis- (1) + fortune.
Spenser's coinage, 1590, as a name for his personification of vainglory, from brag, with augmentative ending by analogy to the Italian words then in vogue in England.

meanings.definitionCn: 易受影响的;易受感动的;易受感染的

etymology.story: 1550s, from be- + friend (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 取消，撤消；下反对命令召回

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French contremander "reverse an order or command" (13c.), from contre- "against" (see contra-) + mander, from Latin mandare "to order" (see mandate (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 革新的， 创新的， 新颖的； 富有革新精神的

root.meaning: bring in new things, alter established practices

morphology.mnemonic: bring in new things, alter established practices + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1796 (with an isolated use from c.

meanings.definitionCn: 静止的；不活动的；沉寂的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Latin exactus, past participle of exigere "require, enforce, demand, collect (money);" see exact (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 资源丰富的；足智多谋的；机智的

etymology.story: 1807, from resource + -ful.

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 警告/提醒 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Anglo-French premunition, Middle French premonicion, from Late Latin praemonitionem (nominative praemonitio) "a forewarning," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin praemonere "forewarn," from prae "before" (see pre-) + monere "to warn" (see monitor (n.))..
1816, "pertaining to the brain," from French cérébral (16c.), from Latin cerebrum "the brain" (also "the understanding"), from PIE *keres-, from root *ker- (1) "top of the head" (see horn (n.)).
"skip about in sport," 1580s; earlier gambade (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 消除；镇压；压碎；使…咯吱咯吱的响
噪声控制；嘎吱声；压倒对方的反驳；压碎的一堆

etymology.story: 1620s, "to fall, drop, or stomp (on something soft) with crushing force," possibly imitative of sound made in the process.

root.meaning: device that breaks an electrical circuit

morphology.mnemonic: su + device that breaks an electrical circuit

etymology.story: 1580s, from Latin suffusus, past participle of suffundere "overspread, pour beneath, pour upon" (see suffusion).

meanings.definitionCn: 任性，反复无常；随想曲，怪想

etymology.story: "sudden change of mind," 1660s, from French caprice "whim" (16c.), from Italian capriccio "whim," originally "a shivering," possibly from capro "goat," with reference to frisking, from Latin capreolus "wild goat" (see cab).

meanings.definitionCn: 流浪的；流浪者的；浪荡的；漂泊的

etymology.story: "disaster," 1848, from French débâcle "downfall, collapse, disaster" (17c.), a figurative use, literally "breaking up (of ice on a river)," extended to the violent flood that follows when the river ice melts in spring; from débâcler "to free," from Middle French desbacler "to unbar," from des- "off" + bacler "to bar," from Vulgar Latin *bacculare, from Latin baculum "stick" (see bacillus).
1610s, from Modern Latin magnetismus (see magnet + -ism).

meanings.definitionCn: 需要久坐的； 惯于久坐不动的； 定居的， 不迁徙的； 沉积的

etymology.story: 1590s, "remaining in one place," from Middle French sédentaire (16c.) and directly from Latin sedentarius "sitting, remaining in one place," from sedentem (nominative sedens), present participle of sedere "to sit; occupy an official seat, preside; sit still, remain; be fixed or settled," from PIE root *sed- (1) "to sit" (source also of Sanskrit a-sadat "sat down," sidati "sits;" Old Persian hadis "abode;" Greek ezesthai "to sit," hedra "seat, chair, face of a geometric solid;" Old Irish suide "seat, sitting;" Welsh sedd "seat," eistedd "sitting;" Old Church Slavonic sežda, sedeti "to sit;" Lithuanian sedmi "to sit;" Russian sad "garden," Lithuanian soditi "to plant;" Gothic sitan, Old English sittan "to sit;" see sit).

meanings.definitionCn: 阴谋（尤指政治上的）；阴谋集团

etymology.story: 1520s, "mystical interpretation of the Old Testament," later "society, small group meeting privately" (1660s), from French cabal, in both senses, from Medieval Latin cabbala (see cabbala).

Old English bealu-full "dire, wicked, cruel," with -ful + bealu "harm, injury, ruin, evil, mischief, wickedness, a noxious thing," from Proto-Germanic *balwom (source also of Old Saxon balu, Old Frisian balu "evil," Old High German balo "destruction," Old Norse bol, Gothic balwjan "to torment"), from PIE root *bhelu- "to harm." During Anglo-Saxon times, the noun was in poetic use only (in compounds such as bealubenn "mortal wound," bealuðonc "evil thought"), and for long baleful has belonged exclusively to poets.

meanings.definitionCn: 剧毒的；恶性的；有恶意的
预先确定；预先决定；预先查明

etymology.story: 1620s, originally theological, from pre- + determine or else from Late Latin praedeterminare (Augustine).

morphology.mnemonic: watershed, separation between river valleys + …者/…器

etymology.story: early 15c., Latin agent noun from dividere (see divide (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 抄本， 副本； 文字记录

etymology.story: "written copy of a document," c.

1727, from French troubadour (16c.) "one of a class of lyric poets in southern France, eastern Spain, and northern Italy 11c.-13c.," from Old Provençal trobador, from trobar "to find," earlier "invent a song, compose in verse," perhaps from Vulgar Latin *tropare "compose, sing," especially in the form of tropes, from Latin tropus "a song" (see trope).

morphology.mnemonic: 直/统治 + 与…有关的/像…的

etymology.story: 1620s, from Middle French rectangulaire (16c.) or formed in English from Latin stem of rectangle + -ar.

mid-15c., from Middle French précis "condensed, cut short" (14c.) and directly from Medieval Latin precisus, from Latin praecisus "abrupt, abridged, cut off," past participle of praecidere "to cut off, shorten," from prae "before" (see pre-) + caedere "to cut" (see -cide; for Latin vowel change, see acquisition).

meanings.definitionCn: 力量，能力；电力，功率；政权，势力；[数] 幂
激励；供以动力；使…有力量
借影响有权势人物以操纵权力的
较远的； 不可告人的， 隐秘的， 秘密的
