meanings.definitionCn: 波动；起伏；挥手示意；摇动；呈波形
波动；波浪；高潮；挥手示意；卷曲

etymology.story: "moving billow of water," 1520s, alteration (by influence of wave (v.)) of Middle English waw, which is from Old English wagian "to move to and fro" (cognate with Old Saxon, Old High German wag, Old Frisian weg, Old Norse vagr "water in motion, wave, billow," Gothic wegs "tempest;" see wag (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 乡村的；纯朴的；粗野的；手工粗糙的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Latin rusticus "of the country, rural; country-like, plain, simple, rough, coarse, awkward," from rus (genitive ruris) "open land, country" (see rural).
1570s, from tip (v.1); compare drowsy, flimsy, tricksy.
late 14c., from Old French prudent "with knowledge, deliberate" (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 独裁者，专制君主；独断独行的人

morphology.mnemonic: 自我/自动 + 统治/权力

etymology.story: 1803, from French autocrate, from Greek autokrates "ruling by oneself, absolute, autocratic," from autos- "self" (see auto-) + kratia "rule," from kratos "strength, power" (see -cracy).

meanings.definitionCn: 退缩；抖缩；蜷缩；弯腰屈膝

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + tenu + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: "to make thin, to make less," 1520s, from Latin attenuatus "enfeebled, weak," past participle of attenuare "to make thin, lessen, diminish," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + tenuare "make thin," from tenuis "thin" (see tenet).
"to place (two or more objects) side by side or close together," 1826, a back-formation from juxtaposition or else from French juxtaposer (18c.).
Old English laðian "to hate, to be disgusted with," from lað "hostile" (see loath).

morphology.mnemonic: 轮，转 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1550s, from Latin rotationem (nominative rotatio) "a turning about in a circle," noun of action from past participle stem of rotare "turn round, revolve, whirl about, roll," from PIE *ret- "to run, roll" (see rotary)..

late 14c., from Latin temeritatem (nominative temeritas) "blind chance, accident; rashness, indiscretion, foolhardiness," from temere "by chance, at random; indiscreetly, rashly," related to tenebrae "darkness," from PIE root *teme- "dark" (source also of Sanskrit tamas- "darkness," tamsrah "dark;" Avestan temah "darkness;" Lithuanian tamsa "darkness," tamsus "dark;" Old Church Slavonic tima "darkness;" Old High German dinstar "dark;" Old Irish temel "darkness").
1530s, agent noun from intrude.

meanings.definitionCn: 无穷小的；无限小的；极小的

suffix.meaning: thing or animal spoken about before.

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 结束/限制 + thing or animal spoken about before. + esimal

etymology.story: 1710 (1650s as a noun), "infinitely small, less than any assignable quantity," from Modern Latin infinitesimus, from Latin infinitus "infinite" (see infinite) + ordinal word-forming element -esimus, as in centesimus "hundredth."

meanings.definitionCn: 诉讼的；有异议的，引起争论的；爱争论的
交际的；爱说话的，健谈的；无隐讳交谈的

morphology.mnemonic: com + 使…/具有…的 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: late 14c., "that communicates," from French communicatif, from Latin communicat-, past participle stem of communicare "impart, inform" (see communication).

meanings.definitionCn: 一般的， 普通的， 通用的； 种的， 属的

etymology.story: 1670s, "belonging to a large group of objects," formed in English from Latin gener-, stem of genus "race, kind" (see genus) + -ic.

meanings.definitionCn: 赞同， 认可；代言；背书； 在背面签名<

etymology.story: "praise highly, sing the praises of," late 14c., from Old French lauder "to praise, extol," from Latin laudare "to praise, commend, honor, extol, eulogize," from laus (genitive laudis) "praise, fame, glory." Probably from an echoic PIE root *leu- and cognate with Old English leoð "song, poem, hymn," from Proto-Germanic *leuthan (source also of Old Norse ljoð "strophe," German Lied "song," Gothic liuþon "to praise").

late 14c., "hateful, odious, atrocious," from Old French hainos "inconvenient, awkward; hateful, unpleasant; odious" (12c., Modern French haineux), from haine "hatred, hate," from hair "to hate," from Frankish, from Proto-Germanic *hatjan, from PIE *kad- "sorrow, hatred" (see hate (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 典型的；特有的；象征性的

etymology.story: "order forbidding ships from certain other nations from entering or leaving a nation's ports," 1590s, from Spanish embargo "seizure, arrest; embargo," noun of action from embargar "restrain, impede, arrest, embargo," from Vulgar Latin *imbarricare, from assimilated form of in- "into, upon" (see in- (2)) + *barra (see bar (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: [贸易] 报价单；引用语；引证

etymology.story: mid-15c., "numbering," later (1530s) "marginal notation," noun of action from quote (v.) or else from Medieval Latin quotationem (nominative quotatio), noun of action from past participle stem of quotare "to number." Meaning "an act of quoting" is from 1640s; that of "passage quoted" is from 1680s.
unit of electrical power, 1882, in honor of James Watt (1736-1819), Scottish engineer and inventor.

meanings.definitionCn: 夜晚的； 夜晚发生的； 夜晚活动的

morphology.mnemonic: 夜 + urn + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 15c., from Old French nocturnal "nightly, nocturnal," or directly from Late Latin nocturnalis, from Latin nocturnus "belonging to the night," from nox (genitive noctis) "night," cognate with Old English neaht (see night) + -urnus, suffix forming adjectives of time.

meanings.definitionCn: 腐蚀；腐蚀产生的物质；衰败
[航][流] 涡流；漩涡；（动乱，争论等的）中心；旋风

etymology.story: 1650s, "whirlpool, eddying mass," from Latin vortex, variant of vertex "an eddy of water, wind, or flame; whirlpool; whirlwind," from stem of vertere "to turn" (see versus).

meanings.definitionCn: 声名狼藉的，臭名昭著的

etymology.story: 1540s, "publicly known," from Medieval Latin notorius "well-known, commonly known," from Latin notus "known," past participle of noscere "come to know" (see know).

meanings.definitionCn: 非难的，指责的；控诉的，控告的
加重； 恶化； 愤怒， 恼怒

root.meaning: to make a bad thing worse

morphology.mnemonic: to make a bad thing worse + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 15c., from Middle French aggravation, from Late Latin aggravationem (nominative aggravatio), noun of action from past participle stem of Latin aggravare "make heavier," figuratively "to embarrass further, increase in oppressiveness," from ad "to" (see ad-) + gravare "weigh down," from gravis "heavy" (see grave (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 拉长；伸展得超出的范围

etymology.story: mid-14c., from out + stretch (v.).
early 15c., from or related to Middle Dutch praten "to chatter" (c.

1821, artefact, "anything made by human art," from Italian artefatto, from Latin arte "by skill" (ablative of ars "art;" see art (n.)) + factum "thing made," from facere "to make, do" (see factitious).

1570s, from Latin aviarium "place in which birds are kept," neuter of aviarius "of birds," from avis "bird," from PIE *awi- "bird" (source also of Sanskrit vih, Avestan vish "bird," Greek aietos "eagle")..

morphology.mnemonic: 脚/儿童 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "particular to a people or locality," 1650s (endemical), with -ic + Greek endemos "native, dwelling in (a place), of or belonging to a people," from en "in" (see en- (2)) + demos "people, district" (see demotic).

meanings.definitionCn: 无价值的；琐碎的；无效的；无法律效力的

etymology.story: "trifling, of no value," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 有深刻见解的，富有洞察力的

etymology.story: "with penetrating understanding into character or hidden nature," 1881, from insight + -ful.

meanings.definitionCn: [车辆][医] 救护车；战时流动医院

etymology.story: 1798, "mobile or field hospital," from French (hôpital) ambulant, literally "walking (hospital)," from Latin ambulantem (nominative ambulans), present participle of ambulare "to walk" (see amble).

meanings.definitionCn: 取出；抽出；拔出；抽出物；出身

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 拉/吸引 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., "process of withdrawing or obtaining" (something, from something else), from Old French estraction "extraction, origin" (12c.) or directly from Medieval Latin extractionem (nominative extractio), noun of action from past participle stem of Latin extrahere "to draw out" (see extract (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 爱 + 具有…特征/名词化

meanings.definitionCn: 斜坡；倾斜；斜率；扛枪姿势
喧嚣的；公然的；炫耀的；俗丽的

etymology.story: 1596, in blatant beast, coined by Edmund Spenser in "The Faerie Queen" to describe a thousand-tongued monster representing slander; probably suggested by Latin blatire "to babble." It entered general use 1650s, as "noisy in an offensive and vulgar way;" the sense of "obvious, glaringly conspicuous" is from 1889.

1590s, "sharp, poignant" (of pain or grief), from Latin pungentem (nominative pungens), present participle of pungere "to prick, pierce, sting," figuratively, "to vex, grieve, trouble, afflict," related to pugnus "a fist" (see pugnacious).

meanings.definitionCn: 敷衍的；马虎的；得过且过的

etymology.story: 1580s, from Late Latin perfunctorius "careless, negligent," literally "like one who wishes to get through a thing," from Latin perfungus, past participle of perfungi "discharge, busy oneself, get through," from per- "through" + fungi "perform" (see function (n.)).

mid-15c., "concealed, secret," from Latin latentem (nominative latens) "lying hid, concealed, secret, unknown," present participle of latere "lie hidden, lurk, be concealed," from PIE *late-, suffixed form of root *lādh- "to be hidden" (source also of Greek lethe "forgetfulness, oblivion," Old Church Slavonic lajati "to lie in wait for").

"state of perplexity," 1570s, of unknown origin, perhaps a quasi-Latinism based on Latin quando "when?  at what time?; at the time that, inasmuch," pronominal adverb of time, related to qui "who" (see who).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + gener + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1550s, back-formation from regeneration or else from Latin regeneratus, past participle of regenerare "bring forth again" (see regeneration).

"brightness, radiant energy," Old English leht, earlier leoht "light, daylight," from Proto-Germanic *leukhtam (source also of Old Saxon lioht, Old Frisian liacht, Middle Dutch lucht, Dutch licht, Old High German lioht, German Licht, Gothic liuhaþ "light"), from PIE *leuk- "light, brightness" (source also of Sanskrit rocate "shines;" Armenian lois "light," lusin "moon;" Greek leukos "bright, shining, white;" Latin lucere "to shine," lux "light," lucidus "clear;" Old Church Slavonic luci "light;" Lithuanian laukas "pale;" Welsh llug "gleam, glimmer;" Old Irish loche "lightning," luchair "brightness;" Hittite lukezi "is bright").
1630s, "to feel sick, to become affected with nausea," from nauseat- past participle stem of Latin nauseare "to feel seasick, to vomit," also "to cause disgust," from nausea (see nausea).

morphology.mnemonic: body体（corpor也作corp） + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "of or belonging to the body," late 14c., from Old French corporal (12c., Modern French corporel) "of the body, physical, strong," from Latin corporalis "pertaining to the body," from corpus (genitive corporis) "body" (see corporeal).

"group of rhymed verse lines," 1580s, from Italian stanza "verse of a poem," originally "standing, stopping place," from Vulgar Latin *stantia "a stanza of verse," so called from the stop at the end of it, from Latin stantem (nominative stans), present participle of stare "to stand," from PIE root *stā- "to stand, set down, make or be firm" (see stet).

early 15c., from Old French imputer, emputer (14c.) and directly from Latin imputare "to reckon, make account of, charge, ascribe," from assimilated form of in- "in, into" (see in- (2)) + putare "to trim, prune; reckon, clear up, settle (an account)," from PIE *puto- "cut, struck," suffixed form of root *pau- (2) "to cut, strike, stamp" (see pave).

meanings.definitionCn: 不屈不挠的；不服输的；不气馁的

etymology.story: 1630s, "that cannot be tamed or subdued," from Late Latin indomitabilis "untameable," from in- "not, opposite of, without" (see in- (1)) + *domitabilis, from Latin domitare, frequentative of domare "to tame" (see tame (adj.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 反对/相反 + 生命/生物 + ic

meanings.definitionCn: 一团糟， 凌乱， 混乱； 糊涂， 困惑
将…弄成一团糟； 使困惑， 使糊涂； 混淆

etymology.story: late 14c., "pleasing, gracious; joyful," from Old French jocond or directly from Late Latin iocundus (source of Spanish jocunde, Italian giocondo), variant (influenced by iocus "joke") of Latin iucundus "pleasant," originally "helpful," contraction of *iuvicundus, from iuvare "to please, benefit, help" (see adjutant)..

meanings.definitionCn: 沉重的，笨重的；(文章)冗长的

morphology.mnemonic: to weigh a matter mentally + 具有…性质的

meanings.definitionCn: 传染， 感染， 侵染， 传播； 传染病

morphology.mnemonic: in + 做/制造 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "infectious disease; contaminated condition;" from Old French infeccion "contamination, poisoning" (13c.) and directly from Late Latin infectionem (nominative infectio) "infection, contagion," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin inficere "to spoil, to stain" (see infect).

meanings.definitionCn: 顽固的；顽强的；难处理的

etymology.story: late 14c., of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 开动（机器等）；促使，驱使；激励（人等）

etymology.story: 1590s, from Medieval Latin actuatus, past participle of actuare, from Latin actus (see act (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 移动/迁移 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1778, a back-formation from emigration, or else from Latin emigratus, past participle of emigrare "move away." In 19c.

meanings.definitionCn: 三角（形）；三角关系；三角形之物；三人一组

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French triangle (13c.), from Latin triangulum "triangle," noun use of neuter of adjective triangulus "three-cornered, having three angles," from tri- "three" (see tri-) + angulus "corner, angle" (see angle (n.))..
"full of turns and curves," 1570s, from Latin sinuosus "full of curves, folds, or bendings," from sinus "curve, fold, bend" (see sinus).

meanings.definitionCn: 外国的；相异的，性质不同的；不相容的
牧歌的；牧羊的；乡下风味的

etymology.story: 1610s, earlier bucolical (1520s), from Latin bucolicus, from Greek boukolikos "pastoral, rustic," from boukolos "cowherd, herdsman," from bous "cow" (see cow (n.)) + -kolos "tending," related to Latin colere "to till (the ground), cultivate, dwell, inhabit" (the root of colony).

1590s, from Middle French attester (Old French atester, 13c.) "affirm, attest," from Latin attestari "confirm," literally "bear witness to," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + testari "bear witness," from testis "witness" (see testament).

meanings.definitionCn: 吹毛求疵；苛责；无端的指责

etymology.story: 1540s, from Middle French caviller "to mock, jest," from Latin cavillari "to jeer, mock; satirize, argue scoffingly" (also source of Italian cavillare, Spanish cavilar), from cavilla "jest, jeering," related to calumnia (see calumny)..
1540s, from be- "thoroughly" + Middle English rate "to scold" (late 14c.), from Old French reter "accuse, blame," from Latin reputare (see reputation).

meanings.definitionCn: 放荡的，不检点的；恣意挥霍的

etymology.story: 1520s, "overthrown, routed" (now obsolete in this sense), from Latin profligatus "destroyed, ruined, corrupt, abandoned, dissolute," past participle of profligare "to cast down, defeat, ruin," from pro- "down, forth" (see pro-) + fligere "to strike" (see afflict).

morphology.mnemonic: pred + …者/…器 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1580s, "involving plundering," from Latin praedatorius "pertaining to plunder," from praedator "plunderer," from praedor "to plunder," from praeda "prey" (see prey (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 不足的；有缺陷的；不充分的

etymology.story: 1580s, from Latin deficientem (nominative deficiens), present participle of deficere "to desert, revolt, fail," from de- "down, away" (see de-) + facere "to do, perform" (see factitious)..

meanings.definitionCn: 不适当；不正确；用词错误；[审计] 不正当行为

etymology.story: 1610s, "quality or fact of being improper," from French impropriété (16c.) or directly from Latin improprietas "impropriety," from improprius "improper" (see improper).
1891, possibly from dialectal yip "to cheep like a bird" (early 19c.), from Middle English yippen (mid-15c.), of imitative origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 智慧的；明智的；赋予智慧的

etymology.story: "raw recruit," 1892 in that spelling, popularized by Kipling's "Barrack-Room Ballads," of uncertain origin, perhaps from recruit, influenced by rook (n.1) in its secondary sense, suggesting "easy to cheat." Barrère ["A Dictionary of Slang, Jargon & Cant," 1890] has "Rookey (army), a recruit; from the black coat some of them wear," so perhaps directly from rook (n.1).
1610s, from French ravage "destruction" (see ravage (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 贫瘠的；不育的；(植物)不结果的；无用的
唠叨的；喋喋不休的；多嘴的

etymology.story: 1610s, from Latin garrulus "talkative, chattering," from garrire "to chatter," from PIE root *gar- "to call, cry," of imitative origin (compare Greek gerys "voice, sound," Ossetic zar "song," Welsh garm, Old Irish gairm "noise, cry").

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + com + …主义者/…专家

etymology.story: "performer who takes the accompanying part in music," 1833, from accompany + -ist.

meanings.definitionCn: 宏伟的；堂皇的；浮夸的；宏大的

etymology.story: 1828 (earlier as a French word in English), from French grandiose "impressive, grand in effect" (18c.), from Italian grandioso (which also was borrowed directly into English as a musical term), from Latin grandis "big" (see grand (adj.)).

early 15c., from Middle French permanent (14c.) or directly from Latin permanentem (nominative permanens) "remaining," present participle of permanere "endure, hold out, continue, stay to the end," from per- "through"  (see per) + manere "stay" (see mansion).

meanings.definitionCn: 培养；养育，抚育；抱（希望等）
(Foster)人名；(英、捷、意、葡、法、德、俄、西)福斯特

etymology.story: Old English *fostrian "to supply with food, nourish, support," from fostor "food, nourishment, bringing up," from Proto-Germanic *fostra-, from extended form of PIE root *pa- "to protect; feed" (see food).

early 15c., "gentleness, lightness," from Middle French facilité "easiness, ease," from Latin facilitatem (nominative facilitas) "easiness, ease, fluency, willingness," from facilis "easy" (see facile).

meanings.definitionCn: 群居的； 合群的； 爱交际的

etymology.story: 1660s, "disposed to live in flocks" (of animals), from Latin gregarius "pertaining to a flock; of the herd, of the common sort, common," from grex (genitive gregis) "flock, herd," from PIE *gre-g-, reduplicated form of root *ger- (1) "to gather together, assemble" (source also of Sanskrit gramah "heap, troop;" Greek ageirein "to assemble," agora "assembly;" Latin gremium "bosom, lap;" Old Church Slavonic grusti "handful," gramota "heap;" Lithuanian gurgulys "chaos, confusion," gurguole "crowd, mass").
"chemical decomposition by water," 1879, formed in English from hydro- + Greek lysis "a loosening, a dissolution," from lyein "to loosen, dissolve" (see lose).

meanings.definitionCn: 横梁；光线；电波；船宽；[计量] 秤杆
发送；以梁支撑；用…照射；流露
易怒的，暴躁的；带怒气的；撒娇的

etymology.story: late 14c., peyvesshe "perverse, capricious, silly," of uncertain origin, possibly modeled on Latin perversus "reversed, perverse," past participle of pervertere "to turn about" (see pervert (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 英勇的，勇敢的；华丽的；雄伟的

morphology.mnemonic: 太阳/独自 + 使…化/造成 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1800; noun of action from solidify; perhaps from French solidification.
1640s, "to evaporate through the pores," a back-formation from perspiration and in part from Latin perspirare "to breathe, to blow constantly" (see perspiration).

meanings.definitionCn: 与…竞争， 赶上； 仿真， 模仿

etymology.story: 1580s, back-formation from emulation, or else from Latin aemulatus, past participle of aemulari "to rival."
"combine or bring together" (two or more things), 1825, from synthesis + -ize.
late 14c., from Old French irascible (12c.) and directly from Late Latin irascibilis, from Latin irasci "be angry, be in a rage," from ira "anger" (see ire)..

meanings.definitionCn: 血的；血红色的；多血质的；乐观的

etymology.story: "pertaining to blood," 1510s, from Latin sanguineus, from sanguin-, stem of sanguis (see sanguinary)..

meanings.definitionCn: 强制的；绝对的；断然的；专横的

etymology.story: "decisive," mid-15c., legal term, from Anglo-French peremptorie, from Middle French peremtoire, from Latin peremptorius "destructive, decisive, final," from peremptor "destroyer," from perimpere "destroy, cut off," from per- "away entirely, to destruction" (see per) + emere "to take" (see exempt (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 婉转曲折的说法，累赘的陈述；遁辞

morphology.mnemonic: circum + 地方 + ute + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: 怀恨的；有报仇心的；惩罚的

etymology.story: 1610s, "vengeful," from Latin vindicta "revenge" (see vindication) + -ive; or perhaps a shortening of vindicative based on the Latin word.
1680s (Ben Jonson used it 1610s as a mock-name), from play (n.) + wright (n.)..

1570s, "intensity of expression," from Latin emphasis, from Greek emphasis "an appearing in, outward appearance;" in rhetoric, "significance, indirect meaning," from emphainein "to present, exhibit, display, let (a thing) be seen; be reflected (in a mirror), become visible," from assimilated form of en "in" (see en- (2)) + phainein "to show" (see phantasm).

meanings.definitionCn: 花花公子；喜修饰者；情郎

etymology.story: "attendant suitor of a lady," 1720, from French beau "the beautiful," noun use of an adjective, from Old French bel "beautiful, handsome, fair, genuine, real" (11c.), from Latin bellus "handsome, fine, pretty, agreeable," diminutive of bonus "good" (see bene-).

meanings.definitionCn: 可行的；可能的；可实行的

etymology.story: "capable of being done, accomplished or carried out," mid-15c., from Anglo-French faisible, from Old French faisable "possible, that may be done; easy, convenient," from fais-, stem of faire "do, make," from Latin facere "to make, do, perform" (see factitious).

meanings.definitionCn: 谦逊的；不装腔作势的；不出风头的

root.meaning: to suppose, to take for granted as the basis of argument

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + to suppose, to take for granted as the basis of argument

etymology.story: "unpretentious, modest," 1726, from un- (1) "not" + present participle of assume (v.).
"feel a secret premonition," especially of something evil, c.

meanings.definitionCn: 使先具有；预先灌输情感（或思想等）；使…先怀偏见；先有好感

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + to hold as property

etymology.story: 1610s, "to get possession of beforehand," from pre- + possess.

meanings.definitionCn: 潜能；可能性；[电] 电势

morphology.mnemonic: 力量/能力 + …的人/处于…状态 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., "possible" (as opposed to actual), from Old French potenciel and directly from Late Latin potentialis "potential," from Latin potentia "power, might, force;" figuratively "political power, authority, influence," from potens "powerful" (see potent).

meanings.definitionCn: [保险] 保险计算员；保险精算师

etymology.story: 1550s, "registrar, clerk," from Latin actuarius "copyist, account-keeper," from actus "public business" (see act (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 总共的；粗野的；恶劣的；显而易见的

root.meaning: watch kept on a festival eve

morphology.mnemonic: watch kept on a festival eve + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: late 15c., from Middle French vigilant or directly from Latin vigilantem (nominative vigilans) "watchful, anxious, careful," present participle of vigilare "to watch, keep awake, not to sleep, be watchful," from vigil "watchful, awake" (see vigil).
1650s, "sense of touch or feeling" (with an isolated instance, tacþe from c.
Old English bereafian "to deprive of, take away, seize, rob," from be + reafian "rob, plunder," from Proto-Germanic *raubojanan, from PIE *reup- "to snatch" (see rapid).

meanings.definitionCn: 老饕；美食家；享乐主义者

etymology.story: late 14c., "follower of Epicurus," from Latinized form of Greek Epicouros (341-270 B.C.E.), Athenian philosopher who taught that pleasure is the highest good and identified virtue as the greatest pleasure; the first lesson recalled, the second forgotten, and the name used pejoratively for "one who gives himself up to sensual pleasure" (1560s), especially "glutton, sybarite" (1774).

meanings.definitionCn: 分配；分配物；养家费；命运

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1570s, "action of allotting," from Middle French allotement, from Old French aloter (see allot).
1819 (implied in archived), from archives.

morphology.mnemonic: 强壮/价值 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: late 15c., from Middle French valeureux, from valeur (see valor).
1610s, "rouse to action," from Latin stimulatus, past participle of stimulare (see stimulation).

meanings.definitionCn: 平方的；正方形的；直角的；正直的

etymology.story: 1670s, from counter- + act (v.).
early 15c., "vigor," from Middle French animosité (14c.) or directly from Latin animositatem (nominative animositas) "boldness, vehemence," from animosus "bold, spirited," from animus (see animus).

morphology.mnemonic: 之间/相互 + one who goes with another. + ance

etymology.story: 1783, "intermeddling," from interfere on model of difference, etc.
mid-15c., "to change, transform," from Latin commutare "to often change, to change altogether," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + mutare "to change" (see mutable).
late 14c., from Latin altitudinem (nominative altitudo) "height, altitude," from altus "high" (see old)..
1782, from French classifier, from classe (see class (n.)) + -fier (see -fy).

meanings.definitionCn: 最低点，最底点；[天] 天底

etymology.story: late 14c., in astronomical sense, from Medieval Latin nadir, from Arabic nazir "opposite to," in nazir as-samt, literally "opposite direction," from nazir "opposite" + as-samt "road, path" (see zenith).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 放置 + 行为/过程/结果
共同/联合 + male member, penis + ance

etymology.story: 1713, from converge + -ence.

meanings.definitionCn: 奖学金；固定薪金；定期津贴；养老金

etymology.story: early 15c., "periodical payment; soldier's pay," from Latin stipendium "tax, impost, tribute," in military use "pay, salary," from stips "alms, small payment, contribution of money, gift" + pendere "weigh" (see pendant).

root.meaning: make an indirect reference, point in passing

morphology.mnemonic: make an indirect reference, point in passing + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1540s, from Latin allusionem (nominative allusio) "a playing with, a reference to," noun of action from past participle stem of alludere (see allude).

meanings.definitionCn: 神圣不可侵犯的；极神圣的

etymology.story: "superlatively sacred or inviolable," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 指， 指向； 表明； 瞄准
尖， 尖端； 点， 小数点； 条款， 细目； 分数， 得分； 要点， 论点， 观点

etymology.story: late 14c., "indicate with the finger;" c.

meanings.definitionCn: 欺骗；使着迷；轻松地消磨

etymology.story: early 13c., from be- + guile (v.).

1751, from French début "first appearance," a figurative use from débuter "make the first stroke at billiards," also "to lead off at bowls" (a game akin to bowling), 16c., from but "mark, goal," from Old French but "end" (see butt (n.3)).

morphology.mnemonic: 抓住/理解 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1530s, "seizure, arrest," from Latin prehensionem (nominative prehensio), noun of action from past participle stem of prehendere (see prehensile).

meanings.definitionCn: 容易消散的；逐渐消失的；会凋零的

morphology.mnemonic: age年龄，寿命，时代，时期 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1717, "on the point of becoming imperceptible," from French évanescent, from Latin evanescentem (nominative evanescens), present participle of evanescere "disappear, vanish, pass away," figuratively "be forgotten, be wasted," from assimilated form of ex- "out" (see ex-) + vanescere "vanish" (see vanish).

root.meaning: street noted for clubs, bars, etc.

morphology.mnemonic: street noted for clubs, bars, etc. + ling

etymology.story: "a youth," late 14c., of uncertain origin, possibly from strip (n.1) "long, narrow piece," on the notion of "one who is slender as a strip, whose figure is not yet filled out" + -ling..

meanings.definitionCn: 方面;[数] 维；尺寸；次元；容积 vt标出尺寸

etymology.story: late 14c., "measurement, size," from Latin dimensionem (nominative dimensio) "a measuring," noun of action from past participle stem of dimetri "to measure out," from dis- (see dis-) + metiri "to measure" (see measure (v.)).

1770, "severe homesickness considered as a disease," Modern Latin, coined 1668 in a dissertation on the topic at the University of Basel by scholar Johannes Hofer (1669-1752) as a rendering of German heimweh "homesickness" (for which see home + woe).

meanings.definitionCn: 突出的；强迫人的；冒失的

morphology.mnemonic: 对/向 + 推/挤 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1660s, from Latin obtrus-, past participle stem of obtrudere (see obtrude) + -ive.

meanings.definitionCn: 供选择的；选择性的；交替的
二中择一；供替代的选择

morphology.mnemonic: altern + 使…/具有…的 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1620s, in rhetoric, from Medieval Latin alternativus (see alternative (adj.)).

root.meaning: have the expected or desired effect

morphology.mnemonic: have the expected or desired effect + 身份/状态/技能

etymology.story: early 14c., "performance of labor," from workman + -ship.

late 15c., "sodium carbonate," an alkaline substance extracted from certain ashes (now made artificially), from Italian sida (or Medieval Latin soda) "a kind of saltwort," from which soda was obtained, of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 移情作用的；同感的（等于empathic）

etymology.story: 1909, from empathy on model of sympathetic and meant to be distinct from empathic.

early 15c., from Latin lassitudinem (nominative lassitudo) "faintness, weariness," from lassus "faint, tired, weary," from PIE *led-to-, suffixed form of *led- "slow, weary" (source also of Old English læt "sluggish, slow;" see late (adj.)), from root *le- (2) "to let go, slacken" (see lenient)..

meanings.definitionCn: 土地的；耕地的；有关土地的

etymology.story: 1610s, "relating to the land," from Middle French loy agrarienne "agrarian law," corresponding to Latin Lex agraria, the Roman law for the division of conquered lands, from agrarius "of the land," from ager (genitive agri) "a field," from PIE *agro- (source also of Greek agros "field," Gothic akrs, Old English æcer "field;" see acre).

meanings.definitionCn: 性格外向的人； 爱交际的人

etymology.story: 1916, extravert (spelled with -o- after 1918, by influence of introvert), from German Extravert, from extra "outside" (see extra-) + Latin vertere "to turn" (see versus).

meanings.definitionCn: 难看的，简陋的；吝啬的，自私的

etymology.story: "that which is halfway between extremes," early 14c., from Old French meien "middle, means, intermediary," noun use of adjective from Latin medianus "of or that is in the middle" (see mean (adj.2)).

morphology.mnemonic: 射线/放射 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1898, from French radio-actif, coined by Pierre and Marie Curie from radio-, comb.
1530s, from Latin repulsa "refusal, denial," noun use of fem.

late 14c., hors, earlier hos, from Old English has "hoarse," from Proto-Germanic *haisa- (source also of Old Saxon hes, Old Norse hass, Dutch hees, Old High German heisi, German heiser "hoarse"), perhaps originally meaning "dried out, rough." The intrusive -r- is difficult to explain; it is first attested c.

early 15c., "quality of being straight," from Middle French rectitude (14c.), from Late Latin rectitudinem (nominative rectitudo) "straightness, uprightness," from Latin rectus "straight" (see right (adj.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 站得住脚的； 可防守的

etymology.story: "capable of being held or maintained," 1570s, from Middle French tenable, from Old French (12c.), from tenir "to hold," from Latin tenere "to hold, keep" (see tenet)..

meanings.definitionCn: [药] 兴奋剂；刺激物；酒精饮料

etymology.story: 1772, from French stimulant or directly from Latin stimulantem (nominative stimulans), present participle of stimulare "to prick, urge, stimulate" (see stimulation).

early 15c., "liveliness, vigor," from Old French vivacite or directly from Latin vivacitatem (nominative vivacitas) "vital force, liveliness," from vivax (genitive vivacis) "lively," also "long-lived," from vivere "to live" (see vital)..

meanings.definitionCn: 为...表示感激，感谢；欣赏，赏识，评价

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 价格/价值 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1650s, "to esteem or value highly," from Late Latin appretiatus, past participle of appretiare "to set a price to" (see appraise).

meanings.definitionCn: 不够大的；小于一般尺寸的

etymology.story: early 15c., "that produces a (physical) quality," from Medieval Latin qualitativus "relating to quality," from stem of Latin qualitas "a quality, property, nature" (see quality).

meanings.definitionCn: 杀人的，残忍的；凶残的；蓄意谋杀的

etymology.story: 1530s, a hybrid from murder + -ous.

meanings.definitionCn: 虚构的；假想的；编造的；假装的

morphology.mnemonic: 塑造，虚构 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1610s, "artificial, counterfeit;" 1620s, "existing only in imagination," from Medieval Latin fictitius, a misspelling of Latin ficticius "artificial, counterfeit," from fictus "feigned, fictitious, false," past participle of fingere "to shape, form, devise, feign" (see fiction).
"the sum of the cognitive facilities (except sense or sense and imagination), the capacity for reasoning truth," late 14c.
1560s, from pre- + occupy.

morphology.mnemonic: to esteem (something) more than others + ance

etymology.story: mid-15c., "advancement in position or status;" 1650s as "act of prefering," from Middle French preference (14c., Modern French préférence), from Medieval Latin preferentia, from past participle stem of Latin praeferrere (see prefer).

meanings.definitionCn: 主要倾向，主要趋势，主流；主流派爵士乐

etymology.story: also main-stream, main stream, "principal current of a river," 1660s, from main (adj.) + stream (n.); hence, "prevailing direction in opinion, popular taste, etc.," a figurative use first attested in Carlyle (1831).
1560s, pejorative, "one who believes himself possessed of divine revelations or special communication from God," from Greek enthousiastes "a person inspired," from enthousiazein (see enthusiasm).

meanings.definitionCn: 神化；崇拜，颂扬；尊奉为神

etymology.story: 1600s, from Late Latin apotheosis "deification," from Greek apotheosis, from apotheoun "deify, make (someone) a god," from apo- special use of this prefix, meaning, here, "change" + theos "god" (see theo-)..

morphology.mnemonic: al + chem + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French alchimie (14c.), alquemie (13c.), from Medieval Latin alkimia, from Arabic al-kimiya, from Greek khemeioa (found c.300 C.E.
1580s, from Latin distortus, past participle of distorquere "to twist different ways, distort," from dis- "completely" + torquere "to twist" (see torque (n.)).
late 14c., "fond of disputes," from Middle French litigieux and directly from Latin litigiosus "contentious, quarrelsome," from litigium "dispute, strife," related to litigare (see litigation).

meanings.definitionCn: 人口；[生物] 种群，[生物] 群体；全体居民

morphology.mnemonic: 人/民众 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1610s, from Late Latin populationem (nominative populatio) "a people; a multitude," as if from Latin populus "a people" (see people (n.)).

late 14c., from Old French discerner (13c.) "distinguish (between), separate" (by sifting), and directly from Latin discernere "to separate, set apart, divide, distribute; distinguish, perceive," from dis- "off, away" (see dis-) + cernere "distinguish, separate, sift" (see crisis).
also cancelation, 1530s, from Latin cancellationem (nominative cancellatio), noun of action from past participle stem of cancellare "to cancel" (see cancel).

meanings.definitionCn: 灵活的；柔韧的；易弯曲的

etymology.story: early 15c., "capable of being bent; mentally or spiritually pliant," from Middle French flexible or directly from Latin flexibilis "that may be bent, pliant, flexible, yielding;" figuratively "tractable, inconstant," from flex-, past participle stem of flectere "to bend," which is of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 白天的；每日的，一日间的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Late Latin diurnalis "daily," from Latin dies "day" + -urnus, an adjectival suffix denoting time (compare hibernus "wintery").
1745, from transient + -ence.

meanings.definitionCn: 跨过， 超过， 越过

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French timoureus (14c.), from Medieval Latin timorosus "fearful," from Latin timor "fear, dread, apprehension, anxiety; religious awe, reverence," from timere "to fear, be afraid, dread," of unknown origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 敏捷的， 灵活的， 机敏的

etymology.story: 1580s, from Middle French agile (14c.) and directly from Latin agilis "nimble, quick," from agere "to move, drive" (see act (n.)).
1540s, variant spelling of corps (q.v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 有声的，浊音的；声音的，语音的；声学的

etymology.story: "pertaining to sound," 1793, from Greek phone "voice," from PIE root *bha- (2) "to speak, tell, say" (see fame (n.)) + -ic..

meanings.definitionCn: 再斟满； 添加， 补充

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + full满，全 + 有点…/像…

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French repleniss-, extended present participle stem of replenir "to fill up," from re-, here probably an intensive prefix, + -plenir, from Latin plenus "full" (see plenary).

meanings.definitionCn: 有责任的：顺从的，服从的；有义务的；经得起检验的

etymology.story: 1590s, "liable," from Anglo-French amenable, Middle French amener "answerable" (to the law), from à "to" (see ad-) + mener "to lead," from Latin minare "to drive (cattle) with shouts," variant of minari "threaten" (see menace (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 陶冶， 教化； 启发， 启迪

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to build, construct," also, in figurative use, "to build up morally or in faith," from Old French edefiier (12c., Modern French édifier) "build; install; teach, instruct (morally)," from Latin aedificare "to build, construct," in Late Latin "improve spiritually, instruct" (see edifice).

meanings.definitionCn: 终年的,四季不断的,持久的

morphology.mnemonic: 通过/彻底 + 年 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1640s, "evergreen," formed in English from Latin perennis "lasting through the year (or years)," from per- "through" (see per) + annus "year" (see annual).

meanings.definitionCn: 可以忍受的； 足以支撑的； 养得起的

etymology.story: 1610s, "bearable," from sustain + -able.

meanings.definitionCn: 炎症性的；煽动性的；激动的

morphology.mnemonic: in + to burn, set on fire + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: "tending to rouse passions or desires," 1711, a figurative use from Latin inflammat-, past participle stem of inflammare "to set on fire" (see inflame) + -ory.

meanings.definitionCn: 酒，含酒精饮料；溶液；液体；烈酒

root.meaning: sum of qualities that define a person

morphology.mnemonic: sum of qualities that define a person + ic

etymology.story: adjective and noun both first attested 1660s, from character + -istic on model of Greek kharakteristikos.
1702, "to leave one's companions," from Latin secedere "go away, withdraw, separate; rebel, revolt" (see secession).

mid-15c., intransitive probably from Anglo-French or Old French reuser, ruser, originally used in English of hawks shaking the feathers of the body, but like many hawking terms it is of obscure origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 粗野的；粗鲁的；粗鄙的；笨拙的；乡土气的

etymology.story: 1560s, from boor (n.) + -ish.

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 抓住/理解

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Latin reprehendere "to blame, censure, rebuke; seize, restrain," literally "pull back, hold back," from re- "back" (see re-) + prehendere "to grasp, seize" (see prehensile)..
1550s, from Middle French disparité (16c.), from Late Latin disparitatem (nominative disparitas) "inequality," from dis- "not" (see dis-) + paritas "parity" (see parity).

meanings.definitionCn: 含糖的；极甜的；和颜悦色的

etymology.story: 1670s, "of or like sugar," from Medieval Latin saccharum "sugar," from Latin saccharon "sugar," from Greek sakkharon, from Pali sakkhara, from Sanskrit sarkara "gravel, grit" (see sugar).

meanings.definitionCn: 重复， 复现或复制； 再制造； 再生

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 折叠/交织 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "repeat," from Latin replicatus, past participle of replicare (see reply).

meanings.definitionCn: 断断续续的；散漫的；不连贯的，无条理的

etymology.story: 1580s, "skipping about," from Latin desultorius "hasty, casual, superficial," adjective form of desultor (n.) "a rider in the circus who jumps from one horse to another while they are in gallop," from desul-, stem of desilire "jump down," from de- "down" (see de-) + salire "to jump, leap" (see salient (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 吝啬的；过于节俭的；质量差的

etymology.story: 1590s, from Latin parsimonia "frugality, thrift" (see parsimony) + -ous.

early 14c., transitive and intransitive, from Old French assembler "come together, join, unite; gather" (11c.), from Latin assimulare "to make like, liken, compare; copy, imitate; feign, pretend," later "to gather together," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + simulare "to make like" (see simulation).

meanings.definitionCn: 显而易见的，显著的；值得注意的

etymology.story: 1796, "worthy of notice," from notice (n.) + -able.

meanings.definitionCn: 过渡；转变；[分子生物] 转换；变调

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Latin transitionem (nominative transitio) "a going across or over," noun of action from past participle stem of transire "go or cross over" (see transient)..

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1510s, "condition of being at one (with others)," from atone + -ment.

meanings.definitionCn: 骑士精神（复数chivalries）；骑士制度
击退；抵制；使厌恶；使不愉快

etymology.story: early 15c., "to drive away, remove," from Old French repeller or directly from Latin repellere "to drive back," from re- "back" (see re-) + pellere "to drive, strike" (see pulse (n.1)).

root.meaning: to be scorekeeper, to keep the score in a game or contest

morphology.mnemonic: 不足/在…之下 + to be scorekeeper, to keep the score in a game or contest

etymology.story: 1771, "to draw a line under," from under + score (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 系列，连续；[电] 串联；级数；丛书

etymology.story: 1610s, "a number or set of things of one kind arranged in a line," from Latin series "row, chain, series, sequence, succession," from serere "to join, link, bind together, arrange, attach, put; join in speech, discuss," from PIE root *ser- (3) "to line up, join" (source also of Sanskrit sarat- "thread," Greek eirein "to fasten together in rows," Gothic sarwa (plural) "armor, arms," Old Norse sörve "necklace of stringed pearls," Old Irish sernaid "he joins together," Welsh ystret "row").
1590s, from Latin timiditas "fearfulness, faint-heartedness, cowardice," from timidus "fearful, afraid" (see timid)..

meanings.definitionCn: 富于想象的；有创造力的

etymology.story: late 14c., ymaginatyf, "pertaining to imagination; forming images, given to imagining," from Old French imaginatif and directly from Medieval Latin imaginativus, from imaginat-, stem of Latin imaginari "picture to oneself" (see imagine).

meanings.definitionCn: 血清；浆液；免疫血清；乳清；树液

etymology.story: 1670s, "watery animal fluid," from Latin serum "watery fluid, whey," from PIE root *ser- (2) "to run, flow" (source also of Greek oros "whey;" Sanskrit sarah "flowing," sarit "brook, river").

meanings.definitionCn: 玩厌了的，厌烦于享乐的
名人，名流；著名，名声，名望

etymology.story: late 14c., "solemn rite or ceremony," from Old French celebrité "celebration" or directly from Latin celibritatem (nominative celebritas) "multitude, fame," from celeber "frequented, populous" (see celebrate).
1824; see archaeology + -ist..

root.meaning: to reckon for money given or received, render a reckoning,

morphology.mnemonic: to reckon for money given or received, render a reckoning, + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: mid-15c., "accounting officer, one who renders accounts," from Old French acuntant (Modern French accomptant), from present participle of accompter (see account (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 冷漠的；无动于衷的，缺乏兴趣的

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 感情/疾病 + ic

etymology.story: 1744, from apathy + -ic, on model of pathetic..

meanings.definitionCn: 天生的， 先天的， 天赋的

etymology.story: Old English inboren "native to a place, indigenous, aboriginal," from in (prep.) "within" + boren "brought forth" (see born).

late 14c., "injurious, dangerous," also "absurd, illogical" (senses now obsolete), from Latin inconvenientem (nominative inconveniens) "unsuitable, not accordant, dissimilar," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + convenientem (see convenient).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 感觉/感知 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1714, from obsolete French presentiment (Modern French
1550s, from Middle French absurde (16c.), from Latin absurdus "out of tune; foolish" (see absurdity).

root.meaning: to make smooth or to give shape on a block

morphology.mnemonic: to make smooth or to give shape on a block + ade

root.meaning: a purgative, an act of purging

morphology.mnemonic: a purgative, an act of purging + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: late 15c., "shot, missiles;" later "a stroke in drawing, a short line" (1580s), from Middle French trait "line, stroke, feature, tract," from Latin tractus "drawing, drawing out, dragging, pulling," later "line drawn, feature," from past participle stem of trahere "to pull, draw" (see tract (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: [化学] 溶质；[化学] 溶解物

etymology.story: 1890, "dissolved," from Latin solutus, past participle of solvere (see solve).

meanings.definitionCn: 有用的，可供使用的；耐用的

etymology.story: "ready to do service," early 14c., from Old French servicable, from servise (see service (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 路标， 地标； 里程碑

etymology.story: Old English landmearc "object set up to mark the boundaries of a kingdom, estate, etc.," from land (n.) + mearc (see mark (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 第一百的；一百分之一的
第一百，第一百个；百分之一

root.meaning: division of a county or shire with its own court

morphology.mnemonic: division of a county or shire with its own court + 热

etymology.story: early 14c., "one of 100 equal parts," from hundred + -th (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 无法攻取的；不受影响的；要塞坚固的；不可受孕的

etymology.story: early 15c., imprenable "impossible to capture," from Old French imprenable "invulnerable," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + Old French prenable "assailable, vulnerable" (see pregnable).
1620s, back-formation from veneration, or else from Latin veneratus, past participle of venerari "to reverence, worship."

meanings.definitionCn: 空的；空虚的；空洞的；无意义的

etymology.story: 1640s, "empty" (implied in vacuousness), from Latin vacuus "empty, void, free" (see vacuum (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 事后的想法；后来添加的东西

etymology.story: 1660s, from after + thought..

meanings.definitionCn: 水槽，水槽；低谷期；饲料槽；低气压

etymology.story: Old English trog "wooden vessel, tray, hollow vessel, canoe," from Proto-Germanic *trugaz (source also of Old Frisian, Old Saxon, Old Norse trog, Middle Dutch troch, Dutch trog, Old High German troc, German trog), from PIE *dru-ko-, from root *dru-, *deru- "wood, tree" (see tree (n.)).
"flattering speech," 1590s, from blandish + -ment.
Old English on bedde "in bed," from a- (1) + bed (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 矛盾的；反对的；反驳的；抗辩的

etymology.story: 1530s, "mutually opposed, at variance," from Late Latin contradictorius "containing a contradiction or objection," from contradictus, past participle of contradicere (see contradiction).

meanings.definitionCn: 多汁的；多水分的；多汁性的
陪伴，陪同；伴随，一起发生，伴奏

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + com + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: early 15c., "to be in company with," from Middle French accompagner, from Old French acompaignier (12c.) "take as a companion," from à "to" (see ad-) + compaignier, from compaign (see companion).
1590s, "to meet and do away with," from Late Latin obviatus, past participle of obviare "act contrary to, go against," from Latin obvius "that is in the way, that moves against" (see obvious).
early 15c., from Middle French probité, from Latin probitatem (nominative probitas) "uprightness, honesty," from probus "worthy, good" (see prove)..

meanings.definitionCn: 明了的；清晰明白的；易懂的

etymology.story: late 15c., from Latin perspicuus "transparent, clear, evident," from perspicere "look through, look closely at" (see perspective).

meanings.definitionCn: 立即的,即时的;直接的,最接近的

etymology.story: late 14c., "intervening, interposed;" early 15c., "with nothing interposed; direct," also with reference to time, "without delay, instant," from Old French immediat (14c.), from Late Latin immediatus "without anything between," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + mediatus "in the middle" (see mediate)..
1630s, from Latin excerptum "an extract, selection," noun use of neuter past participle of excerpere "to extract" (see excerpt (v.)).
1812, "dullness," from French platitude "flatness, vapidness" (late 17c.), from Old French plat "flat" (see plateau (n.)); formed on analogy of latitude, etc.

morphology.mnemonic: 向上/提高 + anything plaited or entwined

etymology.story: Old English upbregdan "bring forth as a ground for censure," from up (adv.) + bregdan "move quickly, intertwine" (see braid (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 好捣乱的；好搞派系的；源于派别的

etymology.story: "given to faction, turbulently partisan, dissentious," 1530s, from Middle French factieux and directly from Latin factiosus "partisan, seditious, inclined to form parties," from factionem "political party" (see faction (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: intra + 引导 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1540s, "type of pantomime dance;" 1580s, "clown," from Middle French bouffon (16c.), from Italian buffone "jester," from buffa "joke, jest, pleasantry," from buffare "to puff out the cheeks," a comic gesture, of echoic origin.
1550s, a contraction of a pece (mid-15c.), originally of coins, objects for sale, etc.

meanings.definitionCn: 可怕的，耸人听闻的；火烧似的；苍白的；血红的；华丽而庸俗的

etymology.story: 1650s, "pale," from Latin luridus "pale yellow, ghastly," of uncertain origin, perhaps cognate with Greek khloros (see Chloe).

meanings.definitionCn: 先占；先取；以先买权获得

morphology.mnemonic: to swear profanely + …的场所/与…有关

meanings.definitionCn: 无忧无虑；漫不经心；满不在乎

etymology.story: 1820, from French insouciance "heedless indifference or unconcern," from insouciant "carelessness, thoughtlessness, heedlessness," from in- "not" (see in- (1)) + souciant "caring," present participle of soucier "to care," from Latin sollicitare "to agitate" (see solicit)..
1801, probably an extension of prim (q.v.) in its verbal "dress up" sense; compare Scottish primpit (c.

1530s, "to give (legal) confirmation to," from Latin corroboratus, past participle of corroborare "to strengthen, invigorate," from com- "together" or "thoroughly" (see com-) + roborare "to make strong," from  robur, robus "strength," (see robust).

meanings.definitionCn: 使…变得淡而无味；除掉…的特性
变得平淡无奇；丧失特性

etymology.story: 1660s, from Italian blando "delicate," or Old French bland "flattering, complimentary," both from Latin blandus "smooth-talking, flattering, alluring," perhaps from PIE *mlad-, nasalized variant of *meld-, extended form of root *mel- (see melt).
1550s, from adverbial phrase atonen (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 贬抑， 贬低； 轻蔑， 轻视

etymology.story: early 14c., "degrade socially," from Old French desparagier (Modern French déparager) "reduce in rank, degrade, devalue, depreciate," originally "to marry unequally," and thus by extension the disgrace or dishonor involved in this, from des- "away" (see dis-) + parage "rank, lineage" (see peer (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 美味的东西；令人喜爱的东西

etymology.story: "delicious," 1899, from baby talk.

meanings.definitionCn: 财务主管；会计员；领取奖学金的学生

etymology.story: "treasurer of a college," 1580s, from Anglo-Latin burser "treasurer" (13c.), from Medieval Latin bursarius "purse-bearer," from bursa (see purse (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 乌托邦的；空想的；理想化的

etymology.story: 1550s, with reference to More's fictional country; 1610s as "extravagantly ideal, impossibly visionary," from utopia + -an.

meanings.definitionCn: 可触知的；明确的；确实的

etymology.story: 1580s, "capable of being touched," from Middle French tangible and directly from Late Latin tangibilis "that may be touched," from Latin tangere "to touch" (see tangent (adj.)).

"be about to happen" (usually of something unwanted), 1590s, from Latin impendere "to hang over;" figuratively "to be imminent, be near," from assimilated form of in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + pendere "hang" (see pendant).

meanings.definitionCn: 虔诚的；敬神的；可嘉的；尽责的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Latin pius "dutiful, devout, conscientious, religious; faithful to kindred; inspired by friendship, prompted by natural affections," perhaps [Klein] related to Latin purus "pure, clean" (see pure).
early 15c., "to bring together" (transitive), from Latin congestus, past participle of congerere "to bring together, pile up," from com- "together" (see com-) + gerere "to carry, perform" (see gest).
1670s, coined by Dryden (as wittycism) from witty on model of criticism..

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 近/接近 + 使…/具有…的 + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: 1742, from approximate (adj.) + -ly (2)..
1813, from French diplomate, a back-formation from diplomatique (see diplomatic) on model of aristocrate from aristocratique..
"imaginary being or demon, credited with causing nightmares, and, in male form, consorting with women in their sleep," c.

1650s, from French hutte "a cottage" (16c.), from Middle High German hütte "cottage, hut," probably from Proto-Germanic *hudjon-, which is related to the root of Old English hydan "to hide," from PIE *keudh-, from root (s)keu- "to cover, conceal" (see hide (n.1)).

1530s, "workmanship, the making of anything by craft or skill," from Middle French artifice "skill, cunning" (14c.), from Latin artificium "a profession, trade, employment, craft; making by art," from artifex (genitive artificis) "craftsman, artist," from stem of ars "art" (see art (n.)) + facere "to make, do" (see factitious).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 劳动/工作 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1871, back-formation from collaborator.

morphology.mnemonic: 超/在…之上 + 做/制造 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., in anatomical and mathematical uses, "of or relating to a surface," from Late Latin superficialis "of or pertaining to the surface," from superficies "surface, upper side, top," from super "above, over" (see super-) + facies "form, face" (see face (n.)).
1680s, from Latin opprobrium "disgrace, infamy, scandal, dishonor," from opprobare (see opprobrious)..
"optical illusion of water in sandy deserts," 1812, from French mirage (1753), from se mirer "to be reflected," from Latin mirare (see mirror (n.)).

1763, "blind alley, dead end," from French impasse "impassable road; blind alley; impasse" (18c.), from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + passe "a passing," from passer "to pass" (see pass (v.)).

"to tear roughly," early 15c., from Latin laceratus, past participle of lacerare "tear to pieces, mangle," figuratively, "to slander, censure, abuse," from lacer "torn, mangled," from PIE root *lek- "to rend, tear" (source also of Greek lakis "tatter, rag," lakizein "to tear to pieces;" Latin lacinia "flap of a garment," lancinare "to pierce, stab;" Russian lochma "rag, tatter, scrap;" Albanian l'akur "naked").

meanings.definitionCn: 拆除； 破坏， 毁坏

morphology.mnemonic: 人民 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1540s, from Old French demolition (14c.) "demolition; defeat, rout," from Latin demolitionem (nominative demolitio), noun of action from past participle stem of demoliri "tear down" (see demolish).

meanings.definitionCn: 移置， 转移； 取代

morphology.mnemonic: 不/分离 + 取悦 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1610s, "removal from office;" see displace + -ment.

morphology.mnemonic: make a formal speech + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "prayer," from Late Latin orationem (nominative oratio) "a speaking, speech, discourse; language, faculty of speech, mode of expressing; prayer," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin orare "to pray, plead, speak before an assembly" (see orator).

meanings.definitionCn: 意外事故；伤亡人员；急诊室

etymology.story: early 15c., "chance, accident; incidental charge," from casual (adj.) on model of royalty, penalty, etc.

meanings.definitionCn: 古怪的；异想天开的；反复无常的

root.meaning: caprice, fancy, sudden turn or inclination of the mind

morphology.mnemonic: caprice, fancy, sudden turn or inclination of the mind + 具有…特征/名词化 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1650s, from whimsy + -ical.
1560s, a type of solid figure, from Late Latin prisma, from Greek prisma (Euclid), literally "something sawed," from prizein "to saw" (see prion).

morphology.mnemonic: 强壮/价值 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1650s, "pertaining or relating to leave-taking," from Latin valedictum (past participle of valedicere; see valediction) + -ory.

meanings.definitionCn: 偶像崇拜；盲目崇拜；邪神崇拜

morphology.mnemonic: a person so adored, human object of adoring devotion + ater + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: "worship of idols and images," mid-13c., from Old French idolatrie (12c.), from Vulgar Latin idolatria, contraction of Late Latin idololatria (Tertullian), from Ecclesiastical Greek eidololatria "worship of idols," from eidolon "image" (see idol) + latreia "worship, service" (see -latry)..

meanings.definitionCn: 好学的， 学习勤勉的； 专心的； 谨慎的， 认真的， 仔细的

morphology.mnemonic: stud + 具有…特征/名词化 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1670s, "absurd thing;" 1680s, "words or actions meant to invoke ridicule," from French ridicule, noun use of adjective (15c.), or from Latin ridiculum "laughing matter, joke," from noun use of neuter of ridiculus (see ridiculous)..

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + 推/驱动 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1610s, "expulsion," noun of action formed from propuls-, past participle stem of Latin propellere "to propel" (see propel).
1805, American English, from French butte, from Old French but "mound, knoll" (see butt (n.3)).

root.meaning: be conscious inwardly of (one's state or condition) is from 1680s. Meaning

morphology.mnemonic: be conscious inwardly of (one's state or condition) is from 1680s. Meaning  + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: late 14c., in reference to the body or its parts, "having the function of sensation;" also (early 15c.) "pertaining to the faculty of the soul that receives and analyzes sensory information;" from Old French sensitif "capable of feeling" (13c.) and directly from Medieval Latin sensitivus "capable of sensation," from Latin sensus, past participle of sentire "feel perceive" (see sense (n.)).
1620s, from Latin scintillatus, past participle of scintillare "to sparkle, glitter, gleam, flash," from scintilla "spark" (see scintilla).

Old English sengan "to burn lightly, burn the edges" (of hair, wings, etc.), from Proto-Germanic *sangjanan (source also of Old Frisian of-sendza, Middle Dutch singhen, Dutch zengen, Old High German sengan, German sengen "to singe").
1838, from Latin iratus "angry, enraged, violent, furious," past participle of irasci "grow angry," from ira "anger" (see ire)..

meanings.definitionCn: 印象；效果，影响；压痕，印记；感想；曝光（衡量广告被显示的次数。打开一个带有该广告的网页，则该广告的impression 次数增加一次）

morphology.mnemonic: in + device for pressing cloth + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "mark produced by pressure," also "image produced in the mind or emotions by something external," from Old French impression "print, stamp; a pressing on the mind" (13c.), from Latin impressionem (nominative impressio) "a pressing into, onset, attack," figuratively "a perception, mental impression," literally "a pressing into," from imprimere "press into or upon" (see impress (v.1)).
"unfairness," 1550s, from in- (1) "not, opposite of" + equity.

meanings.definitionCn: 意志的；表示意志的；发自意志的

etymology.story: also pepsine, fermin in gastric juice, used medicinally for cases of indigestion, 1844, coined in German (Theodor Schwann, 1835) from Greek pepsis "digestion," from stem pep- (see peptic) + -in (2)..
Old English gescildan, from the root of shield (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 雕像般的；轮廓清晰的；均衡的

etymology.story: "of or like a statue" in some sense, especially "stately, having a formal dignity and beauty, tall and solidly built," 1823, from statue, patterned on picturesque.

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: late 14c., "study of the structure of living beings;" c.
1520s, "graciousness," from French gratuité (14c.) or directly from Medieval Latin gratuitatem (nominative gratuitas) "free gift," probably from Latin gratuitus "free, freely given" (see gratuitous).

late 12c., from Latin, literally "I believe," first word of the Apostles' and Nicene Creeds, first person singular present indicative of credere "to believe," from PIE compound *kerd-dhe- "to believe," literally "to put one's heart" (source also of Old Irish cretim, Irish creidim, Welsh credu "I believe," Sanskrit śrad-dhā- "faith"), from PIE root *kerd- (1) "heart" (see heart (n.)).
1650s, from instant (n.) on model of spontaneous, etc.

1570s, "fact, occurrence," from Late Latin phænomenon, from Greek phainomenon "that which appears or is seen," noun use of neuter present participle of phainesthai "to appear," passive of phainein (see phantasm).

Old English wriðan (transitive) "to twist or bend," earlier "to bind or fetter," from Proto-Germanic *writhanan (source also of North Frisian wrial, Old High German ridan, Old Norse riða, Middle Swedish vriþa, Middle Danish vride), from PIE *wreit- "to turn, bend" (see wreath).

meanings.definitionCn: 季度的，按季度的；一年四次的
按季度，[农] 一季一次地；纵横四分地

morphology.mnemonic: to put up soldiers + 以…方式/副词化

mid-14c., from Old English Zefferus, from Latin Zephyrus (source also of French zéphire, Spanish zefiro, Italian zeffiro), from Greek Zephyros "the west wind" (sometimes personified as a god), probably related to zophos "the west, the dark region, darkness, gloom." Extended sense of "mild breeze" is c.
"that can be surrendered or given up," 1610s; see alien (adj.) + -able.

meanings.definitionCn: 大修道院，大寺院；修道院中全体修士或修女

etymology.story: mid-13c., "convent headed by an abbot or abbess," from Anglo-French abbeie, Old French abaïe, from Late Latin abbatia, from abbas (genitive abbatis); see abbot..

meanings.definitionCn: 情感；敏感性；感觉；识别力

etymology.story: late 14c., "capability of being perceived by the senses; ability to sense or perceive," from Old French sensibilite, from Late Latin sensibilitatem (nominative sensibilitas), from sensibilis (see sensible).

morphology.mnemonic: 书 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1670s, "the writing of books," from Greek bibliographia "the writing of books," from biblio- + graphos "(something) drawn or written" (see -graphy).

morphology.mnemonic: rise up, lift, sustain + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1570s, perhaps from Spanish boyante, present participle of boyar "to float," from boya "buoy," from Dutch boei (see buoy (n.)).

"unmoved by danger, undaunted," 1690s, from French intrépide (16c.) and directly from Latin intrepidus "unshaken, undaunted, not alarmed," from in- "not" (see in- (1)) + trepidus "alarmed," from PIE *trep-(1) "to tremble" (see trepidation).

meanings.definitionCn: 辛勤努力；经受分娩的阵痛

etymology.story: "labor, toil," mid-13c., from Old French travail "work, labor, toil, suffering or painful effort, trouble; arduous journey" (12c.), from travailler "to toil, labor," originally "to trouble, torture, torment," from Vulgar Latin *tripaliare "to torture," from *tripalium (in Late Latin trepalium) "instrument of torture," probably from Latin tripalis "having three stakes" (from tria "three;" see three + palus "stake;" see pale (n.)), which sounds ominous, but the exact notion is obscure.

morphology.mnemonic: gener + 与…有关的/形容词 + ize

etymology.story: 1751, probably a new formation from general (adj.) + -ize.

meanings.definitionCn: 信任， 相信， 委任， 授权； 把…归于

etymology.story: 1610s, from French accréditer, from à "to" (see ad-) + créditer "to credit" (someone with a sum), from crédit "credit" (see credit (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 冷凝器；[电] 电容器；[光] 聚光器

etymology.story: 1680s, agent noun from condense.

1530s, "fearful, timid," from Latin meticulosus "fearful, timid," literally "full of fear," from metus "fear, dread, apprehension, anxiety," of unknown origin.

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 判定/控告 + 使…/具有…的

morphology.mnemonic: jubil + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French jubilacion "jubilation, rejoicing," and directly from Late Latin iubilationem (nominative iubilatio) "a shouting for joy," noun of action from past participle stem of iubilare "to let out whoops, shout for joy" (see jubilant)..

meanings.definitionCn: [雷达] 雷达，无线电探测器

etymology.story: "electronic system for locating objects by means of radio waves," 1941, acronym (more or less) from radio detecting and ranging.

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + sume + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French presumtuex (12c.; Modern French présomptueux) and directly from Late Latin praesumptuosus, variant of praesumptiosus, from past participle stem of Latin praesumere "anticipate," in Late Latin, "assume" (see presumption).

1540s, "a pinch, a nipping," from obsolete verb twinge "to pinch, tweak," from Old English twengan "to pinch," from Proto-Germanic *twangjan (source also of Old Frisian thwinga, Old Norse þvinga, Danish tvinge, Dutch dwingen, Old High German thwingan, German zwingen "to compel, force"), from PIE *twengh- "to press in on" (see thong).

"relating to muscular motion," 1841, from Greek kinetikos "moving, putting in motion," from kinetos "moved," verbal adjective of kinein "to move" (see cite).
1794, intransitive, back-formation from rotation.

斯巴达的；斯巴达式的；（生活方式）简朴的，清苦的。

etymology.story: early 15c., "citizen of the ancient Greek city of Sparta" (q.v.), from Latin Spartanus.

meanings.definitionCn: 不重要的；不合理的；不合逻辑的

etymology.story: 1620s, "characterized by inconsequence;" 1782, "not worth noticing;" see inconsequent + -al (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 遗传； 继承， 继承物； 遗产， 遗赠

1811, past participle adjective from verb convolute (1690s), from Latin convolutus, past participle of convolvere (see convolution); or perhaps a back-formation from convolution.

"concise, abrupt," 1580s, literally "of or pertaining to the region around ancient Sparta in Greece, probably via Latin Laconicus "of Laconia," from Greek Lakonikos "Laconian, of Laconia," adjective from Lakon "person from Lakonia," the district around Sparta in southern Greece in ancient times, whose inhabitants famously cultivated the skill of saying much in few words.

late 14c., "creeping or crawling animal," from Old French reptile (early 14c.) and directly from Late Latin reptile, noun use of neuter of reptilis (adj.) "creping, crawling," from rept-, past participle stem of repere "to crawl, creep," from PIE root *rep- "to creep, crawl" (source also of Lithuanian replioju "to creep").

meanings.definitionCn: [数] 整数；整体；完整的事物

etymology.story: "a whole number" (as opposed to a fraction), 1570s, from Latin integer (adj.) "intact, whole, complete," figuratively, "untainted, upright," literally "untouched," from in- "not" (see in- (1)) + root of tangere "to touch," from PIE root *tag- "to touch, handle" (see tangent (adj.)).

1590s, with -ize + Latin Tantalus, from Greek Tantalos, king of Phrygia, son of Zeus, father of Pelops and Niobe, punished in the afterlife (for an offense variously given) by being made to stand in a river up to his chin, under branches laden with fruit, all of which withdrew from his reach whenever he tried to eat or drink.

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 取/抓 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1540s, from Medieval Latin receptivus, from Latin recipere (see receive).

mid-15c., from Middle English astouned, astoned (c.

morphology.mnemonic: 松开/解决 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "a solving or being solved," from Old French solucion "division, dissolving; explanation; payment" or directly from Latin solutionem (nominative solutio) "a loosening or unfastening," noun of action from past participle stem of solvere "to loosen, untie, solve, dissolve" (see solve).
1560s, from Latin extrudere "to thrust out, drive away" (see extrusion).

1580s, from Latin opponentem (nominative opponens), present participle of opponere "oppose, object to," literally "set against, set opposite," from ob "against" (see ob-) + ponere "to put, set, place" (see position (n.))..

morphology.mnemonic: su + 填充/补充 + …的场所/与…有关

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + celer + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin acceleratus, past participle of accelerare "to hasten,  quicken," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + celerare "hasten," from celer "swift" (see celerity).

meanings.definitionCn: 洪水；一阵；大雨；突然迸发

etymology.story: early 15c., originally Scottish and northern English, "a sudden flood, especially one caused by heavy rains or a snowmelt," of unknown origin.

etymology.story: 1580s, "relief, raised ornament on vessels, etc.," from Latin emblema "inlaid ornamental work," from Greek emblema (genitive emblematos) "an insertion," from emballein "to insert," literally "to throw in," from assimilated form of en "in" (see en- (2)) + ballein "to throw" (see ballistics).

morphology.mnemonic: 智慧 + 主义/学说/运动
