etymology.story: mid-14c., "to understand," from Latin comprehendere "to take together, to unite; include; seize" (of catching fire or the arrest of criminals); also "to comprehend, perceive" (to seize or take in the mind), from com- "completely" (see com-) + prehendere "to catch hold of, seize" (see prehensile).
mid-15c., from Middle French multiplicité, from Late Latin multiplicitas "manifoldness, multiplicity," from Latin multiplic- (see multiple).

morphology.mnemonic: syn + 时间 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1660s, "existing or happening at the same time," from Late Latin synchronus "simultaneous," from Greek synkhronos "happening at the same time," from syn- "together" (see syn-) + khronos "time" (see chrono-).
1580s, from Middle French bataillon (16c.), from Italian battaglione "battle squadron," from diminutive of Vulgar Latin battalia "battle," from Latin bauttere "to beat" (see batter (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 猥亵的话；粗俗下流的言语

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old French ribauderie "debauchery, licentiousness," from ribalt (see ribald)..
1706 (plural bronchi), from Greek bronkhos "the wind pipe" (see bronchial)..

Old English alter, altar, from Latin altare (plural altaria) "high altar, altar for sacrifice to the great gods," perhaps originally meaning "burnt offerings" (compare Latin adolere "to worship, to offer sacrifice, to honor by burning sacrifices to"), but influenced by Latin altus "high." In Middle English, often auter, from Old French auter.

1844, from French protéine, coined 1838 by Dutch chemist Gerhard Johan Mulder (1802-1880), perhaps on suggestion of Berzelius, from Greek proteios "the first quality," from protos "first" (see proto-) + -ine (2).

"small quantity or portion," late 15c., Scottish, from Latin modicum "a little," noun use of neuter of modicus "moderate, having a proper measure; ordinary, scanty, small, few," from modus "measure, manner" (see mode (n.1))..

morphology.mnemonic: 年 + 与…有关的/形容词

meanings.definitionCn: 独占的;排他的;孤高的;唯一的;高级的

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 关闭 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "so as to exclude;" 1560s, "that excludes," from Medieval Latin exclusivus, from exclus-, past participle stem of excludere (see exclude).

meanings.definitionCn: 盲从的；合于逻辑的；缺乏独创性的

etymology.story: "given to following leaders," 1630s, from Latin sequac-, stem of sequax "that follows, following, seeking after," from sequi "to follow" (see sequel) + -ous.
late 12c., from Old French poverte "poverty, misery, wretched condition" (Modern French pauvreté), from Latin paupertatem (nominative paupertas) "poverty," from pauper "poor" (see poor (adj.)).

1590s, apparently diminutive and frequentative of Middle English dwinen "waste away, fade, vanish," from Old English dwinan, from Proto-Germanic *dwinan (source also of Dutch dwijnen "to vanish," Old Norse dvina, Danish tvine, Low German dwinen), from PIE *dheu- (3) "to die" (see die (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 复杂的，令人费解的；令人困惑的
(尤指金属表面)失去光泽；玷污
早期的；刚开始的；未充分发展的

etymology.story: "recently or just begun," 1530s, from Latin inchoatus, past participle of inchoare, alteration of incohare "commence, begin," probably originally "to hitch up," traditionally derived from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + a verb from cohum "strap (fastened to the oxen's yoke)," a word of obscure origin.

meanings.definitionCn: [法] 遗嘱；圣约；确实的证明

morphology.mnemonic: 见证/证明 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story:
late 13c., "last will disposing of property," from Latin testamentum "a last will, publication of a will," from testari "make a will, be witness to," from testis "witness," from PIE *tri-st-i- "third person standing by," from root *tris- "three" (see three) on the notion of "third person, disinterested witness."

Use in reference to the two divisions of the Bible (early 14c.) is from Late Latin vetus testamentum and novum testamentum, loan-translations of Greek palaia diatheke and kaine diatheke.

meanings.definitionCn: 增加；爬上；使骑上马；安装，架置；镶嵌，嵌入；准备上演；成立（军队等）
山峰；底座；乘骑用马；攀，登；运载工具；底座

etymology.story: 1610s, from Middle French acquiescer (16c.), from Latin acquiescere "to become quiet, remain at rest," thus "be satisfied with," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + quiescere "to become quiet," from quies (genitive quietis) "rest, quiet" (see quiet (n.)).

also hodge podge, hodge-podge, early 15c., hogpoch, alteration of hotchpotch (late 14c.) "a kind of stew," especially "one made with goose, herbs, spices, wine, and other ingredients," earlier an Anglo-French legal term meaning "collection of property in a common 'pot' before dividing it equally" (late 13c.), from Old French hochepot "stew, soup." First element from hocher "to shake," from a Germanic source (such as Middle High German hotzen "shake")..
1570s, from requite + -al (2)..
1640s, from Latin aqua "water" (see aqua-) on analogy of French aqueux "watery" (16c., which, however, is from Late Latin aquosus "abounding in water").

morphology.mnemonic: 光 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1839, from photo- + -graphy.

meanings.definitionCn: 内脏的；出于本能的；发自肺腑的；粗俗的

morphology.mnemonic: viscer + 统治 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1570s, "affecting inward feelings," from Middle French viscéral and directly from Medieval Latin visceralis "internal," from Latin viscera, plural of viscus "internal organ, inner parts of the body," of unknown origin.
1731, from Medieval Latin accentuatus, past participle of accentuare "to accent," from Latin accentus (see accent (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 独裁政治；专制政治；独裁政府；独裁统治的国家

morphology.mnemonic: 自我/自动 + 统治/权力

etymology.story: 1650s, "independent power, self-sustained power," from French autocratie, from Greek autokrateia "ruling by oneself," noun of state from autokrates (see autocrat).

meanings.definitionCn: 切断，毁坏；使…残缺不全；使…支离破碎

etymology.story: 1530s, of things; 1560s, of persons; from Latin mutilatus, past participle of mutilare "to cut off, lop off, cut short; maim, mutilate," from mutilus "maimed" (see mutilation).
Old English forðriht "direct, plain;" see forth + right (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 撩人的；骄奢淫逸的；沉溺酒色的

etymology.story: late 14c., "of or pertaining to desires or appetites," from Old French voluptueux, volumptueuse and directly from Latin voluptuosus "full of pleasure, delightful," from voluptas "pleasure, delight, enjoyment, satisfaction," from volup "pleasurably," perhaps ultimately related to velle "to wish," from PIE *wel- (2) "to wish, will" (see will (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 无力的；无效的；虚弱的；阳萎的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 力量/能力 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: late 14c., "physically weak, enfeebled, crippled," from Old French impotent "powerless, weak, incapable of doing," from Latin imponentem (nominative impotens) "lacking control, powerless, feeble; lacking self-control," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + ponentem (nominative potens) "potent" (see potent).
1550s, from Medieval Latin algebra, from Arabic al jabr ("in vulgar pronunciation, al-jebr" [Klein]) "reunion of broken parts," as in computation, used 9c.

meanings.definitionCn: 贵族；贵族统治；上层社会；贵族政治

etymology.story: 1560s, from Middle French aristocracie (Modern French aristocratie), from Late Latin aristocratia, from Greek aristokratia "government or rule of the best," from aristos "best" (originally "most fitting," from PIE *ar-isto-, superlative form of *ar- "to fit together;" see arm (n.1)) + kratos "rule, power" (see -cracy).

mid-15c., "to take as spouse, marry," from Old French espouser "marry, take in marriage, join in marriage" (11c., Modern French épouser), from Latin sponsare, past participle of spondere "make an offering, perform a rite, promise secretly," hence "to engage oneself by ritual act" (see spondee).

meanings.definitionCn: 鼓舞人心的； 启发灵感的

etymology.story: mid-15c., acqueren, from Old French aquerre "acquire, gain, earn, procure," from Vulgar Latin *acquaerere, corresponding to Latin acquirere "to seek in addition to" (see acquisition).

meanings.definitionCn: 富饶的，肥沃的；能生育的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "bearing or producing abundantly," from Middle French fertil (15c.) and directly from Latin fertilis "bearing in abundance, fruitful, productive," from ferre "to bear" (see infer).

morphology.mnemonic: different in character or quality. + ity

etymology.story: mid-14c., "quality of being diverse," mostly in a neutral sense, from Old French diversité (12c.) "difference, diversity, unique feature, oddness:" also "wickedness, perversity," from Latin diversitatem (nominative diversitas) "contrariety, contradiction, disagreement;" also, as a secondary sense, "difference, diversity," from diversus "turned different ways" (in Late Latin "various"), past participle of divertere (see divert).
1540s, "auditory" (originally of confessions), from Medieval Latin auricularis, from Latin auricula (see auricle).

meanings.definitionCn: 红利，股息；回报，效益；被除数

morphology.mnemonic: watershed, separation between river valleys + end

etymology.story: late 15c., from Middle French dividende "a number divided by another," from Latin dividendum "thing to be divided," neuter gerundive of dividere (see divide).

1590s, from Middle French abstrus (16c.) or directly from Latin abstrusus "hidden, concealed, secret," past participle of abstrudere "conceal," literally "to thrust away," from ab- "away" (see ab-) + trudere "to thrust, push," from PIE *treud- "to press, push, squeeze" (see threat).

late 15c., "make slow or slower," from French retarder "restrain, hold (someone) back, keep (someone from doing something); come to a stop" (13c.) or directly from Latin retardare "make slow, delay, keep back, hinder" (see retardation).
(also steem, extyme), mid-14c., "account, value, worth," from French estime, from estimer (see esteem (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 向上的斜坡；[建] 上斜

etymology.story: 1610s, from Latin acclivitatem (nominative acclivitas) "an ascending direction, an upward steepness," from acclivis "mounting upwards, ascending," from ad- "up" (see ad-) + clivus "hill, a slope," from PIE *klei-wo-, suffixed form of *klei- "to lean" (see lean (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 比…重； 比…更重要； 胜过， 强于

morphology.mnemonic: 超过/外部 + bring one's influence to bear

etymology.story: 1590s, from out (adv.) + weigh (v.).
1530s, from Latin obfuscatus, past participle of obfuscare "to darken," from ob "over" (see ob-) + fuscare "to make dark," from fuscus "dark" (see dusk).

morphology.mnemonic: 呼叫/声音 + ifer + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1590s, a back-formation from vociferation and in part from Latin vociferatus, past participle of vociferari "to cry out, shout, exclaim," from voci-, stem of vox "voice" (see voice (n.)) + ferre "to carry" (see infer).
"existing or happening before the creation of the world," 1731; see ante- + mundane..
structural protein of connective tissue, 1843, from French collagène, from Greek kolla "glue" + -gen "giving birth to" (see -gen)..

morphology.mnemonic: a cumin seed splitter. + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: 1590s, from miser + -ly (1).
1650s, also impower, from assimilated form of en- (1) + power (n.).

morphology.mnemonic: own + er + 身份/状态/技能

etymology.story: 1580s, from owner + -ship.
early 15c., from Latin tumor "swelling, condition of being swollen, a tumor," from tumere "to swell" (see tumid)..
1713, "to turn into bone," a back-formation from ossification, or else modeled on French ossifier (18c.) and formed from Latin os (genitive ossis) "bone" (see osseous) + -fy.
mid-13c., "abundant, plentiful," Middle English compound of ful "full" (see full (adj.)) + -som "to a considerable degree" (see -some (1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 平静的；安详的；清澈的；晴朗的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "clear, calm," from Latin serenus "peaceful, calm, clear" (of weather), figuratively "cheerful, glad, tranquil," of uncertain origin; perhaps from a suffixed variant of PIE *ksero- "dry," source of Greek xeros "dry" (see xerasia).

meanings.definitionCn: 寓言式的；极为巨大的；极好的

morphology.mnemonic: that which is told, + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: early 15c., "mythical, legendary," from Latin fabulosus "celebrated in fable;" also "rich in myths," from fabula "story, tale" (see fable (n.)).

1520s, from Late Latin dilemma, from Greek dilemma "double proposition," a technical term in rhetoric, from di- "two" + lemma "premise, anything received or taken," from root of lambanein "to take" (see analemma).

meanings.definitionCn: 安慰；抚慰；使快乐（过去式solaced，过去分词solaced，现在分词solacing，第三人称单数solaces）

etymology.story: "comfort, console in grief," late 13c.; also in Middle English "entertain, amuse, please," from Old French solacier "comfort, console" (often with a sexual connotation) and directly from Medieval Latin solatiare "give solace, console" (source also of Spanish solazar, Italian sollazzare), from Latin solacium (see solace (n.)).
1610s, "overtaken by night," past participle adjective from belate "to make late, detain," from be- + late.

"feel regret," Old English hreowan "make sorry, distress, grieve" (class II strong verb; past tense hreaw, past participle hrowen), from Proto-Germanic *khrewan (source also of Old Frisian riowa, Middle Dutch rouwen, Old Dutch hrewan, German reuen "to sadden, cause repentance"); in part, blended with Old English weak verb hreowian "feel pain or sorrow," and perhaps influenced by Old Norse hryggja "make sad," both from Proto-Germanic *khruwjan, all from PIE root *kreue- (2) "to push, strike" (see anacrusis).

meanings.definitionCn: 煽动性的；扰乱治安的；参与煽动的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Middle French seditieux, from Latin seditiosus "full of discord, factious, mutinous," from seditio (see sedition).
1640s, from dis- + regard.
also life-time, early 13c., from life (n.) + time (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 斜面；伪善之言；黑话；角落
行话的；哀诉声的；假仁假义的

morphology.mnemonic: 使成为/放入 + 做/驱动

etymology.story: early 15c., "act the part of, represent in performance," from en- (1) "make, put in" + act (v.).

"imprudent, not discrete, lacking good judgment," early 15c., from Medieval Latin indiscretus, from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + Latin discretus "separated, distinct" (in Medieval Latin "discerning, careful"), past participle of discernere "distinguish" (see discern).

meanings.definitionCn: 奸诈的，叛逆的，背叛的；危险的；不牢靠的

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old French trecheros, tricheros "deceitful" (12c.), from trecheor, tricheor "cheat, deceiver, liar, impostor, trickster," agent noun from trechier, trichier "to cheat, trick" (see trick (n.)).
1670s, from scrutiny + -ize.

1540s, from Latin extinguere/exstinguere "quench, put out (what is burning), wipe out, obliterate," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + stinguere "quench," apparently an evolved sense from PIE *steig- "to prick, stick, pierce" (see stick (v.)).
1824, from aeronautic (1784), from French aéronautique, from aéro- (see aero-) + nautique "of ships," from Latin nauticus, from Greek nautikos (see nautical).

meanings.definitionCn: 易受攻击的，易受…的攻击；易受伤害的；有弱点的
友好关系；加入；联盟；从属关系

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1751, "adoption," from French affiliation, from Medieval Latin affiliationem (nominative affiliatio), noun of action from past participle stem of Latin affiliare "to adopt a son," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + filius "son" (see filial).

root.meaning: a fraudulent production, something intended to deceive

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + a fraudulent production, something intended to deceive

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French defrauder, from Latin defraudare "to defraud, cheat," from de- "thoroughly" (see de-) + fraudare "to cheat, swindle" (see fraud).
also moult, mid-14c., mouten, of feathers, "to be shed," from Old English *mutian "to change" (in bemutian "to exchange"), from Latin mutare "to change" (see mutable).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之下 + jug + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., a back-formation from subjugation or else from Latin subiugatus, past participle of subiugare "to subjugate."

morphology.mnemonic: to make pits in + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1819, back-formation from donation.

meanings.definitionCn: 倒空； 枯竭； 消耗

etymology.story: 1807, back-formation from depletion.

meanings.definitionCn: 复发的；周期性的，经常发生的

etymology.story: 1610s, from Middle French recurrent (16c.) and directly from Latin recurrentem (nominative recurrens), present participle of recurrere "run back, hasten back, return" (see recur).

meanings.definitionCn: 富矿带；带来好运之事；幸运

etymology.story: 1844, American English, from Spanish bonanza "a rich lode," originally "fair weather at sea, prosperity," from Vulgar Latin *bonacia, from Latin bonus "good" (see bene-)..

meanings.definitionCn: 惊人的；过分的；恶名昭彰的

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 群体 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1530s, "distinguished, eminent, excellent," from Latin egregius "distinguished, excellent, extraordinary," from the phrase ex grege "rising above the flock," from ex "out of" (see ex-) + grege, ablative of grex "herd, flock" (see gregarious).

meanings.definitionCn: 乐观的；满怀希望的；面色红润的

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old Norse bolr "tree trunk," from Proto-Germanic *bulas (source also of Middle Dutch bolle "trunk of a tree"), from PIE *bhel- (2) "to blow, inflate, swell" (source also of Greek phyllon "leaf," phallos "swollen penis;" Latin flos "flower," florere "to blossom, flourish," folium "leaf;" Old Prussian balsinis "cushion;" Old Norse belgr "bag, bellows;" Old English bolla "pot, cup, bowl;" Old Irish bolgaim "I swell," blath "blossom, flower," bolach "pimple," bolg "bag;" Breton bolc'h "flax pod;" Serbian buljiti "to stare, be bug-eyed;" Serbo-Croatian blazina "pillow")..

meanings.definitionCn: 有意义的;重大的,重要的

morphology.mnemonic: 标记 + 使…化/造成 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1570s, "having a meaning," from Latin significantem (nominative significans, present participle of significare "make known, indicate" (see signify).

meanings.definitionCn: 奉献；使神圣；供神用；献身于

etymology.story: late 14c., from Latin consecratus, past participle of consecrare "to make holy, devote," from com- "together" (see com-) + sacrare (see sacred).

meanings.definitionCn: 彬彬有礼的；顺从的；殷勤的

etymology.story: 1640s, from French complaisant (16c.), in Middle French, "pleasing," present participle of complaire "acquiesce to please," from Latin complacere "be very pleasing" (see complacent, with which it overlapped till mid-19c.).

meanings.definitionCn: 嘲笑的，嘲弄的；可付之一笑的

etymology.story: 1620s, "characterized by derision," from Latin deris-, past participle stem of deridere (see derision) + -ive.
1590s, "to make a catalogue;" see catalogue (n.).
1872, from glycerine + -ol, suffix denoting alcohols..

morphology.mnemonic: 做/驱动 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1560s, "mental tossing to and fro," from French agitation, from Latin agitationem (nominative agitatio) "motion, agitation," noun of action from past participle stem of agitare "move to and fro," frequentative of agere in its sense of "to drive" (see act (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 部分重叠；部分的同时发生

etymology.story: 1560s, from de- "down" + base (adj.) "low," on analogy of abase (or, alternatively, from obsolete verb base "to abuse")..
1610s, from past participle stem of Medieval Latin assassinare (see assassin).

morphology.mnemonic: 在前/预先 + speculator for a fall + ance

etymology.story: 1570s, originally legal, in reference to enforcement of debt obligations, from forbear (v.) + -ance.
1650s, from French complaisance (14c.), in Middle French "care or desire to please," from Medieval Latin complacentia (see complacence)..
in boon companion (1560s), only real survival of Middle English boon "good" (early 14c.), from Old French bon (see bon)..

meanings.definitionCn: 距离； 远方； 一长段时间

etymology.story: late 14c., "to withhold oneself," from Old French abstenir (14c.), earlier astenir (13c.) "hold (oneself) back, refrain, abstain (from), practice abstinence," from Latin abstinere "withhold, keep back, keep off," from ab(s)- "from, away from" (see ab-) + tenere "to hold" (see tenet).

meanings.definitionCn: 缺乏，不存在；缺席，不在；缺席的时间

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French absence (14c.), from Latin absentia, noun of state from absentem (nominative absens), present participle of abesse "be away from, be absent," from ab- "away" (see ab-) + esse "to be" (see essence)..

meanings.definitionCn: 恶意的；恶毒的；蓄意的；怀恨的

etymology.story: early 13c., from Old French malicios "showing ill will, spiteful, wicked" (Modern French malicieux), from Latin malitiosus "wicked, malicious," from malitia "badness, ill will, spite," from malus "bad" (see mal-).

root.meaning: watch kept on a festival eve

morphology.mnemonic: watch kept on a festival eve + ance

etymology.story: 1560s, from Middle French vigilance (16c.), from Latin vigilantia "wakefulness, watchfulness, attention," from vigil "watchful, awake" (see vigil).

meanings.definitionCn: 代理；代用品；遗嘱检验法官
代理；指定某人为自己的代理人

morphology.mnemonic: 超过/在…上 + rog + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin surrogatus, past participle of surrogare/subrogare "put in another's place, substitute," from assimilated form of sub "in the place of, under" (see sub-) + rogare "to ask, propose" (see rogation).

morphology.mnemonic: 超过/外部 + rage + 具有…性质的

meanings.definitionCn: 热切期望的；热心的；挂念的

morphology.mnemonic: 太阳/独自 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1560s, from Latin sollicitus "restless, uneasy, careful, full of anxiety" (see solicit).
1640s, from Latin analogus, from Greek analogos "proportionate, according to due proportion" (see analogy)..

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + brevi + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Latin abbreviatus, past participle of abbreviare "to shorten" (see abbreviation).

meanings.definitionCn: 突降法；虎头蛇尾；令人扫兴的结尾

etymology.story: "the addition of a particular which suddenly lowers the effect," 1701, from anti- + climax (n.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 说模棱两可的话；支吾， 躲闪；推诿；含糊其词

etymology.story: early 15c., equivocaten, from Medieval Latin equivocatus, past participle of equivocare "to call by the same name, be called by the same name, have the same sound," from Late Latin aequivocus "of identical sound" (see equivocation).
"follower, associate, supporter," early 15c., from Old French adherent or directly from Latin adhaerentem (see adherent (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 积满污垢的， 肮脏的

etymology.story: 1610s, from grime + -y (2).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 测试/证明 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "proven effectiveness, excellence," from Old French aprobacion or directly from Latin approbationem (nominative approbatio) "an approval," noun of action from past participle stem of approbare (see approve).

morphology.mnemonic: clear + 使…化/造成 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1610s, "act of clearing or refining" (especially of liquid substances), from French clarification, from Late Latin clarificationem (nominative clarificatio), noun of action from past participle stem of clarificare (see clarify).

Old English munuc "monk" (used also of women), from Proto-Germanic *muniko- (source also of Old Frisian munek, Middle Dutch monic, Old High German munih, German Mönch), an early borrowing from Vulgar Latin *monicus (source of French moine, Spanish monje, Italian monaco), from Late Latin monachus "monk," originally "religious hermit," from Ecclesiastical Greek monakhos "monk," noun use of a classical Greek adjective meaning "solitary," from monos "alone" (see mono-).

meanings.definitionCn: 债券；结合；约定；粘合剂；纽带

etymology.story: 1590s, from Middle French convoquer (14c.), from Latin convocare "to call together" (see convocation).

meanings.definitionCn: 势头；[物] 动量；动力；冲力

etymology.story: 1690s, scientific use in mechanics, "quantity of motion of a moving body," from Latin momentum "movement, moving power" (see moment).
1610s, a back-formation from obstruction or else from Latin obstructus, past participle of obstruere "to block, to stop up" (see obstruction).

early 15c., from Anglo-French oster (late 13c.), Old French oster "remove, take away, take off; evict, dispel; liberate, release" (Modern French ôter), from Latin obstare "stand before, be opposite, stand opposite to, block," in Vulgar Latin, "hinder," from ob "against" (see ob-) + stare "to stand," from PIE root *sta- "to stand" (see stet).
1650s, from Latin octagonos, from Greek oktagononos, literally "eight-angled," from okta- comb.

early 15c., "heat of passion or desire," from Old French ardure "heat, glow; passion" (12c.), from Latin ardorem (nominative ardor) "a flame, fire, burning, heat;" also of feelings, etc., "eagerness, zeal," from ardere "to burn" (see ardent).

meanings.definitionCn: 迷宫；[解剖] 迷路；难解的事物

etymology.story: late 15c., from Middle French and Old French aquatique (13c.), from Latin aquaticus "growing in water; bringing rain," from aqua "water" (see aqua-).

1620s, "kind of open pavilion" (made of light wood, etc., often supported by pillars), from French kiosque (17c.), which is (along with German and Polish kiosk) from Turkish koshk, kiöshk "pavilion, summer house," from Persian kushk "palace, villa; pavilion, portico." They were introduced in Western Europe 17c.

meanings.definitionCn: 连续的；其次的；作为结果而产生的

etymology.story: 1550s, "following," from Old French sequent "following, subsequent," from Latin sequentem (nominative sequens) "next, following," present participle of sequi "to follow" (see sequel).

root.meaning: something which stands for something else

morphology.mnemonic: something which stands for something else + ize

meanings.definitionCn: 思考；精神活动；脑髓作用

etymology.story: 1853, coined by English physiologist Dr.

root.meaning: to guide or direct by a sign, gesture, movement

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + to guide or direct by a sign, gesture, movement

etymology.story: late 14c., from Middle French commocion "violent motion, agitation" (12c., Modern French commotion), from Latin commotionem (nominative commotio) "violent motion, agitation," noun of action from past participle stem of commovere "to move, disturb," from com- "together," or "thoroughly" (see com-) + movere "to move" (see move (v.))..

1799, Modern Latin, coined 1782 by Swedish chemist Torbern Bergman (1735-1784) for gas obtained from sal ammoniac, salt deposits containing ammonium chloride found near temple of Jupiter Ammon (from Egyptian God Amun) in Libya, from Greek ammoniakos "belonging to Ammon." The shrine was ancient already in Augustus' day, and the salts were prepared "from the sands where the camels waited while their masters prayed for good omens" [Shipley].

1540s, probably from -cy + Latin proficientem (nominative proficiens), present participle of proficere "accomplish, make progress; be useful, do good; have success, profit," from pro- "forward" (see pro-) + root of facere "to make, do" (see factitious)..

meanings.definitionCn: 焦虑的；担忧的；渴望的；急切的

etymology.story: 1620s, from Latin anxius "solicitous, uneasy, troubled in mind" (also "causing anxiety, troublesome"), from angere, anguere "choke, squeeze," figuratively "torment, cause distress" (see anger (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 苦工，重活，单调乏味的工作

etymology.story: 1540s, from drudge + -ery..
1570s, from Latin auditorius "pertaining to hearing," from auditor "hearer" (see auditor)..

meanings.definitionCn: 忠告；法庭传票；宗教法庭的诫谕

etymology.story: "warning," late 14c., from Old French monition (13c.) and directly from Latin monitionem (nominative monitio) "warning, admonition, reminding," noun of action from past participle stem of monere "to warn" (see monitor (n.))..

mid-14c., "shopkeeper, especially one who stores, compounds, and sells medicaments," from Old French apotecaire (13c., Modern French apothicaire), from Late Latin apothecarius "storekeeper," from Latin apotheca "storehouse," from Greek apotheke "barn, storehouse," literally "a place where things are put away," from apo- "away" (see apo-) + tithenai "to put, to place" (see theme).

1650s, from Latin olfactorius, from olfact-, past participle stem of olfacere "to get the smell of, sniff," from olere "emit a smell, give off a smell of" (see odor) + facere "to make" (see factitious)..

meanings.definitionCn: 严重地；严格地，严厉地；纯朴地

etymology.story: 1540s, from severe + -ly (2).

meanings.definitionCn: 举起,提拔,振奋,提升...的职位

etymology.story: late 15c., "to raise above the usual position," from Latin elevatus, past participle of elevare "lift up, raise," figuratively, "to lighten, alleviate," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + levare "lighten, raise," from levis "light" in weight (see lever).
As a verb, from 1791, from designate (adj.) or else a back-formation from designation.

meanings.definitionCn: 光滑的；华而不实的；聪明的；熟练的；老套的
[机] 平滑器；[机] 修光工具；通俗杂志

etymology.story: early 14c., "smooth, glossy, sleek" (of skin or hair); sense of "clever in deception" is first recorded 1590s; that of "first-class, excellent" is from 1833.

meanings.definitionCn: 压迫的；沉重的；压制性的；难以忍受的

morphology.mnemonic: 骨 + device for pressing cloth + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1640s, from Medieval Latin oppressivus, from oppress-, past participle stem of opprimere (see oppress).

morphology.mnemonic: gener + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 14c., "body of individuals born about the same period" (historically 30 years but in other uses as few as 17), on the notion of "descendants at the same stage in the line of descent," from Old French generacion "race, people, species; progeny, offspring; act of procreating" (12c., Modern French génération) and directly from Latin generationem (nominative generatio) "generating, generation," noun of action from past participle stem of generare "bring forth, beget, produce," from genus "race, kind" (see genus).
1530s, a back-formation from humiliation or else from Late Latin humiliatus.

meanings.definitionCn: 监督人；负责人；主管；指挥者

morphology.mnemonic: 超/在…之上 + in + 伸展/趋向 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1550s, originally an ecclesiastical word meaning "bishop" or "minister who supervises churches within a district" (ultimately a loan-translation of Greek episkopos "overseer"), from Medieval Latin superintendentem (nominative superintendens), present participle of Late Latin superintendere "oversee," from Latin super "above" (see super-) + intendere "turn one's attention to, direct" (see intend).

meanings.definitionCn: 可计算的；能预测的；可靠的

etymology.story: 1911, from German Gen, coined 1905 by Danish scientist Wilhelm Ludvig Johannsen (1857-1927), from Greek genea "generation, race," from PIE root *gene- (see genus).
early 15c., in the literal sense, "to draw out, extract; branch out," from Latin educere "to lead out, bring out" (troops, ships, etc.; see educate).

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + equi + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1784, from un- (1) "not" + equivocal.

morphology.mnemonic: clean + 以…方式/副词化 + 状态/性质

etymology.story: early 15c., from cleanly + -ness..

"image of a person," 1530s, from Middle French effigie (13c.), from Latin effigies "copy or imitation of something, likeness, image, statue," from or related to effingere "to mold, fashion, portray," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + fingere "to form, shape" (see fiction).

morphology.mnemonic: 持有/保持 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1580s, from Latin omen "foreboding," from Old Latin osmen, of unknown origin; perhaps connected with the root of audire "to hear" [OED] or from PIE *o- "to believe, hold as true" (source also of Greek oiomai "I suppose, think, believe")..
1570s (past participle adjective desicatt is attested from early 15c.), from Latin desiccatus, past participle of desiccare "to make very dry" (see desiccation).

1776, "dense growth of trees and other tangled vegetation," such as that of the swampy regions at the base of the Himalayas in India, from Hindi jangal "desert, forest, wasteland, uncultivated ground," from Sanskrit jangala-s "arid, sparsely grown with trees," a word of unknown origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 编年史作者；纪年表编者

etymology.story: "one who keeps a chronicle of events by year," 1610s, from French analiste; see annals + -ist..
1620s, "to avenge or revenge," from Latin vindicatus, past participle of vindicare "to stake a claim; to liberate; to act as avenger" (see vindication).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 抓住/理解 + able

etymology.story: late 15c., from Latin apprehensibilis "that can be seized," from apprehens-, past participle stem of apprehendere (see apprehend)..
Old English bestreowian; see be- + strew (v.)..
1802, from Modern Latin unilateralis, from unum, neuter of unus "one" (see one) + latus (genitive lateralis) "side" (see oblate (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 轻蔑的；[临床] 恶化的，变坏的

etymology.story: "depreciative, disparaging," 1888, from French péjoratif, from Late Latin peiorat-, past participle stem of peiorare "make worse," from Latin peior "worse," related to pessimus "worst," pessum "downward, to the ground," from PIE *ped-yos-, comparative of root *ped- "to walk, stumble, impair" (see peccadillo).

meanings.definitionCn: 用词不当；误称；写错姓名

etymology.story: mid-15c., "mistaken identification of an accused or convicted person," from Anglo-French, Old French mesnomer "to misname, wrongly name," noun use of infinitive, from mes- "wrongly" (see mis- (2)) + nomer "to name," from Latin nominare "nominate" (see nominate).

meanings.definitionCn: 天真的；坦白的；正直的；朴实的

etymology.story: 1590s, "noble in nature, high-minded; honorably straightforward," from Latin ingenuus "with the virtues of freeborn people, of noble character, frank, upright, candid," originally "native, freeborn," literally "born in (a place)," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + PIE *gen(e)-wo-, suffixed form of root gene- "to give birth, beget, produce" (see genus).

meanings.definitionCn: 优先的；选择的；特惠的；先取的

morphology.mnemonic: to esteem (something) more than others + ance + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1805, from Medieval Latin preferentia, from Latin praeferre (see prefer) + -al (1)..

meanings.definitionCn: 阴冷的；荒凉的，无遮蔽的；黯淡的，无希望的；冷酷的；单调的

etymology.story: Old English cyln, cylen "kiln, oven, furnace for drying or baking," from Latin culina "kitchen, cooking stove," unexplained variant of coquere "to cook" (see cook (n.)).
1590s, from recluse + -ive.
1580s, "natural science," from physic in sense of "natural science." Also see -ics.
"having two sides," 1775, from bi- + lateral.
late 13c., from Anglo-French glorious, Old French glorieus "glorious, blessed" (12c., Modern French glorieux), from Latin gloriosus "full of glory, famous," from gloria (see glory (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 天资， 天赋， 天生的才能

morphology.mnemonic: 适合/技能 + 状态/品质

etymology.story: early 15c., "tendency, likelihood," from Middle French aptitude (14c.) or directly from Late Latin aptitudo (genitive aptitudinis) "fitness," noun of quality from Latin aptus "joined, fitted" (see apt).

root.meaning: a quantity of cream cheese, etc., smeared on a bagel

morphology.mnemonic: 使/变成 + a quantity of cream cheese, etc., smeared on a bagel

etymology.story: Old English bismierwan, besmyrwan (West Saxon), besmerwan (Anglian); see be- + smear (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 糟糕透顶的， 可恶的； 恶劣的， 卑鄙的

etymology.story: late 13c., "morally repugnant; morally flawed, corrupt, wicked; of no value; of inferior quality; disgusting, foul, ugly; degrading, humiliating; of low estate, without worldly honor or esteem," from Anglo-French ville, Old French vil "shameful, dishonorable; low-born; cheap; ugly, hideous," from Latin vilis "cheap, worthless, base, common," of uncertain origin, perhaps from PIE root *wes- (1) "to buy, sell" (see venal).
"want of seriousness, frivolity," 1560s, from Latin levitatem (nominative levitas) "lightness, frivolity," from levis "light" in weight (see lever) + -ity..

meanings.definitionCn: 信赖；信心；受信赖的人或物

etymology.story: late 14c., "renunciation, abandonment or neglect of established religion," from Latin apostasia, from later Greek apostasia, from apostasis "revolt, defection," literally "a standing off" (see apostate).

meanings.definitionCn: 无畏的；勇敢的；不屈不挠的

etymology.story: 1590s, from daunt + -less.

meanings.definitionCn: 多余的；不必要的；奢侈的

morphology.mnemonic: 超/在…之上 + 流动 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: starch-like substance found in the liver and animal tissue, 1860, from French glycogène, "sugar-producer," from Greek-derived glyco- "sweet" (see glyco-) + French -gène (see -gen).

meanings.definitionCn: 管理者；售票员；领队，指挥；导体

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 引导 + …者/…器

etymology.story: 1520s, "one who leads or guides," from Middle French conductour (14c., Old French conduitor), from Latin conductor "one who hires, contractor," in Late Latin "a carrier," from conductus, past participle of conducere (see conduce).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 价格/价值 + able

etymology.story: late 14c.; see apostate (n.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 涩的；收敛性的；止血的；严厉的

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 拉紧/约束 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1540s, from Latin astringentum (nominative astringens), present participle of astringere "to bind fast, tighten, contract," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + stringere "draw tight" (see strain (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 混乱,杂乱;骚乱;失调,疾病

root.meaning: to give orders for or to

morphology.mnemonic: 不/分离 + to give orders for or to

etymology.story: 1520s, "make heavy, burden down," from past participle adjective aggravate "burdened; threatened" (late 15c.), from Latin aggravatus, past participle of aggravare "to render more troublesome," literally "to make heavy" (see aggravation).

morphology.mnemonic: 穿越/转变 + 站立/设置 + …者/…器

etymology.story: small electronic device, 1948, from transfer + resistor, so called because it transfers an electrical current across a resistor.

meanings.definitionCn: 坚持；不屈不挠；固执己见（在辩论中）

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French perseverer "continue, persevere, endure" and directly from Latin perseverare "continue steadfastly, persist," from persevereus "very strict, earnest," from per- "very" (see per) + severus "strict" (see severity).

1836, from Thomas Bowdler (1754-1825), English editor who in 1818 published a notorious expurgated Shakespeare, in which, according to his frontispiece, "nothing is added to the original text; but those words and expressions omitted which cannot with propriety be read aloud in a family."
1580s, "that may be interrogated;" c.

meanings.definitionCn: 暴政；专横；严酷；残暴的行为（需用复数）

etymology.story: late 14c., "cruel or unjust use of power; the government of a tyrant," from Old French tyranie (13c.), from Late Latin tyrannia "tyranny," from Greek tyrannia "rule of a tyrant, absolute power," from tyrannos "master" (see tyrant)..

meanings.definitionCn: 大量的；多卷的，长篇的；著书多的

root.meaning: book forming part of a set

morphology.mnemonic: book forming part of a set + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1610s, "forming a large mass," also "full of turnings and windings," from Late Latin voluminosus, from Latin volumen (genitive voluminis) "volume" (see volume).
early 15c., from Late Latin amicabilis "friendly," a word in Roman law, from Latin amicus "friend," related to amare "to love" (see Amy).
1789, from French philanthropique (18c.), from Greek philanthropikos (adj.), from philanthropia "humanity, benevolence, kindliness" (see philanthropy).
late 15c., from Middle French allevier or directly from Late Latin alleviatus, past participle of alleviare "to lighten," from Latin ad- "to" (see ad-) + levis "light" in weight (see lever).

meanings.definitionCn: 模糊； 费解； 不出名

morphology.mnemonic: su + 放置 + 状态/行为/过程

late 14c., from Old French tumult (12c.), from Latin tumultus "commotion, bustle, uproar, disorder, disturbance," related to tumere "to be excited, swell" (see tumid)..
1660s, "urgent," a back-formation from exigency or else from Latin exigentem (nominative exigens), present participle of exigere "to demand; drive out, drive forth" (see exact (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 粉碎；打碎；破坏；破掉；使散开

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + surrect + 状态/行为/过程

1650s; see equi- + angular.

late 14c., from Old French erroneus and directly from Latin erroneus "vagrant, wandering" (in Late Latin "erroneous"), from erronem (nominative erro) "a wanderer, vagabond," from past participle stem of errare "to wander; to err" (see err).
late 14c., from Old French limitacion and directly from Latin limitationem (nominative limitatio), noun of action from past participle stem of limitare (see limit (v.)).

"de-emphasize," 1968, from down (adv.) + play (v.).

type of musical composition, 1590s, fuge, from Italian fuga, literally "flight," also "ardor," from Latin fuga "a running away, act of fleeing," from fugere "to flee" (see fugitive (adj.)).

late 14c., "to raze, destroy, overthrow, undermine, overturn," from Old French subvertir "overthrow, destroy" (13c.), or directly from Latin subvertere "to turn upside down, overturn, overthrow," from sub "under" (see sub-) + vertere "to turn" (see versus).

Formerly often in a good sense, "witty, amusing," but later implying a desire to be amusing that is often intrusive or ill-timed.

1590s, from Latin perfidiosus "treacherous," from perfidia (see perfidy).

1640s, "strikingly beautiful," from French floride "flourishing," from Latin floridus "flowery, in bloom," from flos "flower" (see flora).

also bibliophil, 1824, from French bibliophile, from biblio- + -phile..

1590s, "burning, glowing, hot," from Latin fervidus "glowing, burning; vehement, fervid," from fervere "to boil, glow" (see brew (v.)).
1630s, back-formation from amputation or else from Latin amputatus, past participle of amputare "to cut off, to prune."
"to set or place apart, to detach so as to make alone," by 1786, a back-formation from isolated (q.v.).

"following in time, later," mid-15c., from Middle French subsequent (14c.) and directly from Latin subsequentem (nominative subsequens), present participle of subsequi "come after in time, follow closely," figuratively "imitate, conform to," from sub "closely, up to" (see sub-) + sequi "follow" (see suit (n.)).
late 14c., from Latin omissionem (nominative omissio) "an omitting," noun of action from past participle stem of omittere (see omit).
1845 in biological sense, from German metabolisch (1839), from Greek metabolikos "changeable," from metabole "a change, changing, a transition" (see metabolism).
late 14c., "terror," from Old French tremor "fear, terror, quaking" (13c.), from Latin tremorem (nominative tremor) "a trembling, terror," from tremere (see tremble (v.)).
