morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 顺序/等级 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: late 14c., "not ordered, lacking order or regularity," from Latin inordinatus "unordered, not arranged," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + ordinatus, past participle of ordinare "to set in order" (see order (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 见证/证明 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "statement, declaration," also "writing testifying to one's qualification or character," early 15c.

meanings.definitionCn: 领先(于),在(…之前);优先,先于

etymology.story: early 15c., "lead the way; occur before," from Middle French preceder and directly from Latin praecedere "to go before," from prae "before" (see pre-) + cedere "to go" (see cede).
early 15c., "relating to the body humors," a native formation from humor (n.), or else from Middle French humoreux "damp," from Old French humor.
1520s, from Middle French derider, from Latin deridere "to ridicule, laugh to scorn" (see derision).

meanings.definitionCn: 感情强烈的；热烈的；(人)有强烈感情的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French vehement, veement "impetuous, ardent" (12c.), from Latin vehementem (nominative vehemens) "impetuous, eager, violent, furious, ardent, carried away," perhaps [Barnhart] from a lost present middle participle of vehere "to carry" (see vehicle).

morphology.mnemonic: day日 + …的人/处于…状态 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1816, from Medieval Latin differentiatus, past participle of differentiare, from Latin differentia (see difference).

meanings.definitionCn: 沉默寡言的；言不如意的

etymology.story: 1834, from Latin reticentem (nominative reticens), present participle of reticere "be silent" (see reticence)..

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 呼叫/声音 + acy

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French avocacie (14c.), from Medieval Latin advocatia, noun of state from Latin advocatus (see advocate (n.))..
late 14c., "relief from misfortune," see discharge (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 数字；手指或足趾；一指宽

etymology.story: late 14c., "numeral below 10," from Latin digitus "finger or toe" (also with secondary meanings dealing in counting and numerals), related to dicere "to say, speak" (see diction).

mid-14c., "deserving of or exciting suspicion," from Old French sospecious, from Latin suspiciosus, suspitiosus "exciting suspicion, causing mistrust," also "full of suspicion, ready to suspect," from stem of suspicere (see suspicion).

"passionate ardor in pursuit of an objective or course of action," late 14c., from Old French zel (Modern French zèle) and directly from Late Latin zelus "zeal, emulation" (source also of Italian zelo, Spanish celo), a Church word, from Greek zelos "ardor, eager rivalry, emulation," "a noble passion" [Liddell & Scott], but also "jealousy;" prom PIE *ya- "to seek, request, desire." From mid-15c.

meanings.definitionCn: 一夫多妻，一妻多夫，多配偶

morphology.mnemonic: 多 + 婚姻 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1590s, from Late Latin polygamia, from Late Greek polygamia "polygamy," from polygamos "often married," from polys "many" + gamos "marriage" (see gamete).

"inscription on a tomb or monument," mid-14c., from Old French epitaphe (12c.) and directly from Medieval Latin epitaphium "funeral oration, eulogy," from Greek epitaphion "a funeral oration," noun use of neuter of epitaphios (logos) "(words) spoken on the occasion of a funeral," from epi "at, over" (see epi-) + taphos "tomb, funeral rites," from PIE root *dhembh- "to bury."
1520s, back-formation from renovation, or else from Latin renovatus, past participle of renovare "renew, restore" (see renovation).
figurative use from 1660s; literal use from 1755, from Latin crepusculum "twilight, dusk," from creper "dusky," which is of unknown origin.

mid-14c., from Late Latin mediatorem (nominative mediator) "one who mediates," agent noun from past participle stem of mediare "to intervene, mediate," also "to be or divide in the middle" (see mediate).

meanings.definitionCn: 清楚的，易懂的，神智清醒的

etymology.story: 1590s, "bright, shining," from Latin lucidus "light, bright, clear," figuratively "perspicuous, lucid, clear," from lucere "to shine," from lux (genitive lucis) "light," from PIE root *leuk- "to shine, be bright" (see light (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 诽谤的；不公平的；引起反感的；易招嫉妒的
善良的， 人道的， 仁慈的， 慈悲的； 促进文明或教化的， 人文的

etymology.story: mid-15c., a parallel variant of human (adj.), with a form and stress that perhaps suggest a stronger association with Latin humanus than with Old French humain.

meanings.definitionCn: 垂直的，成直角的；直立的

morphology.mnemonic: 通过/彻底 + 悬挂/称量/支付 + 与…有关的/像…的

etymology.story: late 15c., from adverb (late 14c.), from Old French perpendiculer, from Latin perpendicularis "vertical, as a plumb line," from perpendiculum "plumb line," from perpendere "balance carefully," from per- "thoroughly" (see per) + pendere "to weigh, to hang" (see pendant).

meanings.definitionCn: 不守规矩的；任性的；难驾驭的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + mark with lines + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: "disposed to resist lawful restraint," c.

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + device for pressing cloth

etymology.story: early 14c., "put down by force," from Old French depresser, from Late Latin depressare, frequentative of Latin deprimere "press down," from de- "down" (see de-) + premere "to press" (see press (v.1)).
late 15c., unexplained alteration of Middle English boistous (c.

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + device for pressing cloth

etymology.story: late 14c., "to check, restrain," from Latin repressus, past participle of reprimere "hold back, curb," figuratively "check, confine, restrain, refrain," from re- "back" (see re-) + premere "to push" (see press (v.1)).
1620s, present participle adjective from appall.
1785, from re- + agent "substance that produces a chemical reaction.".

meanings.definitionCn: 公正的；坦白的；率直的；偷拍的

etymology.story: 1620s, "white," from Latin candidum "white; pure; sincere, honest, upright," from candere "to shine," from PIE root *kand- "to glow, to shine" (see candle).

meanings.definitionCn: 变迁；盛衰；变化无常；变迁兴衰

etymology.story: "a passing from one state to another," whether regular or not, 1560s, from Middle French vicissitude (14c.), from Latin vicissitudinem (nominative vicissitudo) "change, interchange, alternation," from vicissim (adv.) "changeably, on the other hand, by turns, in turn," from vicis "a turn, change" (see vicarious).

"subdivision of an army," 1630s, from French brigade "body of soldiers" (14c.), from Italian brigata "troop, crowd, gang," from brigare "brawl, fight," from briga "strife, quarrel," perhaps of Celtic (compare Gaelic brigh, Welsh bri "power") or Germanic origin..

meanings.definitionCn: 羞怯的；缺乏自信的；谦虚谨慎的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Latin diffidentem (nominative diffidens), present participle of diffidere (see diffidence).
1640s, from Late Latin inimicalis "hostile," from Latin inimicus "unfriendly; an enemy" (see enemy)..

meanings.definitionCn: 强制；强迫；高压政治；威压

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French cohercion (Modern French coercion), from Medieval Latin coercionem, from Latin coerctionem, earlier coercitionem, noun of action from past participle stem of coercere (see coerce)..

late 14c., from Old French corpulent "stout, fat," from Latin corpulentus "fleshy, fat," from corpus "body" (see corporeal) + -ulentus "full of." Leigh Hunt was sent to prison for two years for calling the Prince Regent corpulent in print in 1812..
1550s, "to state the parts of speech in a sentence," verb use of Middle English pars (n.) "part of speech" (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 可修缮的；可补偿的；可挽回的

etymology.story: 1560s, from Middle French reparable (16c.), from Latin reparabilis "able to be restored or regained," from reparare "restore" (see repair (v.1))..

meanings.definitionCn: [物] 轨道，轨线；[航][军] 弹道

morphology.mnemonic: 穿越/转变 + 投/掷 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: "path described by a body moving under the influence of given forces," 1690s, from Modern Latin trajectorium, from trajectorius "of or pertaining to throwing across," from Latin traiectus "thrown over or across," past participle of traicere "throw across, shoot across," from Latin trans- "across" (see trans-) + icere, combining form of iacere "to throw" (see jet (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + rog + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: "special right or privilege granted to someone," late 14c.

meanings.definitionCn: 难懂的， 难捉摸的； 易忘的

etymology.story: 1719, from Latin elus-, past participle stem of eludere "elude, frustrate" (see elude) + -ive.

morphology.mnemonic: in + 折叠/交织 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "to convey (truth) in a fable," from Latin implicatus, past participle of implicare "to involve, entwine, entangle, embrace" (see implication).

meanings.definitionCn: 笨拙的；笨重的；不灵便的；难处理的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + wield + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: late 14c., "lacking strength, powerless," from un- (1) "not" + obsolete wieldy, from Old English wielde "active, vigorous," from Proto-Germanic *walth- "have power" (see wield (v.)).

late 13c., "kingdom," from Old French reaume, probably from roiaume "kingdom," altered (by influence of Latin regalis "regal") from Gallo-Roman *regiminem, accusative form of Latin regimen "system of government, rule" (see regimen).

meanings.definitionCn: 减轻；低估；为…找借口；使人原谅

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + tenu + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin extenuatus, past participle of extenuare "lessen, make small, reduce, diminish, detract from," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + tenuare "make thin," from tenuis "thin" (see tenet).

meanings.definitionCn: 风力计，[气象] 风速计

etymology.story: 1727, from anemo- "wind" + -meter..

meanings.definitionCn: 综合的；广泛的；有理解力的
综合学校；专业综合测验

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 抓住/理解 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: "containing much," 1610s, from French comprehénsif, from Late Latin comprehensivus, from comprehens-, past participle stem of Latin comprehendere (see comprehend).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 神 + 主义/学说/运动

etymology.story: 1580s, from French athéisme (16c.), from Greek atheos "without god" (see atheist).

meanings.definitionCn: 臭的，散发气味的；芬芳的

etymology.story: early 15c., "that has a scent," with -ous + Latin odorifer "spreading odor, fragrant," literally "bearing odor," from odor (see odor) + ferre "to bear, carry" (see infer).

meanings.definitionCn: 机械的；力学的；呆板的；无意识的；手工操作的

morphology.mnemonic: 机械 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., "of or pertaining to machines," from mechanic (adj.) + -al (1); of persons or human actions, "resembling machines, automatic" it is from c.

meanings.definitionCn: 恳求， 祈求； 援用， 援引； 使用， 应用

etymology.story: late 15c., from Old French invoquer, envoquer, envochier "invoke, implore" (12c.), from Latin invocare "call upon, implore," from in- "upon" (see in- (2)) + vocare "to call," related to noun vox (genitive vocis) "voice" (see voice (n.)).
"openness of mind, impartiality, frankness," c.

meanings.definitionCn: [生理][免疫] 溶血（现象）；血细胞溶解

morphology.mnemonic: 超过/外部 + act of putting

etymology.story: 1839, from out + put (v.).

Old English cnedan "to knead, manipulate by squeezing or pressing," from Proto-Germanic *knedan (source also of Old Saxon knedan, Middle Dutch cneden, Dutch kneden, Old High German knetan, German kneten, Old Norse knoða "to knead").

morphology.mnemonic: sy + 测量 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1751, from symmetry + -ical.
1550s, from Latin calumniatus, past participle of calumniari "to accuse falsely," from calumnia "slander, false accusation" (see calumny).

Old English gedæfte "mild, gentle," differentiated in Middle English into daft (q.v.) and this word, via sense of "apt, skillful, adept." Cognate with Gothic gadaban "to be fit," Old Norse dafna "to grow strong," Dutch deftig "important, relevant.".

meanings.definitionCn: 指挥棒；接力棒；警棍；司令棒

etymology.story: 1540s, "a staff used as a weapon," from French bâton "stick, walking stick, staff, club, wand," from Old French baston (12c.) "stick, staff, rod," from Late Latin bastum "stout staff," probably of Gaulish origin or else from Greek *baston "support," from bastazein "to lift up, raise, carry." Meaning "staff carried as a symbol of office" is from 1580s; musical sense of "conductor's wand" is from 1841 (from 1839 as a French word in English).

meanings.definitionCn: 徒劳的；自负的；无结果的；无用的
焦躁的；烦燥的；起波纹的

etymology.story: 1590s, "gnawing; disposed to fret," from fret (n.) (see fret (v.)) + -ful.

meanings.definitionCn: 和谐的，和睦的；协调的；悦耳的

morphology.mnemonic: harmon + 具有…特征/名词化 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1520s, "sounding together tunefully," from Middle French harmonieux (14c.), from harmonie (see harmony).

meanings.definitionCn: 先见，远见；预见；深谋远虑

etymology.story: also fore-sight, early 14c., "insight obtained beforehand;" also "prudence," from fore- + sight (n.).

root.meaning: range of something, place or scene of activity

morphology.mnemonic: range of something, place or scene of activity + oid

etymology.story: "body resembling, but not identical with, a sphere," 1560s, from Latin sphaeroides, from Greek sphairoeides "ball-like, spherical," from sphaira (see sphere) + -oeides "form" (see -oid).

meanings.definitionCn: 拦截；[数] 截距；截获的情报
(陪审团的)裁决,判决；判断；定论

etymology.story: 1530s, alteration of Middle English verdit (c.
1570s, from French reconsidérer and also from re- "back, again" + consider.

morphology.mnemonic: 降落，降临 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: "looking like a corpse," early 15c., from Latin cadaverosus "corpse-like," from cadaver (see cadaver).
late 16c., from Latin cogitatus, past participle of cogitare "to think" (see cogitation).

Old English cristal "clear ice, clear mineral," from Old French cristal (12c., Modern French crystal), from Latin crystallus "crystal, ice," from Greek krystallos, from kryos "frost," from PIE root *kru(s)- "hard, hard outer surface" (see crust).
late 14c., from Latin stupor "insensibility, numbness, dullness," from stupere "be stunned" (see stupid)..

morphology.mnemonic: to bristle, be shaggy, + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1610s, "in a position or condition to recoil," usually with from; from Latin abhorentem (nominative abhorrens), present participle of abhorrere; see abhor.

meanings.definitionCn: 植物园；（供科研等的）树木园

etymology.story: "tree-garden," 1838, from Latin arboretum, literally "a place grown with trees," from arbor "tree," which is of unknown origin, + -etum, suffix used to form the names of gardens and woods..
early 14c., from reuthe "pity, compassion" (see ruth) + -less.
late 14c., from Latin simile "a like thing; a comparison, likeness, parallel," neuter of similis "like" (see similar).

meanings.definitionCn: 组成；作曲（compose的过去分词）；著作

etymology.story: "space between intersecting lines," late 14c., from Old French angle "angle, corner," and directly from Latin angulus "an angle, corner," a diminutive form from PIE root *ang-/*ank- "to bend" (source also of Greek ankylos "bent, crooked," Latin ang(u)ere "to compress in a bend, fold, strangle;" Old Church Slavonic aglu "corner;" Lithuanian anka "loop;" Sanskrit ankah "hook, bent," angam "limb;" Old English ancleo "ankle;" Old High German ango "hook").
mid-15c., from Middle French urgent "pressing, impelling" (14c.), from Latin urgentem (nominative urgens), present participle of urgere "to press hard, urge" (see urge (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 圆/循环 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1817, from cyclic + -al (1)..
early 15c., from Middle French optique, obtique (c.

1520s, "insane person," from Latin fanaticus "mad, enthusiastic, inspired by a god," also "furious, mad," originally, "pertaining to a temple," from fanum "temple, shrine, consecrated place," related to festus "festive" (see feast (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: [交] 栈桥，高架桥；支架，搁凳

etymology.story: early 14c., "a support for something," from Old French trestel "crossbeam" (12c., Modern French tréteu), presumed to be an alteration of Vulgar Latin *transtellum, diminutive of transtrum "beam, crossbar" (see transom).

meanings.definitionCn: 旁边，侧面；坡，岸；一边/侧/方
统治， 支配； 占优势

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + domin + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1590s, from Medieval Latin predominatus, past participle of predominare (see predominant).

root.meaning: set of correlated principles, facts, ideas, etc.

morphology.mnemonic: set of correlated principles, facts, ideas, etc. + ic

etymology.story: 1670s, "pertaining to a system," from French systématique or directly from Late Latin systematicus, from Greek systematikos "combined in a whole," from systema (genitive systematos); see system.

1570s, the vogue, "height of popularity or accepted fashion," from Middle French vogue "fashion, success;" also "drift, swaying motion (of a boat)" literally "a rowing," from Old French voguer "to row, sway, set sail" (15c.), probably from a Germanic source.

meanings.definitionCn: 用石膏处理；施石膏肥料于

etymology.story: substance (hydrated calcium sulphate) used in making plaster, late 14c., from Latin gypsum, from Greek gypsos "chalk," according to Klein, a word perhaps of Semitic origin (compare Arabic jibs, Hebrew gephes "plaster")..
1520s, from Middle French combustible, or directly from Late Latin combustibilis, from Latin combustus, past participle of combuere "to burn up, consume" (see combustion).

meanings.definitionCn: 无法取消的， 不能改变的

etymology.story: also irrevokable, late 14c., from Latin irrevocabilis "that cannot be recalled, unalterable," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + revocabilis (see revoke).

1660s, correction of earlier stupendious "causing astonishment, astounding" (1540s), from Late Latin stupendus "to be wondered at," gerundive of Latin stupere "be stunned, be struck senseless, be aghast, astounded, or amazed" (see stupid).

meanings.definitionCn: 屈尊；俯就；（对某人）表现出优越感

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to yield deferentially," from Old French condescendere (14c.) "to agree, consent, give in, yield," from Late Latin condescendere "to let oneself down," from Latin com- "together" (see com-) + descendere "descend" (see descend).

"passing without continuing," late 14c., from Old French transitoire "ephemeral, transitory" (12c.), from Late Latin transitorius "passing, transient," in classical Latin "allowing passage through," from transitus, past participle of transire "go or cross over" (see transient)..

meanings.definitionCn: 水银的；水星的；墨丘利神的；雄辩机智的；活泼善变的

etymology.story: late 14c., "pertaining to the planet Mercury" (see Mercury).

meanings.definitionCn: 不整洁的（等于frowsy）；有臭味的；霉臭的

etymology.story: also frowsy, 1680s, possibly related to dialectal frowsty (q.v.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 洗牌；推诿，推卸；拖曳，慢吞吞地走；搅乱
洗牌，洗纸牌；混乱，蒙混；拖着脚走

etymology.story: 1520s, disbourse, from Old French desbourser (13c.) "extract (money) from a purse, spend (money)," from des- (see dis-) + bourse "purse" (see bursar).
1790, from keep (v.) + sake; an unusual formation on model of namesake; thus an object kept for the sake of the giver.

early 15c., "suitable, proper," from Latin congruentem (nominative congruens) "agreeing, fit, suitable," present participle of congruere, literally "to come together, agree, correspond with," from com- "with" (see com-) + a lost verb *gruere, *ruere "fall, rush," perhaps from PIE *ghrei- "to rub, grind" (see chrism).
1590s, "expression of sorrow or grief," from Middle French lament and directly from Latin lamentum "a wailing, moaning, weeping" (see lamentation).

meanings.definitionCn: 间歇的；断断续续的；间歇性
凌辱，强奸；对…施暴行；激起愤怒

etymology.story: early 15c., insaciable, from Old French insaciable "ravenous" (15c., Modern French insatiable), or directly from Latin insatiabilis "not to be satisfied," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + satiabilis, from satiare (see satiate).
"to outline, to sketch," 1580s, from Latin adumbratus "sketched, shadowed in outline," past participle of adumbrare "to represent (a thing) in outline" (see adumbration).

"characteristic quality of a musical sound," 1849, from French timbre "quality of a sound," earlier "sound of a bell," from Old French, "bell without a clapper," originally "small drum," probably via Medieval Greek *timbanon, from Greek tympanon "kettledrum" (see tympanum).
mid-15c., "wicked, criminal" (implied in feloniously), from felony + -ous.

"a spiral thing," 1560s, originally of the volutes of Corinthian capitals, from Latin helix "spiral, a volute in architecture," from Greek helix (genitive helikos), a word used of anything in a spiral shape (an armlet, a curl of hair, the tendril of a vine, a serpent's coil), which is related to eilein "to turn, twist, roll," from PIE *wel-ik-, from root *wel- (3) "to turn, revolve" (see volvox).

"apparently but not really proceeding from gaiety," 1630s, from French sardonique (16c.), from Latin sardonius (but as if from Latin *sardonicus) in Sardonius risus, loan-translation of Greek sardonios (gelos) "of bitter or scornful (laughter)," altered from Homeric sardanios (of uncertain origin) by influence of Sardonios "Sardinian," because the Greeks believed that eating a certain plant they called sardonion (literally "plant from Sardinia," see Sardinia) caused facial convulsions resembling those of sardonic laughter, usually followed by death.

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 价格/价值 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Latin depretiatus, past participle of depretiare "to lower the price of, undervalue," from de- "down" (see de-) + pretium "price" (see price (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 电磁体，[电] 电磁铁；电磁石

etymology.story: also electro-magnet, "magnet which owes its magnetic properties to electric current," 1822; see electro- + magnet..

morphology.mnemonic: 围绕/周围 + pher + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1803, from periphery + -al (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 判断力； 谨慎， 审慎； 明智

morphology.mnemonic: separate, distinct + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: 法警；执行官；区镇的地方长官

etymology.story: mid-13c., from Old French baillif (12c., nominative baillis) "administrative official, deputy," from Vulgar Latin *bajulivus "official in charge of a castle," from Latin bajulus "porter," which is of unknown origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 产生；招致；引起；当…的父亲

morphology.mnemonic: 使/变成 + what is got, booty

etymology.story: Old English begietan "to get by effort, find, acquire, attain, seize" (class V strong verb, past tense begeat, past participle begeaton), from be- + get (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 肮脏的；卑鄙的；利欲熏心的；色彩暗淡的

etymology.story: early 15c., "festering," from Latin sordidus "dirty, filthy, foul, vile, mean, base," from sordere "be dirty, be shabby," related to sordes "dirt, filth," from PIE *swrd-e-, from root *swordo- "black, dirty" (source also of Old English sweart "black").

morphology.mnemonic: 大/伟大 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1580s, a back-formation from magnanimity + -ous, or else from Latin magnanimus "highminded," literally "great-souled" (see magnanimity).

meanings.definitionCn: 发光体；杰出人物；知识渊博的人

etymology.story: mid-15c., "lamp, source of (artificial) light," from Old French luminarie (12c.), "lamp, lights, lighting; candles; brightness, illumination," from Late Latin luminare "light, torch, lamp, heavenly body," literally "that which gives light," from Latin lumen (genitive luminis) "light," related to lucere "to shine" (see light (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 相关的；切题的；中肯的；有重大关系的；有意义的，目的明确的

morphology.mnemonic: 提起/相关 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: "pertinent to the matter at hand," 1550s, from Middle French relevant "depending upon," originally "helpful," from Medieval Latin relevantem (nominative relevans), from stem of Latin relevare "to lessen, lighten" (see relieve).

meanings.definitionCn: 威慑， 吓住， 断念

etymology.story: 1570s, from Latin deterrere "to frighten from, discourage from," from de- "away" (see de-) + terrere "frighten" (see terrible).

1690s, as a term in logic, "deductive," from French synthétique (17c.) and directly from Modern Latin syntheticus, from Greek synthetikos "skilled in putting together, constructive," from synthetos "put together, constructed, compounded," past participle of syntithenai "to put together" (see synthesis).
early 15c., "beneficial, convenient," from Medieval Latin commodiosus "convenient, useful," from Latin commodus (see commode).

meanings.definitionCn: 使登基；立…为王；任为主教；崇拜

etymology.story: 1560s, from Latin forceps "pair of tongs, pincers," apparently literally "something with which to grasp hot things," a compound of formus "hot" (see warm (adj.)) + root of capere "to hold, take" (see capable).

meanings.definitionCn: 宣告，公布；声明；表明；赞扬

etymology.story: late 14c., proclamen, from Latin proclamare "cry or call out," from pro- "forth" (see pro-) + clamare "to cry out" (see claim (v.)).

1530s, "waste discharged from the body," from Latin excrementum, from stem of excretus, past participle of excernere "to sift out, discharge," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + cernere "sift, separate" (see crisis).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + to decree, enact

etymology.story: 1530s, from pre- + ordain (q.v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 有礼貌的，高雅的；端正的

etymology.story: 1660s, from Latin decorus "becoming, seemly, fitting, proper," from decus (genitive decoris) "ornament" (see decorate).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + domin + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1570s, from Middle French prédominant (14c.), from Medieval Latin *praedominantem (nominative praedominans), present participle of *praedominare, from Latin prae- "before" (see pre-) + dominari "to rule" (see dominate).
1650s, from Latin pyra and directly from Greek pyra "funeral pyre; altar for sacrifice; any place where fire is kindled," from pyr "fire," cognate with Old English fyr (see fire (n.))..
1835, from purpose (n.) + -ful.

meanings.definitionCn: 正派的；得体的；相当好的

etymology.story: 1530s, "proper to one's station or rank," also "tasteful," from Middle French décent, or directly from Latin decentem (nominative decens) "becoming, seemly, fitting, proper," present participle of decere "to be fitting or suitable," from PIE *deke-, from root *dek- "to take, accept, to receive, greet, be suitable" (source also of Greek dokein "to appear, seem, think," dekhesthai "to accept;" Sanskrit daśasyati "shows honor, is gracious," dacati "makes offerings, bestows;" Latin docere "to teach," decus "grace, ornament").

meanings.definitionCn: 敌意，敌对，对抗；抵制，反对，否决；

etymology.story: early 15c., "hostile action," from Middle French hostilité "enmity" (15c.), or directly from Late Latin hostilitatem (nominative hostilitas) "enmity," from Latin hostilis "inimical," from hostis "enemy" (see guest (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 自我/自动 + 生命/生物 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1797, from auto- + biography.

meanings.definitionCn: 规律性， 规则性； 整齐， 匀称

morphology.mnemonic: regul + 与…有关的/像…的 + ity

etymology.story: 1828, in part a back-formation from pseudonymous, in part from German pseudonym and French pseudonyme (adj.), from Greek pseudonymos "having a false name, under a false name," from pseudes "false" (see pseudo-) + onyma, Aeolic dialectal variant of onoma "name" (see name (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 自然的；自然主义的；博物学的

morphology.mnemonic: birth, + 与…有关的/形容词 + …主义者/…专家 + ic

etymology.story: 1840, in reference to the doctrine of naturalism; from natural + -istic.

1590s, "slender riding whip," probably from a Flemish or Low German word akin to Middle Dutch swijch "bough, twig," or swutsche, variant of Low German zwukse "long thin stick, switch," from Germanic *swih- (source also of Old High German zwec "wooden peg," German Zweck "aim, design," originally "peg as a target," Zwick "wooden peg"), perhaps connected with PIE root *swei- (2) "to swing, bend, to turn."

The meaning "device for changing the direction of something or making or breaking a connection" is first recorded 1797.

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 保持/服务 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., preservacioun "protection from disease," from Old French preservacion (13c.), from Medieval Latin preservationem (nominative preservatio), noun of action from past participle stem of preservare (see preserve (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 助动词；辅助者，辅助物；附属机构
令人震惊的；令人惊骇的
使社会化；使社会主义化；使适应社会生活

morphology.mnemonic: socio + 与…有关的/形容词 + ize

etymology.story: 1828, "to render social," from social (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 野蛮的；残酷的；狂怒的；荒凉的
未开化的人；粗鲁的人；残暴成性的人

etymology.story: 1590s, "of good omen" (implied in auspiciously), from Latin auspicium "divination by observing the flight of birds," from auspex (genitive auspicis) + -ous.

morphology.mnemonic: 错误地/不当地 + 战争 + …者/…器

etymology.story: also misbehaviour, late 15c., from mis- (1) + behavior..
also clock-wise, "in the direction of the hands of a clock," 1879, from clock (n.1) + wise (n.)..
late 14c., from Old French benignité "goodness, kindness" (12c.), from Latin benignitatem (nominative benignitas), from benignus "kindly, kindhearted" (see benign)..
mid-13c., "timid, fearful, full of terror," from fright (n.) + -ful.

meanings.definitionCn: 大厅；休息室；会客室；游说议员的团体
休眠的； 静止的； 隐匿的

etymology.story: late 14c., "fixed in place," from Old French dormant (12c.), present participle of dormir "to sleep," from Latin dormire "to sleep," from PIE root *drem- "to sleep" (source also of Old Church Slavonic dremati "to sleep, doze," Greek edrathon "I slept," Sanskrit drati "sleeps").

meanings.definitionCn: 承认；答谢；报偿；告知已收到

etymology.story: 1550s, a blend of Middle English aknow (from Old English oncnawan "understand," from on + cnawan "recognize;" see know) and Middle English knowlechen "admit, acknowledge" (c.
"an equal distribution of weight," 1650s, a contraction of the phrase equal poise (1550s); see equal (adj.) + poise (n.)..
1728, perhaps a back-formation from amelioration on pattern of French améliorer.
mid-15c., from Middle French décrépit (15c.), from Latin decrepitus "very old, infirm," from de- "down" (see de-) + *crepitus, past participle of crepare "to crack, break" (see raven)..
"having all sides equal," 1560s, from Late Latin aequilateralis, from aequi- (see equal (adj.)) + lateralis (see lateral).

meanings.definitionCn: 一队；民防团；地方武装团队

etymology.story: 1640s (in Anglo-Latin from early 14c.), shortening of posse comitatus "the force of the county" (1620s, in Anglo-Latin from late 13c.), from Medieval Latin posse "body of men, power," from Latin posse "have power, be able" (see potent) + comitatus "of the county," genitive of Late Latin word for "court palace" (see comitatus).
1510s, "act of overthrowing," from over- + throw (n.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 遗传的， 遗传性的； 可继承的， 世袭的

etymology.story: early 15c., "transmitted in a line of progeny," hereditarie, from Latin hereditarius "inherited; of or relating to an inheritance," from hereditas "heirship, inheritance" (see heredity).

"to stop the flow of" (especially of blood), early 14c., from Old French estanchier "cause to cease flowing (of blood), stop, hinder; extinguish (of fire); tire, exhaust, drain" (Modern French étancher), from Vulgar Latin *stancare, perhaps contracted from *stagnicare, from Latin stagnum "pond, pool" (see stagnate).
1640s, "quick remark," from French repartie "an answering blow or thrust" (originally a fencing term), noun use of fem.

late 14c., from Old French preambule (13c.) and directly from Medieval Latin preambulum, neuter adjective used as a noun, properly "preliminary," from Late Latin praeambulus "walking before," from Latin prae- "before" (see pre-) + ambulare "to walk" (see amble (v.))..
Old English hordian "to hoard," from the root of hoard (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 特产； 特色菜；专业； 专长
诱捕；诱入陷阱；进入罗网

etymology.story: formerly also insnare, 1570s, from en- (1) "make, put in" + snare (n.).

1590s, from Middle French squalide and directly from Latin squalidus "rough, coated with dirt, filthy," related to squales "filth," squalus "filthy," squalare "be covered with a rough, stiff layer, be coated with dirt, be filthy," of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 一致的，协调的；(话语等)条理清楚的

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + _here + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1550s, from Middle French cohérent (16c.), from Latin cohaerentem (nominative cohaerens), present participle of cohaerere "cohere," from com- "together" (see co-)  + haerere "to stick" (see hesitation)..

meanings.definitionCn: 不可侵犯的， 不容亵渎的， 不容破坏的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 暴力/违反 + able

etymology.story: mid-15c., "that is to be kept without violation" (of an oath, etc.), from Latin inviolabilis "inviolable, invulnerable," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + violabilis "that may be injured, easily wounded," from violare "to do violence to" (see violation).

meanings.definitionCn: 秘密的，私下的；偷偷摸摸的

etymology.story: 1560s, from Latin clandestinus "secret, hidden," from clam "secretly," from adverbial derivative of base of celare "to hide" (see cell), perhaps on model of intestinus "internal."

meanings.definitionCn: 插曲；一段情节；插话；有趣的事件

etymology.story: 1670s, "commentary between two choric songs in a Greek tragedy," also "an incidental narrative or digression within a story, poem, etc.," from French épisode or directly from Greek epeisodion "an episode," literally "an addition," noun use of neuter of epeisodios "coming in besides," from epi "in addition" (see epi-) + eisodos "a coming in, entrance" (from eis "into" + hodos "way").

meanings.definitionCn: 回答者， 响应者； 调查对象； 被告

etymology.story: 1520s, "one who answers," from Latin respondentem (nominative respondens), present participle of respondere (see respond)..

early 14c., "to estimate the worth of, to appraise," from Old French ponderer "to weigh, poise" (14c., Modern French pondérer) and directly from Latin ponderare "ponder, consider, reflect," literally "to weigh," from pondus (genitive ponderis) "weight" (see pound (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 越越限度；违反(法律规则)

etymology.story: late 15c., from Middle French transgresser (14c.), from Latin transgressus, past participle of transgredi "to step across, step over" (see transgression).

morphology.mnemonic: 对/向 + 投/掷 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1660s, "thrust forth, push out," from Latin exertus/exsertus, past participle of exerere/exserere "thrust out, put forth," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + serere "attach, join; arrange, line up" (see series).

meanings.definitionCn: 过度地；不适当地；不正当地

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + two二 + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: late 14c., "without due moderation; improperly, unsuitably;" see undue + -ly (2).

late 14c., "contemptuous, arrogant, showing haughty disregard of others," from Latin insolentem (nominative insolens) "arrogant, immoderate," also "unaccustomed, unwonted," literally "unusual, unfamiliar," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + solentem, present participle of solere "be accustomed, be used to; cohabit with," from Proto-Italic *sol-e-.

mid-12c., from Old French guile "deceit, wile, fraud, ruse, trickery," probably from Frankish *wigila "trick, ruse" or a related Germanic source, from Proto-Germanic *wih-l- (source also of Old Frisian wigila "sorcery, witchcraft,"  Old English wig "idol," Gothic weihs "holy," German weihen "consecrate"), from PIE root *weik- (2) "consecrated, holy.".

meanings.definitionCn: 休眠； 催眠状态； 冬眠； 隐匿

etymology.story: "situated in the rear, pertaining to the rear, toward the back," late 14c., probably from an unrecorded Old English adjective from hinder (adv.) "behind, back, afterward," but treated as a comparative of hind (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 名声好的， 高尚的； 受尊敬的； 值得信赖的

etymology.story: 1610s, from repute + -able.

morphology.mnemonic: 使/变成 + tr + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1844, from betroth + -al (2).

meanings.definitionCn: 附加物， 附件； 附加语， 修饰语

etymology.story: 1590s, from Latin adiunctus "closely connected, joined, united," past participle of adiungere "join to" (see adjoin).

meanings.definitionCn: 无私的， 为他人着想的

etymology.story: 1853, from French altruiste (adj.), from altruisme (see altruism) + -ic..

1530s (in Latin form sycophanta), "informer, talebearer, slanderer," from Middle French sycophante and directly from Latin sycophanta, from Greek sykophantes "false accuser, slanderer," literally "one who shows the fig," from sykon "fig" (see fig) + phainein "to show" (see phantasm).

"one who translates spoken languages; a translator of written texts," late 14c., from Old French interpreteor, entrepreteur, from Late Latin interpretator "an explainer," agent noun from interpretari "explain, expound" (see interpret)..

sea-storm of severest intensity, 1550s, a partially deformed adoptation from Spanish huracan (Gonzalo Fernandez de Oviedo y Valdés, "Historia General y Natural de las Indias," 1547-9), furacan (in the works of Pedro Mártir De Anghiera, chaplain to the court of King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella and historian of Spanish explorations), from an Arawakan (W.

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + culp + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: "to clear from suspicion of wrong or guilt," 1650s, from Medieval Latin exculpatus, past participle of exculpare, from Latin ex culpa, from ex "from" (see ex-) + culpa ablative of culpa "blame, fault."
1580s, from mis- (1) + count (n.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 次要的,二级的;中级的,第二的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Latin secundarius "pertaining to the second class, inferior," from secundus (see second (adj.)).
1510s, "to shake slightly, jostle," apparently a frequentative of jog (v.), though attested earlier than it.

meanings.definitionCn: [遗] 转基因动物，转化的动物
冷酷的；不知过错的；坚持不懈的；不屈不挠的

morphology.mnemonic: to bite back, + 没有…的/无…的

etymology.story: 1590s, from remorse + -less.

meanings.definitionCn: 冒犯，有意冒犯；面对；公开侮辱
注意的；深切注意的；深切留心的

etymology.story: "cautious, wary," 1540s, from heed (n.) + -ful..
1630s, from Medieval Latin sermonizari, from Latin sermo (see sermon).

meanings.definitionCn: 进入；接入；到达；享用权；入口
原始的,远古的,早期的;粗糙的,简单的

morphology.mnemonic: 第一/首要 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: Old English ofercuman "to reach, overtake," also "to conquer, prevail over," from ofer (see over) + cuman "to come" (see come (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 总数， 和；金额；算术题

etymology.story: early 14c., "to count, count up, calculate, reckon," from Old French sommer "to count, add up," or directly from Medieval Latin summare, from summa (see sum (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 对/向 + 石 + 使…/具有…的

meanings.definitionCn: 使负义务；强使，强迫；对…施以恩惠
有责任的，有义务的；必需的

etymology.story: 1540s, "to bind, connect;" 1660s, "to put under moral obligation," back-formation from obligation, or else from Latin obligatus, past participle of obligare (see oblige).

meanings.definitionCn: 视野范围，见地，知识范围

etymology.story: "to know, understand, take cognizance of," a word surviving mainly in Scottish and northern England dialect, from Middle English kennen, "make known; give instruction to; be aware, know, have knowledge of, know how to; recognize by sight; see, catch sight of," a very common verb, from Old English cennan "make known, declare, acknowledge" (in late Old English also "to know"), originally "cause to know, make to know," causative of cunnan "to become acquainted with, to know" (see can (v.)).
1590s, "pass off in the form of a vapor or liquid," from Middle French transpirer (16c.), from Latin trans- "through" (see trans-) + spirare "to breathe" (see spirit (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 粘附 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French eretical, heretical and directly from Medieval Latin haereticalis, from haereticus (see heretic)..

meanings.definitionCn: 键入；锁上；调节…的音调；提供线索
厌恶人类；厌世，愤世嫉俗

morphology.mnemonic: 错误地/不当地 + 人类 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1650s, from Greek misanthropia "hatred of mankind," from misanthropos (see misanthrope)..

meanings.definitionCn: 演说者；演讲者；雄辩家；原告

etymology.story: late 14c., "one who pleads or argues for a cause," from Anglo-French oratour (Modern French orateur), from Latin orator "speaker," from orare "to speak, speak before a court or assembly, pray, plead," from PIE root *or- "to pronounce a ritual formula" (source also of Sanskrit aryanti "they praise," Homeric Greek are, Attic ara "prayer," Hittite ariya- "to ask the oracle," aruwai- "to revere, worship").
1590s, from penury + -ous, or else from Medieval Latin penuriosus, from Latin penuria "penury." Originally "poverty-stricken, in a state of penury;" meaning "stingy" is first attested 1630s.

morphology.mnemonic: a short time; + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1570s, "comedic entertainment," from merry + -ment.

meanings.definitionCn: 平凡的，没有特色的；难以区别的；莫可名状的
不属任何类型的人；难以形容的人或物

morphology.mnemonic: 非/无 + 向下/移除/取消 + 写

etymology.story: 1680s, "not hitherto described," in scientific usage, coined from non- + Latin descriptus, past participle of describere (see describe).

meanings.definitionCn: 少说，少报；保守地说；有意轻描淡写

etymology.story: 1781, from under + state (v.).
late 13c., from Latin centurionem (nominative centurio), "Roman army officer, head of a centuria" (a group of one hundred); see century..

"insect," 1620s (earliest reference is to bedbugs), of unknown origin, probably but not certainly from or influenced by Middle English bugge "something frightening, scarecrow" (late 14c.), a meaning obsolete since the "insect" sense arose except in bugbear (1570s) and bugaboo (q.v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 飞驰；急速进行；急急忙忙地说

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 知道 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1620s, from Middle French prescient (15c.) and directly from Latin praescientem (nominative praesciens), present participle of praescire (see prescience)..

meanings.definitionCn: 挑剔的；受批判的（名词censoriousness，副词censoriously）

morphology.mnemonic: 发光/闪耀 + …者/…器 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: "fond of criticizing," 1530s, from Latin censorius "pertaining to a censor," also "rigid, severe," from censor (see censor (n.)).
mid-15c., sompnolent, from Old French sompnolent (Modern French somnolent) or directly from Latin somnolentus "sleepy, drowsy," from somnus "sleep" (see Somnus).

1580s, "intercept" (obsolete), a back-formation from intervention, or else from Latin intervenire "to come between, intervene; interrupt; stand in the way, oppose, hinder," from inter "between" (see inter-) + venire "to come" (see venue).
early 15c., from Latin dissentire "differ in sentiments, disagree, be at odds, contradict, quarrel," from dis- "differently" (see dis-) + sentire "to feel, think" (see sense (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 顶点；[昆] 头顶；[天] 天顶

etymology.story: 1560s, "the point opposite the base in geometry," from Latin vertex "highest point," literally "the turning point," originally "whirling column, whirlpool," from vertere "to turn" (see versus).

meanings.definitionCn: 启示；揭露；出乎意料的事；被揭露的真相

etymology.story: part of the ear, 1650s, from Latin auricula "ear," diminutive of auris (see ear (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: [细胞][组织] 卵；卵子；卵形装饰

etymology.story: (plural ova), 1706, from Latin ovum "egg," cognate with Greek oon, Old Norse egg, Old English æg, all perhaps from PIE root *awi- (see egg (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 严厉的责难；擦破的皮肤

etymology.story: early 15c., from Late Latin excoriatus, past participle of excoriare "flay, strip off the hide," from Latin ex- "off" (see ex-) + corium "hide, skin" (see corium).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 呼叫/声音 + 使…/具有…的

meanings.definitionCn: 遗腹的；父亡后出生的；死后的；身后的

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之后 + hum + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "born after the death of the originator" (author or father), from Late Latin posthumus, from Latin postumus "last, last-born," superlative of posterus "coming after, subsequent" (see posterior).

1550s, "state of being crowded" (now obsolete); 1640s, "fact of occurring often;" from Latin frequentia "an assembling in great numbers, a crowding; crowd, multitude, throng," from frequentem (see frequent).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之下 + 投/掷 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "obedience, submission; servitude, bondage; lordship, control," from Anglo-French subjectioun, Old French subjection "submission; subjugation; inferior condition; captivity" (12c., Modern French sujétion), from Latin subjectionem (nominative subjectio) "a putting under," noun of action from past participle stem of subicere (see subject (n.))..
mid-14c., "sternness, harshness," from Old French austerite "harshness, cruelty" (14c.) and directly from Late Latin austeritatem (nominative austeritas), from austerus (see austere).

meanings.definitionCn: 导弹的；可投掷的；用以发射导弹的
动态的；动力的；动力学的；有活力的
部门；扇形，扇区；象限仪；函数尺

etymology.story: 1560s, "section of a circle between two radii," from Late Latin sector "section of a circle," in classical Latin "a cutter, one who cuts," from sectus, past participle of secare "to cut" (see section (n.)).

late 15c., from obsolete Middle English demean "handle, manage, conduct," later "behave in a certain way" (early 14c.), from Old French demener (11c.) "to guide, conduct; to live, dwell," from de- "completely" (see de-) + mener "to lead, direct," from Latin minare "to threaten," in Late Latin "to drive (a herd of animals);" see menace.

meanings.definitionCn: 沉默寡言的；无言的，不太说话的

etymology.story: "habitually silent," 1771, back-formation from taciturnity, or from French taciturne (15c.), from Latin taciturnus "not talkative, noiseless.".

meanings.definitionCn: 学究；书呆子；卖弄学问的人；空谈家

etymology.story: 1580s, "schoolmaster," from Middle French pédant (1560s) or directly from Italian pedante, literally "teacher, schoolmaster," of uncertain origin, apparently an alteration of Late Latin paedagogantem (nominative paedagogans), present participle of paedagogare (see pedagogue).

meanings.definitionCn: 抢夺， 掠夺； 蹂躏， 破坏
疑问，质问；疑问号 ；[计] 查询

etymology.story: early 13c., from Old French degré (12c.) "a step (of a stair), pace, degree (of relationship), academic degree; rank, status, position," said to be from Vulgar Latin *degradus "a step," from Late Latin degredare, from Latin de- "down" (see de-) + gradus "step" (see grade (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 水 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: "containing no water," 1809, a modern coinage from Greek an-, privative prefix (see an- (1)), + hydor "water" (see water (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 以对话体写的文章；谈话，会话

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 说话 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: mid-15c., "discourse," from Latin colloquium "conference, conversation," literally "a speaking together," from com- "together" (see com-) + -loquium "speaking," from loqui "to speak" (see locution).

1650s, from French cogent "necessary, urgent" (14c.), from Latin cogentem (nominative cogens), present participle of cogere "to curdle; to compel; to collect," literally "to drive together," from com- "together" (see co-) + agere "to drive" (see act (n.))..

1590s, from Latin subiacentem (nominative subiacens) "lying beneath," present participle of subiacere "to lie underneath, lie near, adjoin," from sub- "under," also "close to" (see sub-) + iacere "to throw" (see jet (v.))..

morphology.mnemonic: 数/数量 + 使…/具有…的 + …者/…器

etymology.story: 1540s, from Late Latin numerator "counter, numberer," agent noun from numerat-, past participle stem of numerare "to count, number," from numerus "a number" (see number (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 使成管状；把…装管；用管输送

etymology.story: 1610s, from Middle French tube (15c.), from Latin tubus "tube, pipe," of unknown origin.

morphology.mnemonic: 船 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French naval (14c.) and directly from Latin navalis "pertaining to a ship or ships," from navis "ship," from PIE *nau- (2) "boat" (source also of Sanskrit nauh, accusative navam "ship, boat;" Armenian nav "ship;" Greek naus "ship," nautes "sailor;" Old Irish nau "ship;" Welsh noe "a flat vessel;" Old Norse nor "ship").

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 悬挂/称量/支付

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin expendere "pay out, weigh out money," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + pendere "to pay, weigh" (see pendant).

meanings.definitionCn: [眼科] 近视；目光短浅，缺乏远见

etymology.story: "short-sightedness," 1727, medical Latin, from Late Greek myopia "near-sightedness," from myops "near-sighted," literally "closing the eyes," from myein "to shut" (see mute (adj.)) + ops (genitive opos) "eye" (see eye (n.))..
1630s, from assimilated form of in- (1) "not, opposite of" + legible.

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + animal of vast size + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1550s, "to point out," from Latin demonstratus, past participle of demonstrare "to indicate, describe" (see demonstration).

meanings.definitionCn: 爱他主义者；利他主义者

etymology.story: 1842, from French; see altruism + -ist..

mid-14c., from Latin concupiscentia "eager desire," from concupiscens, present participle of concupiscere, inceptive of concupere "to be very desirous of," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + cupere "to long for" (see cupidity).

meanings.definitionCn: 吹牛，炫耀；喇叭或号角嘹亮的吹奏声
寂寞的；悲哀的；阴沉的；忧郁的

etymology.story: late 13c., with -ful, from Middle English dole "grief" (early 13c.), from Old French doel (Modern French deuil), from Late Latin dolus "grief," from Latin dolere "suffer, grieve," which is of uncertain origin.
1530s, from Latin vitiatus, past participle of vitiare "to make faulty, injure, spoil, corrupt," from vitium "fault, defect, blemish, crime, vice" (see vice (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 双手灵巧的；怀有二心的

morphology.mnemonic: ambi + adj. 右边的，右侧的 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1640s, with -ous, from ambidexter (adj.) "double-dealing" (1610s), from French ambidextre or directly from Latin ambidexter, literally "right-handed on both sides," from ambi- "both" (see ambi-) + dexter "right-handed" (see dexterity).

meanings.definitionCn: 严峻的；简朴的；苦行的；无装饰的

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old French austere (Modern French austère) and directly from Latin austerus "dry, harsh, sour, tart," from Greek austeros "bitter, harsh," especially "making the tongue dry" (originally used of fruits, wines), metaphorically "austere, harsh," from PIE *saus- "dry" (see sere (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 鲜艳的;生动的,栩栩如生的

etymology.story: 1630s, from French vivide and perhaps also directly from Latin vividus "spirited, animated, lively, full of life," from vivus "alive," from PIE *gweie- (1) "to live" (see bio-).
early 15c., from Old French fraternel "brotherly, fraternal," and directly from Medieval Latin fraternalis, from Latin fraternus "friendly, closely allied," literally "brotherly" (see fraternity).
early 14c., "consent, approval," from Old French assent, a back-formation from assentir (see assent (v.))..
1777, back-formation from adulation..

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 推/驱动 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French compulsion, from Latin compulsionem (nominative compulsio) "a driving, urging," noun of action from past participle stem of compellere "compel" (see compel).
early 15c., "having steps or ridges," from Medieval Latin gradualis, from Latin gradus "step" (see grade (n.)).
mid-14c., "contaminate, impair the purity of," from Latin corruptus, past participle of corrumpere (see corrupt (adj.)).
1610s, from Latin praecludere "to close, shut off; hinder, impede," from prae- "before, ahead" (see pre-) + claudere "to shut" (see close (v.)).
a salt of phosphoric acid, 1795, from French phosphate (1787), from phosphore (see phosphorus) + -ate (3)..

"gigantic statue," late 14c., from Latin colossus "a statue larger than life," from Greek kolossos "gigantic statue," which is of unknown origin, used by Herodotus of giant Egyptian statues, and used by Romans of the bronze Helios at the entrance to the harbor of Rhodes.

meanings.definitionCn: 传输；传播；发射；传达；遗传

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + memory记忆 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1590s, from Latin commemoratus, past participle of commemorare "bring to remembrance" (see commemoration).

meanings.definitionCn: 科学技术；工业技术；应用科学

morphology.mnemonic: 技术 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1610s, "a discourse or treatise on an art or the arts," from Greek tekhnologia "systematic treatment of an art, craft, or technique," originally referring to grammar, from tekhno- (see techno-) + -logy.

1550s, "an index, list, catalogue," from Late Latin repertorium "inventory, list," from Latin repertus, past participle of reperire "to find, get, invent," from re-, intensive prefix (see re-), + parire, archaic form of paerere "produce, bring forth," from PIE root *pere- (1) "to bring forth" (see pare).

meanings.definitionCn: 冷漠，无兴趣，漠不关心；无感情

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 感情/疾病 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1580s, from Middle French médiocre (16c.), from Latin mediocris "of middling height or state, moderate, ordinary," figuratively "mediocre, mean, inferior," originally "halfway up a mountain," from medius "middle" (see medial (adj.)) + ocris "jagged mountain" (cognate with Greek okris "peak, point," Welsh ochr "corner, border," Latin acer "sharp;" see acrid).
"matter used in vaccination," 1846, from French vaccin, noun use of adjective, from Latin vaccina, fem.
early 15c., from Old French credulité (12c.), from Latin credulitatem (nominative credulitas) "easiness of belief, rash confidence," noun of quality from credulus (see credulous)..

meanings.definitionCn: 乐趣；滋味，风味; 兴趣

etymology.story: 1670s, from French zeste "piece of orange or lemon peel used as a flavoring," of unknown origin.

Old English drugað, drugoð "drought, dryness, desert," from Proto-Germanic *drugothaz, from Germanic root *dreug- "dry" (cf high/height) with *-itho, Germanic suffix for forming abstract nouns (see -th (2)).

meanings.definitionCn: 公正的， 没有偏见的

root.meaning: dot marking end of a sentence

morphology.mnemonic: dot marking end of a sentence + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

root.meaning: hinged flap on the side of a table

morphology.mnemonic: hinged flap on the side of a table + let

etymology.story: 1787 as a term in botany; 1867 as a term in printing and publication; diminutive of leaf (n.) with -let..
1630s, from Latin sapidus "savory, having a taste," from sapere (see sapient).
1821, apparently a back-formation from gullibility.

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 脚/儿童 + ite

etymology.story: "expressing praise," 1550s, from Middle French laudatoire and directly from Late Latin laudatorius, from Latin laudare "to praise" (see laud)..

early 14c., "quality of being humble," from Old French umelite "humility, modesty, sweetness" (Modern French humilité), from Latin humilitatem (nominative humilitas) "lowness, small stature; insignificance; baseness, littleness of mind," in Church Latin "meekness," from humilis "humble" (see humble (adj.)).
mid-15c., from Middle French comparatif, from Latin comparativus "pertaining to comparison," from comparat-, past participle stem of comparare (see comparison).

morphology.mnemonic: 力量/能力 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin potentem (nominative potens) "powerful," present participle of *potere "be powerful," from potis "powerful, able, capable; possible;" of persons, "better, preferable; chief, principal; strongest, foremost," from PIE root *poti- "powerful; lord" (source also of Sanskrit patih "master, husband," Greek posis, Lithuanian patis "husband").

meanings.definitionCn: 迟疑的；踌躇的；犹豫不定的

etymology.story: 1640s, probably a back-formation from hesitancy, or else from Latin haesitantem.
1640s, from Latin cognatus "of common descent," from com- "together" (see co-) + gnatus, past participle of gnasci, older form of nasci "to be born" (see genus).

meanings.definitionCn: 开始， 开端； 毕业典礼

etymology.story: late 13c., "beginning," from Old French comencement "beginning, start" (Modern French commencement), from comencier (see commence).

meanings.definitionCn: 有功绩的；有价值的；值得称赞的

etymology.story: early 15c., "deserving of divine grace," from Latin meritorius "that for which money is paid, that by which money is earned," from meritus, past participle of merere "to earn" (see merit (n.)).
1530s, from Latin recantare "recall, revoke," from re- "back" (see re-) + cantare "to chant" (see chant (v.)).
"curl hair," 1560s, of obscure origin.
viewing apparatus on a submarine, 1899, formed in English from peri- "around" + -scope "instrument for viewing." Earlier (1865) a technical term in photography.
Old English wræððu "anger," from wrað "angry" (see wroth) + -þu, from Proto-Germanic -itho (as in strength, width etc.; see -th (2))..

meanings.definitionCn: 确立，牢固；用壕沟围住；挖掘

etymology.story: also intrench, 1550s, implied in intrenched, from en- (1) "make, put in" + trench (n.).

"principle, opinion, or dogma maintained as true by a person, sect, school, etc.," properly "a thing held (to be true)," early 15c., from Latin tenet "he holds," third person singular present indicative of tenere "to hold, grasp, keep, have possession, maintain," also "reach, gain, acquire, obtain; hold back, repress, restrain;" figuratively "hold in mind, take in, understand."

The Latin word is from PIE root *ten- "to stretch" (source also of Sanskrit tantram "loom," tanoti "stretches, lasts;" Persian tar "string;" Lithuanian tankus "compact," i.e.

morphology.mnemonic: in + 关闭 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: "characterized by including a great deal, leaving little out," c.

mid-14c., "specially prepared or arranged display," from Old French spectacle "sight, spectacle, Roman games" (13c.), from Latin spectaculum "a public show, spectacle, place from which shows are seen," from spectare "to view, watch, behold," frequentative form of specere "to look at," from PIE *spek- "to observe" (see scope (n.1))..

in verbal phrase run amok first recorded 1670s, from Malay amuk "attacking furiously." Earlier the word was used as a noun or adjective meaning "a frenzied Malay," originally in the Portuguese form amouco or amuco.

meanings.definitionCn: 剽窃， 抄袭； 剽窃物

etymology.story: 1620s, from -ism + plagiary (n.) "plagiarist, literary thief" (1590s), from Latin plagiarius "kidnapper, seducer, plunderer, one who kidnaps the child or slave of another," used by Martial in the sense of "literary thief," from plagiare "to kidnap," plagium "kidnapping," from plaga "snare, hunting net," perhaps from PIE *plag- (on notion of "something extended"), from root *plak- (1) "to be flat" (see placenta)..
1580s, from Italian motto "a saying, legend attached to a heraldic design," from Late Latin muttum "grunt, word," from Latin muttire "to mutter, mumble, murmur" (see mutter)..

meanings.definitionCn: 熟识的；知晓的；有知识的

etymology.story: early 13c., "personally known;" past participle adjective from acquaint (v.).

1620s, "young, youthful," from Latin iuvenilis "of or belonging to youth, youthful," from iuvenis "young person, one in the flower of his or her age" (in Roman use, the period just beyond adolescence, from age 21 or 25 to 40), noun use of an adjective meaning "young" (source also of French jeune; see young (adj.)).

root.meaning: be conscious inwardly of (one's state or condition) is from 1680s. Meaning

morphology.mnemonic: be conscious inwardly of (one's state or condition) is from 1680s. Meaning  + 有…倾向的 + ity

etymology.story: 1803, from sensitive + -ity.
1750, from Latin pervas-, past participle stem of pervadere (see pervade) + -ive..

morphology.mnemonic: 劳动/工作 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: late 14c., "hard-working, industrious," from Old French laborios "arduous, wearisome; hard-working" (12c., Modern French laborieux), from Latin laboriosus "toilsome, wearisome, troublesome," also "inclined to labor, industrious," from labor "toil, exertion" (see labor (n.)).
1680s, from French spasmodique, from Medieval Latin spasmodicus, from Greek spasmodes "of the nature of a spasm," from spasmos (see spasm) + -odes "like" (see -oid).

meanings.definitionCn: 有同情心的；赞同的；n.交感神经

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/一起 + 感情/疾病 + ic

etymology.story: 1640s, "pertaining to sympathy," from Modern Latin sympatheticus, from late Greek sympathetikos "having sympathy," from sympathein, from sympathes "having a fellow feeling, affected by like feelings" (see sympathy).

meanings.definitionCn: 诡辩的；强词夺理的；诡辩法的
无关紧要的， 无意义的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 标记 + 使…化/造成 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1650s, "without meaning," also "answering to no purpose," from in- (1) "not, opposite of" + significant.

meanings.definitionCn: 难吃的；没有香味的；令人讨厌的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + sav + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: also unsavoury, early 13c., "tasteless, insipid," from un- (1) "not" + savory (adj.).

"enormous," 1590s, from Gargantua, name of the voracious giant in Rabelais' novels, supposedly from Spanish/Portuguese garganta "gullet, throat," which is from the same imitative root as gargle (v.)..

1590s, from Middle French languide (16c.) and directly from Latin languidus "faint, listless, and sluggish from weakness, fatigue, or want of energy," from languere "be weak, be fatigued, be faint, be listless," from PIE *langu-, from root *(s)leg- "to be slack" (see lax).

morphology.mnemonic: 通过/彻底 + 取/抓 + able

etymology.story: early 15c., "perceptive," from Late Latin perceptibilis "perceptible," from Latin percept-, past participle stem of percipere (see perceive).

meanings.definitionCn: （在内）变动；平行，列为一行；延伸；漫游；射程达到

etymology.story: late 14c., from Latin Aurora, the Roman goddess of dawn, from PIE *ausus- "dawn," also the name of the Indo-European goddess of the dawn, from root *aus- (1) "to shine," especially of the dawn (source also of Greek eos "dawn," auein "to dry, kindle;" Sanskrit usah, Lithuanian ausra "dawn;" Latin auster "south wind," usum "to burn;" Old English east "east")..
1875, from glamour + -ous, with typical dropping of the -u- in derivatives (see -or).
"train (an infant or animal) to forego suckling," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 刺状的；多刺的；尖尖的；难弄的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French desprover "refute, contradict," from des- (see dis-) + prover (see prove).

late 14c., from Old French deriver "to flow, pour out; derive, originate," from Latin derivare "to lead or draw off (a stream of water) from its source" (in Late Latin also "to derive"), from phrase de rivo (de "from" + rivus "stream;" see rivulet).

meanings.definitionCn: 痛苦的；忧虑的；贫困的；受损的
使痛苦；使紧张；使困苦（distress的过去分词）

etymology.story: past participle adjective from distress.
also reconnoitre, 1707, "make a survey," from older French reconnoitre (Modern French reconnaître), from Old French reconoistre "to identify" (see recognize)..

mid-15c., "pleasure," from Medieval Latin complacentia "satisfaction, pleasure," from Latin complacentem (nominative complacens), present participle of complacere "to be very pleasing," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + placere "to please" (see please).

meanings.definitionCn: 维护，坚持；断言；主张；声称

etymology.story: 1670s, from French penchant, noun use of present participle of Old French pencher "to incline," from Vulgar Latin *pendicare, a frequentative formed from Latin pendere "to hang" (see pendant (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 不合理的，不合逻辑的，荒谬的

etymology.story: late 15c., "not endowed with reason" (of beasts, etc.), from Latin irrationalis/inrationalis "without reason, not rational," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + rationalis "of or belonging to reason, reasonable" (see rational (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 冰期的； 冰川的； 寒冷的， 冰冷的； 冷若冰霜的

etymology.story: 1650s, "cold, icy," from French glacial or directly from Latin glacialis "icy, frozen, full of ice," from glacies "ice," probably from a suffixed form of PIE root *gel- (2) "cold, to freeze" (source also of Latin gelu "frost;" see cold (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 娇养；溺爱（等于mollycoddle）；用文火煮

root.meaning: to attend to in practice, to keep, follow

morphology.mnemonic: to attend to in practice, to keep, follow + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "performance of a religious rite," from Latin observationem (nominative observatio) "a watching over, observance, investigation," noun of action from past participle stem of observare (see observe).

late 14c., ascendent, astrological use is earliest, from Middle French ascendant (noun and adjective) and directly from Latin ascendentem (nominative ascendans), present participle of ascendere "to mount, ascend, go up" (see ascend).
1610s, from re- "again" + furbish, on model of French refourbir.

meanings.definitionCn: 象征的；表意的；作为对某事的保证的
碎片,小部分,一点儿;分数

morphology.mnemonic: 打破 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., originally in the mathematical sense, from Anglo-French fraccioun (Old French fraccion, "a breaking," 12c., Modern French fraction) and directly from Late Latin fractionem (nominative fractio) "a breaking," especially into pieces, in Medieval Latin "a fragment, portion," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin frangere "to break (something) in pieces, shatter, fracture," from Proto-Italic *frang-, from a nasalized variant of PIE root *bhreg- "to break" (source also of Sanskrit (giri)-bhraj "breaking-forth (out of the mountains);" Gothic brikan, Old English brecan "to break;" Lithuanian brasketi "crash, crack;" Old Irish braigim "break wind").

meanings.definitionCn: 滤波器；[化工] 过滤器；筛选；滤光器

etymology.story: 1570s (transitive), from French filtrer or from Medieval Latin filtrare, from filtrum "felt" (see filter (n.)).
"pertaining to or characterized by torture," late 15c., from Anglo-French torturous, from Old French tortureus, from Latin tortura (see torture (n.))..

1550s, "wait, defer action," from Latin expectare/exspectare "await, look out for; desire, hope, long for, anticipate; look for with anticipation," from ex- "thoroughly" (see ex-) + spectare "to look," frequentative of specere "to look at" (see scope (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 无情的；不屈不挠的；不可阻挡的；无法改变的

etymology.story: "unyielding, unrelenting," 1550s, from Middle French inexorable and directly from Latin inexorabilis "that cannot be moved by entreaty, unyielding," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + exorabilis "able to be entreated," from exorare "to prevail upon," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + orare "pray" (see orator).
Old English soðian "show to be true," from soð "true" (see sooth).

1570s, "a (social) moving, stirring, agitation," from Middle French émotion (16c.), from Old French emouvoir "stir up" (12c.), from Latin emovere "move out, remove, agitate," from assimilated form of ex- "out" (see ex-) + movere "to move" (see move (v.)).

early 15c., "to fix the amount (of a tax, fine, etc.)," from Anglo-French assesser, from Medieval Latin assessare "fix a tax upon," originally frequentative of Latin assessus "a sitting by," past participle of assidere "to sit beside" (and thus to assist in the office of a judge), from ad- "to" (see ad-) + sedere "to sit" (see sedentary).

meanings.definitionCn: 守财奴；吝啬鬼；（石油工程上用的）凿井机

etymology.story: 1540s, "miserable person, wretch," from Latin miser (adj.) "unhappy, wretched, pitiable, in distress," a word for which "no acceptable PIE pedigree has been found" [de Vaan].

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 写 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., in law, "the right to something through long use," from Old French prescription (13c.) and directly from Latin praescriptionem (nominative praescriptio) "a writing before, order, direction," noun of action from past participle stem of praescribere "write before, prefix in writing; ordain, determine in advance," from prae "before" (see pre-) + scribere "to write" (see script (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 忧虑的；不安的；敏悟的；知晓的

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 抓住/理解 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: late 14c., "capable of perceiving, fitted for mental impression," from Medieval Latin apprehensivus, from Latin apprehensus, past participle of apprehendere (see apprehend).

meanings.definitionCn: 教区的；狭小的；地方范围的

etymology.story: late 14c., "pertaining to a parish," from Anglo-French parochiel (late 13c.), from Old French parochial, from Late Latin parochialis "of a parish" (c.

root.meaning: dot marking end of a sentence

morphology.mnemonic: dot marking end of a sentence + ic + ity

etymology.story: 1805, from French périodicité (1796), from périodique, from Latin periodicus (see periodic)..

meanings.definitionCn: 投降；提交（物）；服从；（向法官提出的）意见；谦恭

etymology.story: late 14c., "act of referring to a third party for judgment or decision," from Old French submission or directly from Latin submissionem (nominative submissio) "a lowering, letting down; sinking," noun of action from past participle stem of submittere "to let down, put down, lower, reduce, yield" (see submit).

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: early 15c., "defense, justification," from Late Latin apologia, from Greek apologia "a speech in defense," from apologeisthai "to speak in one's defense," from apologos "an account, story," from apo- "from, off" (see apo-) + logos "speech" (see lecture (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 可论证的；可议的；可疑的

etymology.story: 1610s, from argue + -able..

"beginning, commencing," 1660s, from Latin incipientem (nominative incipiens), present participle of incipere "begin, take up; have a beginning, originate," from in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + -cipere, comb.

meanings.definitionCn: 有叶的；叶状的；成薄片的

etymology.story: 1660s (implied in foliated), "to apply silver leaf," from Medieval Latin foliatus "leaved, leafy," from Latin folium "a leaf" (see folio).

meanings.definitionCn: 完全的；彻底的；无条件的

etymology.story: "False & malicious misrepresentation of the words or actions of others, calculated to injure their reputation" [Fowler], mid-15c., from Middle French calomnie (15c.), from Latin calumnia "trickery, subterfuge, misrepresentation, malicious charge," from calvi "to trick, deceive," from PIE root *kel- (6) "to deceive, confuse" (source also of Greek kelein "to bewitch, seduce, beguile," Gothic holon "to deceive," Old Norse hol "praise, flattery," Old English hol "slander," holian "to slander")..

meanings.definitionCn: 有洞察力的；聪颖的；敏锐的

etymology.story: 1630s, formed as an adjective to perspicacity, from Latin perspicax "sharp-sighted, having the power of seeing through; acute" (see perspicacity).

meanings.definitionCn: 改名的；微行的；隐姓埋名的

morphology.mnemonic: 低于/不足 + 皮肤 + ic

etymology.story: 1830, from hypo- "under" + derma "skin" + -ic.

meanings.definitionCn: 喧闹；活跃；裙撑；热闹的活动

etymology.story: "be active," 1570s (bustling "noisy or excited activity" is from early 15c.), frequentative of Middle English bresten "to rush, break," from Old English bersten (see burst (v.)), influenced by Old Norse buask "to make oneself ready" (see busk (v.)), or it might be from busk (v.) via a frequentative form buskle.

meanings.definitionCn: 极大的，巨大的；惊人的；极好的

etymology.story: 1630s, "awful, dreadful, terrible," from Latin tremendus "fearful, to be dreaded, terrible," literally "to be trembled at," gerundive form of tremere "to tremble" (see tremble (v.)).

early 15c., from Middle French condenser (14c.) or directly from Latin condensare "to make dense," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + densare "make thick," from densus "dense, thick, crowded," a word used of crowds, darkness, clouds, etc.

morphology.mnemonic: any kind of corporeal matter + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1650s, "to make real, to give substance to," from Modern Latin substantiatus, past participle of substantiare, from Latin substantia "being, essence, material" (see substance).

meanings.definitionCn: 不同种类的，各式各样的

etymology.story: mid-13c., "not alike" (sense now in diverse); late 13c., "separate, distinct; various," from Old French divers (11c.) "different, various, singular, odd, exceptional, wretched, treacherous, perverse," from Latin diversus "turned different ways," in Late Latin "various," past participle of divertere (see divert).
1540s, from Latin conspicuus "visible, open to view, striking," from conspicere "to look at, observe, see, notice," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + specere (see scope (n.1)).

1769, from Latin raucus "hoarse" (also source of French rauque, Spanish ronco, Italian rauco), related to ravus "hoarse," from PIE echoic base *reu- "make hoarse cries" (source also of Sanskrit rayati "barks," ravati "roars;" Greek oryesthai "to howl, roar;" Latin racco "a roar;" Old Church Slavonic rjevo "I roar;" Lithuanian rekti "roar;" Old English rarian "to wail, bellow").

meanings.definitionCn: 模糊的，不清楚的；朦胧的；难以清楚辨认的

etymology.story: 1580s, "not seen or heard clearly," from Latin indistinctus "not distinguishable, confused, obscure," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + distinctus (see distinct).

early 15c., "holding of a tenement," from Anglo-French and Old French tenure "a tenure, estate in land" (13c.), from Old French tenir "to hold," from Vulgar Latin *tenire, from Latin tenere "to hold" (see tenet).

1610s, from French obésité and directly from Latin obesitas "fatness, corpulence," from obesus "that has eaten itself fat," past participle of obdere "to eat all over, devour," from ob "over" (see ob-) + edere "eat" (see edible)..

meanings.definitionCn: 给…盖上品质证明印记；使具有…标志

etymology.story: 1721, official stamp of purity in gold and silver articles, from Goldsmiths' Hall in London, site of the assay office; see hall + mark (n.1).

root.meaning: device that breaks an electrical circuit

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + device that breaks an electrical circuit + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: 柔和的，温和的；文雅的，娴雅的

etymology.story: early 15c., "gracious, kindly, pleasant, delightful," from Latin suavis "agreeable, sweet, pleasant (to the senses), delightful," from PIE root *swad- "sweet, pleasant" (see sweet (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: （人或动物）不安的，易受惊的；难驾驭的

root.meaning: to shy or be skittish, caper, frolic

morphology.mnemonic: to shy or be skittish, caper, frolic + 有点…/像…

etymology.story: early 15c., "very lively, frivolous," perhaps from Scandinavian base *skyt- (stem of Old Norse skjota "to shoot, launch, move quickly"), from PIE root *skeud- "to shoot, to chase, to throw, to project" (see shoot (v.)).

late 14c., "flow of rhythm in verse or music," from Middle French cadence, from Old Italian cadenza "conclusion of a movement in music," literally "a falling," from Vulgar Latin *cadentia, from neuter plural of Latin cadens, present participle of cadere "to fall" (see case (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + pair + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "reconciliation," from Old French reparacion and directly from Late Latin reparationem (nominative reparatio) "act of repairing, restoration," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin reparare "restore, repair" (see repair (v.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 选举权；投票；参政权；代祷；赞成票

etymology.story: late 14c., "intercessory prayers or pleas on behalf of another," from Old French sofrage "plea, intercession" (13c.) and directly from Medieval Latin suffragium, from Latin suffragium "support, ballot, vote; right of voting; a voting tablet," from suffragari "lend support, vote for someone," conjectured to be a compound of sub "under" (see sub-) + fragor "crash, din, shouts (as of approval)," related to frangere "to break" (see fraction).
also semi-conscious, 1838, from semi- + conscious.

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 喊/叫

etymology.story: late 14c., from Middle French déclamer and directly from Latin declamare "to practice public speaking, to bluster," from de- intensive prefix + clamare "to cry, shout" (see claim (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 临时的，暂时的；中间的；间歇的
过渡时期，中间时期；暂定

etymology.story: "time intervening," 1560s, from Latin interim (adv.) "in the meantime, meanwhile," originally "in the midst of that," from inter- "between" (see inter-) + im, ancient adverb from stem of pronoun is "this, that." As an adjective from c.

morphology.mnemonic: 使成为/放入 + bring about by unusual effort

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to drive by physical force; to try, attempt, strive; to fortify, strengthen a place;" late 14c.

meanings.definitionCn: 易碎的，脆的，易损坏的；虚弱的，脆弱的

etymology.story: 1510s, "liable to sin, morally weak;" c.

meanings.definitionCn: 需细心的，辛苦的，煞费苦心的；勤勉的；刻苦的
辛苦； 勤勉； 刻苦

etymology.story: 1550s (n.), 1690s (adj.), paynes taking, from plural of pain (n.) + present participle of take (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 秘密的；偷偷摸摸的；促进分泌的

etymology.story: "inclined to secrecy," 1815 (implied in secretiveness); see secret (n.) + -ive.

meanings.definitionCn: 健谈者；善谈者；擅长讲故事的人

etymology.story: "storyteller, person skilled in relating anecdotes," 1828, from French raconteur, from raconter "to recount, tell, narrate," from re- (see re-) + Old French aconter "to count, render account" (see account (v.); and compare recount (v.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: 搬运/承担 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1640s, from Latin ferocis, oblique case of ferox "fierce, wild-looking" (see ferocity).
1847, from French colloide (1845), from Greek kolla "glue" + -oeides "form" (see -oid)..
1610s, "pertaining to giants," from Latin gigant- stem of gigas "giant" (see giant) + -ic.

meanings.definitionCn: 数值的；数字的；用数字表示的（等于numeric）

morphology.mnemonic: 数/数量 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1620s, from Latin numerus "a number" (see number (n.)) + suffix -ical.

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 向/朝/在 + just

meanings.definitionCn: 符合条件的;(尤指婚姻等)合适(意)的

etymology.story: early 15c., "fit or proper to be chosen," from Old French eligible "fit to be chosen" (14c.), from Late Latin eligibilis "that may be chosen," from Latin eligere "choose" (see election).

meanings.definitionCn: 能忍耐的；可忍受的；能持久的

etymology.story: 1590s, from be- + smirch.

1744, from French glacier (16c.), from Savoy dialect glacière "moving mass of ice," from Old French glace "ice," from Vulgar Latin *glacia (source also of Old Provençal glassa, Italian ghiaccia), from Latin glacies "ice" (see glacial).
"final demand," 1731, from Modern Latin, from Medieval Latin ultimatum "a final statement," noun use of Latin adjective ultimatum "last possible, final," neuter of ultimatus (see ultimate).
"steal," especially in a small, sly way, 1560s, slang, perhaps from c.

1560s, "distinguished by greatness, renowned," from Latin illustris "lighted, bright, brilliant;" figuratively "distinguished, famous," probably a back-formation from illustrare "make light, light up, illuminate," figuratively "embellish, distinguish, make famous" (see illustration).
1712, "unaware, not marked by conscious thought," from un- (1) "not" + conscious.

"process of reasoning," 1520s, from Latin ratiocinationem (nominative ratiocinatio) "a reasoning, calm reasoning," from past participle stem of ratiocinare "to calculate, deliberate," from ratio (see ratio) + -cinari, which probably is related to conari "to try" (see conation)..

meanings.definitionCn: 空闲的， 闲置的；无用的， 无效的；懒散的

etymology.story: 1580s, from Italian canto "song," from Latin cantus "song" (see chant (v.)).

Old English bliþe "joyous, kind, cheerful, pleasant," from Proto-Germanic *blithiz "gentle, kind" (source also of Old Saxon bliði "bright, happy," Middle Dutch blide, Dutch blijde, Old Norse bliðr "mild, gentle," Old High German blidi "gay, friendly," Gothic bleiþs "kind, friendly, merciful").

meanings.definitionCn: 致敬；招呼；向欢呼；猛发；使像下雹样落下（过去式hailed，过去分词hailed，现在分词hailing，第三人称单数hails）

etymology.story: "frozen rain, pellets of ice falling in showers," Old English hægl, hagol (Mercian hegel) "hail, hailstorm," also the name of the rune for H, from Proto-Germanic *haglaz (source also of Old Frisian heil, Old Saxon, Old High German hagal, Old Norse hagl, German Hagel "hail"), probably from PIE *kaghlo- "pebble" (source also of Greek kakhlex "round pebble")..

meanings.definitionCn: 被动的，消极的；被动语态的

etymology.story: late 14c., in grammatical sense (opposed to active), Old French passif "suffering, undergoing hardship" (14c.) and directly from Latin passivus "capable of feeling or suffering," from pass-, past participle stem of pati "to suffer" (see passion).
1520s, from Latin efficacia "efficacy, efficiency," from efficax (genitive efficacis) "powerful, effectual, efficient," from stem of efficere "work out, accomplish" (see effect (n.)).
"at an early period," early 14c., from betime (c.
1560s, devest (modern spelling is c.
mid-15c., from Latin obliviosus "forgetful, that easily forgets; producing forgetfulness," from oblivion (see oblivion).

meanings.definitionCn: 非正式的；不拘礼节的；随便的；通俗的；日常使用的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "lacking form; not in accordance with the rules of formal logic," from in- (1) "not, opposite of" + formal (adj.).
